unit.name: Compounding Control & Recipe Management System (MES/SCADA)
unit.cfj: Execute, record, and enforce compounding recipes and process parameters

meta.method: Ulwick ODI (Importance × Satisfaction → Opportunity), needs scoring v2

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of safety-critical or quality-critical control defects remaining undetected after simulation
rows.plain: Make sure serious safety or product-quality problems in the controls are found during testing. This helps prevent hidden problems from appearing during real production.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected safety- or quality-critical control defects post-simulation strike at the core of the CFJ, since simulation is the primary gate before live control; when such defects escape, consequences can be catastrophic equipment damage or compromised sterile product batches.
rows.sat_rat: Simulation catches many defects but coverage gaps mean safety- and quality-critical defects routinely remain undetected, a well-known chronic weakness. Extended simulation and test campaigns take substantial preparation time yet still miss defects. Designing simulation coverage that would expose safety/quality-critical defects demands rare test-engineering expertise. Simulation environments and test development are expensive and coverage is still incomplete.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of nonsterile or unintended material entering the batch through a manually established flow path
rows.plain: Make sure unwanted or unclean material cannot enter the product through a path set up by hand. This helps protect the product when the equipment is being operated manually.
rows.imp_rat: Nonsterile material entering the batch via a manual flow path strikes at the core of liquid batching's job of producing spec-compliant, sterile product; when it occurs the whole batch is contaminated and potentially harmful, so severity is catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Manually established flow paths are among the least controlled steps, and misconnections or non-sterile paths remain a recurring source of unintended material ingress. Manually setting up and sanitizing a flow path is time-consuming before transfer can proceed. Correctly establishing an aseptic manual flow path is highly technique-dependent and error-prone. Sterile hoses, connectors, and steam/SIP resources for each manual path add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that residual product, cleaning agents, or hazardous material remain in the batching system at handoff
rows.plain: Make sure no product, cleaning liquid, or dangerous material is left in the mixing equipment when it is handed over. This helps protect the next person who uses it.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product, cleaning agent, or hazardous material remaining at handoff is central to the clean-transfer purpose of the system and carries catastrophic cross-contamination and safety consequences for the next batch.
rows.sat_rat: Retained residues at handoff remain a common finding because complex batching geometry resists complete clearing. Achieving verified handoff cleanliness may require multiple flush cycles that extend the schedule. Confirming complete removal at handoff depends on skilled sampling and interpretation not always available on site. Repeated cleaning and lab verification consume solvents and testing budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual product or hazardous cleaning material remaining in the system at transfer
rows.plain: Make sure no product or dangerous cleaning material is left in the system when it is handed over. This helps protect the people who take responsibility for it next.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or hazardous cleaning material remaining at transfer directly defeats the batching system's job of delivering a clean, correct-composition batch; central to the CFJ and catastrophic since it can poison a downstream batch or harm consumers.
rows.sat_rat: Residual product or cleaning chemical frequently lingers in dead legs and crevices, so clean-at-transfer is inconsistently achieved. Flush-and-verify cycles before transfer take real time and often require repetition. Judging whether a system is truly clean of product and cleaning agents requires process knowledge and sampling skill. Cleaning media, solvents, and verification sampling add cost but are standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loaded control logic allowing an equipment state that compromises sterile processing conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s programmed instructions cannot put it into a condition that makes the clean medicine-processing area unsafe. This helps keep the medicine protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Loaded control logic permitting an equipment state that compromises sterile processing is central to the configure/control job (high relevance), and a sterility breach means product recall and patient risk (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: GMP design review and interlock verification catch most cases, but subtle state combinations affecting sterility still slip through despite mature review practice. Pre-load logic reviews in preparation add days of review effort but are routinely scheduled into validation timelines. Detecting logic that permits sterility-compromising states relies heavily on scarce senior controls and process engineers reading logic by hand. Review labor and rework are budgeted but expensive, especially when defects surface late in preparation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of sterile batch contamination during the interruption or resumption
rows.plain: Prevent the medicine from becoming contaminated when production is stopped and then started again. The process must be brought back safely without exposing the batch to unwanted material.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during interruption/resumption of a sterile batch is highly relevant to maintaining continuous sterility and, when it happens, irreversibly compromises the batch with patient-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: There is little robust automated safeguard for maintaining sterility across interruption and resumption, leaving this a poorly served, deviation-prone situation. Interruptions force hold, re-sanitization, or re-verification that extend the batch timeline. Judging whether sterility survived an interruption and safely resuming demands expert assessment. Holds and potential re-sterilization or scrap on resumption are costly.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm thresholds
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of interlock logic permitting equipment to enter an unsafe state
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s safety locks cannot allow it to enter a dangerous condition. The warning limits must also be set so problems are noticed at the right time.
rows.imp_rat: Interlock logic permitting an unsafe equipment state is central to the software's protective control function (high relevance) and can cause equipment damage, injury, or sterility loss (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established interlock verification and FAT/SAT practices handle the common unsafe-state paths well, though rare untested combinations remain a recurring gap. Interlock verification testing is time-consuming but a standard, planned part of preparation. Confirming interlock logic prevents unsafe states demands experienced safety and controls engineers exercising careful judgement. Verification and simulation resources cost money but are far cheaper than an unsafe-state incident.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that contamination is introduced during a compounding step
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of bringing germs into the product while combining its ingredients.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during a compounding step is somewhat adjacent to recipe execution (relevance 8) but can cause patient death, the worst outcome (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination control depends on procedures and gowning rather than system enforcement, so residual risk remains chronically high. Contamination often only becomes evident at post-hoc testing rather than in real time during the step. Preventing contamination relies heavily on operator aseptic technique and discipline that tools cannot enforce. A contaminated batch is a total loss, and current controls only partially prevent it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a fallback response places equipment or the sterile fill-finish process in an unsafe state
rows.plain: Make sure the backup response puts the equipment and the clean medicine-making process into a safe condition when something goes wrong. This prevents a failure from creating a new danger.
rows.imp_rat: A fallback that places equipment or the sterile process in an unsafe state defeats the safety purpose of fallback logic and is highly central to reliable control; consequences include equipment damage, personnel harm, and destroyed sterile batches.
rows.sat_rat: Fallback logic is designed for safety but is under-tested, so unsafe fallback states remain a real recurring risk. Testing fallback and failure responses adds time and is often abbreviated under schedule pressure. Verifying safe fallback behavior across failure modes requires deep controls and safety expertise. Exhaustive fault-injection testing is costly, so it is frequently limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that calibration is applied to the wrong input or output signal channel
rows.plain: Make sure the calibration is applied to the correct signal connection. Otherwise, the readings or controls for the wrong part of the system could be affected.
rows.imp_rat: Applying calibration to the wrong signal channel corrupts the core configure/control function; the consequence is systematically wrong process readings and control actions that can ruin batches or damage equipment before detection.
rows.sat_rat: Channel mix-ups remain a common calibration error given dense I/O and manual verification despite loop-check practices. Confirming the correct signal channel during calibration adds point-by-point verification time. Correct channel calibration requires careful technicians tracing wiring and I/O maps. Wrong-channel calibration causes rework and re-verification cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect ingredient is charged into the batch
rows.plain: Prevent the wrong ingredient from being added to the medicine. Using the wrong ingredient could make the product unsafe or ineffective.
rows.imp_rat: Charging a wrong ingredient directly defeats the CFJ of executing correct recipes (relevance 10) and can produce toxic or fatal product (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Mature MES barcode-gated charging substantially reduces wrong-ingredient events, though manual overrides and non-serialized items leave residual risk. Barcode/weigh-scan verification at charging happens in-line and adds little delay to the operator's step sequence. Electronic material verification offloads the burden of manual ingredient identification onto the MES, reducing reliance on operator vigilance. Wrong-ingredient batches are costly to scrap, so the verification investment is generally justified and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of charging the wrong ingredient into a supply tank or feed line
rows.plain: Make sure the correct ingredient is put into the supply tank or feed line. Using the wrong ingredient could affect the product being made.
rows.imp_rat: Charging the wrong ingredient into a supply tank or line is a root-cause recipe integrity failure (relevance 10) that can adulterate every downstream batch, a severe outcome (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Where scan-to-confirm and dedicated lines exist wrong-ingredient events are rare, but many plants still rely on manual identity checks that occasionally fail. Verifying ingredient identity before charging adds a checking step to each addition. Correct ingredient routing still leans heavily on operator attention and label reading where manual additions occur. Barcode/RFID verification systems cost to install but prevent expensive wrong-ingredient batch losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing sterile-process containment during emergency shutdown
rows.plain: Avoid letting germs enter or escape when the system is stopped in an emergency. This helps keep the process safely sealed and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Losing sterile containment during emergency shutdown ties the safety-stop function to sterility (high relevance) and directly causes contamination and batch loss (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Emergency shutdown often prioritizes safety over sterility, so containment during an abrupt stop is inconsistently preserved and remains a recurring gap. Containment-preserving shutdown sequencing takes time to close valves in order, which conflicts with the need for rapid stopping. Designing shutdown logic that maintains sterility requires specialized process and validation expertise not always fully captured. Loss of containment can force costly line decontamination and batch loss, which current sequencing only partly avoids.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that connected field devices exchange incorrect process values or control commands after the interface update
rows.plain: Prevent connected devices from sending or receiving the wrong measurements or instructions after their connections are changed. This helps the process run according to the actual conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Field devices exchanging incorrect process values or control commands after an interface update directly corrupts the core control/monitor loop, potentially driving equipment to dangerous states.
rows.sat_rat: Interface mapping and factory/site acceptance testing are established but subtle scaling or mapping errors after updates still slip through. Interface updates are validated in windows, so verification time is planned. Confirming field devices exchange correct values after an interface update requires specialized protocol and integration expertise. Wrong process values can spoil product or damage equipment, and thorough interface testing is resource-heavy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-critical process hazards omitted from interlock and emergency-shutdown logic
rows.plain: Make sure every serious process danger is covered by automatic safeguards that stop or shut down the equipment when needed. Leaving one out could allow a dangerous situation to continue.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting safety-critical hazards from interlock/ESD logic means the DCS cannot protect against events it was never told to detect, central to safe control (relevance 9); consequences are catastrophic and irreversible (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Mature IEC substantially reduces omitted hazards, but residual gaps from human oversight in workshops remain a recognized recurring failure mode. HAZOP and LOPA studies during preparation take weeks of workshops, but they are a scheduled, budgeted activity that rarely bottlenecks the wider project timeline. Complete hazard capture depends heavily on scarce senior process-safety engineers and facilitators whose judgement determines whether a scenario is even identified. The formal PHA process is expensive in expert time, yet cheap relative to the incident cost it prevents, so it is reliably funded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compounding resumes with an incorrect recipe revision or parameter set after a disruption
rows.plain: After a production problem, the process should not restart with the wrong instructions or settings. This helps prevent the product from being made incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Resuming with wrong recipe revision after disruption is a real enforcement gap (relevance 8) that can ruin a batch already partly processed (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Standard MES does version the recipe, but disruption recovery is precisely where checkpoint state and human handoff break down, leaving a real recurring failure mode. Restart after a disruption often stalls while the overseer manually confirms the active recipe revision across MES screens and paper batch records. Correct resumption leans heavily on an experienced overseer remembering exactly where the batch was and which revision governs it, a fragile point of judgement. Wrong-revision restarts can scrap an entire compounded lot, so the financial exposure of this need is high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of control and monitoring disruption during an active sterile fill-finish batch
rows.plain: Keep interruptions to process controls and monitoring as short as possible while a sterile production run is underway. This helps the run continue safely and with needed information available.
rows.imp_rat: Control/monitoring disruption during an active sterile batch directly attacks the continuous monitoring core of the CFJ; even brief blindness during fill can invalidate product and force costly batch rejection.
rows.sat_rat: Redundancy exists in some architectures but seamless bumpless transfer during an active batch is not reliably achieved. Any disruption during an active sterile fill-finish batch directly extends or ruins the batch, and current failover/switchover is rarely fast enough to be invisible. Minimizing disruption during a live batch requires highly skilled operators reacting under pressure with no room for error. Disruption mid-batch typically forces scrapping of high-value sterile product, a severe unrecovered cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of coordinated commands not executed by all intended devices
rows.plain: Make sure every intended machine receives and carries out coordinated instructions. If one machine misses a command, the overall process may not happen as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Coordinated command execution across all intended devices is central to the CFJ of controlling multiple industrial devices; if some devices miss commands, coordinated processes fail. Severity is high because partial execution can leave equipment in unsafe or inconsistent states causing damage or product loss.
rows.sat_rat: Group-command and confirmation practices exist but true transactional all-or-nothing execution across devices is rarely guaranteed, leaving partial-execution gaps. Coordinated commands dispatch nearly simultaneously, so partial execution is a correctness rather than speed problem. Ensuring all devices act relies on engineered coordination logic rather than operator skill. A coordinated command executed by only some devices can leave the process in an inconsistent, costly state.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-boundary breaches at product-contact interfaces during compounding
rows.plain: Prevent breaks in the barriers that keep the product germ-free where the equipment touches it during mixing. This helps keep germs out of the product.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-boundary breaches at product-contact interfaces are contamination events tangential to recipe logic (relevance 7) but potentially lethal (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: MES/SCADA has little visibility into physical sterile-boundary breaches, leaving this need largely dependent on manual practice. Sterile-boundary breaches are typically detected only after the fact, not prevented during the interface event. Aseptic connection integrity relies almost entirely on manual operator technique at product-contact points. Breaches force batch rejection, an expense that current practice poorly averts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of simultaneous commands placing devices in incompatible operating states
rows.plain: Prevent different commands from making equipment move into states that conflict with each other. This helps the equipment work safely and as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Simultaneous commands driving devices into incompatible states is a direct failure of the software's coordination role and highly central to the CFJ; the result is mechanical conflict, equipment damage, or unsafe process states.
rows.sat_rat: Configured interlocks catch known incompatible combinations, but unanticipated simultaneous-command cases remain a recurring source of trouble. Interlock evaluation is fast, so the challenge is completeness of the interlock set rather than speed. Defining all incompatible-state interlocks demands deep process knowledge and is easy to leave incomplete. Incompatible device states can damage equipment or spoil batches, an expensive failure current interlocks partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual material from the previous formulation contaminating the next batch during the recipe change
rows.plain: Keep leftover material from the previous product out of the next batch when changing recipes. This prevents the new batch from being mixed with the wrong product.
rows.imp_rat: Carryover from the previous formulation during a recipe change is core to multi-product batching integrity, and cross-contamination severely compromises the next batch's identity, purity, and safety.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP cycles with rinse-sampling reliably control carryover for most products, though hard-to-clean geometries and potent residuals leave recurring risk. CIP/SIP cleaning between formulations is a lengthy changeover activity. Validated cleaning cycles reduce reliance on operator judgement once the recipe is established. Cleaning agents, water, energy, and downtime make changeover cleaning expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failover leaves critical process parameters without active monitoring or control
rows.plain: Make sure the backup system continues watching and controlling the important process conditions if the main system fails. A switch to the backup must not leave the process unattended.
rows.imp_rat: Failover leaving critical parameters unmonitored/uncontrolled is a direct failure of redundancy meant to sustain the core job; the consequence is a silent loss of oversight that can destroy a sterile batch.
rows.sat_rat: Established redundant SCADA/PLC failover reliably keeps most parameters monitored, though edge cases and misconfigured tags still leave occasional gaps. Failover is designed to be near-instant, so monitoring gaps are usually short in duration. Configuring failover so no critical parameter is orphaned requires strong system-architecture expertise up front. Redundant monitoring paths add hardware and licensing cost but are commonly justified in regulated sterile facilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a manual override moves a critical sterile fill-finish parameter outside approved limits
rows.plain: Prevent a temporary manual change from pushing an important clean-filling setting beyond its approved safe limits. This helps keep the product safe and the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: A manual override moving a critical fill-finish parameter outside approved limits is core to safe control (high relevance); consequences are contaminated sterile product reaching patients (catastrophic severity), though overrides are less frequent than automated paths.
rows.sat_rat: Overrides by design bypass normal automated guards, so soft limits and permission tiers help but consistent enforcement is weaker than for ordinary setpoints. Override actions are immediate, so any limit enforcement must happen inline without slowing the operator's response. Safeguarding manual overrides against unsafe values depends heavily on operator training and vigilance, since overrides are intentionally granted elevated authority. An override that breaches sterile limits can destroy an entire fill-finish lot, a high-value loss that current practice only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which remote process status differs from actual sterile fill-finish equipment conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the status shown from a remote location matches what the filling equipment is actually doing. This helps remote users make decisions based on the real situation.
rows.imp_rat: Divergence between remote status and actual equipment conditions strikes at the core monitoring job; operators acting on false status can allow contamination or dangerous conditions to persist undetected, making both proxies very high.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time data links keep remote views close to actual conditions, but stale data, comms lag and unmonitored variables let the remote picture diverge from reality. Remote status updates propagate quickly, so latency is usually small. Interpreting whether remote status truly reflects equipment condition requires operator experience. Divergent remote status can cause costly wrong decisions, mitigated by standard telemetry at no extra cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of material with compromised sterile status entering the compounding path
rows.plain: Prevent materials whose germ-free condition may have been compromised from entering the mixing process. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Compromised-sterile material entering the path is upstream contamination control (relevance 7) with catastrophic patient-safety stakes (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Status verification is procedural and record-based, so compromised-sterility material can still enter the path when records are incomplete. Sterility status of materials is verified through separate QA workflows that can lag the compounding start. Judging compromised sterile status depends on human review of records rather than automated gating. Using compromised material scraps the batch, a cost only partly controlled by status checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of adding an ingredient not specified for the current programmed step
rows.plain: Add only the ingredient called for in the current step. This prevents the wrong ingredient from being added to the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Adding an unspecified ingredient at a step is a fundamental recipe-integrity failure (relevance 10) and yields a cross-contaminated or adulterated batch requiring disposal (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Programmed step sequencing with valve interlocks reliably prevents out-of-step additions in automated lines, though manual add points remain a residual exposure. Sequenced recipe steps enforce order without pausing the operator. Step interlocks reduce reliance on the operator knowing the correct addition sequence. Interlock logic is inexpensive relative to the batch-loss cost it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical sterile fill-finish alarms are not presented to the remote operator
rows.plain: Make sure the remote operator receives all important warnings about the clean-filling process. This allows problems to be noticed and handled promptly from another location.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to present critical alarms to the remote operator defeats the monitoring purpose central to the CFJ. Severity is catastrophic since an unseen sterile fill-finish alarm can lead to undetected excursions harming patients and destroying batches.
rows.sat_rat: Remote HMI alarm distribution works well when configured, but suppression, filtering and connectivity gaps mean critical alarms can occasionally fail to reach the remote operator. Alarm propagation to remote clients is normally fast, though network latency can delay presentation. Recognizing which alarms are critical still depends on operator configuration and training. Remote alarm distribution systems are standard but well-designed rationalization requires investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compounding beginning with unconfirmed product-contact-path sterility
rows.plain: Do not begin mixing or preparing the product until it is confirmed that every product-contact pathway is germ-free. This prevents contamination from starting at the beginning of the work.
rows.imp_rat: Beginning with unconfirmed path sterility is a contamination-control precondition adjacent to execution (relevance 7) but can cause fatal contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Sterility confirmation is largely a checklist/procedural step, so unconfirmed starts still occur without hard system interlocks. Confirming path sterility before start relies on manual sign-offs that can delay or be shortcut. Operators must interpret sterilization records rather than rely on an automated readiness gate. Starting with unconfirmed sterility risks total batch loss, poorly guarded today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operator intervention needed to accommodate an ingredient property variation compromises the sterile boundary during batching
rows.plain: Let the operator handle differences in an ingredient without breaking the protected clean barrier around the batch. This helps keep the batch free from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Operator intervention to handle ingredient variation breaching the sterile boundary is central to robust sterile batching, and any such breach severely compromises the batch with potential patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: Because the response is manual and ad hoc, each intervention is a fresh opportunity to compromise the boundary, keeping outcomes inconsistent. Improvising around an ingredient property variation mid-batch interrupts and lengthens the run. Accommodating variability without breaching sterility relies almost entirely on scarce operator expertise and judgement in the moment. Interventions and any resulting holds or scrap add unplanned cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compounding proceeds with process parameters outside approved limits
rows.plain: Prevent mixing or preparation from continuing when the process settings are outside the approved limits, so the product is made according to required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Enforcing approved parameter limits is the essence of the scope's job (relevance 10); running outside limits yields out-of-spec or unsafe batches (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipe enforcement with hard/soft limits is mature and widely deployed, giving high consistency though gaps remain for manual steps and unmodeled parameters. Modern MES/SCADA enforces parameter limits in real time so out-of-range conditions are flagged or blocked without delay. Automated interlocks reduce reliance on operator vigilance, though correctly configured limit sets still require skilled recipe engineers. Preventing out-of-limit batches avoids scrapped material and rework, delivering strong cost avoidance where enforcement is configured.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a setpoint change driving an aseptic-process parameter outside its validated range during an active batch
rows.plain: Make sure changing a control target during a running batch cannot push an important clean-processing condition outside its tested safe range. This helps keep the batch under control.
rows.imp_rat: A setpoint change driving an aseptic parameter outside its validated range strikes directly at the software's core job of correctly controlling process parameters (high relevance); in fill-finish this risks batch loss and patient-harming contamination (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature validated-recipe and setpoint-clamping practice in GMP control systems catches most out-of-range setpoints, but misconfigured or unbounded parameters remain a recurring gap. Range checks execute in real time so validated limits are enforced without added operator delay during setpoint entry. Configured alarm and interlock limits shift the burden away from operator judgement, though defining the validated ranges still demands process-validation expertise. Preventing an out-of-range excursion avoids scrapping a costly aseptic batch, and the configuration cost is amortized across many runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading an incorrect ingredient component or lot into the new batch
rows.plain: Make sure the new batch receives the correct ingredient and batch lot. This prevents the wrong material from being used in the product.
rows.imp_rat: Loading an incorrect ingredient or lot directly defeats the CFJ of preparing a correct batch (relevance 10), and the consequences—an entire adulterated or mislabeled batch, potential recall or patient harm—are severe (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Scan-to-confirm against the recipe with weigh-verify is mature and widely deployed, so wrong-ingredient loads are uncommon though manual override and unscanned totes remain residual gaps. Barcode/RFID scanning at material staging adds only seconds and rarely delays the batching start. Verification is largely automated so it no longer depends on an operator memorizing lot numbers, though attentive staging discipline is still required. Scanner infrastructure and MES integration carry moderate cost but prevent expensive scrap of misformulated batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loading a control-logic revision other than the approved revision for the intended fill-finish unit
rows.plain: Make sure the approved control instructions for the intended filling machine are loaded, rather than a different version. This helps the machine operate as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Loading an unapproved control-logic revision defeats the configuration-integrity core of the job and is catastrophic, as running unvalidated logic on sterile fill equipment risks systemic product failure and regulatory violations.
rows.sat_rat: Version control and controlled-download workflows make loading an unapproved revision uncommon where discipline is enforced. Revision verification before load is a quick controlled step in mature change management. Checking approved revision is procedural given version control, requiring modest skill. Loading a wrong revision is costly, so version-control tooling is a justified standard investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that combined parameter drift remains undetected while individual readings remain within approved limits
rows.plain: Detect when several process settings drift together even if each separate reading still appears acceptable, so a developing problem is not missed.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected combined drift while individual readings pass limits is a subtle enforcement gap (relevance 7) that can silently produce nonconforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Single-parameter limit checking is the norm and multivariate monitoring is largely absent, leaving this need essentially unserved. Detecting combined drift requires cross-parameter analysis that no one performs in real time during a shift. Recognizing interacting drift while each reading stays in-limit demands advanced multivariate insight almost no overseer applies manually. Undetected combined drift can quietly ruin batches, an expensive latent failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the batching cycle with parameters that differ from the confirmed recipe or quantity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of starting a mixing or production run with settings or an amount different from the approved instructions. This helps ensure the run makes the intended product in the intended quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Starting the cycle with off-recipe parameters produces an out-of-spec batch, which is core to the batching job (relevance 10); a fully misbatched lot is costly and potentially unsafe (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: MES-to-controller recipe locking with electronic signatures is standard and dependable, though manual parameter edits at the HMI still leave an occasional deviation path. Recipe download and electronic confirmation take moments and do not meaningfully slow cycle initiation. The control system holds the confirmed recipe so the operator needs little expertise to align parameters, only to acknowledge prompts. Recipe management software is a fixed overhead that avoids costly off-spec batches from parameter mismatch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a manual override fails to supersede the automated control action during an exception or emergency
rows.plain: Make sure a person's emergency command can take control when the automatic system must be overridden. This helps stop the equipment or change its action during an unusual or dangerous situation.
rows.imp_rat: A manual override failing to supersede automation during an emergency negates the operator's last-resort control (high relevance); loss of override in a crisis can cause injury or catastrophic batch/sterility loss (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Priority and mode-arbitration logic generally works, but edge cases where automation reasserts control still occur, leaving a real recurring gap. Override precedence is enforced within the control scan cycle, so timing is adequate when logic is correct. Ensuring overrides truly supersede automation depends on correctly engineered priority logic rather than operator skill. A failed override during an emergency can allow costly damage or unsafe conditions to persist.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual contamination remaining in the product-contact path from prior production
rows.plain: Make it less likely that leftover contamination from earlier production remains in the path that touches the product. This helps keep the next product safe and germ-free.
rows.imp_rat: Residual contamination from prior production is a cleaning/changeover concern near execution (relevance 7) with potentially lethal cross-contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and status tracking help, but confirmation of residual removal is often manual and not system-enforced per run. Cleaning verification is typically a batch-preparation activity, not confirmed in real time at run start. Residual-contamination assurance depends on cleaning validation and visual inspection skill. Cross-contamination causes rejection, a loss only partly mitigated by cleaning programs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a parameter adjustment compromises the sterile state of the batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changing mixing settings makes the batch no longer germ-free. This helps protect the batch while achieving the desired mixing.
rows.imp_rat: A parameter adjustment breaching sterility is central to safely operating the batching process and carries severe consequences since a compromised sterile state means batch loss or unsafe product.
rows.sat_rat: Adjustments are typically managed procedurally rather than interlocked against sterility, so a change occasionally compromises the sterile state. Making a parameter adjustment safely often requires pausing and re-confirming boundary status, adding time. Knowing which adjustments threaten sterility requires deep process knowledge, a scarce expertise. Adjustments that trigger re-verification or holds consume time and material resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile product integrity during disruption recovery
rows.plain: Make it less likely that sterile products become contaminated while work is being restored after a disruption. This helps protect the product when normal operations are interrupted.
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile integrity during disruption recovery is contamination control in a recovery scenario (relevance 6) with catastrophic potential (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and aseptic training help, but disruption recovery introduces non-routine interventions where sterility assurance is genuinely fragile. Sterile recovery steps are slow and any delay increases contamination exposure, working against the overseer. Preserving sterility during recovery demands high aseptic expertise and strict technique that not every shift member reliably applies. A sterility breach destroys the batch and can idle a suite, an extremely costly outcome.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps executed with aseptic parameters outside approved limits
rows.plain: Reduce how often recipe steps are carried out while sterile-processing settings are outside their approved limits. This matters to keep the process within safe and reliable conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Aseptic parameters out of limits during steps sits between recipe enforcement and contamination control (relevance 7); breach can be fatal (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring exists but is not always tightly linked to recipe-step gating, leaving recurring out-of-limit execution. Aseptic parameters are monitored continuously so out-of-limit steps can be flagged during execution. Operators still interpret many aseptic conditions rather than relying on hard automated limits. Steps run outside aseptic limits jeopardize the batch, a cost partially contained by monitoring.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into sterile product-contact areas during calibration
rows.plain: Prevent germs from entering areas that touch the product while measuring equipment is being adjusted. This keeps the product protected during the adjustment.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination into sterile product-contact areas during calibration directly undermines the core job of producing clean batches (high relevance) and carries catastrophic consequences—contaminated sterile product can cause recalls or patient harm (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Introducing contamination during sterile calibration remains a recognized risk that current gowning and wipe-down practice only partly controls. Aseptic calibration under controlled conditions is slow because of gowning, staging, and post-work re-sanitization. Calibrating sensors in sterile product-contact zones without breaching asepsis demands high aseptic-technique skill that is scarce. Sterile consumables, cleanroom time, and potential re-sterilization make each calibration expensive.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm thresholds
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of alarm thresholds failing to signal a critical sterile fill-finish process deviation
rows.plain: Make sure warning limits reliably alert workers when an important clean-production process goes wrong. This helps them respond before the problem becomes more serious.
rows.imp_rat: Alarm thresholds failing to signal a critical fill-finish deviation is central to the monitoring job (high relevance); an unflagged deviation lets contaminated product proceed undetected (very severe).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization practices cover most critical deviations, but misconfigured or missing thresholds still occasionally fail to signal, leaving recurring gaps. Verifying alarm thresholds against process deviation criteria is a defined but lengthy configuration-check step. Setting and confirming thresholds that reliably catch critical deviations needs process specialists who know the fill-finish envelope. Alarm rationalization work is labor-intensive but standard budgeted effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that live validation checks trigger unintended control actions in sterile fill-finish equipment
rows.plain: Check the new setup without causing the filling equipment to perform an unintended action. This helps confirm the setup is correct without disrupting production or safety.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended control actions triggered by validation checks bear on the monitoring/control job but occur in a specific validation context. Severity is catastrophic in sterile fill-finish where an unintended action can ruin an entire aseptic batch and risk patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: Simulation/force modes usually prevent unintended actions during live checks, but errors still occasionally propagate to real equipment. Live validation checks require careful staged execution that adds time. Running live checks without triggering actions demands experienced engineers who understand active control paths. Simulation modes and isolation reduce risk at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator overrides the wrong control action or control point during an exception
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the operator chooses the wrong control or acts on the wrong part of the controls when handling an unusual situation. This helps prevent an incorrect response from causing problems.
rows.imp_rat: Overriding the wrong control action or point during an exception is central to safe manual intervention and severe — a misdirected override during a fault can worsen the very condition it aimed to correct.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation dialogs and point labeling reduce wrong-target overrides, but crowded HMIs under exception conditions still leave a meaningful error rate. Overriding under exception is fast to execute, so the error is about correctness rather than delay. Picking the right control point to override during a stressful exception depends heavily on operator attention and expertise, which is where mistakes happen. Overriding the wrong point can damage equipment or spoil product, imposing real cost when it happens.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control commands or device communications malfunction after patch application
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment still receives commands and communicates properly after the software updates. Problems after the update could cause the process to work incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Post-patch malfunction of control commands or device communications directly undermines the software's control function. Severity is very high since a patch failing silently can disable control mid-production with hazardous consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and patch-validation practices are mature in regulated environments but comms regressions still slip through, keeping this only moderately served. Patches are applied in maintenance windows, so post-patch verification time is planned rather than rushed. Confirming that control commands and device comms still work after patching requires skilled regression testing knowledge. A comms malfunction after patching can halt production, and validated patch testing is resource-intensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator overlooks a critical process-variable excursion during operation
rows.plain: Make sure the operator notices important changes in the process, such as readings going outside safe limits. Missing these changes could allow a problem to continue.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical process-variable excursion strikes directly at the monitoring role central to the CFJ. Severity is very high because an unaddressed excursion can escalate to unsafe conditions or ruined product.
rows.sat_rat: Trends, limits and alarms help, but operator overload and poor display design still let critical excursions go unnoticed with meaningful frequency. Excursions are flagged in near-real-time, so timing is not the primary shortfall. Detecting a critical excursion still relies heavily on sustained operator vigilance amid dense displays, which is inherently error-prone. Overlooked excursions cause costly deviations, and effective attention aids require design effort beyond baseline tools.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a safety trip fails to initiate when an abnormal condition requires it
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that the safety shutdown fails to start when something dangerous happens. This helps stop the equipment before people or the product are harmed.
rows.imp_rat: A safety trip that fails to initiate on an abnormal condition strikes at the DCS's core protective job (relevance 10); the consequence is potential explosion, release, or fatality (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: SIL-rated safety instrumented systems with 1oo2/2oo3 voting and scheduled proof testing make undetected demand failures rare, which is why this is among the best-served needs. Dedicated SIS logic solvers execute trip functions in milliseconds independent of the DCS, so response time is essentially never the failing factor. IEC and certified SIS logic remove most operator judgement from the trip decision, though initial proof-test design still needs safety specialists. Redundant sensors, voting logic and certified safety PLCs are expensive but standard capital in any DCS-controlled hazardous process.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with an out-of-specification strength, concentration, grade, or purity is accepted
rows.plain: Avoid accepting material whose strength, amount, type, or cleanliness is outside the required limits. Using material that meets those limits helps ensure the product is made correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting out-of-spec strength/purity material feeds bad input to compounding (relevance 8) and can produce lethal potency errors (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Established incoming-QC and release workflows reliably catch out-of-spec strength or purity before use. CoA review and QA release occur before dispensing, adding upfront time but not per-step delay. Specification conformance is checked against documented limits, reducing operator judgement burden. Accepting off-spec material wastes an entire batch, and release gating largely prevents that loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of adjusting the wrong control parameter or connected device during an active batch
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that someone changes the wrong setting or connected device while a batch is running. This helps prevent the batch from being made incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Adjusting the wrong parameter or device during an active batch defeats accurate control (high relevance) and typically ruins the in-process batch with contamination potential (very severe).
rows.sat_rat: Grouped displays and confirmation prompts help, but dense parameter screens still permit plausible mistakes that established practice does not fully eliminate. Selecting a parameter is quick, so the risk lies not in duration but in choosing correctly under time pressure. Avoiding the wrong parameter during a live batch depends heavily on operator familiarity and attention, with limited software safeguards against a plausible-but-wrong choice. An erroneous adjustment mid-batch can ruin high-value aseptic product, a cost current interfaces only modestly guard against.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that offsetting ingredient deviations conceal an out-of-tolerance dose during end-to-end verification
rows.plain: Make it very unlikely that one ingredient being too high hides another ingredient being too low during a full batch check. Each ingredient amount should be correct on its own.
rows.imp_rat: Offsetting ingredient deviations concealing an out-of-tolerance dose defeats end-to-end verification (high relevance) and is severe—an undetected out-of-spec batch can be released undetected (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard component-total checks rarely isolate individual doses that cancel out, so this failure mode routinely slips through end-to-end verification. End-to-end mass-balance verification can be run quickly by software, but reconciling offsetting deviations often triggers slow manual investigation. Detecting that two compensating errors mask an out-of-tolerance dose demands experienced review that few operators reliably apply during routine batching. Undetected offsetting deviations that surface later cause costly rejected or reworked batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of wetted surface area retaining hazardous residues after decontamination
rows.plain: After cleaning, as little as possible of the wet surface should still have dangerous residue on it. This helps protect people and prevent the next use from being affected.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residues retained on wetted surfaces after decontamination compromise the safe-handoff aspect of the job; severity is high due to toxic exposure and cross-contamination risk, though it is a decontamination-specific concern.
rows.sat_rat: Complex internal geometries and crevices mean measurable residue commonly persists after decontamination, a persistent gap. Measuring wetted-surface residue via swabbing or rinse sampling is slow and iterative. Quantifying surface residue and interpreting decontamination adequacy requires specialized analytical skill. Swab testing, rinse analysis, and repeat decon add meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with an incorrect identity is accepted as conforming
rows.plain: Prevent materials from being accepted when they are not the materials they are supposed to be. This helps ensure the correct materials are used in production.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting wrong-identity material as conforming corrupts inputs to the recipe (relevance 8) and can be fatal (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Incoming identity testing plus dispensing scans form a mature, reliable double-check against misidentification. Identity verification via label/barcode at receipt and dispensing is fast and routine. Barcode and ID testing reduce reliance on operator recognition of correct identity. Wrong-identity material causes total batch failure, well guarded by ID testing and scanning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a control command is applied to a device other than the intended target
rows.plain: A control instruction should affect only the device the operator intended to control. Confirming the target helps prevent the wrong equipment from starting, stopping, or changing settings.
rows.imp_rat: Applying a control command to the wrong device is a direct failure of the core control job (high relevance); mis-targeted actuation can wreck a batch or trigger unsafe equipment states (very severe).
rows.sat_rat: Unique device tagging and confirmation dialogs in modern SCADA make wrong-target commands uncommon, though poorly labeled or duplicated tags still cause occasional misrouting. Device addressing and selection are near-instant once configured, so targeting adds no meaningful delay. Correct device tagging and clear HMI layouts reduce reliance on operator memory, but ambiguous naming still forces careful attention. A misdirected command can damage equipment or spoil product, and current tag-based routing largely averts this at modest configuration cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that out-of-bound process parameters are allowed to proceed during compounding
rows.plain: Prevent the mixing process from continuing when important settings are outside the allowed limits. This helps keep the product consistent and prevents problems caused by incorrect processing.
rows.imp_rat: Allowing out-of-bound parameters to proceed is a direct enforcement failure (relevance 8) that can yield contaminated or unsafe product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Established systems routinely block or flag out-of-bound parameters, though configuration gaps mean the protection is not absolute. Mature MES/SCADA platforms enforce parameter bounds in real time, so no evaluation-time delay is added once configured. Configuring bound enforcement still requires control expertise, but the underlying capability is standard and well understood. Preventing out-of-bound proceeding avoids costly rejected batches, a well-recognized payoff.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of parameter changes being applied to the wrong control device in the sterile fill-finish line
rows.plain: A changed setting should be applied only to the intended control device, not another one. This helps the filling process run with the correct settings.
rows.imp_rat: Applying parameter changes to the wrong device is a direct failure of the software's targeted configuration function and highly severe, since misconfigured sterile equipment can breach process limits and spoil product.
rows.sat_rat: Tag conventions and download confirmations prevent most wrong-device changes, but human targeting errors still occur in busy lines. Confirming the target device before applying changes adds a verification step but little delay. Correct device targeting relies on operator attention and clear tag naming rather than deep expertise. Wrong-device changes cause costly rework, motivating standard verification practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of personnel interventions that expose the aseptic zone during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce how often people need to reach into or otherwise disturb the sterile work area during mixing. This lowers the chance of bringing in contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel interventions exposing the aseptic zone are contamination-control acts loosely tied to recipe execution (relevance 6) but a leading cause of fatal contamination (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Personnel interventions remain a leading contamination source with little system-level prevention beyond procedures. Interventions occur in real time and current systems do little to reduce their duration. Minimizing zone-exposing interventions depends on operator technique and process design, not tooling. Each intervention raises contamination risk and potential batch loss, poorly mitigated today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unauthorized recipe or parameter change is released to production
rows.plain: Prevent unapproved changes to the recipe or machine settings from being used in production. This helps keep production within approved requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized recipe/parameter change released to production undermines enforcement (relevance 9) and can propagate defective batches at scale (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled recipe management with enforced approval gates is a mature standard practice, though shadow parameters and privileged overrides remain residual risks. Electronic change-control workflows route approvals promptly but can still stall pending required signatories. Role-based access and e-signatures reduce dependence on individual diligence to catch unauthorized changes. Blocking unauthorized releases avoids costly recalls and rejected batches, a clear resource saving.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the dosing tolerance permits batch composition to deviate from its specified range
rows.plain: Make sure the amount of each ingredient added keeps the final mixture within its approved recipe range. This helps ensure every batch has the intended makeup.
rows.imp_rat: Dosing tolerance directly governs whether the batch composition lands in spec, which is the defining functional job of batching; an out-of-range deviation ruins the batch and can cause underdose/overdose harm, though it is often detectable before release.
rows.sat_rat: Modern dosing systems hold composition within specified ranges reliably, though tolerance stack-up at extreme ratios remains a gap. Tight dosing tolerances are achieved without extra time once dosing systems are tuned. Automated dosing control reduces dependence on skilled manual metering to stay in spec. High-accuracy dosing pumps and metering hardware carry a cost premium.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of batch contamination during transfer to the intermediate vessel
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from getting into the batch while it is moved into another container. This protects the batch from being spoiled or made unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during transfer to the intermediate vessel is highly relevant since transfer is a core batching step, and contamination there irreversibly compromises product safety.
rows.sat_rat: Closed sterile transfer to intermediate vessels is a maturing practice that controls most contamination, though connection integrity remains a residual failure point. Transfer through sterile connections is reasonably quick once the path is set up. Validated transfer assemblies reduce but do not remove aseptic-technique dependence. Single-use transfer sets and validated connectors are recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unintended valve, pump, or filling-machine action during the mode transition
rows.plain: Prevent valves, pumps, or filling equipment from operating unexpectedly while the machine changes from one operating setting to another. This helps protect the product, equipment, and workers.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended valve/pump/filling-machine action during mode transition is central to trustworthy control and dangerous when it occurs, potentially breaching containment or contaminating fills and damaging equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Bumpless-transfer and defined-state transition practices reduce glitches, but transition edge cases still occasionally cause unintended actuation. Mode transitions execute quickly, so the exposure window for spurious actuation is brief but present. Preventing unintended actuation during transitions requires careful state-machine engineering rather than operator skill. A spurious valve or pump action during transition can breach sterility or waste product at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that execution feedback indicates completion when the target device has not completed the commanded action
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s completion message is shown only after the device has actually finished the requested action.
rows.imp_rat: False completion feedback corrupts the monitoring function that is core to the CFJ, causing the system to advance based on incomplete actions. Severity is very high as downstream steps proceed on invalid assumptions, risking process failure.
rows.sat_rat: Systems often confirm command receipt rather than physical end-state, so feedback can indicate completion before valves/actuators actually finish, a recurring known gap. Confirmation feedback is typically near-real-time so timing is not the limiting issue. Interpreting whether a 'done' signal truly reflects physical completion still demands experienced judgement. False completion can advance a sequence prematurely and waste batches, with limited free safeguards.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recommending acceptance while material compliance gaps remain
rows.plain: Do not recommend accepting something while required rules or material requirements are still unmet. This helps prevent materials from being approved before they are fully suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Recommending acceptance with compliance gaps is a release-decision error somewhat downstream of execution (relevance 7) but can release harmful product to patients (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and dual sign-off catch many gaps but residual compliance gaps still slip through because coverage depends on human diligence. Reviewers spend considerable time cross-checking records before signing off, so a clean recommendation is not fast under today's practice. Avoiding acceptance while gaps remain relies heavily on experienced reviewers manually spotting what automated checks miss. A wrongly cleared batch carries enormous recall, remediation and regulatory-exposure costs that the status quo only partly guards against.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of breaching the sterile boundary during ingredient addition
rows.plain: Keep the germ-free area sealed while ingredients are added. This prevents outside contamination from entering the product.
rows.imp_rat: Breaching the sterile boundary during addition is critical in sterile applications (relevance 7); a breach compromises the entire sterile batch with severe safety and compliance consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Sterile connectors and RTP ports improve outcomes but manual breaches at the addition boundary remain a recurring, hard-to-eliminate failure mode. Establishing and verifying sterile connections during addition slows the operation considerably. Maintaining the sterile boundary at addition points demands high aseptic skill and is easily compromised by lapses. Sterile connectors, single-use assemblies, and validated transfer methods are costly consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a setpoint adjustment moves a critical parameter outside validated operating limits during an active batch
rows.plain: Lower the chance that changing a machine setting during production pushes an important condition outside its tested safe range. This helps keep the batch within safe and approved conditions.
rows.imp_rat: A setpoint adjustment pushing a critical parameter outside validated limits during an active batch strikes at the heart of controlled preparation and can cause immediate off-spec, unsafe, or non-compliant product.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced setpoint clamping and alarm limits in modern control systems reliably block excursions, though limits that are mis-configured or absent still let some through. Software range checks validate entries instantly without slowing the operator. Configured high/low limits offload the burden of remembering validated ranges from the operator. Preventing out-of-range moves avoids the cost of deviations and lost batches at little added expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operating parameters set outside approved sterile fill-finish process limits
rows.plain: Reduce the number of machine settings that fall outside the approved limits for making sterile products. This helps keep the process safe and within requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Operating parameters set outside approved sterile limits is squarely within the software's configure/control mandate and severe, as out-of-spec conditions directly compromise sterility and product quality.
rows.sat_rat: Configurable limit enforcement and review procedures keep most parameters in range, though manual entry still allows occasional out-of-limit settings. Verifying parameters against approved limits is a defined checklist step in preparation. Range-checking parameters is fairly procedural once approved limits are documented, needing moderate skill. The check is inexpensive relative to the risk it mitigates.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that an active alarm remains unacknowledged because operator access is denied
rows.plain: Reduce how long an active warning stays unconfirmed because the operator cannot access it. This helps the operator respond to the problem sooner.
rows.imp_rat: An unacknowledged active alarm due to denied operator access strikes at the core monitoring job; if operators cannot acknowledge and respond, an unfolding process fault can escalate to equipment damage or safety incident.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems reliably raise the alarm, but tying acknowledgment to access rights creates a predictable failure path where the right person is unavailable. When access is denied the alarm sits unacknowledged until a properly privileged operator is located, and that lookup-and-escalate delay is a real recurring lag. Knowing who holds acknowledge rights and reaching them relies on tribal knowledge and shift discipline rather than an automated handoff. The platform cost is already sunk, but an unacknowledged critical alarm risks process excursions that carry real financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that displayed device states differ from actual device states during coordinated control
rows.plain: Lower the chance that the control screen shows a machine in a different condition than it is actually in. This helps people control several machines correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Displayed device states diverging from actual states corrupts the monitoring foundation of the CFJ; operators act on false information, leading to wrong interventions and undetected unsafe conditions.
rows.sat_rat: Cyclic polling and quality flags keep displays largely accurate, but communication stalls and stale-value handling still produce occasional mismatches. Display refresh occurs at short polling intervals, so lag between actual and shown state is usually brief. Operators must sometimes judge whether displayed state is trustworthy, which relies on experience with the system's quirks. Acting on a stale display can trigger wrong decisions and costly deviations that current polling only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting input indications that leave the correct control response unclear
rows.plain: Reduce the number of conflicting messages from sensors or controls that make it unclear how to respond. This helps the operator choose the right action.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting input indications leaving the correct control response unclear directly undermine the control function; the operator may take a wrong or delayed action with serious process consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo alarm rationalization and signal validation only partially resolve conflicting indications, so ambiguous situations that leave the correct action unclear persist. Reconciling conflicting inputs forces the operator to pause and investigate, adding delay before the correct response is chosen. Interpreting contradictory indications to infer the true state leans heavily on experienced operator judgement that is scarce and error-prone under pressure. The wrong call from conflicting inputs can waste product or trigger unnecessary shutdowns, but no direct spend is required to live with the ambiguity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches with composition outside target because incoming ingredient concentration differs from its expected value
rows.plain: Reduce the number of batches whose mixture is off target because an ingredient arrives stronger or weaker than expected. This helps keep each batch’s composition consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Composition outside target from incoming ingredient concentration variance strikes at the core job of producing a correctly formulated batch; consequences are serious rejected batches though usually caught and reworkable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Most sites trust supplier CoA values without in-house verification, so real lot-to-lot concentration variation still pushes batches off target with some regularity. Certificate-of-analysis review is fast, but true at-line concentration confirmation adds testing time many sites skip. Correcting a batch for incoming concentration variation requires analytical and formulation skill that is unevenly available on the floor. Off-target composition from concentration drift wastes ingredient and forces rework, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that affected compounded material advances to filling before disruption-related quality impact is established
rows.plain: Stop material affected by a production problem from reaching the filling step before its quality has been checked. This prevents unsuitable material from being put into containers.
rows.imp_rat: Advancing affected material to filling before impact assessment is a disruption-handling gap (relevance 6) that can release harmful product downstream (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo holds rely on manual quarantine flags and human discipline under time pressure, so affected material advancing prematurely remains a genuine recurring risk. Holding material until quality impact is established adds delay that pressures the overseer to release before assessment is complete. Deciding whether disruption-affected material is safe demands scarce quality judgement that the overseer often lacks in the moment. Advancing compromised material to filling wastes fill components and can force downstream recalls, a severe cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded parameter values and actual compounding conditions
rows.plain: Keep the recorded settings as close as possible to the conditions that actually occurred while ingredients were being mixed and prepared. This helps make the records trustworthy and reveals real process problems.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between record and actual conditions defeats the recording obligation (relevance 9) and is a serious data-integrity finding (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Direct instrument capture is dependable, but conditions read manually or inferred still drift from what gets recorded. Real-time SCADA polling narrows the window between actual conditions and their record for automated tags. Judging whether a recorded value truly reflects conditions still leans on operator familiarity where sensing is indirect. Existing process instrumentation covers most conditions without new spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unauthorized recipe version governs batch execution
rows.plain: Make sure workers use only the approved version of the production instructions for the batch. This prevents an outdated or unauthorized set of instructions from controlling the work.
rows.imp_rat: An unauthorized version governing execution is a direct enforcement breach (relevance 9) that can systematically produce unsafe batches (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Modern MES/SCADA enforce approved-version execution effectively, making unauthorized-version execution rare. Version control checks are largely automated, so confirming the governing version is quick. System-managed version locking reduces the expertise needed to ensure the right recipe runs. Preventing unauthorized versions avoids costly batch losses and investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between recorded parameter values and values actually observed during compounding
rows.plain: Keep recorded measurements as close as possible to what was actually seen while the mixture was being made. This helps ensure the production record can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values diverging from observed values corrupts the recording pillar of the CFJ (relevance 9) and creates data-integrity/regulatory exposure (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Where instruments feed the MES directly, recorded-versus-observed fidelity is high, but manual-entry steps remain a recurring source of small deviations. Automated data acquisition captures many parameters at observation time, though manual readings still lag and introduce transcription gaps. Operators must still notice and correctly transcribe values where automation is absent, demanding attention that varies by shift. Sensor and MES infrastructure exists in most modern facilities, so incremental cost per record is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that remote control commands are not applied as intended to the sterile fill-finish process
rows.plain: Make sure commands sent from another location change the germ-free filling process exactly as intended. This prevents remote mistakes from affecting the product or equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Remote commands not applied as intended to the fill-finish process is a core failure of remote control fidelity (high relevance); misapplied commands can compromise sterile product (very severe).
rows.sat_rat: Command acknowledgment and write-verify practices exist, but remote links introduce dropout and stale-state risks that make full assurance imperfect. Remote command transmission is fast, but network latency can occasionally delay confirmation of applied state. Operating remotely reduces on-site presence needs while relying on the system to confirm commands, adding some skill in interpreting feedback. A remote command not applied as intended can cause silent process deviation and product loss that current acknowledgment schemes only partly catch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of treating a transient excursion as a stable recipe endpoint
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a brief, temporary change is mistaken for a steady sign that the recipe is finished. This helps prevent the process from stopping too early or at the wrong time.
rows.imp_rat: Treating a transient excursion as endpoint mis-terminates the recipe (relevance 8) leaving product under-processed (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Stability-confirmation logic (dwell timers, trend checks) is inconsistently implemented, so transient excursions are still frequently mistaken for endpoints. Distinguishing transient from stable requires a settling/dwell period that current systems handle inconsistently. Recognizing a false-stable endpoint demands experienced operator judgement that is not well supported by tools. Acting on a transient endpoint wastes material and forces rework, a cost poorly mitigated today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial, particulate, or cross-product contamination while loading ingredient components
rows.plain: Prevent germs, dust, particles, or material from another product from getting into the ingredients while they are being loaded. This helps keep the ingredients clean and prevents products from being mixed accidentally.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during ingredient loading is a direct pathway into the batch (relevance 8); cross-product contamination can trigger recalls and harm (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Even with SOPs and monitoring, manual component loading remains a leading contamination-ingress point where failures still recur despite best practice. Aseptic loading procedures, gowning, and line clearance add substantial time to each ingredient addition. Contamination-free loading depends heavily on trained aseptic technique and constant operator vigilance, a scarce and error-prone dependency. Cleanroom operation, disposables, and environmental monitoring make contamination control expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unreleased or rejected material lot is accepted for use
rows.plain: Prevent materials that have not been approved or have been rejected from being used. This helps ensure only acceptable materials enter the process.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an unreleased/rejected lot puts unqualified material into the batch (relevance 8) with severe patient-safety and compliance consequences (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine/release status enforcement in inventory and MES reliably blocks unreleased or rejected lots from use. Release-status checks are electronic and instantaneous at dispensing where inventory is integrated. System status flags remove the operator's need to judge lot release manually. Using rejected lots would scrap batches, and status locking prevents that cost effectively.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of missing or conflicting confirmation of connected device stop states after shutdown initiation
rows.plain: After an emergency stop begins, make sure every connected device is clearly confirmed to have stopped. This prevents uncertainty about whether dangerous movement or activity is still happening.
rows.imp_rat: Confirming device stop states is central to the software's monitoring/control job during shutdown; missing or conflicting confirmation means the system cannot verify a safe state, and the consequence is potential equipment or personnel harm from assumed-but-unconfirmed stops.
rows.sat_rat: Shutdown sequences confirm stop states for most devices, but devices lacking positive feedback can leave ambiguous or conflicting confirmation, a recurring gap. Stop-state confirmations are gathered quickly after shutdown initiation. Interpreting missing or conflicting stop confirmations still requires operator judgement. Uncertain shutdown states risk unsafe conditions, mitigated by standard feedback at no extra cost.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm thresholds
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning interlocks or alarm thresholds to the wrong equipment tags
rows.plain: Make sure each automatic safety stop and warning limit is assigned to the correct piece of equipment. This prevents the wrong equipment from stopping or being watched.
rows.imp_rat: Misassigned interlocks or alarm thresholds relate to configuration of safety monitoring, a key part of the job. Severity is very high because a misplaced interlock disables protection on the wrong equipment, potentially allowing catastrophic conditions.
rows.sat_rat: Tag-mapping errors are a persistent problem given the volume and manual nature of assignment, only partly caught by review. Verifying interlock and alarm tag assignments is a slow manual cross-check. Correct tag assignment across many equipment points requires careful, attentive engineering work. Mis-assignment causes rework and re-validation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect recipe parameters being activated for the next batch
