rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the next batch starts with the wrong instructions or settings. This helps prevent the batch from being made incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Activating incorrect recipe parameters for the next batch strikes the heart of batching (very high relevance) and produces an entire out-of-spec batch with potential safety and scrap consequences (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-bound parameter activation with change control is a mature, dependable practice, though manual overrides leave an occasional path to incorrect parameters. Approved parameters download automatically with the recipe, adding no meaningful delay. Automated parameter activation removes reliance on operators entering values correctly. Parameter management is a low incremental cost within existing recipe systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of critical parameter excursions beyond specified limits during production
rows.plain: Keep important production settings within their allowed limits. This helps the process produce safe and consistent results.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameter excursions are exactly what the system must prevent (relevance 9); frequent excursions degrade quality and safety (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA control loops and alarms reduce excursion frequency well for automated parameters but manual-controlled steps still drift. Continuous SCADA monitoring flags excursions promptly so corrective action can begin quickly. Automated alarms reduce the operator vigilance needed to spot parameter drift. Excursions risk batch loss, and closed-loop control curbs some but not all of that expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with contamination or container compromise is accepted for use
rows.plain: Do not use material if it may be contaminated or if its container may be damaged. Material should be accepted only when it is in suitable condition.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting contaminated or container-compromised material introduces contamination into compounding (relevance 7) with potentially lethal outcome (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Receiving inspection and quarantine catch most compromised containers, though subtle contamination can pass visual checks. Container inspection is a quick receiving step but relies on manual observation timing. Detecting container compromise or contamination depends on inspector visual skill. Accepting compromised material risks batch loss, mostly averted by receiving inspection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing control-system access during sterile fill-finish activity
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of losing the ability to reach and use the equipment controls during clean production work. This helps the team continue to monitor and manage the process.
rows.imp_rat: Losing control-system access during active sterile fill-finish directly removes the operator's ability to control and monitor, negating the CFJ. Severity is very high as loss of control during critical aseptic operations can force batch loss or unsafe conditions.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant controllers and networks make control-loss during fill-finish uncommon, but remote-access and thin-client sessions still occasionally drop. Reconnection after a dropout can take seconds to minutes, risking interruption during critical fill. Maintaining access relies on IT/network engineering rather than operator skill during the activity. Redundant networks and servers cost more but are standard in GMP sterile installations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of control and monitoring disruption during sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Keep interruptions to process control and monitoring as short as possible during sterile production. This helps the operation continue safely and with fewer gaps in oversight.
rows.imp_rat: Control/monitoring disruption during fill-finish production undermines continuous oversight central to the job; while similar to item 9, general-production framing makes consequences serious but slightly less acute than mid-active-batch.
rows.sat_rat: Hot-standby and redundant I/O are mature enough to keep most disruptions brief, though failover glitches still occur. Disruption duration during production is partly mitigated by redundant controllers and hot-standby architectures that shorten outages. Restoring control quickly still leans on experienced staff who can diagnose and switch over rapidly. Downtime in sterile production is expensive, and redundancy hardware to reduce it is a significant capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that overlapping control authority permits conflicting commands to the sterile fill-finish process
rows.plain: Prevent more than one person or system from being able to give conflicting commands to the process that puts sterile products into their final containers. Clear control prevents mistakes and unsafe actions.
rows.imp_rat: Overlapping control authority allowing conflicting commands undermines deterministic control (relevant); conflicting commands to fill-finish equipment can cause unpredictable unsafe states and sterility loss (very severe).
rows.sat_rat: Command-arbitration and ownership-lock mechanisms exist but overlapping remote/local authority is a known trouble area that is inconsistently handled. Conflicting commands resolve within scan cycles, so the issue is arbitration correctness rather than speed. Preventing overlapping authority requires careful role and lock engineering that demands scarce configuration expertise. Conflicting commands can drive product loss or equipment stress, which current locking only partly prevents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe steps omitted during a compounding batch
rows.plain: Make sure no required instruction is skipped while preparing a batch. Completing every step helps the batch turn out as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required steps during a batch is a direct recipe-execution failure (relevance 9) leaving product incomplete or unsafe (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Order enforcement makes omitted steps rare, though manual or off-system steps can still be skipped. Enforced sequencing prevents skipping steps that would otherwise be caught only late. Interlocked workflows reduce the skill needed to keep track of the full step list. Step interlocks are configured within the existing recipe framework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of process or cleaning residue remaining in the batching system after draining and flushing
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover processing material and cleaning liquid as possible from the mixing system before storing it for a long time. This helps prevent buildup, damage, and contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Residual process or cleaning residue after draining and flushing carries directly into the next batch, undermining the prep job's core purpose; consequences include adulterated product and potential chemical contamination of consumers.
rows.sat_rat: Draining and flushing remove the bulk of residue but consistently leave films and low-point pockets behind. Draining and flushing to reduce residue is time-consuming and often iterative. Optimizing flush sequences to reach low points needs process familiarity. Flush media and disposal of spent flush add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connected industrial devices that remain operating after authorized emergency shutdown initiation
rows.plain: Make sure as few connected machines and devices as possible keep running after an approved emergency shutdown begins. This helps stop the danger quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Devices continuing to run after authorized emergency shutdown is a critical safety-function failure of the control job (high relevance); persistent operation during an emergency risks injury and sterility loss (catastrophic severity).
rows.sat_rat: Safety-rated shutdown chains and dedicated safety PLCs are mature and highly redundant, so devices remaining active after a valid E-stop are rare. Hardwired and software E-stop logic acts within the shutdown sequence timing, so residual running devices are the concern rather than speed here. Emergency shutdown is designed to work with a single action requiring no specialized skill from the operator. Reliable full shutdown prevents cascade damage and is a well-justified investment enforced by safety standards.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a startup or shutdown sequence advances while process or equipment prerequisites remain unmet
rows.plain: Do not let startup or shutdown move to the next step until all required conditions are met. This helps prevent equipment or the process from being started or stopped unsafely.
rows.imp_rat: Advancing a startup/shutdown sequence with prerequisites unmet directly undermines the DCS's sequencing safeguards (relevance 9); it can cause severe equipment damage or hazardous release (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Well-established interlock/permissive practice reliably blocks premature advancement in most cases, with residual gaps where prerequisites were never captured in logic. Permissive checks are evaluated instantly before each sequence step advances. Interlock and permissive logic is engineered up front, reducing runtime operator judgement, but its completeness depends on the designer's rigor. Sequential permissives are standard configured logic with negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that removing a deprecated module causes loss of control or monitoring of a sterile fill-finish process
rows.plain: Make sure removing an old software part does not stop the system from controlling or watching the germ-free filling process. This helps keep the process under control after the removal.
rows.imp_rat: Losing control or monitoring of a sterile process from module removal is a maintenance-triggered failure of the core monitor/control job; when it happens mid-batch the sterility and safety consequences are catastrophic and irreversible.
rows.sat_rat: Dependency mapping and staged validation catch most hidden couplings, but undocumented interdependencies occasionally still cause loss of control or monitoring. Deprecated-module removal is scheduled inside maintenance windows, so duration is seldom the issue. Distinguishing a truly deprecated module from one still coupled to live control functions demands deep system knowledge that is not always fully documented. Dependency analysis and regression testing add moderate cost that is generally accepted for regulated systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material affected by an unresolved storage excursion is accepted for use
rows.plain: Make sure material is not used when it may have been affected by a storage problem that has not yet been resolved. This helps prevent unsuitable material from entering production.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting material under unresolved storage excursion introduces possibly degraded input (relevance 7) with serious quality/safety risk (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Storage-excursion tracking is less integrated than release status, so affected lots can slip through when holds are not flagged in the system. Storage-excursion holds depend on QA processes that may not be resolved before material is needed. Recognizing an unresolved storage excursion requires linking monitoring data to the lot, often a manual review. Using excursion-affected material risks batch loss, only partly controlled by hold status.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the opened product-contact path during sealing-component replacement
rows.plain: Keep dirt or germs from entering the opened product path while replacing seals and gaskets. This protects the product from contamination during the repair.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the product-contact path during seal replacement is a maintenance-time error, so somewhat less central to routine batching, but consequences are severe — an undetected contaminated batch causing recall or patient harm.
rows.sat_rat: The opened path during sealing-component work is an established contamination risk that manual practice controls only partially. Seal and gasket replacement is fast mechanically but the surrounding clean-open-reclean sequence dominates the time. Fitting seals into an opened product-contact path cleanly relies on careful hygienic handling that is technician-dependent. Seals are cheap but the associated recleaning and revalidation drive the real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unintended control outputs compromising sterile process conditions during recalibration
rows.plain: Prevent the controls from sending unexpected commands while they are being adjusted. This helps keep the germ-free process in the required condition.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended outputs during recalibration compromising sterile conditions is relevant to safe control transitions; contamination during a maintenance mode is severe though recalibration is an infrequent, controlled window.
rows.sat_rat: Bypass/simulate modes and interlocks exist in modern control software but are inconsistently applied, so unintended outputs during recalibration remain a real recurring hazard. Recalibration windows are scheduled during batch downtime, so time pressure is moderate but rushed recalibrations still occur. Preventing stray control outputs during recalibration depends heavily on experienced technicians who know to place loops in manual or safe states first. A compromised sterile batch is enormously costly, and current practice provides no automatic financial safeguard beyond procedure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the batching cycle while sterile boundary conditions are compromised
rows.plain: Do not start mixing and batching while the barriers that keep the process clean are damaged, open, or otherwise not working properly. This helps prevent contamination of the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating a cycle with compromised sterile boundary conditions is central to sterile batching (relevance 7) and would sterility-fail the whole batch with high safety stakes (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Filter integrity tests and pressure interlocks catch many compromised-boundary starts, but undetected micro-breaches still occasionally allow a cycle to begin compromised. Pre-batch integrity checks and pressure holds add setup time before the cycle can begin. Interpreting integrity-test results and confirming boundary status requires trained judgement. Integrity-test instrumentation and the batches held for confirmation carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recommending acceptance while a critical recipe-control or compliance requirement remains unproven
rows.plain: Do not recommend accepting a batch while an important production or compliance requirement still lacks proof. Acceptance should wait until the requirement has been demonstrated.
rows.imp_rat: Recommending acceptance with an unproven critical control is a release-decision defect (relevance 6) that can release unsafe or contaminated product (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices and acceptance checklists exist but frequently let unproven items slip through under schedule pressure, so this remains a recurring risk. Assembling the full body of proof before signing off is slow, and reviewers often face pressure to recommend before every requirement is closed. Judging whether a critical requirement is truly proven demands seasoned validation and GMP expertise that is scarce and unevenly distributed across review teams. The cost of a premature acceptance is high, but there is little dedicated budget for the deep independent scrutiny needed to prevent it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among approved sequence, interlock, and exception-handling requirements
rows.plain: Resolve disagreements between the approved operating steps, safety checks, and instructions for handling unusual situations. This helps the equipment respond consistently and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among sequence, interlock, and exception-handling requirements are central to correct control logic; latent conflicts can defeat safety interlocks and cause unpredictable, hazardous behavior in production.
rows.sat_rat: With little automated conflict-checking, unresolved requirement conflicts routinely persist into later stages, making this one of the least-served needs. Reconciling sequence, interlock and exception-handling requirements is a lengthy manual analysis. Detecting requirement conflicts demands scarce senior engineers who hold the whole design in their heads. Manual conflict resolution is labor-intensive and late-found conflicts are expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the finished batch becomes contaminated during discharge
rows.plain: Keep the completed batch from becoming contaminated while it is being emptied or transferred. This protects the quality of the finished product.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of the finished batch during discharge occurs at the final gate before product leaves the system, so it is highly central; the consequence is a fully contaminated finished batch that may reach customers unmitigated.
rows.sat_rat: Closed discharge into validated containers keeps finished-batch contamination uncommon, though connection points and filling steps remain residual risk. Closed-transfer discharge adds little time versus open handling. Closed and automated discharge routes reduce the operator finesse needed to avoid contamination. Closed-system hardware carries capital cost but prevents expensive contamination losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of evaluating a batch against limits from the wrong approved recipe revision
rows.plain: Avoid judging a production batch using limits from the wrong approved version of its instructions. Using the correct version helps ensure the batch is judged by the right requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating against the wrong revision's limits corrupts enforcement judgment (relevance 8) and can pass a truly nonconforming batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Approved-revision locking in modern MES makes wrong-revision evaluation fairly rare in steady state, though edge cases and revision changeovers still slip through. Evaluating against approved limits is usually near-instant once the correct revision is loaded in the system. The system carries the limits, so the operator does not need to recall them, but confirming the right revision still requires attention. Judging a batch against wrong limits risks releasing bad product or scrapping good product, a meaningful cost either way.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unintended equipment actuation during parameter configuration in the sterile fill-finish line
rows.plain: Prevent the equipment from starting or moving unexpectedly while its settings are being entered. This protects people and prevents damage during setup.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended actuation during configuration is a control-safety error (relevant) that during fill-finish can breach sterility or damage equipment while operators expect no motion (very severe).
rows.sat_rat: Offline configuration and permissive controls prevent most unintended actuation, though live-edit mistakes still occur occasionally. Safe configuration procedures with equipment de-energized add setup time but are routinely followed. Avoiding unintended actuation during configuration depends on disciplined operators following lockout and offline-config practices. Downtime for offline configuration has a cost but is accepted as standard practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unintended sterile product path exposure during sequence execution
rows.plain: Keep the product's protected path exposed for as little unintended time as possible while the equipment follows its steps. This helps reduce the chance of contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile path exposure duration is somewhat downstream of the software's core control job, but when it occurs the consequence is catastrophic — batch contamination, patient safety risk, and regulatory recall — so severity is maximal.
rows.sat_rat: Automated sequences bound exposure windows reasonably, but transition delays and hold conditions can still extend unintended exposure beyond target. Minimizing exposure duration depends on how fast sequences transition, which current automation handles adequately but not optimally. Sequence timing is preconfigured, so operators need little skill during execution. Prolonged sterile path exposure risks costly contamination, and tight timing must be engineered rather than obtained free.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of malfunctioning emergency stops, guards, alarms, limit switches, or shutdown interlocks incorrectly recorded as passing the functional test
rows.plain: Avoid marking safety devices as passing when they are actually not working correctly. This includes emergency shutoffs, guards, warnings, position switches, and automatic shutdowns.
rows.imp_rat: Falsely recording a malfunctioning e-stop, guard, or interlock as passing is a testing-integrity issue peripheral to the batching process itself, but severity is maximal since a defeated safety device can cause serious injury.
rows.sat_rat: False-pass recording of a malfunctioning safety device is a serious, recurring failure that manual checklist testing does not reliably catch. Verifying each interlock, e-stop, and alarm actually trips takes time that pressured schedules erode. Correctly confirming a protective device truly functions rather than assuming it passed requires diligent, knowledgeable testing. Thorough functional verification adds labor and downtime cost that encourages shortcutting.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of critical process variables deviating beyond validated limits during automatic regulation
rows.plain: Keep important process conditions within the tested safe ranges while the system adjusts them automatically. This helps the process remain stable and produce the expected result.
rows.imp_rat: Keeping critical variables within validated limits during automatic regulation is the essence of the DCS's control job (relevance 10); excursions can damage equipment or breach product/safety limits (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature closed-loop control with alarming keeps most critical variables inside limits, though poorly tuned or interacting loops still cause occasional excursions. PID loops act continuously in real time, so corrective action against drift is effectively immediate. Good regulation still depends on competent loop tuning and configuration, a skill that varies widely across sites. Regulatory control is core DCS functionality with no incremental cost beyond the base system.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production batches whose executed parameters conflict with the approved recipe
rows.plain: Make sure the settings actually used during production match the approved instructions. This helps ensure every batch is made the way it was authorized.
rows.imp_rat: Executed parameters conflicting with approved recipe is a direct enforcement failure (relevance 9) yielding nonconforming batches (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: When recipes are managed electronically the executed parameters closely track the approved master, but manual and offline steps still introduce conflicts. Executed-vs-approved comparison happens automatically during batch execution so conflicts surface in real time. Automated recipe download reduces the skill needed to reproduce parameters correctly compared with manual transcription. Preventing parameter conflicts reduces scrapped batches and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of internal fluid pathways retaining microbial or chemical residue after the cycle
rows.plain: Remove germs and chemical residue from the inside passages that carry liquid. Leftover residue could contaminate the next product.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial or chemical residue retained in internal pathways after the cleaning cycle directly undermines the sanitary preparation job and can cause severe contamination or sterility failures downstream.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with conductivity/rinse verification reliably removes most residue, though dead legs and shadowed geometry occasionally retain residual, keeping it well but not perfectly served. Automated CIP cycles run on validated time programs, so cleaning duration is predictable and largely hands-off. Validated CIP recipes offload most cleaning judgement from technicians, though troubleshooting incomplete cleaning still needs expertise. CIP consumes water, chemicals, and energy but at controlled, budgeted rates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the originating supervisor relinquishes control authority before the recipient gains it
rows.plain: Avoid any gap where the person giving up control no longer has it, but the person taking control does not have it yet. This prevents confusion and loss of control.
rows.imp_rat: If the originating supervisor relinquishes control authority before the recipient gains it, the process is left with no controller during handover — a control gap that can cause an uncontrolled process excursion.
rows.sat_rat: Two-phase confirm handshakes make a gap where the originator drops control before the recipient holds it uncommon, though not impossible during comms glitches. Handshake-based control transfer protocols complete quickly, so timing of the handoff is rarely the pain point. Following the transfer procedure is routine and does not demand rare expertise once operators are trained. Transfer logic is a standard platform feature with negligible added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of declaring a recipe endpoint before required conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: Do not say the process is finished until all required conditions have been met. This helps ensure the product receives the full treatment.
rows.imp_rat: Declaring endpoint prematurely means the recipe was not fully executed (relevance 8), producing under-processed product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Automated endpoint gating exists but many endpoints still depend on operator declaration, leaving frequent premature-call risk. Endpoint confirmation logic runs continuously without adding operator wait beyond required measurement time. Endpoint judgement still leans heavily on operator interpretation when criteria are not fully automated. Premature endpoints cause reprocessing or scrap, a recurring cost only partially contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of confirming a recipe step before all required actions and checks are complete
rows.plain: Do not mark a process step complete until every required action and check is finished. This prevents the work from moving ahead too soon.
rows.imp_rat: Confirming a step before actions/checks complete is a false-execution enforcement failure (relevance 8) that can advance an incomplete unsafe batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Where checks are interlocked confirmation is reliable, but many completeness judgements still rest on the operator. Interlocks can block premature confirmation without adding operator delay. Enforced checklists reduce reliance on operator judgement about completeness. Configuring completion interlocks is a design-time engineering cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of parameter-limit overrides without required authorization or documented rationale
rows.plain: Prevent people from changing required process settings without approval or a written explanation. This helps keep production controlled and properly documented.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized limit overrides subvert enforcement (relevance 8) and can permit unsafe conditions plus regulatory findings (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Systems can require authorization and rationale for overrides, but weak reason-code discipline and privileged bypasses leave real recurring gaps. Override capture at the point of action is quick when the system prompts for it. Enforced reason-code and authorization prompts reduce reliance on operators remembering to document rationale. Undocumented overrides drive costly investigations and potential batch rejection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cleaning solution bypasses required piping or vessels during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning liquid flows through every required pipe and container instead of skipping any part of the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning solution bypassing required piping or vessels leaves uncleaned surfaces that directly defeat the preparation job's hygiene requirement; the consequence is hidden contamination that can carry into subsequent batches undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Automated valve sequencing and flow verification help, yet undetected bypass of a branch or vessel still occurs on complex systems, keeping this only acceptably served. Verifying correct flow-path valve alignment before a cycle adds setup time that is usually built into the routine. Confirming every vessel and line is actually swept requires understanding of the piping and valve matrix that leans on experienced staff. Undetected bypass wastes a full cycle's chemicals and time, but such events are periodic rather than constant.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering adjacent sterile process lines through opened interfaces
rows.plain: Keep germs or other unwanted material from entering nearby clean process lines through open connections. This protects the cleanliness of the connected systems.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering adjacent sterile process lines through opened interfaces threatens the sterile integrity the CFJ relies on; a breach can compromise multiple downstream sterile products, an irreversible and costly outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Interface capping and isolation practices generally protect adjacent lines, though contamination breaches still occur when discipline lapses. Blanking and capping opened interfaces to protect adjacent sterile lines is a quick standard step. Maintaining sterile-boundary integrity during decommissioning requires cleanroom-aware discipline not universal on demo crews. Caps, blanks, and covers are cheap protective items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which an ingredient charge deviates from the recipe quantity
rows.plain: Add as close as possible to the amount of ingredient called for in the recipe. This helps the finished product have the intended composition.
rows.imp_rat: Quantity deviation from recipe is central to accurate compounding (relevance 9); large deviations affect strength/potency but are graded rather than binary catastrophic (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Interfaced scales with recipe tolerance gating catch most quantity deviations, but manual transcription and coarse manual dispensing still allow drift. Tolerance-checked weighing gives immediate accept/reject feedback without slowing the charge. Automated tolerance bands relieve the operator from judging whether a weight is close enough by eye. Over/under charges waste material and cause rework, and scale integration modestly mitigates that expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adjusted settings exceed validated operating limits during the batch
rows.plain: Keep changed mixing settings within the tested and approved operating range during production. Settings outside that range could affect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Adjusted settings exceeding validated operating limits during the batch directly threatens the controlled-preparation core job; severity is high due to off-spec and compliance breach, close to item 11's concern.
rows.sat_rat: This closely overlaps need is handled by the same limit-enforcement logic, so excursions are uncommon where those interlocks are properly configured. Automated limit enforcement adds no meaningful delay to setpoint changes. Validated-range interlocks reduce reliance on operator knowledge of acceptable bounds. Blocking out-of-limit settings averts costly deviation and rework at negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which indicated critical parameter values differ from actual conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the displayed readings for important process conditions match what is really happening. This allows workers to control the process based on trustworthy information.
rows.imp_rat: Indicated critical values differing from actual conditions corrupts the enforcement signal (relevance 8) and can allow undetected unsafe processing (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Calibration programs bound indicated-vs-actual divergence, but between calibrations undetected drift remains possible. Sensor readings display in real time so no delay in seeing indicated values. Detecting a sensor drift versus true value requires calibration knowledge beyond routine operation. Acting on wrong indicated values can waste batches, mitigated only by periodic calibration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between the recorded quantity and the quantity actually added
rows.plain: Make sure the amount recorded is the same as the amount actually added. This keeps the production record accurate and prevents mistakes in the recipe.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded and actual quantity added corrupts the record (relevance 8) and misrepresents potency, a serious defect (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Scale-to-MES links reduce quantity discrepancies substantially, yet manual overrides and partial additions still cause mismatches. Weigh-scale integration can post quantities immediately, but manual dispense confirmation adds delay. Correctly reconciling target versus actual dispensed amount depends on operator care during weighing. Integrated scales are common and add little marginal cost once installed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a controller, network, or node fault escalating before operator detection
rows.plain: Make sure a problem in a control unit, connection, or device does not get worse before an operator notices it. Early notice gives people a chance to respond.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected escalation of controller/network/node faults undermines the DCS's fault-containment and alarming role (relevance 8); a cascading fault can cause widespread control loss (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Built-in diagnostics, watchdogs, and redundancy catch most faults before they cascade, though undetected slow-degradation faults still occasionally escalate. Diagnostic and alarm systems flag faults in near real time, but noisy alarm floods can delay recognition of the escalating one. Distinguishing a benign transient from an escalating fault still leans heavily on experienced operators and engineers. Redundant hardware and monitoring tools carry cost but are standard DCS features already paid for.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient sources mapped to an incorrect tank, line, pump, valve, or dosing channel
rows.plain: Make sure each ingredient source is connected to the correct equipment. This prevents an ingredient from being sent to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Mapping an ingredient source to the wrong tank, line, pump, or valve directly causes wrong-ingredient batches (high relevance) with severe consequences—cross-dosing errors can render product unsafe (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Commissioning checks catch most mismapping, but undocumented changes and manual hose connections leave recurring misroute risk. Verifying source-to-destination mapping is a one-time commissioning task rather than a per-batch delay. Correct mapping depends on engineering knowledge and careful P&ID interpretation, making it skill-sensitive. Mapping verification is low material cost but errors cause expensive downstream rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that troubleshooting activity unintentionally alters the validated control state
rows.plain: Fixing software problems should not accidentally change settings that have already been checked and confirmed. This matters because changed settings could affect how the system works.
rows.imp_rat: Troubleshooting that unintentionally alters the validated control state directly threatens configuration integrity; in a regulated sterile setting this can silently corrupt control behavior and invalidate compliance, giving high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Read-only diagnostics and audit trails help, but live troubleshooting still frequently touches parameters and relies on human care rather than enforced guards. Troubleshooting under production pressure is time-constrained, but this is not primarily a time problem. Whether troubleshooting inadvertently alters the validated state hinges almost entirely on the individual engineer's discipline and expertise, which is scarce and error-prone. An unnoticed state change can invalidate a batch, a heavy cost, with little automatic prevention.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required unsafe equipment states omitted from functional testing
rows.plain: Make sure testing includes every situation in which the equipment could become unsafe. This helps confirm that the safety shutoffs work when they are needed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required unsafe equipment states from functional testing is a validation/testing gap somewhat peripheral to routine batching, but the severity is very high because an untested unsafe state can lead to injury or equipment failure.
rows.sat_rat: Omission of required unsafe-state tests is a recognized chronic gap since manual test coverage frequently falls short of the full required set. Functionally testing every unsafe state and fault condition is time-consuming and often abbreviated under schedule pressure. Knowing which unsafe states must be provoked and how to force them safely demands high controls/safety expertise that is scarce. Comprehensive fault-state testing costs downtime and specialist labor, tempting omission.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recovery testing alters live control parameters governing sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.plain: Make sure testing the backup system does not change the active settings that control sterile filling. This keeps the ongoing production conditions stable.
rows.imp_rat: Recovery testing altering live sterile fill-finish parameters is somewhat peripheral to routine control but severe: any inadvertent change to sterile conditions can contaminate an entire batch, causing irreversible product loss and compliance exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Many sites lack a true isolated mirror, so recovery testing can bleed into live parameters and the safeguards against this are weak and manual. Recovery testing is typically deferred to non-production periods, so its timing rarely disrupts throughput. Isolating test activity from live sterile-fill parameters demands scarce expertise in test-environment segregation and change control. Testing on or near live systems risks batch loss, but dedicated validated test environments are costly and often unavailable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a critical active alarm during simultaneous alarm activity
rows.plain: Make sure an important active warning is not missed when many warnings appear at once. Missing one could allow a serious problem to continue.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical alarm during an alarm flood concerns the monitoring function under stress conditions. Severity is high since a missed critical alarm amid noise can allow a serious fault to progress unchecked.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm prioritization and shelving exist but chattering and flood conditions routinely cause the truly critical alarm to be buried, a chronic industry pain. Alarms appear immediately, so the problem is not latency but volume during floods. During alarm floods operators must rapidly triage many signals, a heavy cognitive burden that standard tools only partly relieve. Missed critical alarms in floods drive expensive incidents, and alarm rationalization projects are costly and often incomplete.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe steps omitted during batch execution
rows.plain: Leave out fewer required steps when following the instructions for making a production run. This helps ensure the product is made completely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required steps directly breaks recipe execution (relevance 9); missing a step can leave product unsafe or ineffective (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: EBR step-completion gating reliably prevents skipping mandatory steps, though non-enforced manual notes remain a gap. Forced-step acknowledgement adds minor prompting time but prevents costly restarts. Mandatory electronic sign-offs remove the operator's need to remember every required step. Omitted steps causing batch loss are expensive, and step gating avoids much of that waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of executed batches containing compounding parameters that differ from the approved recipe
rows.plain: Have fewer production runs in which the mixing settings differ from the approved instructions. This helps ensure the product is made as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Batches with parameters differing from approved recipe is the definitional enforcement failure (relevance 9) yielding nonconforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: MES parameter enforcement and automatic deviation flagging make this one of the better-served needs today. Comparing executed batch parameters against the approved recipe is increasingly automated so deviations surface quickly. Enforced recipes reduce reliance on operator skill, though interpreting flagged differences still needs review. Automated enforcement prevents costly off-recipe batches before they complete, saving material and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps executed out of the prescribed order by coordinated devices
rows.plain: Make sure connected machines carry out recipe steps in the required order, so the process is performed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Executing recipe steps out of order by coordinated devices directly breaks the sequencing that industrial control software must enforce. Severity is very high because misordered steps can cause reactions, contamination, or equipment damage.
rows.sat_rat: S88 sequential function charts and interlocks reliably enforce step order across coordinated devices, though cross-device synchronization edge cases occasionally cause skips. Sequential phase execution proceeds automatically without operator-induced delay. Sequence engines encode step order so operators need not manually enforce it. Out-of-order execution can ruin product, and standard sequencing tools prevent it without added spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of starting a recipe step with equipment outside the required state
rows.plain: Do not start a recipe step until the equipment is in the required condition. This helps prevent errors and unsafe or poor-quality results.
rows.imp_rat: Starting a step with equipment outside required state violates enforced preconditions (relevance 8) and can damage the batch or equipment (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Equipment interlocks and state checks exist for major utilities but coverage is uneven, leaving gaps for un-instrumented conditions. Equipment-state checks at step start can be automated but often still require manual confirmation, adding minor delay. Interlocks reduce the operator's burden of verifying equipment state before proceeding. Starting in a wrong equipment state can spoil the step, a cost partly avoided by interlocks.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded parameter values differing from the values actually observed
rows.plain: Have fewer recorded settings that differ from what was actually seen during production. This keeps the production record accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values differing from observed values undermines the recording job (relevance 8) and is a data-integrity violation (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Differing recorded values are markedly reduced by automation but persist wherever human keying remains. Automated capture avoids the delay of periodic manual logging that lets errors accumulate. Fewer manual entries mean less reliance on operator transcription accuracy. The recording infrastructure is largely sunk cost in existing systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reported quality indicators misrepresent recorded recipe execution or process parameters
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that reported quality results give a misleading picture of the instructions followed or the settings used during production. This helps people make decisions using an accurate view of performance.
rows.imp_rat: Quality indicators misrepresenting recorded execution corrupts the trustworthiness of records (relevance 7) and can drive wrong release decisions (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Direct system-sourced indicators reduce misrepresentation, but manual calculations, spreadsheets and mapping errors still distort reported values. Automated derivation of indicators from source records is fast once configured. Validating that indicator logic faithfully reflects execution requires analytical skill. Misrepresentative indicators cause misdirected costs and regulatory exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an obsolete recipe version is applied during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that outdated mixing instructions are used to prepare a product. This helps ensure the product is made according to the current requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Applying an obsolete recipe version during compounding violates enforcement (relevance 8) and yields product against outdated, possibly unsafe specs (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Approved-version enforcement is a standard MES feature that reliably prevents obsolete recipes where properly configured, so this need is largely met. Version control in modern recipe-management systems applies the correct version automatically without manual delay. Recipe versioning is a mature, well-documented capability requiring modest configuration skill. Preventing obsolete-recipe use avoids expensive scrap and reprocessing.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between reported and actual batch quality attributes
rows.plain: Make sure the reported product quality matches the product’s actual quality. This matters because decisions should be based on accurate results.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between reported and actual batch quality attributes corrupts the verification that the job was done (high relevance) and is severe because bad batches pass as good, risking release of defective product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated assays and electronic batch records keep reported-versus-actual deviation modest, though sampling representativeness and manual data entry leave recurring gaps. Lab QC testing to confirm actual attributes introduces significant reporting lag. Interpreting analytical results and reconciling them to reported values needs trained QC personnel. Analytical testing, sampling, and documentation are ongoing cost drivers.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient quantity values distorted by unit or scaling errors
rows.plain: Avoid entering ingredient amounts incorrectly because of confusing measurement units or changes in scale, so each batch has the right ingredients.
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient quantity values distorted by unit or scaling errors strike the heart of accurate batching; the consequence is a mis-formulated batch that may be unsafe or unusable, directly failing the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems with unit fields reduce these errors, but manual transcription still allows them to slip through. Unit or scaling errors are typically caught late, adding rework time when they surface. Preventing unit/scaling distortion depends on careful data entry and reviewer attention, a skill-dependent step. Data validation costs little but an undetected scaling error can scrap a whole batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that the production environment remains outside aseptic limits during compounding
rows.plain: Spend less time with the production area not meeting the required germ-free conditions while the ingredients are being mixed. This lowers the chance that the product becomes contaminated.
rows.imp_rat: Environment out of aseptic limits during compounding is environmental control adjacent to the scope (relevance 6) with severe contamination consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring detects the excursion but does not shorten it, so duration outside limits remains variable. Recovery time to aseptic limits after a disturbance is largely uncontrolled and monitored only passively. Bringing the environment back within limits depends on operator and HVAC response judgement. Prolonged out-of-limit environment threatens the batch, an expense weakly controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of nonconforming preparations not signaled by monitored quality indicators
rows.plain: Have fewer preparations that fail to meet requirements without being flagged by the quality checks being watched. This helps identify problems before they go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Nonconforming preparations not signaled by monitored indicators is a detection-reliability failure (relevance 6) that can let harmful product pass (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Indicator coverage is incomplete and many quality failure modes are not captured by monitored parameters, so escapes remain common. Monitored indicators update near real time but detection of nonconformance can lag actual quality failure. Deciding which indicators truly signal nonconformance requires substantial quality expertise not fully automated. Missed signals allow nonconforming product to advance, incurring downstream and recall costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving a tested protective control bypassed or incorrectly reset after functional testing
rows.plain: Make sure a safety feature that was tested is turned back on and set correctly afterward. This helps ensure the machine will respond safely if a dangerous condition occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving a protective control bypassed or wrongly reset after testing is a maintenance-state error somewhat peripheral to batching itself, but severity is very high as an unguarded system can cause injury during subsequent operation.
rows.sat_rat: Left-bypassed or mis-reset safety controls remain a well-documented recurring incident cause that current permit/tag practice only partly prevents. Restoring and re-verifying every bypass after testing is a final step often rushed at job close-out. Tracking which protective controls were bypassed and confirming correct reset depends on technician memory and discipline. Reset verification adds little material cost but the consequences of a missed reset are severe.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of executing recipe steps outside the approved sequence
rows.plain: Lower the chance that recipe instructions are carried out in the wrong order. This helps ensure the process follows the approved method.
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence step execution violates recipe enforcement (relevance 9) and can cause reactions or contamination, though many sequences are recoverable (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic phase/sequence enforcement is a mature MES capability that reliably blocks out-of-sequence execution where deployed. Enforced step sequencing prevents out-of-order actions instantly rather than requiring after-the-fact rework time. The MES electronic batch record enforces the workflow so operators need not memorize the correct order. Sequence violations that ruin batches are expensive, and enforcement avoids those losses at modest system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contaminants to product-contact surfaces during rinsing
rows.plain: Keep germs and bits of unwanted material from getting onto product-touching surfaces while they are rinsed between batches.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing microbial or particulate contaminants during rinsing directly corrupts product-contact surfaces, central to the CFJ of preparing safe liquid batches; consequences can render entire batches unsafe and trigger recalls or health harm.
rows.sat_rat: Qualified CIP and rinse validation make rinse-introduced contamination rare in well-maintained systems, though poor drainage and rinse-water quality still cause occasional excursions. Validated CIP/rinse cycles run on fixed timed sequences without adding operator effort. Automated cleaning cycles largely remove reliance on operator technique for rinse integrity. Rinse water, cleaning agents and utilities are ongoing costs, but far cheaper than a contamination event.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which automated control resumes before the operator releases the manual override
rows.plain: The system should not take control back from the operator until the operator has let go of the manual control. This helps ensure the operator stays in charge while making an adjustment.
rows.imp_rat: Automated control resuming before manual-override release directly undermines the operator's authoritative control central to the job; when it occurs, it can cause unexpected equipment motion while personnel are engaged, risking injury and process damage.
rows.sat_rat: Handshake logic requiring explicit override release exists in mature systems, yet timing races and incomplete conditions still occasionally allow early resumption. The timing of automation resumption is governed by logic conditions rather than operator speed. Correct release-handshake design reduces operator burden, but relies on engineered interlocks being complete. Premature auto-resume can cause conflicting action and product loss, an occasional but costly failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of selecting a mode incompatible with the current sterile fill-finish process state
rows.plain: Avoid choosing a working mode that does not fit what the sterile filling and finishing process is currently doing.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a mode incompatible with the current sterile fill-finish state is central to correct process control and severe — it can compromise sterility, ruining product batches and risking patient safety.
rows.sat_rat: State-based mode interlocks and recipe systems block many illegal transitions, yet configuration gaps still let incompatible modes be selected in specific process states. Mode selection itself is instantaneous, so an incompatible choice costs time only through the recovery it triggers. Judging which mode fits the current sterile fill-finish state relies on operator process knowledge, and mode interlocks only partly relieve that burden. A wrong mode in a sterile fill-finish run can spoil product batches worth significant value, though the software itself adds no cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connected field devices that lose communication after the interface update
rows.plain: Fewer connected pieces of equipment should stop communicating after their connection settings are updated. This helps the updated equipment continue working together.
rows.imp_rat: Field devices losing communication after an interface update breaks the monitor-and-control link to the plant floor; loss of visibility and control over those devices can halt or endanger the process.
rows.sat_rat: Device auto-discovery, diagnostics and standardized protocols make comms loss after updates detectable and usually recoverable, so it is fairly well handled. Reconnection after an interface update is generally fast once configuration is correct. Restoring lost device communication requires networking and fieldbus expertise but follows well-understood diagnostics. Lost device comms halts affected loops, incurring downtime cost until resolved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production interruption while the industrial control software is restored
rows.plain: Get production running again more quickly when restoring the software used to control sterile filling and finishing.
rows.imp_rat: Restoring the control software to resume production directly relates to the software's operational continuity job; prolonged interruption of sterile fill-finish causes major batch loss and costly downtime, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Backup/restore tooling is mature, but restoring a fully validated running state quickly is not consistently reliable. Software restore times are shortened by imaging and virtualization but full validated restoration of control software can still stall production for hours. Rapid restoration depends on skilled staff following validated recovery procedures under pressure. Every hour of sterile-line downtime during restore represents large lost-production cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a consequential excursion is overlooked among nonactionable indications during the shift
rows.plain: Lower the chance that an important process problem is missed among alerts that do not require action during a work shift. This helps ensure serious problems are noticed and handled.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a consequential excursion amid noise is an alarm-management concern (relevance 6); a missed critical excursion can produce unsafe product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Poorly rationalized alarm systems mean genuine excursions are routinely buried, so this need is served badly today. Sifting consequential excursions from alarm noise consumes attention the overseer cannot spare in real time. Distinguishing an actionable excursion amid nuisance alarms demands experienced judgement and constant vigilance, the classic alarm-flood problem. An overlooked excursion can spoil a batch, so the cost of a miss is high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material beyond its expiration or retest date is accepted for use
rows.plain: Do not accept materials that are past their expiration date or their required review date. This helps ensure only approved materials are used.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting expired/beyond-retest material feeds degraded input (relevance 8) risking sub-potency or safety issues (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Expiry and retest-date gating in inventory systems is mature and reliably blocks expired material. Expiry/retest dates are checked automatically at dispensing without operator delay. System date-checking removes reliance on the operator reading and comparing dates. Expired-material batch loss is well prevented by automated date enforcement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that ingredient proportions are incorrect after scaling the batch quantity
rows.plain: Make sure the ingredient amounts stay in the correct proportions when the batch size is changed. This helps produce the intended mixture.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect ingredient proportions after scaling directly defeats the preparation job (very high relevance); the whole batch is off-formula, causing waste or unsafe product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-management scaling reliably preserves proportions for standard formulations, with rare errors mostly from non-linear or edge-case ingredients. Automated scaling computes proportions instantly at batch definition. Recipe scaling logic removes the manual arithmetic that once required careful operator attention. Scaling is a built-in software function with negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the control process with incorrect batch control parameters
rows.plain: The process should be started with the correct settings for the particular batch. This helps prevent the batch from being made incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating with incorrect batch parameters is central to correctly configuring the controlled process. Severity is high as a bad batch parameter can ruin the entire batch and require costly rework or scrap.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe templates and range checks catch many errors but manual override and free-text entry still let wrong parameters slip through with real recurring gaps. Parameter entry and verification before a batch start adds modest setup time that established procedures accommodate. Correct parameterization still leans heavily on operator diligence and process knowledge despite templates. Bad batch parameters can scrap expensive material, and prevention relies on manual checks rather than free automation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a recovered backup contains missing or corrupted control configurations, recipes, or history
rows.plain: Make sure a restored backup has all the needed computer settings, production instructions, and records, without missing or damaged information. This helps the operation recover correctly after a failure.
rows.imp_rat: A recovered backup with missing or corrupted configs, recipes, or history defeats the purpose of restoration; the consequence is inability to resume validated production and potential loss of regulatory records, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Automated backup verification and integrity checks are established practice, so corrupt-backup surprises are increasingly rare though not impossible. Backup restoration is quick, but verifying completeness of the restored data takes additional time. Detecting missing or corrupted configs in a recovered backup requires expertise in validation of restored data. Corrupt backups can force costly re-configuration or requalification if undetected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the allowable aseptic hold interval between recipe steps
rows.plain: Keep the time between production steps within the allowed limit while protecting the product from germs. This helps maintain the product's cleanliness during processing.
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding aseptic hold interval between steps is a contamination-timing constraint tied to sequencing (relevance 6) with severe sterility consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Hold-timer enforcement is inconsistently automated, so exceeding aseptic hold limits still occurs. Aseptic hold intervals are tracked but timers are often manual and easy to overrun. Tracking allowable hold windows depends on operator awareness of the limits. Exceeding hold intervals can force batch rejection, a cost weakly guarded.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm thresholds
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between configured interlocks and alarm thresholds and approved safety requirements
rows.plain: The system’s safety protections and warnings should match the approved safety rules as closely as possible. This helps ensure the equipment responds appropriately when a dangerous condition occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between configured interlocks/alarms and approved safety requirements undermines correct configuration (relevant) and can allow undetected unsafe conditions and compliance failure (severe), though it is a latent rather than immediate error.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement-to-configuration conflicts are common because reconciliation is manual and documents drift, so this is only partially served. Cross-checking configured interlocks and alarms against approved safety requirements is a slow, largely manual traceability exercise. It takes expert reviewers to reconcile as-configured settings against requirement documents without automated help. Manual traceability consumes significant engineering hours and rework budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the standby controller, network, or power path fails to assume service during failover testing
rows.plain: Make sure the spare controller, communication connection, or power supply takes over successfully when the main one fails during a test. This helps keep the operation running during a failure.
rows.imp_rat: Standby failure to assume service defeats the redundancy that is fundamental to a costly DCS's availability promise (relevance 7); a failed failover means total control loss when it matters most (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Standby paths are tested periodically, but latent failures in rarely-exercised standby components mean failover surprises still occur despite redundancy design. Failover tests are scheduled during planned windows so time pressure is moderate, though verifying every path lengthens the window. Executing and interpreting failover tests without disrupting live control requires experienced commissioning engineers. Maintaining true redundant standby hardware doubles some hardware cost, an accepted DCS design premium.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material risks in recorded or enforced compounding recipes and parameters that remain undetected
rows.plain: Make sure important risks in written or enforced mixing instructions and settings are found before approval. This helps prevent unsafe or incorrect preparation.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected material risks in recorded recipes is a detection concern (relevance 6) whose escape can lead to serious quality escapes (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Detection is inconsistent because it hinges on individual judgement rather than systematic risk enumeration. Risk detection in recipes and parameters is a slow analytical task with no quick shortcut today. Spotting latent material risks depends almost entirely on the expertise and imagination of senior reviewers. Undetected risks that surface later are far costlier than the review effort, which itself is expert-intensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composition nonuniformity within the scaled batch
rows.plain: Make the materials in the larger batch as evenly mixed as possible. This helps ensure that each portion has the intended composition.
rows.imp_rat: Composition nonuniformity within the scaled batch is the definitional core of the batching/preparation job; severity is high because non-uniform product fails spec, though it is typically scrapped rather than causing irreversible harm.
rows.sat_rat: Well-characterized agitation and blend-time studies make in-spec uniformity the routine outcome, though dead zones and scale-up effects still occasionally cause stratification. Validated mixing recipes with defined agitation times deliver uniform batches without extra operator time. Once mixing parameters are qualified the operator needs little judgement, reducing skill dependence substantially. Properly specified agitation adds modest energy cost while preventing costly non-uniform rejects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical parameters assigned limits that conflict with the approved recipe
rows.plain: Reduce the number of important process settings whose allowed limits disagree with the approved production instructions. Matching limits help workers run the process correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameters assigned limits conflicting with the approved recipe corrupt the enforcement baseline (relevance 8) so batches pass against wrong bounds (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled recipe parameters keep limits aligned, though manual SCADA overrides can still diverge. Recipe-defined limits load automatically without configuration delay at runtime. Approved-recipe limit management removes operator responsibility for setting limits. Conflicting limits can misjudge quality, mostly prevented by controlled recipes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time a critical parameter remains outside specified limits before correction takes effect
rows.plain: Shorten the time that an important process setting stays outside its allowed range before it is brought back. Quick correction helps keep the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: Time a critical parameter stays out of limits before correction directly affects enforcement effectiveness (relevance 8); prolonged excursion damages product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Correction latency depends on control-loop tuning and operator response, giving good but variable performance. Alarms and closed-loop control shorten the interval between excursion and correction considerably. Automated control response reduces dependence on the operator reacting fast enough. Shorter out-of-limit time limits material degradation and scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of altering validated control configurations or batch recipes during installation repair
rows.plain: Avoid changing approved control settings or product instructions while repairing the software installation. This helps ensure the process still works as it was approved.
rows.imp_rat: Altering validated configurations or batch recipes during repair undermines the software's configuration integrity; in a regulated sterile context an unintended recipe change can invalidate batches and breach GMP compliance, making severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Strong configuration management, checksums and change control in regulated software make unintended recipe alteration fairly well controlled, though not eliminated. Repairs are done in controlled windows so time seldom forces shortcuts on configuration integrity. Avoiding accidental changes to validated recipes during repair depends on disciplined, expert change control. Version-control and configuration-management tools that protect recipes add cost but are widely adopted.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recontaminating cleaned product-contact surfaces before batch materials are introduced
rows.plain: Keep cleaned surfaces that touch the product from getting dirty again before the materials are added. This helps keep the product free from unwanted contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Recontamination after cleaning but before charging negates the cleaning step central to a safe batch (relevance 8), leading to contaminated product with high consequence (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Clean-hold-time limits and closed systems help, but recontamination during the gap before batching remains a real recurring vulnerability. Keeping circuits closed and pressurized after cleaning until batching adds hold-time management overhead. Maintaining post-clean integrity depends on operators respecting clean-hold windows and closed-system discipline. Nitrogen blanketing or clean-hold consumables add modest cost to protect cleaned surfaces.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing a liquid other than the designated liquid into a required circuit during priming
rows.plain: Put only the intended liquid into the required pipes and equipment when filling them for use. This helps prevent the process from being contaminated or disrupted by the wrong liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing the wrong liquid into a circuit during priming directly undermines batch identity and purity, a central concern, with severe consequences of a mis-formulated or unsafe batch.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated circuits and interlocks prevent most wrong-liquid priming, but shared manifolds still leave a real risk of cross-introduction. Confirming the correct priming liquid adds a verification step before flow. Avoiding cross-liquid priming depends on operator line identification and valve alignment knowledge. Dedicated lines and verification hardware cost more than shared manifolds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of recorded process data lost during compounding interruptions
rows.plain: Avoid losing the information recorded about the process when mixing is paused or interrupted. This keeps the production record complete and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded data lost during interruptions creates record gaps (relevance 7) that can force batch rejection and cause compliance issues (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Buffering and redundancy protect most data, but control interruptions still risk losing un-committed process data. Recovery routines aim to restore data quickly after an interruption. Restoration depends on IT support rather than operator skill during outages. Redundant storage and UPS add real infrastructure cost that not all sites fully implement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batch record identifies a recipe revision other than the one executed
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the production record names a different recipe version from the one actually used. This matters because the record must accurately show how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Batch record citing wrong revision is a core recording/traceability defect (relevance 8) with serious data-integrity consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Where an EBR is in place the executed revision is bound to the record reliably; residual risk lives in manual or hybrid paper systems. Electronic batch records auto-stamp the executed revision, so recording it costs essentially no time. Automatic capture removes reliance on the operator to transcribe the correct revision identifier. A record naming the wrong revision triggers costly investigations, but automation keeps this uncommon.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the displayed setpoint differs from the value applied to the connected device after adjustment
rows.plain: Make sure the value shown on the screen matches the value actually sent to the connected machine. This prevents the machine from operating differently than the worker expects.
rows.imp_rat: A displayed setpoint diverging from the applied device value strikes at the core configure-and-monitor job, since operators act on displayed values. Severity is high because acting on a false setpoint leads to wrong process conditions and defective output.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop write-verify handshakes in validated systems make mismatch between displayed and applied setpoints uncommon, though rare comms glitches or scaling errors persist. Setpoint write-back and read-confirm happen in milliseconds so the operator sees applied values almost instantly. Mature HMI/SCADA readback and confirmation dialogs mean operators need little special expertise to trust the displayed value. Standard confirmation logic is built into modern control platforms at no incremental cost to the user.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that practice commands affect live sterile fill-finish equipment
rows.plain: Make sure test commands cannot operate the real equipment used to fill sterile products.
rows.imp_rat: Practice commands leaking to live sterile equipment is a training/simulation-mode isolation concern, moderately central, but severe because any unintended actuation on live fill equipment can ruin a batch.
rows.sat_rat: Dedicated simulation or offline environments exist but are not universal, so accidental effects on live equipment remain a real risk in many installations. Switching to a simulation or test mode is quick when such a mode exists. Keeping practice commands off live equipment relies on operators correctly selecting isolated modes, a vigilance-dependent step. A practice command reaching live sterile equipment can destroy a lot, a severe cost current isolation only partly guards.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical departure is overlooked amid simultaneous deviation indications
rows.plain: An important problem should not be missed when several warning signs appear at the same time. Finding it promptly helps prevent a larger problem.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical departure amid simultaneous deviations is an alarm/detection concern (relevance 6); a missed critical departure can yield unsafe product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-flood management and prioritization are weak in many systems, so critical departures are frequently overlooked. Simultaneous alarms appear at once, but discerning the critical one among them consumes reaction time. Prioritizing a critical departure amid alarm floods depends heavily on operator experience and attention. Missing the critical one amid noise can lose a batch, poorly mitigated today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unplanned process-control loss during cybersecurity patch deployment
rows.plain: Prevent the equipment’s control or operation from being unexpectedly disrupted while computer security updates are installed.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of process control during patch deployment is a real but bounded operational scenario for the DCS (relevance 6); an unplanned trip mid-patch can cause a hazardous or costly upset (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Even with vendor-tested patches and redundant controllers, unexpected process trips during patch deployment remain a recurring, feared event with no reliable elimination. Patch windows are scheduled around production but staging and validation stretch deployment across long maintenance windows that still risk overrun. Safely patching a live DCS demands scarce OT-security specialists who understand both the vendor stack and process risk, and that expertise is thin. Redundant test benches, vendor-validated patch bundles, and downtime coordination are expensive but budgeted as necessary evils.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying endpoint criteria from the wrong recipe version to the active batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of using the end-point rules from an outdated recipe for the current batch. This helps ensure the batch is judged using the correct instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Applying endpoint criteria from the wrong recipe version to the active batch is a direct enforcement error (relevance 8) that can mis-terminate and produce nonconforming product (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: MES recipe version control reliably ties endpoint criteria to the active approved recipe. Version-controlled recipes load instantly without operator lookup time. Recipe version management removes the operator's burden of picking the right version. Wrong-version endpoints scrap batches, well prevented by version control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between recorded parameter values and actual compounding conditions
rows.plain: Make recorded settings match the conditions that actually existed while ingredients were being combined. This helps reveal when the process did not run as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between recorded values and actual conditions defeats the recording obligation (relevance 8) and is a serious data-integrity finding (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA-recorded values closely track actual conditions where instruments are calibrated, so recorded-versus-actual discrepancy is fairly well controlled. Automated sensors log values continuously, so there is no delay introducing discrepancy from manual timing. Direct instrument capture removes the transcription skill that used to introduce discrepancies. Discrepancies trigger investigations, but automated capture keeps these costs contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating the control process before sterile fill-finish prerequisites are satisfied
rows.plain: Do not start the control process until all conditions needed to fill and seal clean medicine containers have been met. This helps prevent the product from being made in an unsuitable state.
rows.imp_rat: Starting the control process before sterile prerequisites are met is fairly central to the initiation logic of the job and severe in outcome, since it can compromise an entire batch's sterility from the outset.
rows.sat_rat: Start-permissive interlocks and electronic batch prerequisites are mature GMP practice, making premature starts fairly uncommon though not impossible if permissives are incomplete. Prerequisite checks run quickly at start so they add little delay to batch initiation. Automated permissive checks reduce reliance on operators remembering every prerequisite. Starting without prerequisites met can spoil a batch, and permissive gating largely prevents this at low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connected devices entering inconsistent operating states during initiation
rows.plain: Make sure all connected machines begin in matching and expected operating conditions. This helps prevent them from working against one another when the process starts.
