rows.imp_rat: Connected devices entering inconsistent states during initiation undermines the coordinated control the software must provide. Severity is high because inconsistent states at startup can cause collisions, spills, or damaged equipment.
rows.sat_rat: State interlocks and startup sequences keep devices consistent in most cases, but timing races and partial initialization still produce inconsistent states occasionally. Coordinated startup sequencing runs quickly under automation without added delay. Interlock logic encodes consistency rules so operators rely less on their own coordination. Inconsistent states can damage equipment, and interlocking is a standard no-extra-cost design practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting an unapproved or obsolete recipe version for the next batch
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that someone chooses a recipe that has not been approved or is no longer current for the next batch. This helps ensure the batch is made using the correct instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an unapproved or obsolete recipe version is central to producing the correct batch, and using the wrong recipe severely compromises product identity, compliance, and safety.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipe version control with approval workflows and obsolete-version lockout is mature and reliable, so selecting an obsolete recipe rarely occurs. Selecting the current recipe from a managed system is immediate and non-blocking. Version control removes the need for the operator to know which revision is approved. Recipe-management software is a fixed overhead that prevents costly wrong-version batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded parameter values that differ from values applied or observed during compounding
rows.plain: Make sure recorded process settings match what was actually used or observed while the material was being mixed. This keeps the production record accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values differing from applied/observed values is a core recording defect (relevance 8) with data-integrity consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Electronically captured readings rarely differ from applied values once instruments feed the record directly, making this well served. Direct electronic capture eliminates the lag that manual re-entry once introduced. Automatic data acquisition removes dependence on careful manual transcription. Fewer transcription errors mean fewer costly record reconciliations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into the ingredient feed lines and supply tanks during priming and charging
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that germs or unwanted particles enter the ingredient pipes and supply tanks while they are being filled and prepared for use. This helps keep the ingredients clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminating feed lines and supply tanks during priming/charging propagates into every batch drawn from them (relevance 8), making the consequence widespread contamination and scrap (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Closed charging systems and sterile filtration handle most cases reliably, but open-manway ingredient additions remain a recurring contamination gap. Sterile charging of feed lines and supply tanks adds handling time at each ingredient introduction. Operators must correctly execute aseptic tank charging and line priming, so outcomes remain dependent on careful technique. Sanitary fittings, filters and disposable transfer sets add material cost that plants already absorb.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required control configuration elements omitted during restoration
rows.plain: Make sure every required control setting is restored when the software is brought back from a backup. Missing settings could cause the equipment to operate incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required control configuration elements during restoration is central to reliably returning the system to service; a missing element can leave the process in an incorrect or unsafe control state.
rows.sat_rat: Full-image backups and configuration checklists reduce omissions, but partial or manual restores still leave gaps. Restoration is done in windows, so omitted elements are a completeness issue rather than a time issue. Ensuring no configuration element is omitted during restoration relies on thorough expert review or complete backup sets. Missing config elements can force costly re-work or delayed line startup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-process interlocks and permissives omitted from control sequences
rows.plain: Make sure all required safety checks and automatic stops for the sterile process are included in the control instructions. This helps prevent the process from continuing when an unsafe condition occurs.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted sterile-process interlocks/permissives are important to correct sequencing in specific industries (relevance 7); the consequence is batch loss or contamination with health/compliance stakes (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Structured requirements tracing catches most interlocks, but omissions in complex sterile sequences still surface during qualification with real regularity. Deriving sterile-process interlocks from URS and P&IDs is a slow iterative activity that often extends the specification phase. Correctly translating GMP sterile requirements into interlock logic demands rare combined pharma-process and controls expertise that most teams lack in depth. Specialist consultants and repeated review cycles add cost, though this is accepted as unavoidable for regulated fill-finish projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of imported data overwriting existing validated destination configuration
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that bringing in saved settings replaces the approved settings already in the receiving system. This helps preserve the setup that has already been checked and accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Imported data overwriting validated configuration is a data-management concern adjacent to the core control job. Severity is high because losing validated configuration can force revalidation and produce incorrect control behavior.
rows.sat_rat: Import tools often lack robust merge/overwrite protection, so accidental overwrites of validated destinations remain a real recurring hazard. Guarding imports against overwriting validated configs adds review and backup time. Avoiding destructive imports depends on operator vigilance and understanding of import behavior. Overwriting validated configuration triggers costly recovery and re-validation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the selected recipe version does not match the approved batch order
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the wrong recipe is used for an approved production order. Matching the recipe to the order helps ensure the batch is made as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Selected recipe not matching the approved batch order is a selection/enforcement error (relevance 8) that produces the wrong product against orders (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: MES order-recipe linkage reliably ensures the executed version matches the approved batch order. Batch-order-to-recipe binding is automatic with no lookup delay. Order-recipe matching is system-enforced, not left to operator selection. Running the wrong recipe scraps a batch, well prevented by order binding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of issued commands not executed by the target device
rows.plain: Make sure devices carry out the commands sent to them. This helps prevent the equipment from being in the wrong state or performing the wrong action.
rows.imp_rat: Issued commands not executed by the target device is a direct reliability failure of the core control job (high relevance); dropped commands can leave the process in an unintended state with real product/safety impact (severe).
rows.sat_rat: Read-back verification and communication watchdogs make undetected non-execution fairly uncommon on well-maintained networks. Execution feedback returns quickly, so unexecuted commands are usually detectable without long delay. Detecting non-execution relies on feedback loops rather than operator skill, though interpreting failures needs some experience. Unexecuted commands can cause missed process steps and rework, mitigated by write-back checks at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the specified and actual ingredient quantity added at a programmed step
rows.plain: Make the amount of ingredient added at each planned step match the required amount as closely as possible. This helps ensure the final product has the intended recipe.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between specified and actual quantity at a step is the essence of accurate batching (relevance 9); moderate deviations may be caught or corrected, but larger ones spoil the batch (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Load-cell and Coriolis-based step dosing is a mature practice holding tight tolerances, but drift, in-flight material, and viscous residuals produce recurring small deviations. Automated dosing at each step runs to setpoint without operator waiting beyond normal fill time. Closed-loop dosing removes most manual finesse, though calibrating flow and weigh instruments still needs trained technicians. Metering and load-cell hardware plus recalibration add cost, offset by tighter yield and less rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connected devices that fail to enter their required startup states
rows.plain: Make sure all connected devices reach the states they need to be in when starting. This helps the control process begin safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Devices failing to enter required startup states directly defeats the software's job of bringing controlled equipment to correct operating condition. Severity is high because an incomplete startup can halt production or leave equipment in a hazardous partial state.
rows.sat_rat: Startup permissive checks and state feedback reliably catch most devices that fail to reach required states, though silent failures without feedback still occur. Startup state confirmation is automated and prompt. Sequence logic checks device states so operators need not manually verify each one. Failed startups waste time and material, and readback checks are standard included features.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter entries containing an incorrect unit, sign, or decimal placement
rows.plain: Reduce entries with the wrong measurement unit, plus or minus sign, or decimal point. Correct entries help prevent incorrect decisions and actions.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect unit/sign/decimal in a parameter entry is a recording defect that can drive dangerous misdosing (relevance 7); magnitude errors can be catastrophic (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Input validation catches many unit and sign errors, but free-form or misconfigured fields still let some through. Format validation catches unit and decimal errors at entry rather than at review. Automated range and unit checks reduce reliance on operator precision. Validation masks are configured once within field definitions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that specified wetted materials are incompatible with the formulation or sanitation requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the materials that touch the product work safely with the product and can meet cleaning and sanitation needs. This helps prevent damage, contamination, or ineffective cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Specifying wetted materials incompatible with the formulation or sanitation undermines both product quality and cleanability (high relevance) and is severe—systemic contamination or corrosion across many batches (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard material spec sheets and sanitation compatibility guidance handle typical cases, yet gaps between formulation and cleaning-agent exposure are a known recurring failure. Cross-checking wetted materials against formulation and sanitation regimes lengthens specification review modestly. Requires combined knowledge of formulation chemistry and CIP/SIP sanitation effects, a rare skill combination. Wrong material selection drives rework and requalification costs, though the check itself is low-cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of production batches executed with obsolete recipe revisions
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should be made using an outdated version of the recipe. Using the current recipe helps keep production consistent and within required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Executing with obsolete revisions directly violates recipe enforcement (relevance 8) and produces batches against outdated, possibly unsafe specs (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe lifecycle management with effective-dating is mature, though interfaced or paper-parallel systems occasionally allow obsolete revisions through. Effective-revision enforcement at batch start prevents obsolete downloads instantly. Automatic serving of the current effective revision removes operator judgement about which version to use. Avoiding obsolete-revision batches prevents rejected product and rework spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control authority is transferred to an unintended user or station
rows.plain: Make sure control is handed only to the intended person or workstation.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring control authority to an unintended user or station is fairly central to safe operation and severe: an unauthorized party could issue harmful commands to live equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Explicit station addressing plus confirmation makes transfer to an unintended user rare, though ambiguous naming or crowded station lists occasionally misdirect it. Confirming the intended recipient adds a brief step but does not materially slow operations. Selecting the correct target station is a guided, low-skill action in most modern HMIs. Addressing and confirmation are built-in, so there is no meaningful cost to avoiding misrouted control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the ingredient quantity loaded and the batch target
rows.plain: Load an ingredient amount that is as close as possible to the amount required for the batch. This helps keep the finished product consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between loaded quantity and batch target directly determines batch correctness (relevance 9); consequences range from rework to scrap depending on magnitude (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Total-batch weigh control is standard and generally accurate, though cumulative dosing error, tank tare drift, and clingage keep small target deviations occurring. Gravimetric batch totalizing reaches target automatically without adding wait time. The system tracks cumulative mass so operators need little skill to hit the batch target. Weigh-vessel instrumentation is capital-intensive but reduces giveaway and out-of-spec loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approved recipe revision or parameter set is unavailable at scheduled batch start
rows.plain: Have the approved instructions and settings ready when the batch is scheduled to start. This helps prevent delays and ensures the batch uses the correct process.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable approved recipe at batch start blocks the core execute job (relevance 8); it mainly delays or risks improvised start (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems keep approved revisions accessible, though approval-workflow lags and library sync gaps occasionally leave a revision missing at start. Approved recipes are normally pre-loaded before the shift so availability at start is usually immediate. Retrieving the right approved revision requires little expertise once the recipe library is maintained. An unavailable recipe delays a batch window and ties up staff, but this is infrequent.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible retained chemical residues being combined during segregation and packaging
rows.plain: Keep leftover chemicals that could react badly from being mixed while they are separated and packed for disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Combining incompatible retained chemical residues during segregation and packaging is a waste-handling concern peripheral to batch preparation, but severity is catastrophic due to potential for violent reactions or toxic release.
rows.sat_rat: Chemical-compatibility segregation is well-established practice, so incompatible mixing during packaging is relatively uncommon. Segregating and packaging by compatibility adds sorting time that is planned into waste handling. Preventing dangerous combinations relies on trained personnel reading SDS and applying compatibility knowledge. Separate containers and segregation add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of interruption to environmental controls needed to maintain sterile conditions during patch application
rows.plain: Keep the clean-air and other environmental protections from being interrupted for long while software updates are installed.
rows.imp_rat: Interruption to environmental controls during patching is somewhat peripheral to the core control function and tied to maintenance timing. Severity is very high in sterile contexts since any lapse in environmental control can compromise sterility of the whole batch.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant environmental control paths help but are not universally present, so interruptions during patching remain a recurring risk. Environmental controls must stay up during patching, but current practice often requires taking controllers offline, creating unavoidable interruption windows. Sequencing patches to keep HVAC/environmental loops running demands expert planning of dependencies. Any lapse in sterile environmental control risks costly contamination or requalification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-compounding and contamination-control constraints omitted from fit criteria
rows.plain: Fewer requirements for making products free from contamination and for working with sterile materials should be left out when judging whether an approach is suitable. Including them helps ensure the approach can be used safely.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting contamination-control constraints from fit criteria is a design-completeness concern outside live execution (relevance 5) but misses controls with severe safety stakes (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control constraints are frequently under-represented in generic fit checklists, so critical constraints are omitted and inconsistently caught. Sterile and contamination-control constraints are numerous and cross-referenced, and enumerating them fully exceeds the assessment window. It takes rare combined aseptic-processing and control-system expertise to translate contamination-control constraints into fit criteria. A missed sterility constraint can invalidate batches, making omissions extremely expensive relative to review cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a significant input change and the appropriate control response
rows.plain: Make the equipment respond correctly as soon as an important input changes. This helps keep the process stable and on target.
rows.imp_rat: Timely control response to a significant input change is the essence of real-time process control; a slow response can cause out-of-spec conditions or overshoot, though most single events are correctable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Deterministic scan-based execution gives consistent, timely control responses, though poorly tuned loops can lag on significant input changes. Control loops respond to input changes within tuned scan/execution times that are typically well within process requirements. Loop tuning to achieve responsive control still needs engineering expertise. Responsive control is achieved with standard PID execution at no extra material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded addition quantities with a missing or incorrect unit of measure
rows.plain: Record the amount of each added material with the correct unit, such as kilograms or liters. This prevents confusion about how much was used.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/incorrect unit on a recorded addition quantity is a recording defect (relevance 7) that can misrepresent dose with serious potency implications (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: System-enforced units make missing or wrong UOM rare where master data is complete. Pre-defined units on dispense fields avoid delay from correcting them later. Fixed unit-of-measure fields relieve operators from specifying units correctly. Unit definitions are set in material and recipe master data.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous or regulated chemical residue left in the batching system after disposal activities
rows.plain: Leave as little dangerous or legally controlled chemical residue as possible in the mixing system after it is emptied for disposal. This helps protect people and the environment.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous or regulated chemical residue left after disposal activities is a downstream/end-of-life concern less central to producing a batch, but consequences are severe: regulatory violation and environmental or personnel harm.
rows.sat_rat: Residue reduction is achievable but inconsistently reaches low regulatory levels in aged batching systems. Reducing regulated residue often means added flush cycles that lengthen the job. Driving residue down to regulatory thresholds needs knowledgeable selection of cleaning chemistry and methods. Every increment of residue removed adds solvent, labor, and hazardous-disposal cost, and leftover residue drives up regulated waste tonnage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into product-contact circuits during priming
rows.plain: Prevent germs and small particles from getting into the parts that carry the product while they are being filled with liquid. This helps keep the product clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing microbial/particulate contamination into product-contact circuits during priming is central to a preparation system whose output must be safe (relevance 8), and contaminated product can cause serious harm and batch loss (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature CIP/SIP and closed-transfer practice makes contamination during priming uncommon in well-run plants, though occasional breaches still occur. Priming with sterile-grade filtration and aseptic connections adds setup time but is a routine part of established start-up sequences. Aseptic priming demands trained operators and adherence to SOPs, so residual contamination events still trace back to skill and vigilance lapses. Single-use aseptic connectors and sterile filters raise consumable spend but are standard budgeted items in regulated facilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a deviation indication fails to reach the operator during the departure
rows.plain: Make sure the operator is warned when the work starts to go off course. This lets the operator correct the problem during the departure process.
rows.imp_rat: A deviation indication failing to reach the operator blocks timely enforcement response (relevance 7); an unseen departure can persist and harm the batch (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: SCADA alarming reliably reaches operators for configured points, but unconfigured or suppressed alarms can still fail to notify. Alarms annunciate in real time so notification is prompt when configured. Alarm routing reduces reliance on the operator constantly scanning for departures. A missed alarm can cost a batch, partly guarded by redundant annunciation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of captured entries lost or overwritten during compounding
rows.plain: Reduce the number of recorded entries that disappear or are replaced while the product is being mixed. This preserves a complete and trustworthy record of the work.
rows.imp_rat: Lost or overwritten captured entries directly breaks the recording job (relevance 7) and is a serious data-integrity/GMP violation (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Database commit and audit trails prevent most loss or overwrite, though buffering gaps and concurrent edits occasionally lose entries. Transactional databases commit entries continuously so little is lost to timing. Persistence is handled by the system, requiring no operator effort. Reliable storage is a built-in feature of validated systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that updated control logic produces out-of-spec batching conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changed control software causes the production mixture to fall outside its required limits. This helps keep the finished product consistent and acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Updated control logic producing out-of-spec batching conditions directly governs whether batches meet specification, central to the CFJ; a bad logic change can silently ruin many batches before detection, a high-severity outcome.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change-control and factory-acceptance/pre-release testing catch most out-of-spec logic, yet edge-case interactions still slip through into production periodically. Regression testing and simulated batch runs before release consume days but are budgeted into planned maintenance windows. Verifying control logic against spec demands scarce controls engineers who understand both PLC code and process chemistry, a persistent bottleneck. Test batches, wasted materials, and engineering time make validation runs expensive but broadly accepted as necessary.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting the wrong batch phase, parameter, or target value when adjusting an active process setpoint
rows.plain: Avoid choosing the wrong step, setting, or desired value when changing an active process. This helps prevent the process from running incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting the wrong batch phase, parameter, or target when adjusting a setpoint is central to correct process control; an operator error here can ruin a batch or breach limits, making severity high though it is preventable operator ambiguity.
rows.sat_rat: Structured recipe phases and confirmation prompts reduce mis-selection, yet ambiguous HMI layouts leave a real residual error rate. HMI navigation to the correct phase and parameter is quick once operators are familiar with the interface. Selecting the right setpoint among many phases still relies on operator attention, and cluttered HMIs invite the wrong pick under pressure. A wrong setpoint selection can spoil a batch, but such errors are individually infrequent so aggregate cost is moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of uncommanded industrial device actions when the restored operational state is activated
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that industrial equipment starts doing things on its own when its saved settings are turned back on. This helps prevent unexpected or unsafe actions.
rows.imp_rat: Uncommanded device actions on restored-state activation is a state-transition error (moderately relevant to control integrity); a surprise actuation during restart can damage equipment or compromise sterility (severe).
rows.sat_rat: Restart/restore sequences frequently re-energize outputs unpredictably, and standard practice relies on manual pre-checks rather than robust safe-start guarantees. State restoration is done during planned windows, so duration is rarely the primary concern. Verifying that a restored state won't trigger uncommanded device motion requires deep expertise in the specific control logic and device states. An uncommanded actuation on activation can destroy product and equipment, and there is little automatic cost protection today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that input or feed variation exceeds the controllable operating range
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changes in incoming material or signals become too large for the equipment to handle. This helps keep the process under control.
rows.imp_rat: When input/feed variation exceeds the controllable range the software cannot fulfill its regulation job; this pushes the process out of spec, though it reflects a physical limit boundary rather than always causing catastrophic loss.
rows.sat_rat: Control software constrains and alarms on limits, but it cannot fully prevent feed/input variation from exceeding controllable range, leaving a persistent uncontrolled-disturbance gap. Detecting range excursions is prompt but does not prevent the underlying feed variation. Keeping inputs within controllable range depends on upstream operator and process management judgement more than the control software. Out-of-range feed causes off-spec product and waste, with limited free software safeguards to prevent it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between recorded event chronology and actual execution chronology
rows.plain: Keep the order and timing recorded in the records aligned with what actually happened during the work. This helps people trust the records and reconstruct the work accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between recorded and actual event chronology is a data-integrity defect in recording (relevance 7) with serious regulatory implications (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic chronology is generally faithful, though asynchronous manual entries can distort actual order. System clocks timestamp events as they occur, preserving chronology automatically. Automated timestamping removes reliance on operators to note event order. Timestamping uses existing system clocks at no added cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from authorized emergency shutdown initiation until connected industrial devices cease operation
rows.plain: After an authorized emergency shutdown begins, all connected industrial equipment should stop as quickly as possible. This helps limit the time that a dangerous situation continues.
rows.imp_rat: Latency between shutdown command and device stop degrades emergency response (relevant to the control job); a slow stop prolongs hazardous conditions (severe), though eventual shutdown limits it below total failure.
rows.sat_rat: Deterministic safety PLC scan times and hardwired paths make the stop latency consistent and predictable across activations. Safety-instrumented shutdown paths are engineered for deterministic sub-second stop times and are the core metric they optimize. Achieving fast stop requires no operator skill since the response is automated once initiated. Fast-acting safety hardware carries cost but is mandated and standardized, so it is a solved expenditure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected batches with incomplete recipe and process-parameter records
rows.plain: Complete the records showing the recipe and operating settings for every affected production batch. This gives investigators the information needed to understand what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete recipe/parameter records fail the recording pillar (relevance 7); incomplete records can block release and violate GMP (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: EBR/MES capture most parameters reliably, but manual entries, instrument gaps and interface failures still leave some records incomplete. Electronic batch records capture parameters automatically as the batch runs, avoiding after-the-fact assembly delays. Automated data capture reduces reliance on staff to manually complete records. Incomplete records force costly reconstruction and can render batches unreleasable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of sterile-boundary breaches caused by overlapping operator activity
rows.plain: Two or more operators working at the same time should not cause a break in the clean boundary around the process. This helps keep the product protected from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Sterile-boundary breaches from overlapping operator activity are a procedural contributor to contamination rather than a core process step; when they occur they can breach sterility and spoil batches, a severe consequence.
rows.sat_rat: SOPs and interlocks help, but sterile-boundary breaches from concurrent human activity remain a persistent, behavior-driven failure mode. Coordinating operator activity to avoid sterile-boundary overlap can slow work through sequencing and waiting. Avoiding overlapping intrusions depends heavily on trained aseptic technique and disciplined behavior. A single boundary breach can scrap a costly batch, making occasional breaches expensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a brief parameter excursion occurs between captured observations during recipe execution
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a process setting briefly goes outside its allowed range between recorded checks. This matters because a short problem could be missed.
rows.imp_rat: A brief excursion between observations escapes capture (relevance 6); such gaps can hide out-of-limit conditions affecting quality (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where sampling is periodic rather than continuous, short excursions genuinely slip between observations, a real gap. Brief excursions between samples pass in seconds, faster than periodic observation intervals catch. Catching transient excursions demands continuous vigilance no human can sustain between captures. An unseen transient can compromise a batch, creating latent cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that output calibration causes unintended equipment actuation in the sterile fill-finish area
rows.plain: Adjust the measuring signals without accidentally making the equipment move or start. This is especially important in the clean area where medicine is put into containers.
rows.imp_rat: Output calibration causing unintended actuation is a narrower calibration-mode error (moderately relevant); still severe because unexpected motion in the sterile area can breach containment or injure staff.
rows.sat_rat: Standard calibration-with-isolation practice usually prevents actuation, but errors on output channels can still trigger unintended movement. Calibration in preparation is scheduled with equipment isolated, adding planned time. Preventing actuation during output calibration requires trained technicians using proper simulation and isolation techniques. Calibration tooling and isolation procedures carry modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting control actions by remote and on-site operators during sterile fill-finish activity
rows.plain: Prevent remote and on-site workers from giving conflicting instructions at the same time. This helps keep the medicine-filling work safe and controlled.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting remote and on-site operator actions is a coordination gap moderately central to multi-operator control; when it occurs it can drive contradictory device states that disrupt or contaminate sterile activity.
rows.sat_rat: Ownership locks and access arbitration help, but concurrent remote/on-site operation is a persistent coordination weak point with recurring conflicts. Contention resolves quickly once detected, so timing is not the primary issue. Coordinating remote and on-site operators leans on procedural discipline and communication skill more than software enforcement. Conflicting actions can cause deviations and product loss that current locking partly avoids.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial contamination onto product-contact surfaces during cleaning
rows.plain: Prevent germs from getting onto surfaces that touch the product while those surfaces are being cleaned. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial contamination onto product-contact surfaces during cleaning undermines the hygienic foundation of preparation (relevance 8) and risks a contaminated batch and patient safety (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Validated SIP and sanitization is a mature, widely deployed practice that reliably delivers clean surfaces with rare excursions. Automated CIP/SIP cycles run to validated schedules, consuming predictable cleaning time between batches. Validated automated cleaning removes most operator judgement, lowering the skill needed to achieve sterile surfaces. Cleaning chemicals, water and energy are significant but well-controlled recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of production batches initiated before required recipe approvals are complete
rows.plain: Prevent production batches from starting until all required instructions have been reviewed and approved. This helps ensure production follows accepted and permitted methods.
rows.imp_rat: Starting before approvals complete is a control-gate failure (relevance 7); it risks unvalidated production and compliance violation (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Enforced release-to-production gates are common, but production pressure and manual overrides still let some batches begin before approvals are fully complete. Approval-status checks at batch start are automated and quick where the system enforces gating. System gating removes reliance on a supervisor manually verifying approval completeness. Preventing premature starts avoids wasted material and re-execution costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an entered setpoint fails to take effect in the active batch
rows.plain: Make sure a new target setting actually takes effect in the batch that is running. Otherwise, the process may continue using the old setting.
rows.imp_rat: An entered setpoint failing to take effect in the active batch undermines the reliability of process control central to the job; the batch drifts off target though the failure is often detectable and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop feedback and setpoint-echo confirmation catch most non-applied entries, though comms glitches and unconfirmed manual writes still occasionally fail silently. Control systems apply confirmed setpoints essentially instantly, and failures to take effect surface fast via feedback. Operators must still confirm the change registered, requiring some attentiveness where feedback is weak. A setpoint silently not applying can ruin a batch, but robust confirmation loops keep such losses infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active recipe revisions lacking required validation or change-control evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the number of current production instructions that have not been properly tested or supported by records showing that changes were reviewed. This helps ensure production follows approved and reliable instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Active revisions lacking validation/change-control evidence is a governance gap somewhat aside from execution (relevance 6) but a serious compliance and quality risk (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Change-control systems track status but active revisions lacking complete validation evidence still slip through where linkage is not enforced. Linking validation evidence to revisions is a manual, sometimes slow curation task. Confirming adequacy of validation and change-control evidence relies on quality specialists' judgement. Missing evidence triggers costly remediation and re-validation efforts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering controlled production areas while batching-system electrical infrastructure is accessed
rows.plain: Keep dirt and other unwanted material out of the controlled production area while electrical parts of the mixing system are being accessed. This protects the cleanliness of the area.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering controlled areas during electrical-infrastructure access moderately relevant to preserving the aseptic environment around batching, with serious contamination consequences for concurrent production.
rows.sat_rat: Preventing particulate or personnel-borne contamination during electrical access relies on ad hoc containment that frequently leaves gaps, so protection is only partial. Erecting containment or isolating an electrical enclosure area from clean production space adds meaningful setup time before work can begin. Balancing electrical access with contamination control requires cross-disciplinary judgement that few maintenance techs hold alone. Temporary barriers, gowning, and area re-cleaning after panel access impose real recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sequences, parameter limits, and interlocks that the recommended approach cannot enforce
rows.plain: Make sure the recommended approach can require the right order of steps, keep settings within allowed limits, and prevent unsafe actions. This shows whether the approach can actually control the work as needed.
rows.imp_rat: An approach unable to enforce required sequences/interlocks undermines the enforcement mandate (relevance 6); unenforced controls permit unsafe processing (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Interlock and parameter-limit enforcement is sometimes demonstrated but seldom exhaustively verified, leaving unenforceable cases unknown at recommendation time. Verifying every sequence, limit, and interlock takes far longer than the evaluation schedule permits, so coverage is partial. Judging whether a candidate can truly enforce interlocks requires automation-engineering skill that the influencer often must borrow. Unenforceable interlocks discovered post-purchase drive rework and custom development spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recalibration uses inaccurate or outdated reference values
rows.plain: Make sure equipment is reset using correct, current reference values. Old or inaccurate values could make the equipment operate incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Recalibration using inaccurate or outdated reference values corrupts the measurement basis of control; downstream decisions become systematically wrong, though the error propagates gradually rather than instantly.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo depends on manual document lookups and version control that frequently let outdated or wrong reference values slip through, so the outcome remains inconsistent. Verifying reference values against master standards adds noticeable delay to recalibration windows, but it is not primarily a time problem. Confirming that reference values are current and traceable relies heavily on experienced metrology or validation staff who know which sources are authoritative. Maintaining traceable reference standards costs money, yet the failure itself is not chiefly a cost overrun.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of device linking disrupting active sterile fill-finish control
rows.plain: Connect the equipment without interrupting or upsetting the active sterile production process. The connection should not interfere with ongoing control of that process.
rows.imp_rat: Device linking is a configuration activity moderately central to control, but if it disrupts active sterile fill-finish control the consequence is batch loss and possible contamination, raising severity considerably.
rows.sat_rat: Staged commissioning during preparation usually isolates linking from active control, though occasional disruptions still occur. Scheduling device linking to avoid active control adds planning time. Linking devices without disrupting active control needs experienced network/controls engineers. Disruption causes costly downtime, motivating careful staged practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting an incorrect or unapproved recipe revision for the batch
rows.plain: Prevent the wrong approved instructions, or instructions that have not been approved, from being used for the batch. This helps ensure the batch is made the intended way.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting an unapproved recipe revision relates to configuring the control process correctly, though it is one step among many. Severity is high in regulated production since an unapproved recipe produces non-compliant, unsellable batches.
rows.sat_rat: Batch software with electronic signatures and revision locking prevents most unapproved-recipe use, yet parallel dev/prod environments and manual selection leave residual risk. Recipe selection from an approved library is quick with no meaningful delay. Version control offloads the burden from operator memory, but selecting the right revision still needs some judgement. Wrong-revision batches are costly to remediate, and electronic version control is a standard included capability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of control and monitoring disruption caused by purge activity during sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Reduce how long production controls and monitoring are interrupted while the system is being flushed. This helps keep sterile production under control.
rows.imp_rat: Control and monitoring disruption from purge activity touches the CFJ but is confined to a specific transient operation. Severity is high because losing visibility during production creates a window for undetected faults.
rows.sat_rat: Purge routines are predictable but the monitoring blackout they cause is poorly mitigated by standard practice. Purge activity inherently pauses control and monitoring, and there is little tooling to shorten this window during live production. Timing purges to minimize disruption relies on operator judgement about batch phase. Purge-induced disruption during sterile production can waste in-process product and consumables.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the root cause of a compounding disruption
rows.plain: Identify the true underlying cause of a problem that interrupts mixing or preparation, rather than blaming the wrong cause. This helps the team fix the problem effectively and prevent it from happening again.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying disruption root cause is investigative, downstream of execution (relevance 5); wrong cause allows recurrence and possible bad release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: With fragmented data and thin real-time diagnostics, the status quo frequently produces a wrong or incomplete root cause, making this a chronic pain. Root-cause identification typically drags out well beyond the shift, delaying corrective action. Correctly diagnosing a compounding disruption relies on scarce senior engineering and process expertise rarely present on every shift. Misdiagnosis leads to repeated failures and wasted troubleshooting labor and material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical compounding scenarios lacking sufficient enforcement evidence
rows.plain: Have fewer important mixing situations without enough proof that the required rules were followed. This helps show that each mixing process meets its obligations.
rows.imp_rat: Critical scenarios lacking enforcement evidence is a validation-completeness concern (relevance 5); unproven enforcement can fail in production (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recommendations commonly rest on demonstrations of a few happy-path scenarios, leaving most critical scenarios with insufficient enforcement evidence. Gathering enforcement evidence for every critical scenario is time-intensive and usually truncated before completion. Knowing what constitutes sufficient enforcement evidence for a scenario requires validation expertise not consistently on hand. Thin evidence leads to costly rework when unproven scenarios fail in qualification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of validating an obsolete or unintended control configuration for the sterile fill-finish system
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of checking an outdated or unintended set of controls for the sterile filling system. This helps ensure the test applies to the setup that will actually be used.
rows.imp_rat: Validating an obsolete or unintended configuration is a moderately central verification-integrity issue; when it slips through, an incorrect baseline governs sterile operations, risking systematic non-compliance.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration management practices largely prevent validating obsolete configs, though drift between documented and installed states occasionally causes errors. Confirming the configuration under validation is current adds minor checking time. Identifying the correct configuration is procedural with good documentation and change control. Validating an obsolete configuration wastes significant validation effort, incentivizing checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from target consistency after a parameter adjustment
rows.plain: Keep the mixture as close as possible to the desired evenness after changing the mixing settings. This helps maintain consistent product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from target consistency after a parameter adjustment is central to achieving the target product quality of the preparation job; consequences are off-spec batches that are typically correctable rather than harmful.
rows.sat_rat: Without in-line consistency feedback and model-based control, post-adjustment deviation from target recurs and is corrected reactively. Re-attaining target consistency after an adjustment costs settling and re-test time. Predicting how a parameter change moves consistency toward target leans on operator experience. Overshoot and corrective re-work consume ingredient and time, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface area retaining previous-batch residue after cleaning
rows.plain: Remove as much leftover material from product-touching surfaces as possible, so residue from the previous batch does not affect the next one.
rows.imp_rat: Residual previous-batch residue after cleaning risks cross-contamination and is important to batch integrity (relevance 8); consequences depend on residue toxicity but generally require rework or scrap (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin and swab-validated CIP reliably clears residue on accessible surfaces, though shadowed geometries and deadlegs still retain traces. Adequate CIP flow, time and temperature to remove residue extends the cleaning window. Validated cleaning recipes reduce reliance on operator skill to reach residue targets. Reaching low residue levels drives water, chemical and energy consumption upward.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of deleting retention-required batch and audit records during the purge
rows.plain: Do not erase production records or activity history that must be kept when removing old system data. These records may be needed later for tracking or review.
rows.imp_rat: Deleting retention-required batch and audit records is peripheral to real-time control but severe in consequence — irreversible loss of compliance evidence can trigger regulatory sanction and product recalls.
rows.sat_rat: Retention-locking and archive segregation features are common and generally prevent required-record deletion, though misconfigured purges still occur. Purge operations are scheduled, so timing is not the core issue for record deletion. Correctly configuring retention rules to exclude required records during purge takes expert knowledge of retention policy. Deleting retention-required records risks regulatory penalties far exceeding storage-saving benefits.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of judging chemically incompatible wetted materials suitable for the intended application
rows.plain: Make sure the materials that touch the process liquid will not react badly with it or make the process unsafe. This helps ensure the equipment is suitable for its intended use.
rows.imp_rat: Judging chemically incompatible wetted materials suitable is a design-selection error, moderately central to batching operation (relevance) but severe—material degradation can leach contaminants or cause failures (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility charts, USP Class VI data and supplier declarations cover common cases, but nonstandard chemistries still produce recurring misjudgements. Material compatibility review adds review cycles but is typically done in parallel with other evaluation tasks. Correctly judging chemical compatibility of wetted materials against a specific formulation requires specialized materials-science and process expertise that is not always on hand. Compatibility errors surface late and are costly to remediate, but upfront review itself is inexpensive.

rows.source_job: Integrate with plant and enterprise systems
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that integrated data or commands are mapped to unintended DCS control points
rows.plain: Make sure information and commands are connected to the correct plant control points. A wrong connection could cause the wrong equipment or process to respond.
rows.imp_rat: Integrated data/commands mapped to unintended control points corrupts DCS data integrity (relevance 6); wrong control points can trigger unintended and hazardous actions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Interface testing exists but proprietary multi-system integration remains error-prone, with unintended control-point mappings a known persistent risk. Validating integration mappings across systems is a slow, cross-team verification effort that often lags the schedule. Correct mapping of integrated data and commands requires scarce integration architects who understand both source and DCS namespaces. Mapping errors surface late and force expensive rework across interconnected systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between occurrence of an execution departure and operator recognition
rows.plain: Shorten the time between a process mistake happening and the operator noticing it. Faster awareness allows the problem to be corrected sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time between departure occurrence and operator recognition governs correction speed (relevance 7); slow recognition prolongs harm (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Recognition latency is low for alarmed parameters but longer for conditions relying on manual observation. Real-time alarms shorten the gap between departure and operator recognition substantially. Automated detection reduces the vigilance operators must maintain to notice departures. Faster recognition limits the material lost during a departure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps confirmed with required parameter records missing
rows.plain: Reduce the number of process steps marked as finished when required records of their settings or results are missing. This helps ensure completion is supported by the needed evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Steps confirmed with missing parameter records fails recording completeness (relevance 7); gaps impede release evidence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Where parameter-to-step gating is configured it works well, but not all steps are consistently interlocked. Systems can block step confirmation until required parameters are entered, avoiding later rework. Gating logic offloads the check that all parameters accompany a step from the operator. Linking parameters to step confirmations is a configuration effort within the recipe.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an operator-screen control commands the wrong process equipment
rows.plain: Make sure each button or control on the operator's screen operates the intended equipment, so the wrong machine is not started or changed.
rows.imp_rat: An operator screen commanding the wrong equipment defeats the human-machine interface accuracy central to DCS operation (relevance 7); it can cause immediate unsafe actuation (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Graphics testing during FAT catches most mislinked controls, though some wrong-equipment bindings still escape to later stages. Verifying every operator-screen control against its target device is a methodical checkout that consumes graphics-testing time. Graphics-to-tag verification is largely procedural, needing care more than rare expertise. Standard HMI configuration tools make this checkable at modest incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process conditions moving outside validated limits after a setpoint or operating-mode adjustment
rows.plain: After changing a target setting or operating method, keep the process within its proven safe and acceptable range.
rows.imp_rat: Excursions outside validated limits after setpoint/mode changes are central to safe regulation transitions (relevance 8); consequences include off-spec product or equipment stress, serious but often recoverable (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Configured ramp limits and clamps prevent many excursions, but abrupt or ill-considered adjustments still occasionally push conditions past validated limits. Setpoint ramps and mode transitions execute promptly once initiated by the operator or recipe. Safe transitions rely on operators and engineers configuring appropriate rate limits and interlocks, demanding real process knowledge. Ramp and mode-change logic is native to the DCS and adds no material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controlled variables remaining outside their designated safe states before DCS power-down
rows.plain: Before shutting down the control system, bring as many operating settings as possible into their planned safe conditions. This helps reduce risks during the shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving controlled variables outside safe states before power-down defeats a key DCS shutdown safeguard (relevance 7); it can leave equipment in a hazardous unmonitored state (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Mature interlock logic, fail-safe designs, and documented shutdown procedures make leaving a variable in an unsafe state uncommon where those practices are in place. Bringing controlled variables to safe states before power-down follows established shutdown sequences that take predictable, bounded time in most plants. Experienced operators and commissioning-grade engineers know the safe-state sequencing, though the judgement is not trivial and depends on scarce plant-specific knowledge. Executing a controlled safe-state shutdown consumes staff time and lost production but requires no special material spend beyond normal decommissioning effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that removing the industrial control software interrupts control or monitoring of active sterile fill-finish equipment
rows.plain: Remove the control program without interrupting equipment that is still filling and finishing sterile products. This helps keep active equipment under control and observation.
rows.imp_rat: Software removal interrupting control/monitoring of active equipment is a lifecycle-edge error (moderately central); losing control of running sterile equipment is very severe though it occurs only during uninstall/upgrade.
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP change-control and impact-assessment practice reliably prevents active-equipment interruption during planned removal, though rare human-error mishaps still occur. Coordinated shutdown windows are planned well in advance in pharma settings, so timing is rarely the binding constraint on avoiding disruption. Removal near active sterile fill-finish equipment requires experienced automation and validation engineers, and such expertise is scarce but typically retained for GMP decommissioning. Formal change control and staged removal add cost, but this is budgeted as normal capital retirement activity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous pressure, stored energy, or process liquid during diagnosis and repair
rows.plain: Protect technicians from dangerous pressure, trapped energy, or process liquid while they find and fix a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to stored energy or hazardous liquid is a maintenance-side error, less central to the batching job itself (relevance 5), but its consequences—serious injury or death—are catastrophic (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Mature OSHA/regulated LOTO and permit-to-work practice reliably prevents most exposure incidents, though residual failures occur when a hidden energy source or accumulator is overlooked. LOTO and de-energization procedures add time to every job but are routinely built into maintenance schedules, so the time cost is predictable rather than a chronic pain. Executing a proper energy-isolation sequence on a multi-utility batching skid demands trained, authorized technicians who understand every stored-energy source, which remains a real skill dependency. Lockout hardware, permits, and downtime carry modest recurring cost that is broadly accepted as the price of safe work.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting status indications between the control software and connected devices
rows.plain: Reduce disagreements between what the control program says and what connected equipment reports. This helps workers understand the real condition of the system.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting status indications between software and connected devices weakens the reliable-monitoring aspect of the job; the ambiguity can mislead operators, though it is often detectable before causing direct harm.
rows.sat_rat: Data-quality flags and heartbeat monitoring reduce conflicting indications, though intermittent comms and buffering still create sporadic inconsistencies. Status updates are frequent so discrepancies are typically short-lived. Reconciling conflicting indications requires some operator interpretation but is aided by data-quality flags. Conflicting status can lead to misinformed action and rework, mitigated at modest cost by quality tagging.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into exposed product-contact areas during repair
rows.plain: Keep exposed areas that touch the product free from contamination while workers fix a problem.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced into exposed product-contact areas during repair moderately affects batch integrity (relevance) and is severe because it can taint subsequent product (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Contamination ingress during open repair is a chronic risk poorly contained by manual practice, so it is only partially served. Repairing an opened product-contact component under contamination control extends job duration through cleaning and clearance steps. Keeping an exposed wetted surface clean during hands-on repair depends heavily on disciplined aseptic technique that varies by technician. Repair-then-reclean-and-revalidate cycles consume significant chemicals, labor, and lost production.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of residual pressure, chemical, electrical, or mechanical hazards left unidentified before decommissioning
rows.plain: Before taking equipment out of service, find any remaining pressure, chemical, electrical, or moving-part dangers. This helps prevent injuries during removal or shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving residual pressure, chemical, electrical, or mechanical hazards unidentified before decommissioning is peripheral to routine batching output (moderate relevance) but severe when it occurs, since undetected stored energy can cause serious injury during teardown.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-decommissioning hazard assessments exist as practice but consistently miss hazards on aged or poorly documented systems. A thorough pre-decommissioning hazard survey takes significant time that projects frequently underbudget. Comprehensively enumerating pressure, chemical, electrical, and mechanical hazards requires broad multidisciplinary expertise rarely held by one person. The assessment labor is a modest fraction of overall project cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the demonstrated configuration and conditions and the intended sterile fill-finish application
rows.plain: Make sure the demonstration setup and conditions closely match how the system is meant to be used to fill and finish germ-free products.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between demonstrated configuration and the intended sterile fill-finish application is a qualification/validation alignment concern moderately relevant to fitness-for-use, with serious consequences if a system is misapplied to sterile duty.
rows.sat_rat: Demos and reference installations rarely match the exact intended conditions, so residual mismatch is a common and under-controlled failure. Verifying demonstrated configurations against intended conditions often requires site visits or FAT observation that extend schedules. Judging whether a vendor demonstration truly represents the intended sterile application takes experienced process engineers who can spot subtle mismatches. Travel, witnessed testing and demo setups add cost, and undetected mismatches trigger expensive change orders later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of batch nonuniformity caused by variation in incoming ingredient viscosity
rows.plain: Keep each production run as consistent as possible when the incoming ingredient is thicker or thinner than usual. This helps the finished product remain uniform.
rows.imp_rat: Batch nonuniformity from incoming viscosity variation is a real cause of off-spec product relevant to mixing quality, but it is one contributing factor among several and usually yields correctable batch defects rather than severe harm.
rows.sat_rat: Fixed mixing recipes seldom adapt to lot-to-lot viscosity swings, so non-uniformity from this source recurs without dedicated in-line rheology feedback. Compensating for incoming viscosity variation typically requires added mixing or heating time that extends the batch. Judging how to adapt agitation to a more viscous incoming lot leans on operator experience that is not consistently codified. Extra energy and occasional rework from viscosity-driven non-uniformity add recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to stored mechanical or process energy during service
rows.plain: Protect technicians from energy that remains stored in pumps and mixing equipment while they are being serviced. This includes pressure, moving parts, and other forces that could suddenly be released.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to stored mechanical/process energy during service is peripheral to the batching function (relevance 5) but can cause fatal or disabling injury (severity 10).
rows.sat_rat: Standardized energy-dissipation and zero-energy verification is well established and consistently effective, with rare lapses on infrequently serviced components. Verifying stored mechanical/process energy is discharged (springs, accumulators, actuators) is a defined step that adds bounded time to service. Recognizing all stored-energy points on pumps, actuated valves, and pressurized vessels still relies on technician training and experience. Bleed-down and verification tooling is inexpensive relative to the injury and downtime it prevents.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that adjusting one critical parameter drives another beyond specified limits
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that changing one important setting pushes another setting outside its allowed range. This helps keep the process stable and safe.
rows.imp_rat: One adjustment driving another parameter out of limits is a coupling-stability issue in enforcement (relevance 6) that can create hidden excursions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Most control is single-variable, so multivariable interactions are inconsistently managed and remain a real risk. Interacting-parameter effects appear in real time but are hard to anticipate quickly. Understanding how adjusting one parameter drives another requires significant process expertise. Cross-coupled excursions can ruin batches, poorly guarded by single-loop control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required critical-parameter observations missing during the compounding run
rows.plain: Reduce the share of required checks of important process conditions that are missing during a mixing run. This helps show whether the process stayed under control from start to finish.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required critical-parameter observations weakens both recording and enforcement (relevance 7); gaps undermine release evidence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Critical observations are largely enforced, though manual critical checks can still slip during high-tempo operations. Real-time monitoring flags missing critical observations during the run rather than afterward. Automated critical-parameter prompts reduce dependence on operator vigilance for the most important readings. Critical-parameter monitoring rides on existing instrumentation and control layers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the installed software release differs from the approved validated release
rows.plain: Make sure the installed software is the same approved version that was tested for use. This helps ensure the equipment works as expected and remains within approved operating conditions.
rows.imp_rat: An installed release differing from the approved validated release is largely a change-control/compliance matter rather than direct real-time control. Severity is high because running an unvalidated release invalidates the batch and creates regulatory and behavioral risk.
rows.sat_rat: Checksums, version stamps and controlled installers make installed-vs-approved mismatches uncommon under standard IT/validation practice. Verifying installed release against approved release is a fast checksum/version step. Release verification is largely automated and needs little specialist skill. Verification tooling is cheap relative to the risk of a mismatched release.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that data needed by active sterile fill-finish control functions is purged
rows.plain: Keep important information from being automatically deleted while active production controls still need it. This helps the controls keep working and preserves the information needed to monitor the process.
rows.imp_rat: Purging data still needed by active control functions is a moderately central data-management concern; loss during operation can disrupt active control logic and jeopardize batch integrity and records.
rows.sat_rat: Backup-before-purge discipline and data-mapping reduce accidental purging of needed data, yet poorly catalogued dependencies still allow occasional over-deletion. Data-classification review before purge takes time but fits within planned decommissioning schedules. Correctly identifying which data active control functions still depend on requires specialist data-architecture and process knowledge that is often incomplete. Retention analysis and staged backups add cost but are routine in validated environments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of process oscillation or overshoot caused by control adjustments
rows.plain: Keep the process from repeatedly swinging back and forth or going too far after control adjustments. This helps the process settle at the desired level more smoothly.
rows.imp_rat: Oscillation and overshoot from control adjustments strike at the software's control-quality job; while they degrade product quality, they are usually bounded and correctable rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: PID and advanced control reduce oscillation well when tuned, but drift and interacting loops mean overshoot recurs and needs periodic retuning. Oscillation settling adds process time, which good tuning minimizes but does not eliminate. Controlling overshoot depends heavily on skilled loop tuning, a scarce expertise. Overshoot wastes material and energy, and avoiding it requires tuning effort rather than a free fix.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of execution departures that end before detection
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times work goes off course and finishes before anyone notices. This helps people find and correct mistakes before the work is completed.
rows.imp_rat: Departures that end before detection escape enforcement and record (relevance 6); undetected excursions can compromise the batch unnoticed (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: High-speed historians capture some transients, but sampling gaps mean brief departures still end undetected. Short-lived departures may end before the scan or operator notices them. Catching transient departures depends on operator attentiveness and data resolution. Undetected transients can still affect quality, an underaddressed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable required measurements during endpoint determination
rows.plain: Shorten the time that necessary measurements are unavailable when deciding whether a process has reached its required endpoint. This helps workers make timely and reliable decisions about when to stop.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable measurements during endpoint determination blocks correct endpoint enforcement (relevance 6); blind endpoint calls can under/over-process (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Sensor availability is generally good but gaps in required measurements still stall endpoint decisions. Waiting for unavailable measurements during endpoint determination causes real delay. Working around missing measurements requires operator improvisation. Delays idle resources and risk product, partly controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of field devices wired to incorrect input or output terminals
rows.plain: Reduce the number of devices connected to the wrong connection points. This helps sensors and equipment send and receive the correct signals.
rows.imp_rat: Miswired field devices corrupt the sensor/actuator interconnection the DCS depends on (relevance 6); wrong readings or commands can drive dangerous actions, though usually caught in commissioning (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point checkout catches most errors eventually, but incorrect terminations remain one of the most common recurring defects found in this stage. Verifying wiring assignments against loop sheets is a lengthy manual checkout that frequently stretches the preparation schedule. The work is more diligence than deep skill, but relies on attentive technicians whose vigilance varies across long termination lists. Rework from miswired terminals during checkout is a common, avoidable cost sink for many projects.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile batch during sampling
rows.plain: Prevent germs or other unwanted material from getting into the sterile batch while a sample is taken. This helps keep the batch safe and usable.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during sampling is a discrete step less central to the whole batching job (relevance 6), but can invalidate results or contaminate the sterile batch (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Closed aseptic sampling valves reduce ingress but sampling remains a well-known contamination route where failures still happen. Aseptic sampling with sanitized valves and flush volumes adds handling time per sample. Contamination-free sampling relies on careful aseptic technique, making it operator-skill dependent. Sterile sample assemblies and discarded flush material add per-sample cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material compounding-record discrepancies omitted from evaluation
rows.plain: Reduce the share of differences in material records that are left out of the review. This helps ensure record mistakes or conflicts are properly considered before decisions are made.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting record discrepancies from evaluation is a review-completeness gap peripheral to executing recipes (relevance 5); undetected issues carry moderate-high risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: MES exception reports help but discrepancies buried in free-text or paper records are routinely missed. Manually reconciling compounding records to surface every material discrepancy is a lengthy line-by-line effort. Judging which discrepancies are material requires trained review judgement that is inconsistently available. Overlooked discrepancies cause expensive downstream failures while thorough review consumes reviewer hours.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required parameter or recipe-step entries missing from the compounding record
rows.plain: Record every required setting and mixing step in the product record. Complete records make it possible to confirm exactly how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Missing required parameter/step entries from the record fails the recording pillar (relevance 7) and blocks defensible release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory entry logic makes missing required entries rare where the recipe is well built. Entry-time validation prevents record gaps from propagating to later stages. Field-level enforcement lessens the operator's memory load for required entries. Validation rules are configured once and reused across batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that cleaning temperature, flow, or chemical concentration remains outside validated limits during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep cleaning heat, liquid flow, and cleaning chemical strength within their approved ranges throughout the cleaning cycle. This helps ensure the equipment is cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning temperature, flow, or chemical concentration drifting outside validated limits compromises cleaning efficacy that the batching system depends on; sustained excursions leave contamination risk and validation failures, though a brief excursion may be recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Instrumented CIP with logged parameters keeps cycles within validated limits most of the time, with brief excursions during ramp or upset the main residual gap. CIP controllers hold temperature, flow, and concentration to setpoint automatically, minimizing time spent out of limits. Automated closed-loop control removes most manual parameter management, though deviation diagnosis needs skill. Sensor calibration and utility supply carry ongoing cost but excursions are relatively cheap to correct.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of process oscillation or overshoot following a live setpoint change
rows.plain: Keep the process from swinging back and forth or going too far after a live target change. This helps it reach the new target steadily and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Overshoot following a live setpoint change is central to smooth control transitions; effects are typically transient and recoverable, so severity stays moderate despite high relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Setpoint ramping and filtering reduce live-change overshoot reliably where applied, but default step changes still cause avoidable oscillation. Settling time after a setpoint change is short with well-tuned loops. Managing overshoot on live setpoint changes relies on tuning skill and setpoint-ramping know-how. Overshoot on setpoint moves can spoil product, mitigated by ramping features included in standard tools.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contaminants into product-contact areas during service
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from getting into areas that touch the product while equipment is being repaired or maintained. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contaminants during service moderately affects the clean-batch objective (relevance) and is severe since post-service contamination can compromise multiple downstream batches (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established hygienic-service SOPs cover most cases but recurring contamination gaps persist, keeping this acceptably rather than well served. General service in product-contact areas is bounded but slowed by cleaning and clearance protocols. Avoiding contamination during service requires consistent hygienic discipline that remains technician-dependent. Cleaning consumables and re-verification add ongoing cost to routine service.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process parameters missing from the compounding batch record
rows.plain: Make sure the production record includes every process setting that must be recorded while materials are being combined. This provides a complete record of how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Required parameters missing from the batch record fails the recording job (relevance 7); gaps impede release and reconstruction (severity 7).
