rows.sat_rat: Electronic records enforce completeness well, so missing required parameters are uncommon where the recipe is properly configured. Mandatory-field enforcement catches omissions at entry time rather than at review. Required-field logic offloads the burden of remembering every parameter from the operator. Configuring required fields is a one-time engineering cost in the recipe build.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving a prospective supplier whose quality management system fails sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Avoid approving a new supplier whose quality controls do not meet the needs for making sterile products. This helps ensure that supplied materials and services are dependable.
rows.imp_rat: Approving a supplier failing sterile fill-finish QMS is an upstream procurement error moderately connected to the on-line batching job, but the downstream consequence of systemic contamination is severe and far-reaching.
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier qualification programs, ISO/GMP certification checks and audit protocols catch most deficient QMS cases, though undetected gaps still occasionally slip through. Supplier quality audits and QMS reviews take weeks to schedule and complete, adding real delay to acquisition timelines. Assessing whether a QMS truly meets sterile fill-finish requirements demands scarce senior QA auditors whose judgement varies. Formal audits and third-party certifications carry meaningful expense but are a routine, budgeted line in pharma procurement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that valid operator authorization is rejected during the emergency
rows.plain: A person who is allowed to stop the equipment in an emergency should be recognized and not turned away. This ensures the shutdown can happen when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Rejecting valid operator authorization during an emergency impairs the ability to act, though authorization is a supporting rather than primary control function. Severity is high because being locked out during an emergency can prevent life- or asset-protecting intervention.
rows.sat_rat: Robust RBAC authenticates valid operators reliably, but rigid session timeouts or lockouts can wrongly reject valid credentials precisely during emergencies. Authorization checks are instantaneous and do not add delay in an emergency. Role-based access is configured by administrators, requiring little operator skill at the moment of use. Access-management systems are standard infrastructure with no per-use cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a collected sample does not represent the batch as a whole
rows.plain: Make sure the collected sample accurately reflects the entire lot, rather than just one part of it. This makes the quality decision more reliable.
rows.imp_rat: A non-representative sample defeats the purpose of in-process testing that gates batch quality; the consequence is accepting an out-of-spec batch based on false assurance, a serious quality-escape risk.
rows.sat_rat: Grab sampling from a single point often misrepresents an incompletely mixed batch, and this remains a common weakness without in-line or multi-point sampling. Drawing a representative sample after adequate mixing costs some wait time. Knowing when a batch is homogeneous enough for a valid grab sample relies on operator judgement. Unrepresentative samples cause false decisions and reprocessing, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable data-integrity and regulatory expectations omitted from evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the evaluation includes all relevant rules for keeping records accurate and meeting government requirements. This prevents important compliance checks from being left out.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting regulatory expectations from evaluation is an assessment-completeness concern peripheral to real-time execution (relevance 5) but carries compliance risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement checklists exist but are frequently incomplete or outdated, so omissions recur across evaluations. Assembling the full set of applicable expectations for each evaluation is slow and often done under deadline pressure. Knowing which regulatory expectations actually apply demands scarce regulatory expertise that varies between reviewers. Missed expectations trigger costly rework and findings, and maintaining current requirement libraries is a standing overhead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of prescribed recipe steps absent from the completed execution record
rows.plain: Make sure the final work record includes all the steps that were supposed to be followed for making the product. This helps show that the work was completed correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Prescribed steps absent from execution record is a recording-completeness defect (relevance 7) that undermines traceability and release (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Enforced step gating makes absent steps in the record uncommon in a properly configured EBR. Sequential electronic workflows record each executed step as it is completed. Guided step-by-step execution removes reliance on operators recalling which steps to log. Step sequencing is part of the standard recipe configuration cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cleaning-agent residue remaining in tanks, lines, pumps, mixers, valves, and dispensing points after cleaning
rows.plain: As little cleaning liquid as possible should remain in the tanks, pipes, pumps, mixing equipment, valves, and points where product is dispensed. This helps prevent the cleaning liquid from getting into the product.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-agent residue left in the system contaminates the next batch chemically (relevance 7); consequences are meaningful adulteration but often detectable and correctable (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Conductivity and TOC endpoint verification make cleaning-agent carryover uncommon, but low-flow points still occasionally retain residue. Final rinse and conductivity-verified flush steps add measurable time to each cleaning cycle. Automated rinse-to-conductivity endpoints reduce the judgement needed to confirm chemical removal. Extra rinse water and analytical checks add cost aimed at eliminating chemical carryover.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control authority is transferred before the recipient confirms readiness to control the process
rows.plain: Control of the process should not be handed over until the other person confirms that they are ready. This helps prevent a gap or confusion in who is operating it.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring authority before the recipient confirms readiness is central to smooth handover; if the recipient is not prepared, the process may run unattended briefly, though slightly less severe than a full control gap.
rows.sat_rat: Mature accept/confirm handshakes largely prevent premature transfer, but not every legacy system enforces recipient confirmation, leaving residual exposure. Requiring the recipient to accept before transfer completes adds minimal delay and is well tolerated. Acknowledging readiness is a simple prompt-driven action needing no special skill. The confirmation handshake is a native feature with no added resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of active compounding intervals with unavailable execution-condition data
rows.plain: Make sure information about the conditions during each active mixing period is available. This helps identify when the process may not have run as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Intervals with unavailable execution-condition data leave gaps in both recording and enforcement (relevance 6) that can hide unsafe conditions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Historian and SCADA availability is high, but network or sensor gaps still leave intervals without condition data. Data-unavailable intervals interrupt smooth execution timing. Operators must compensate manually when execution-condition data drops out. Data gaps risk uncontrolled processing, a partly contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of contaminated liquid released into the work area or waste stream during decontamination
rows.plain: As little contaminated liquid as possible should spill into the work area or be sent into waste during cleaning. This helps protect people and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminated liquid released into the work area or waste stream during decontamination is a decontamination-phase environmental/safety issue less central to producing a batch, with severe exposure and compliance consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Bunding, mats, and capture systems reliably contain most decon liquid, with occasional escapes during high-volume flushing. Setting up containment before decontamination adds setup time that is routine. Effective containment placement requires modest, widely held skill. Uncontained contaminated liquid entering the waste stream increases hazardous-disposal volume and cost, which containment directly limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the open fluid path during element replacement
rows.plain: Replacing a filter or similar part should not let dirt or germs enter the open path carrying the liquid or material. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the open fluid path during element (filter) replacement moderately affects batch integrity (relevance) and is severe since a compromised fluid path taints product directly (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Open-path exposure during element change is a known contamination window only partly mitigated by technique and local flushing. Filter-element replacement is a defined quick task, but flushing and clearance afterward add time. Swapping an element without contaminating the open fluid path requires standard hygienic handling most techs can perform. Elements, gaskets, and post-change flush chemicals are recurring but modest costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing microbial or particulate contamination into the batch during the sample draw
rows.plain: Taking a sample should not introduce germs or particles into the batch. This helps keep the batch clean and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during the sample draw is a localized event (relevance 6); it can compromise the batch or yield false readings with notable consequence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Closed single-use sampling systems substantially cut particulate and microbial ingress during the draw, though connection points still present residual risk. Closed sampling devices draw quickly once connected, adding modest time. Single-use closed samplers lower the aseptic skill needed versus open draws. Single-use sampling manifolds are recurring consumable costs but modest per draw.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which exception, deviation, and recovery needs are inadequately evaluated
rows.plain: Thoroughly evaluate what is needed when something unusual happens, work falls outside the plan, or the process must recover. This helps ensure the proposed approach can handle problems safely and effectively.
rows.imp_rat: Inadequate evaluation of exception/recovery needs is a design-readiness concern peripheral to execution (relevance 5) with moderate impact when disruptions occur (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Exception and recovery handling is chronically under-evaluated because demos emphasize normal flow, so these needs are inadequately assessed. Exception, deviation, and recovery paths are the last thing examined and usually run out of evaluation time. Evaluating recovery behavior demands hard-won operational and control-recovery expertise that is scarce. Poorly evaluated recovery needs cause expensive downtime and batch loss in production.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of missing or stale critical parameter readings during production
rows.plain: Shorten the time that important readings are missing or no longer current during production. This helps workers notice and respond to problems sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Missing/stale critical readings during production blinds enforcement (relevance 6); operating blind can let excursions go uncontrolled (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Redundant instrumentation limits data gaps, but sensor faults still produce stale readings occasionally. Stale or missing readings introduce decision delays during production. Judging around stale data requires operator experience. Missing data can force conservative overprocessing, a modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical recipe-control and record-integrity requirements omitted from acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Include all important recipe controls and record-accuracy requirements in the pass-or-fail rules. This helps ensure the system works correctly and keeps trustworthy records.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical requirements from acceptance criteria is an evaluation-completeness gap peripheral to execution (relevance 5) with real compliance risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement-to-criteria mapping tools help, but critical items still get dropped when specifications are ambiguous or evolving. Deriving acceptance criteria from requirements is a time-intensive upfront activity often compressed late in the project. Knowing which recipe-control and record-integrity requirements are critical requires deep domain and regulatory judgement not all reviewers possess. Omissions are cheap to make and expensive to discover later, and thorough criteria development is under-resourced.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recipe-step and process-parameter enforcement gaps remain undetected during fit assessment
rows.plain: Make sure testing reveals any missing controls that should enforce recipe steps and operating settings. This helps show whether the proposed approach can reliably control the process.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected enforcement gaps during fit assessment is a design-evaluation concern peripheral to live execution (relevance 5) with moderate downstream risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Fit assessment relies on vendor assertions and slide decks rather than adversarial testing, so enforcement gaps very commonly remain undetected. Detecting hidden enforcement gaps within the short fit-assessment window is rarely achievable without lengthy hands-on probing that timelines don't allow. Spotting a latent enforcement gap requires deep control-logic knowledge that most evaluators lack, so gaps stay invisible. Undetected gaps become costly change orders once the system is deployed against real recipes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical parameter adjustment crosses the opposite specified limit
rows.plain: When changing an important setting, keep it from moving past the allowed limit in the other direction. This helps keep the process safe and working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: An adjustment overshooting the opposite limit is a control-stability concern in enforcement (relevance 6); overshoot can push product out of spec (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Well-tuned PID/closed-loop control limits opposite-limit overshoot, but manual adjustments still risk it. Control loops respond quickly enough that overshoot windows are short. Avoiding overshoot to the opposite limit depends on control tuning rather than operator skill. Overshoot excursions can degrade product, a cost curbed by tuned control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material additions whose recorded timestamp or sequence differs from actual execution
rows.plain: The recorded time and order of adding materials should more often match what really happened. This makes it easier to trace how the product was made.
rows.imp_rat: Addition timestamps/sequence differing from actual is a recording-accuracy defect (relevance 6) with data-integrity and traceability impact (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Automated additions timestamp reliably, but manually logged additions still show timing or sequence mismatches. Integrated dispensing systems timestamp additions immediately, reducing sequence drift. Automated capture cuts the operator's role in recording addition order. Dispensing integration leverages existing weigh and scan equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile-process deviations without operator annunciation
rows.plain: Reduce serious problems in the sterile process that happen without an alarm or other notice to the operator. This helps operators respond before the problem causes greater harm.
rows.imp_rat: Unannounced sterile-process deviations relate to the DCS's operator-notification role in specific sectors (relevance 6); undetected deviations can compromise product safety and compliance (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: ISA- -management practice covers the common cases well, yet critical deviations occasionally slip through incomplete rationalization. Building the alarm and annunciation rationalization list is time-consuming but runs in parallel with other design work. Deciding which deviations warrant operator annunciation requires experienced alarm-rationalization judgement that is not uniformly available. Alarm rationalization workshops consume engineering hours but use standard tooling, keeping incremental cost moderate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records containing values inconsistent with the actual compounding event
rows.plain: Records about problems during medicine mixing should more accurately match what actually happened. Accurate records help people understand and properly address the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records with values inconsistent with the actual event is a record-integrity defect in investigation documentation (relevance 5) with notable compliance risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Deviation records frequently contain values inconsistent with the actual event because they are reconstructed manually after the fact. Deviation records are often written after the event, delaying accurate capture. Accurately describing a deviation depends heavily on operator recall and judgement. Deviation documentation is largely manual labor with little tooling support.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a parameter excursion and its recognition by the shift overseer
rows.plain: The shift supervisor should notice out-of-range process conditions more quickly. Faster recognition allows problems to be addressed before they grow.
rows.imp_rat: Delay before the shift overseer recognizes an excursion slows escalation (relevance 6) with moderate impact given operator-level detection also exists (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Real-time alarms help but noise and delayed escalation mean recognition is inconsistently timely. The lag between an excursion and the overseer noticing it depends on alarm latency and monitoring cadence, often minutes too long. Faster recognition currently depends on an attentive operator watching the right screen at the right moment. Delayed recognition extends off-spec production, wasting material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required abnormal and failure scenarios that cannot be exercised in the test environment
rows.plain: Make sure the test setup can recreate all the unusual and breakdown situations that must be checked, so problems are found before real use.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to exercise abnormal/failure scenarios in test undermines confidence that the control software handles faults (moderate relevance), and undetected failure-handling gaps can lead to serious in-production incidents (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: A large share of abnormal and failure scenarios simply cannot be exercised in typical test environments, leaving these paths validated only in production. Constructing test setups that can force abnormal and failure scenarios is time-consuming and often deferred under schedule pressure. Devising and safely injecting failure conditions demands specialized test engineering skill that is scarce. Fault-injection rigs and fully representative test environments are costly, so coverage is often sacrificed to save money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the required ingredient charge level in the feed lines and supply tanks
rows.plain: Keep the amount of ingredient entering the supply lines and tanks as close as possible to the required amount, so the process starts with the right quantity.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from required charge level in feed lines/tanks affects batch accuracy (relevance 8); moderate charge errors typically cause rework rather than catastrophe (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated flow/level instrumentation holds charge deviation tight in most systems, with drift and manual top-ups as residual sources of error. Metered charging to target level is fast once flow and level instruments are set up. Automated level and flow control reduces the operator finesse previously needed to hit charge targets. Flow meters and level transmitters are a standard capital cost that pays back in charge accuracy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of drawing the in-process sample from the wrong batch or sampling point
rows.plain: Make sure the sample comes from the correct batch and the correct place, so its test results describe the product being made.
rows.imp_rat: Sampling the wrong batch or point undermines the quality verification tied to batch release; a mis-sourced sample can pass a bad batch or reject a good one, causing significant quality and disposition errors.
rows.sat_rat: Sample labeling and defined sample ports cut wrong-source errors, but manual sampling still allows mix-ups where identification is not enforced. Barcode or dedicated sampling-point identification adds negligible time. Labeling and scanning reduce reliance on operator memory for which batch or port to sample. A mis-sourced sample can invalidate testing and force resampling, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that loss of required utilities interrupts the cycle before completion
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the cleaning cycle stops before it is finished because an essential supply, such as power, water, air, or another service, is lost. This helps ensure the equipment is properly cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of utilities interrupting the cycle before completion directly halts the batching process central to the CFJ; consequences are aborted or reprocessed batches, typically recoverable but wasteful and disruptive.
rows.sat_rat: Utility monitoring and controller hold/resume logic handle most interruptions gracefully, though a genuine utility loss still commonly voids and repeats the cycle. Utility loss mid-cycle forces a restart, and detecting and recovering adds unplanned time. Diagnosing an interrupted cycle and deciding on safe restart requires operator judgement about cleaning state. An aborted cycle wastes chemicals and water and demands a repeat, imposing real but occasional cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the completion criteria for a recipe step
rows.plain: Make it clear exactly what must be true for a recipe step to be considered finished. This prevents people from having to guess when to move on.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous step-completion criteria weakens consistent enforcement (relevance 6) and can cause premature or late step closure of moderate impact (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Ambiguous completion criteria remain a common source of inconsistent step confirmation across operators. Clear criteria speed decisions, but ambiguous steps force operators to pause and interpret. Vague completion wording places heavy interpretive burden on operator experience and training. Improving criteria clarity is largely a documentation effort with modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical sterile fill-finish alarm is overlooked among simultaneous active alarms
rows.plain: Make it less likely that an important warning about the sterile product-filling process is missed when many warnings appear at once. This matters because missing it could allow a serious problem to continue.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a critical sterile fill-finish alarm among simultaneous alarms directly undermines the DCS's operator-notification job (relevance) and can lead to batch loss or patient-safety exposure (severity), but the ceiling caps it at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Prioritization and grouping help, yet during alarm floods a critical fill-finish alarm can still be missed, keeping this a recurring pain. Alarms annunciate instantly, so the delay is in the operator noticing, not the system. Spotting one critical alarm within a crowded list demands sustained operator attention and experience, a chronic vigilance burden. Alarm prioritization tools exist but effective rationalization projects are costly and often deferred.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical compounding execution and enforcement requirements omitted from feasibility assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the feasibility review does not leave out important requirements for carrying out and enforcing the mixing process. This helps reveal practical problems before a decision is made.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting execution/enforcement requirements from feasibility assessment is a planning-completeness concern peripheral to running batches (relevance 5) with moderate downstream risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Requirement lists today are assembled ad hoc from checklists and memory, so critical enforcement requirements are routinely omitted and the gap goes unnoticed until validation. Feasibility assessments are typically compressed into vendor demo cycles, leaving little time to enumerate every critical execution and enforcement requirement. Correctly surfacing all critical compounding execution requirements demands scarce cross-disciplinary expertise in GMP, MES, and process control that few individual influencers possess. Omissions surface only after commitment, converting cheap upfront analysis into expensive late-stage remediation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict among software responses assigned to interdependent devices for the same fault
rows.plain: Make sure connected devices do not give conflicting instructions when the same problem occurs, so the equipment responds safely and predictably.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting responses among interdependent devices for the same fault (relevance) can produce inconsistent control behavior, with moderate-to-high severity because contradictory actions may worsen a fault, though such cases are bounded and configurable.
rows.sat_rat: There is little tool support for detecting conflicting fault responses across devices, so such conflicts routinely go unnoticed until an event triggers them. Analyzing coordinated fault responses across interdependent devices is a slow cross-system design effort. Ensuring non-conflicting responses among interdependent devices demands deep systems-level controls expertise that is rare. Undetected response conflicts risk expensive equipment damage or unsafe states, far exceeding the design labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a recipe-step confirmation being lost after entry
rows.plain: Make sure the system does not lose the record that a recipe step was confirmed. This prevents completed work from appearing unfinished.
rows.imp_rat: A lost step-confirmation after entry is a recording-persistence defect (relevance 6) that undermines execution evidence (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Committed confirmations are rarely lost, though session or sync failures create occasional exceptions. Confirmations are committed immediately on entry, minimizing exposure to loss. Persistence requires no operator action once a step is confirmed. Durable write handling is standard in validated MES platforms.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter records with timestamps inconsistent with the actual execution sequence
rows.plain: Make sure each recorded measurement has the correct time and follows the real order of the work. This creates an accurate record of what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Timestamps inconsistent with actual sequence is a record-integrity defect (relevance 6) that undermines audit trail and can trigger findings (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Automated timestamps are usually sequence-consistent, but late manual entries can appear out of order. Server-synchronized clocks apply consistent timestamps in real time. Operators need no special skill for sequence integrity when the system stamps automatically. Time synchronization is standard infrastructure already in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of conflicting endpoint indications across required variables
rows.plain: Make sure the required measurements point to the same point at which the recipe is complete. This prevents confusion about when to stop the process.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting endpoint indications across variables complicate correct termination (relevance 6) with moderate risk of wrong endpoint calls (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Systems rarely reconcile multi-variable endpoint conflicts, leaving operators to resolve them inconsistently. Conflicting indications force delay while operators reconcile them. Resolving conflicting endpoint variables requires seasoned judgement. Wrong resolution wastes batches, weakly guarded by current tools.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of relevant recipe variants and operating ranges absent from feasibility evaluation
rows.plain: Check all important recipe versions and operating conditions when deciding whether the process can work. This helps avoid approving a process that has not been properly tested.
rows.imp_rat: Recipe variants/ranges absent from feasibility evaluation is a coverage-completeness concern outside live execution (relevance 5) with moderate downstream risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Feasibility evaluation typically covers representative recipes rather than the full variant space, so relevant variants and ranges are routinely absent. Cataloguing all recipe variants and operating ranges is slow, so evaluations sample only a subset. Deciding which variants and ranges matter demands process knowledge that the influencer may not hold directly. Variants missed in evaluation surface as expensive reconfiguration after commitment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty about whether the active batch follows the approved recipe and sequence
rows.plain: Reduce the time spent unsure whether the active batch is following the approved instructions and order of steps. Quick confirmation helps prevent an incorrect process from continuing.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of uncertainty about recipe conformance affects timely enforcement (relevance 7); prolonged ambiguity risks continuing a bad batch (severity 7).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sequence steps skipped or executed out of order within a batch
rows.plain: Make sure every required step in a production run is completed in the correct order. This helps the batch turn out as intended and avoids missed steps.
rows.imp_rat: Skipped or out-of-order batch steps undermine the sequencing job the DCS performs (relevance 7); consequences include ruined batches or unsafe conditions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Structured batch managers strongly enforce step order and completion, making skipped or out-of-order steps uncommon under normal operation. Automated batch phase execution advances steps at machine speed without manual pacing. ISA- engines enforce sequencing so operators need little judgement about order, though recipe authoring requires expertise. Batch execution modules are a licensed but routine part of pharma DCS deployments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from operator confirmation until the new setpoint governs the connected device
rows.plain: Make the equipment use a newly approved setting as quickly as possible. This reduces the delay between the worker’s confirmation and the equipment’s response.
rows.imp_rat: The delay from operator confirmation to the setpoint governing the device is central to responsive control, but a modest lag is usually recoverable and rarely catastrophic, keeping severity moderate.
rows.sat_rat: The path from confirmation to governing setpoint is deterministic and fast in mature systems, so this is consistently well served. Modern controllers apply confirmed setpoints to devices within a scan cycle, making the delay negligibly small. No special skill is required as the propagation is automatic. Fast setpoint propagation is inherent to standard controllers with no added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recalibrated values are not retained after activation or restart
rows.plain: New control settings should still be saved and in use after the system is turned on or restarted. Otherwise, the system may return to old settings and behave incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Recalibrated values not retained after restart affects configuration integrity; if lost, the process runs on stale parameters, producing off-spec output, though it is detectable at next calibration.
rows.sat_rat: Modern controllers reliably persist parameters to non-volatile memory, so value loss after restart is now fairly uncommon. Persisting recalibrated values is near-instant, so time is not the concern. Ensuring values persist across restart requires knowing to commit/save to non-volatile memory, a routine skilled step. Lost recalibration means re-doing the work, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibrated signals with an incorrect range or scaling configuration
rows.plain: Make sure calibrated readings use the correct limits and measurements. This prevents the equipment from interpreting signals incorrectly.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect range or scaling on calibrated signals is rework affecting measurement fidelity, moderately central to control accuracy; consequences are typically detected and re-worked before major harm, so severity is mid-range.
rows.sat_rat: Loop checks catch many scaling errors, but incorrect ranges still slip through given manual entry across many signals. Verifying range and scaling per signal is a repetitive, time-consuming loop check. Correct scaling configuration needs instrumentation knowledge but is well-understood work. Incorrect scaling causes rework and potential quality impact, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between assessment scenarios and actual recipe variants and process-parameter ranges
rows.plain: Make sure the situations used to judge an approach match the actual product versions and equipment settings that will be used. This makes the assessment useful for real work.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between assessment scenarios and actual ranges is a validation-coverage concern outside live execution (relevance 5) with moderate downstream risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Assessment scenarios are often idealized or vendor-supplied and diverge from real recipe and parameter ranges, undermining conclusions. Aligning test scenarios to true recipe ranges takes iteration that schedules rarely permit. Constructing scenarios that faithfully mirror actual parameter ranges requires strong process characterization skill. Mismatched scenarios yield misleading conclusions that cost money to correct later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish control disruption caused by certificate replacement
rows.plain: Keep the sterile filling process from being interrupted for long when its security certificates are replaced. This helps production continue safely while the certificates are renewed.
rows.imp_rat: Certificate replacement causing sterile fill-finish disruption interrupts the controlled process (relevance moderate) and can ruin a sterile batch (severity moderate-high), but it's a maintenance-driven, infrequent trigger. Capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Hot certificate swap is rarely supported, so replacements routinely require disruptive service cycling. Certificate replacement frequently forces a control restart that interrupts sterile production for a meaningful window. Swapping certificates without breaking trust chains needs security-savvy staff not always present on site. Interrupting sterile fill-finish scraps product, but the measured error is the disruption duration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of communication-loss or invalid-state scenarios without a defined software response
rows.plain: Make sure the software has a planned safe response whenever communication is lost or a signal is not valid. This prevents the equipment from reacting unpredictably.
rows.imp_rat: Communication-loss or invalid-state scenarios without a defined response (relevance) leaves gaps in fault handling, with meaningful severity since undefined behavior during faults can be unsafe, but capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage of communication-loss and invalid-state handling is typically incomplete because scenarios are enumerated manually and gaps persist undetected. Cataloguing every communication-loss and invalid-state scenario and defining responses is a thorough, time-consuming design exercise. Anticipating all invalid-state and comms-loss cases requires experienced failure-mode analysis that is in short supply. Undefined responses to comms loss can cause costly unsafe behavior, dwarfing the design effort saved by skipping them.

rows.source_job: Connect field devices to I/O channels
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of field devices connected to DCS I/O channels other than their designated channels
rows.plain: Make sure each sensor or other connected device is connected to the correct input on the control system. This prevents the system from responding to the wrong information.
rows.imp_rat: Devices on wrong I/O channels corrupt the DCS signal mapping (relevance 6); consequences mirror miswiring but are often caught in loop checks (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Loop-check procedures reliably find mismatches, yet the sheer channel count means such errors are found frequently rather than prevented. Confirming each device sits on its designated I/O channel adds significant loop-check time before startup. Matching devices to channels is routine but error-prone manual cross-referencing that depends on consistent technician attention. Channel-assignment errors drive costly troubleshooting and re-termination during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of electronic batch-record or audit-trail data lost during installation repair
rows.plain: Keep as much as possible of the electronic production records and change history when repairing the software installation. This preserves the information needed to understand what was made and what changes occurred.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of electronic batch-record or audit-trail data during repair is administratively peripheral to real-time control, but in a GMP-regulated environment lost audit trails can invalidate batches and trigger compliance failures, elevating severity.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous historians, redundant storage and Part make electronic-record loss during repair uncommon. Batch-record archiving runs continuously so repairs rarely need extra time to preserve data. Protecting audit-trail data during repair relies on knowing correct backup/export procedures. Redundant historians and archiving add cost but are standard in regulated facilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of injury from stored energy or pressurized liquid released during decommissioning
rows.plain: Prevent injury from trapped pressure or stored force being released while the equipment is taken out of service. This makes dismantling safer.
rows.imp_rat: Stored-energy or pressurized-liquid release during decommissioning is peripheral to the day-to-day batching job but can cause serious operator injury when it occurs, giving low relevance yet high severity.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and stored-energy-release standards make most incidents avoidable, though surprise residual pressure in dead legs still causes uncommon but serious failures. Lockout/tagout and energy-isolation procedures add scheduled time but are a routine part of decommissioning workflows. Executing a stored-energy isolation reliably still depends on trained, certified technicians who understand the specific system's hydraulics and pneumatics. The gauges, blanks, and isolation hardware are inexpensive relative to the injury and downtime an incident would cause.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in recipe endpoint criteria presented to the operator
rows.plain: Make it clear to the worker how to tell when the recipe has reached the required end point. Clear instructions reduce mistakes about when to stop.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous endpoint criteria presented to the operator weakens consistent endpoint enforcement (relevance 6) causing moderate over/under-processing risk (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic recipes present defined criteria, but qualitative endpoints still leave interpretive ambiguity. Clear endpoint display is quick to read where the EBR presents defined criteria. Ambiguous endpoint criteria force operators to apply interpretation and experience. Ambiguity-driven wrong endpoints waste material, only partly mitigated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish control functions omitted from the process-control requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the process rules cover all important functions needed to fill the product safely and keep it free from harmful contamination. Leaving out a key function could affect product quality or safety.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical control functions from requirements is fairly central because missing functions leave the process uncontrolled (relevance), though it is a specification-phase error typically caught before operation (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo user requirement specifications routinely miss subtle sterile control functions because there is no standardized, complete checklist enforced during acquisition, so gaps recur. Assembling a complete list of critical sterile fill-finish control functions early in acquisition is slow because it requires cross-referencing regulatory guidance against the process design. Correctly identifying every critical control function demands scarce combined DCS-and-aseptic-processing expertise that most acquisition teams lack in-house. Omissions surface late and force costly change orders or revalidation, but the requirements-drafting effort itself is not a major spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recalibrated control parameters entered outside approved process ranges
rows.plain: Keep newly adjusted control settings within the approved safe and effective limits. Settings outside those limits could make the process work improperly.
rows.imp_rat: Recalibrated parameters entered outside approved ranges is moderately central to correct configuration; out-of-range values can push equipment toward unsafe or non-compliant operation.
rows.sat_rat: Built-in input range-limit and validation features in control software reliably reject most out-of-range entries, making this among the better-served needs. Range checks are applied at entry, adding negligible time. Range enforcement reduces reliance on the operator's judgement about valid values, lowering skill dependence. Out-of-range entries risk product loss, but validation limits are cheap to configure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required quality indicators calculated from incomplete compounding records
rows.plain: Make sure required measures of quality are based on complete mixing records. This helps ensure quality results are accurate and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Quality indicators computed from incomplete records is a derived-data concern somewhat downstream (relevance 5) but can mislead release decisions (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Completeness checks before calculation are inconsistently enforced, so indicators derived from partial records still occur. Indicators compute automatically but may run before records are fully complete. Knowing whether the underlying record set is complete requires expert review not always automated. Indicators from incomplete data drive wrong decisions and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the control process remains blocked by unresolved interlocks
rows.plain: Shorten the time the control process is held up by safety conditions that have not yet been cleared. This helps the process start sooner without ignoring safety checks.
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged blocking by unresolved interlocks impedes the control job's throughput, but interlocks are protective; the main consequence is downtime rather than catastrophic harm.
rows.sat_rat: Interlock displays reliably show that something is blocked, but locating and clearing the cause is inconsistently supported across systems. Unresolved interlocks stall the process and clearing them often depends on manual diagnosis, so blocked duration is a real and recurring loss. Finding which interlock is holding and why requires experienced troubleshooting of the logic. Extended interlock blocks idle equipment and can waste in-process material, carrying tangible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required configuration objects or historical records omitted during import
rows.plain: Bring over nearly all of the settings and past records that are needed. Missing information could make the new system incomplete or inaccurate.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required configuration objects during import (relevance) undermines correct control setup, and missing critical config or history (severity) can cause misoperation, though this is caught during commissioning validation.
rows.sat_rat: Import tools often lack robust completeness reconciliation, so silently omitted objects and records remain a recurring problem. Verifying that every configuration object and record imported completely is a laborious reconciliation step. Confirming import completeness across object types requires familiarity with both source and target schemas, a nontrivial skill. Re-importing or manually filling omitted objects consumes labor, and omissions found late are costly to fix.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of associating endpoint evidence with the wrong batch or recipe step
rows.plain: Make sure the test results showing that a process step is complete are linked to the correct product group and step. This prevents decisions from being based on the wrong evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Associating endpoint evidence with the wrong batch/step is a recording-traceability defect (relevance 6) that can misrepresent conformance (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: EBR automatically binds evidence to the active batch and step, reliably preventing misassociation. Automatic batch/step context tagging adds no operator time. System-managed batch context removes reliance on operator record-keeping. Misassociated evidence causes investigations and loss, well contained by MES context.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of critical parameter oscillation near or across specified limits
rows.plain: Keep important process conditions from repeatedly swinging near or beyond their allowed limits. This makes the process steadier and reduces the risk of going out of range.
rows.imp_rat: Parameter oscillation near limits is a control-quality concern (relevance 6) causing intermittent minor excursions of moderate impact (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Tuned control loops usually suppress oscillation, though poorly tuned or manual loops still oscillate near limits. Oscillation is visible on trends immediately but damping takes time to tune. Diagnosing and reducing oscillation near limits requires control-tuning skill. Oscillation stresses product quality, a cost partly contained by control tuning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of trapped pressure or hazardous liquid being released unexpectedly during draining
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure or dangerous liquid from escaping suddenly while the system is being emptied. This protects people and nearby equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected release of trapped pressure or hazardous liquid during draining is a maintenance-safety issue peripheral to batch production, yet serious operator injury makes severity high.
rows.sat_rat: Even careful draining leaves pockets of trapped pressure or liquid in complex batching manifolds, so unexpected release remains a recurring hazard. Controlled draining with venting takes time but is a standard sequenced operation. Recognizing where trapped pressure can hide in low points and dead legs requires system-specific expertise that is not universally present. Draining hardware and temporary containment are low-cost items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of control-loop instability following tuning-parameter changes
rows.plain: Keep the process steady after changing its control settings. This prevents the system from swinging back and forth or reacting unpredictably.
rows.imp_rat: Loop instability after tuning changes affects regulation quality (relevance 6); oscillations degrade product and stress equipment but are typically detectable and correctable (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Without disciplined test procedures, parameter changes still trigger oscillation or hunting more often than operators would like. Tuning changes take effect immediately, but validating stability afterward can require extended observation. Loop stability after retuning depends heavily on the engineer's control expertise, and skilled tuners are scarce so errors are common. Tuning tools exist but auto-tuners are underused; instability can waste product and energy during recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of imported tags, parameters, or records mapped to incorrect destinations
rows.plain: Make sure imported labels, settings, and records are placed in the correct locations. This helps prevent errors in the existing setup.
rows.imp_rat: Tags mapped to incorrect destinations (relevance) can drive wrong control actions, with severity moderately high because misdirected commands affect real devices, but such mapping errors are typically detectable in verification.
rows.sat_rat: Mapping errors during import are common because automated validation of destination correctness is weak in typical tools. Checking that imported items landed at correct destinations demands slow spot-checking or full mapping review. Correct destination mapping relies on human interpretation of naming and structure, which is error-prone without deep familiarity. Mis-mapped data forces rework and can corrupt downstream configuration, raising remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of identifying the wrong cause of a device fault surfaced during sterile fill-finish operation
rows.plain: Correctly identify what caused a device problem during the sterile filling operation. Finding the real cause helps workers fix the problem without creating new ones.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying a fault cause in sterile fill-finish can trigger costly wrong interventions (severity elevated), but this diagnostic error is secondary to core control and stays capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic tools and alarm history give clues but rarely pinpoint root cause, leaving frequent misidentification in complex faults. Misdiagnosing a fault sends the technician down a wrong path, greatly lengthening resolution time. Correctly identifying a device fault's root cause in a sterile fill-finish context depends heavily on scarce expert diagnostic skill. A wrong cause means wrong repairs, wasted parts, prolonged downtime and possible batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recipe and parameter changes with incomplete audit-trail entries
rows.plain: Record complete and accurate details whenever a recipe or setting is changed, including what changed and who made the change. This provides a reliable history of the process.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete audit-trail entries for changes is a data-integrity/change-control defect (relevance 6) with significant regulatory exposure (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Regulated electronic systems maintain robust automatic audit trails, making this a mature, well-served need with only edge-case gaps at system boundaries. Audit-trail entries are written automatically at the moment of change with no added effort. System-generated trails remove reliance on staff to manually log changes correctly. Complete audit trails reduce audit findings and investigation labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of manual commands sent to the wrong valve, motor, or actuator
rows.plain: Reduce the number of manual instructions sent to the wrong valve, motor, or moving part. This matters because the wrong equipment could move or change state unexpectedly.
rows.imp_rat: Manual commands to the wrong actuator concern operator interaction with the DCS (relevance 6); misdirected commands can cause dangerous equipment movement (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Faceplate confirmations and clear graphics reduce misdirected commands substantially, though wrong-device actions still occur under stress or cluttered displays. Command confirmation dialogs add a small delay but do not meaningfully slow operation. Correct target selection still relies on operator attention to faceplates and tag names, a vigilance-dependent task. Confirmation and graphics features are built into the DCS HMI at no extra cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous electrical or process energy during field-device disconnection
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous electricity or process energy while disconnecting devices in the machine. This reduces the chance of shock, burns, or other injury.
rows.imp_rat: Technician energy exposure during disconnection is peripheral to the DCS's control job (relevance 4); however the consequence is severe personal injury or death (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Well-established LOTO and energy-verification protocols make exposure incidents rare, though human error in complex multi-source DCS field wiring still occurs. Lockout-tagout and de-energization verification add methodical steps that slow field-device disconnection but are routinely scheduled. Safe disconnection depends heavily on trained, qualified technicians correctly identifying and isolating multiple energy sources, so it remains skill-and-vigilance intensive. Standard PPE, lockout hardware, and testing instruments are inexpensive relative to the overall disposal effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operator authorization blocks access to a hierarchy level needed during operation
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a worker’s permissions prevent access to a level of the system needed to operate it. This matters because the worker may be unable to complete the task.
rows.imp_rat: Authorization wrongly blocking access to a needed hierarchy level impedes the software's operational-control job; during a critical operation this can prevent timely intervention, though workarounds sometimes exist, keeping severity from maxing out.
rows.sat_rat: Established role/privilege models handle the common cases well, yet edge-case hierarchy misconfigurations that block a needed level remain a recurring residual failure. Role-based access hierarchies in modern SCADA/DCS resolve authorization at login speed so genuine operators rarely wait, but misconfigured levels still cause occasional operational stalls. Correctly configuring the access hierarchy demands scarce administrator expertise, and gaps show up only when an operator hits a wall mid-operation. Access-control frameworks are bundled into control platforms so there is little marginal cost, though a lockout during production can waste batch time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch results are evaluated against an incorrect or obsolete specification
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that a batch is judged using quality limits that are wrong or no longer current. This matters because the decision about the batch could be incorrect.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating batch results against an obsolete specification is an administrative/QA error, moderately central to confirming the job (relevance), and severe because it can pass out-of-spec product or reject good product (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Validated electronic batch records with enforced revision control make evaluation against an obsolete spec uncommon at maturely automated sites, yet residual failures persist wherever change control is manual. Recipe-management and MES systems retrieve the effective specification version quickly at batch start, though manual paper-based operations still add lookup delay. Modern recipe control removes most operator judgement about which spec applies, but paper or spreadsheet-driven sites still rely on QA vigilance to catch obsolete revisions. Electronic version control is inexpensive to maintain once deployed, though the underlying MES/document-control system carries a real capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reported quality indicators lacking traceability to source compounding records and applicable requirements
rows.plain: Make sure every reported measure of product quality can be traced back to the original mixing records and the rules it must meet. This helps people check where the result came from and whether it is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Quality indicators lacking traceability to source records is a data-integrity/audit concern somewhat downstream (relevance 5) with meaningful compliance risk (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Integrated MES provides good record-level traceability, but linkage to applicable requirements is often manual and incomplete. Drill-down from indicator to source record is quick in integrated systems. Establishing traceability to applicable requirements still needs quality knowledge to map correctly. Poor traceability lengthens audits and inflates compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required configuration, project, and runtime data omitted from the stored backup
rows.plain: Make sure the saved backup contains nearly all the setup, project, and current-use information needed to restore the software. This matters because missing information could make recovery incomplete or difficult.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting configuration/project/runtime data from backups undermines the ability to restore control (moderate relevance), and an incomplete backup discovered during a real recovery can extend outages significantly (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Backup scopes are often manually defined and drift over time, so required data is frequently omitted from stored backups. Omitted data forces re-collection later, extending recovery scope though time is not the core. Defining a complete backup scope of configuration, project and runtime data requires expert knowledge of the system. Missing data causes costly reconstruction rather than direct spend during backup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to identify the cause of a software-surfaced device fault while sterile fill-finish production is interrupted
rows.plain: Shorten the time needed to find why a device has failed while germ-free medicine production is stopped. This matters because finding the cause sooner can help restart production sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Slow cause identification during an interrupted sterile production run is relevant to keeping operations running (moderate relevance), and extended interruption of aseptic production carries batch-loss consequences (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostic tooling is fragmented and cause identification is inconsistent, so this need remains poorly served under time pressure. Root-cause identification during an interrupted sterile run is slow, driven by manual log correlation and cross-referencing. Finding the cause depends heavily on scarce expert technicians who know the equipment and sequence intimately. Every minute of interrupted sterile fill-finish is extremely costly, magnifying the impact of slow diagnosis.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that accessing product-contact areas during inspection introduces contamination
rows.plain: Avoid bringing dirt or germs onto surfaces that touch the product while checking for wear and leaks. This helps keep the product safe.
rows.imp_rat: Accessing product-contact areas during inspection introducing contamination moderately affects batch cleanliness (relevance) and is severe because inspection is routine and could repeatedly seed contamination (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Because inspection is minimally invasive, contamination introduction is less likely and better controlled by routine practice than during repair. Inspection access is relatively quick since it is less invasive than repair, adding limited time. Inspecting product-contact areas without contaminating them needs care but less finesse than active repair, so skill demand is moderate. Inspection consumes fewer consumables than repair, keeping cost impact modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing pressurized or hazardous residual material during disconnection
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that trapped pressure or dangerous leftover material will escape when equipment connections are opened. This matters because the release could injure people or contaminate the area.
rows.imp_rat: Releasing pressurized/hazardous residual material during disconnection is peripheral to producing the batch but poses serious injury and chemical-exposure risk when it happens.
rows.sat_rat: Standard bleed-and-verify practice catches most cases, but residual material trapped behind valves or in flexible hoses still releases unexpectedly with real frequency. Depressurizing and verifying lines before breaking connections is a known step that adds predictable time to disconnection. Correctly identifying which connections still hold pressure or hazardous material demands experienced judgement that is often in short supply on demolition crews. Containment mats, spill kits, and capture equipment are modest costs against the liability of an uncontrolled release.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an undiscovered process or utility connection remains live during disconnection
rows.plain: Avoid leaving an unnoticed pipe, cable, or other connection carrying pressure, electricity, or material when it is disconnected. This helps prevent unexpected releases or injuries.
rows.imp_rat: A live undiscovered utility connection during disconnection is peripheral to making a batch but can cause severe injury or uncontrolled release when energy is unexpectedly present.
rows.sat_rat: Outdated or missing P&IDs mean undiscovered live connections are a chronic and dangerous surprise despite isolation checklists. Tracing and verifying every utility connection is slow and often compressed under demolition schedule pressure. Finding undocumented or hidden live connections depends heavily on scarce institutional knowledge and skilled tracing, especially on old systems. Line-tracing tools and verification labor cost little compared to the consequences of a missed live connection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transient or faulty sensor readings are treated as actual process changes
rows.plain: Avoid treating a brief or incorrect reading from a sensor as a real change in the process. This helps prevent unnecessary or harmful responses.
rows.imp_rat: Treating transient sensor readings as real process changes can trigger wrong control actions (relevance moderate-high to the monitoring job) and cause spurious trips or product deviation (severity moderate-high). Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Basic rate-of-change and range checks exist but transient or faulty sensor spikes are still frequently treated as real, so this is only partially served. Signal validation adds negligible processing delay when configured, but tuning filters consumes engineering time. Distinguishing transient noise from real process change often falls to skilled engineers who configure filtering and to operators interpreting readings. Validation and filtering logic requires engineering configuration effort, adding project cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a hazardous energy release or chemical exposure during system inspection
rows.plain: Avoid releasing stored energy or exposing people to chemicals while inspecting the system. This helps protect workers during the final safety review.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous energy release or chemical exposure during inspection is a safety event tangential to producing batches (lower relevance) but severe, with potential for serious injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: When isolation is confirmed first, inspection incidents are rare, but overlooked stored energy occasionally catches inspectors. Verifying de-energization and drain-down before inspection adds a short, standard hold. Safe inspection of retired systems draws on the same LOTO and hazard-recognition skills routinely trained for. Inspection is low-material-cost and mostly labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required control functions unavailable after DCS failover
rows.plain: Reduce the number of control functions that stop working after the system switches to its backup equipment. This helps keep the process running when the main equipment fails.
rows.imp_rat: Required control functions unavailable after failover defeats the DCS's redundancy purpose (relevance) and can leave the process uncontrolled during a fault (severity), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor redundancy architectures are mature and failover testing is standard, though not every control function is exercised so gaps remain. Testing control availability across failover scenarios adds dedicated test time to the preparation stage. Designing meaningful failover tests requires experienced redundancy engineers who understand the platform's behavior. Redundancy testing uses standard platform features, keeping cost contained relative to its value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intended control devices linked to the wrong physical device identity
rows.plain: Reduce the number of control devices connected to the wrong physical piece of equipment. This helps ensure that commands affect the intended equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Linking a control device to the wrong physical identity (relevance) risks commanding the wrong equipment, with meaningful severity, yet this is a configuration-time ambiguity bounded by identity verification steps.
rows.sat_rat: Address/identity verification practices exist but remain partly manual, so wrong-device linkages still occur with moderate frequency. Verifying each logical device against its physical identity is a manual walk-down that adds preparation time. Matching control device links to real hardware identities requires field knowledge that ties software to physical assets. Wrong device linkage can trigger costly misoperation if undetected, but correction during preparation is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of configured control devices reachable from unauthorized network segments
rows.plain: Keep fewer control devices connected to network areas that unauthorized people or systems can reach. This helps prevent unwanted access or changes.
rows.imp_rat: Control devices reachable from unauthorized network segments is a security-exposure defect; only moderately central to the control/monitor job (relevance moderate) but the consequences of unauthorized access can be severe (severity moderate-high), still averaged under 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Reachability from unauthorized segments remains a persistent weakness because flat legacy OT networks and misconfigured rules routinely leave devices exposed. Segmenting networks and setting access rules during preparation adds configuration time that is often compressed under schedule pressure. Proper segmentation, VLANs and firewall rules for OT require specialized OT-security skills that are chronically scarce. Firewalls and managed switches cost money but the larger issue is design effort, not hardware spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of device faults surfaced during sterile fill-finish operation that lack the device state and sequence-stage context needed for diagnosis
rows.plain: Make sure more equipment problems come with enough information about the equipment's condition and what step it was performing to find the cause. This helps workers diagnose and fix problems faster.
rows.imp_rat: Faults lacking device-state and sequence context during sterile fill-finish hamper diagnosis (moderate relevance), and in a sterile operation poor context can prolong contamination-risk downtime (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Many fault messages surface without the accompanying device state or sequence-stage context, forcing diagnosis from incomplete information a recurring gap. When context is missing, technicians lose time gathering device state and sequence stage from separate sources. Reconstructing device state and sequence-stage context from raw faults requires skilled technicians familiar with the batch sequence. Enriching faults with contextual data requires engineering configuration effort, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned interruption to live sterile fill-finish production during disaster recovery testing
rows.plain: Shorten unexpected interruptions to live production of germ-free products while testing how the operation would recover from a disaster. This helps limit lost production and disruption.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interruption to live sterile fill-finish during DR testing directly stops production (relevance moderate-high), and interrupting a sterile run carries real batch-loss consequences (severity moderate), pinned at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard DR testing rarely fully isolates from live production, so unplanned interruptions during exercises remain a poorly mitigated, high-consequence risk. DR tests on live sterile fill-finish lines are slow to stage because production cannot simply be halted for exercise windows. Executing DR testing without disturbing GMP production demands scarce specialists spanning validation, automation and sterile process, which are hard to assemble. Any unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish scraps costly batches and consumes revalidation effort, making the stakes very high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between executing a recipe step and capturing its corresponding record
rows.plain: Record each recipe step as soon as it is completed. This creates a more accurate record of what was done and when.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between executing a step and capturing its record is a recording-timeliness issue (relevance 6) with moderate integrity impact (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Step-linked prompting keeps records timely, but busy operators still batch confirmations after the fact. Electronic batch records prompt confirmation as steps execute, shortening the capture gap. Guided workflows reduce the operator effort needed to remember when to record. EBR licensing is a notable but already-committed cost in most plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of inter-step holding beyond the approved limit
rows.plain: Keep the waiting time between work steps within the approved limit. Long waits can affect the process or the material being made.
rows.imp_rat: Inter-step holding beyond approved limit is a timing constraint partly tied to quality (relevance 5); overholding can degrade material moderately (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Without automated hold gating, inter-step holds routinely drift beyond approved limits. Inter-step hold duration is often managed manually, so overruns persist. Keeping holds within limits relies on operator scheduling attention. Overlong holds risk product loss, poorly contained today.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unplanned fill-finish control disruption caused by communication verification
rows.plain: Keep unexpected interruptions to sterile product production as short as possible when checking that equipment is communicating correctly. This limits lost production time.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned fill-finish control disruption from communication verification halts a sterile process (relevance moderate), and stopping fill-finish can scrap costly sterile product (severity moderate-high). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Verification steps are fairly standardized, so unplanned disruptions are moderate but not eliminated. Communication verification often requires halting fill-finish control briefly, and this interruption is the central concern. Interpreting verification results relies on technicians who understand the communication topology. Disrupting a sterile fill-finish run wastes product but the need targets the disruption duration itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between observing a process parameter and recording it
rows.plain: Record a process reading as soon as possible after observing it. A short delay makes the record more likely to reflect what was actually happening.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between observing and recording a parameter is a recording-timeliness concern (relevance 6) with moderate data-integrity impact (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Automated capture makes the observe-to-record delay consistently near-zero, though manual points still lag. Continuous electronic acquisition records parameters essentially at the moment of observation for instrumented tags. Little operator skill is needed when the system timestamps automatically. Automated timestamping runs on existing hardware with negligible added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether a captured parameter matches the parameter actually used during compounding
rows.plain: Minimize the time spent unsure whether a saved reading is the same reading that was actually used while making the product. Quick confirmation makes the records more trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether captured value matches actual is a verification-timeliness concern (relevance 6) with moderate integrity impact (severity 6).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether a recorded parameter matches the completed compounding action before step confirmation
rows.plain: Minimize the time spent unsure whether a recorded reading matches the work that was actually completed before confirming the step. This helps prevent a step from being approved based on an incorrect record.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about record-action match before confirmation is a verification-timeliness issue (relevance 6) with moderate consequences (severity 6).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting control actions initiated for the same process equipment from different operator stations
rows.plain: Prevent different workers at different control stations from sending conflicting instructions to the same machine or equipment. This helps the process run safely and as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting commands from different stations concern multi-operator coordination in the distributed DCS (relevance 6); contention can produce erratic equipment behavior, usually detectable (severity 6).
rows.sat_rat: Equipment-module ownership and command locking prevent most conflicting actions, but multi-console setups still occasionally allow contention where locks are not configured. Ownership arbitration resolves instantly when contention is detected. Operators must coordinate verbally when authority is ambiguous, relying on procedural discipline rather than the system. Equipment-ownership and lock features are standard DCS functionality with no added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a displayed process-variable reading differs from the actual plant condition
rows.plain: Make sure the number shown on the screen matches what is actually happening in the facility. This helps people make safe and correct decisions.
rows.imp_rat: A displayed process variable differing from actual plant condition strikes the DCS's core sensing-and-display job (relevance) and can mislead operators into harmful decisions (severity), pinned at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Signal-quality flags and range checks catch many bad readings, but silent sensor drift or freeze can still make the display diverge from reality. Values refresh at fast scan rates so displays track the plant with minimal lag. Detecting a stuck or drifted reading still relies on operator cross-checking and instrument knowledge. Signal validation and redundant instruments add cost but are common on critical measurements.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a user can adjust or override sterile fill-finish controls without authorization
rows.plain: Make sure only approved users can change or bypass the controls used to make products without contamination. This prevents unauthorized changes that could affect product safety.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized adjustment or override of sterile fill-finish controls is central to the DCS's access-control role (relevance) and can cause contamination or non-compliant product (severity), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Part models are mature and widely deployed, so unauthorized override of sterile controls is well constrained though misconfiguration can still occur. Configuring role-based access and electronic-signature controls is a well-defined setup task that fits normal preparation timelines. Modern DCS platforms provide standard security frameworks, reducing reliance on specialist expertise to enforce authorization. Access-control configuration uses built-in platform features at low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that redundant controllers, networks, or power paths become unavailable from the same component loss
rows.plain: Make sure a single failed part cannot take down backup controls, communication connections, and power at the same time. This helps the system keep working if one part fails.
rows.imp_rat: Common-cause loss of redundant controllers, networks, or power paths negates the DCS's fault-tolerance architecture (relevance) and can cause total control loss (severity), capped at 6.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established redundancy design practice addresses common-cause well, but subtle shared dependencies still occasionally survive review. Verifying independence of redundant paths is a focused design-review task that fits normal schedules. Identifying hidden common-cause dependencies requires seasoned architects, and this analysis is only as good as their experience. Genuine physical separation of power and network paths raises hardware cost, which is usually budgeted for critical systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from operator initiation of a manual override until the override takes effect
rows.plain: Make the manual override take effect as soon as the operator starts it, so they can quickly take control when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between operator initiation and a manual override taking effect matters for responsive control, but modern systems act quickly; a short lag is usually tolerable except in fast-moving upsets.
rows.sat_rat: Override latency is generally consistent, with only occasional lag from network congestion or slow field devices. Modern control networks execute manual override commands within scan-cycle latency, so the initiation-to-effect delay is typically very short. Issuing an override is a direct HMI action requiring little specialized skill. Fast override execution is inherent to the platform and adds no cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by software removal
rows.plain: Keep unexpected interruptions to sterile product production as short as possible when removing control software. This helps the line return to work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Software removal is a peripheral lifecycle activity relative to the core control job, but disruption to active sterile fill-finish during removal still causes significant batch loss, giving elevated severity.
