rows.sat_rat: Rehearsed cutover plans make short, bounded disruption the norm, though unexpected rollback needs can extend it. Software removal is normally executed during scheduled production stoppages, so disruption duration is deliberately minimized and predictable. Sequencing removal to shorten downtime relies on experienced planners who understand the production calendar and system dependencies. Downtime carries high production cost, which motivates tight scheduling and rehearsed cutover procedures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual pressure or hazardous fluid while opening the filter housing
rows.plain: Prevent a worker from being exposed to trapped pressure or harmful liquid when opening the filter container. This helps avoid injury or contact with the liquid.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to pressure or hazardous fluid when opening a filter housing is a maintenance-safety error tangential to the batching outcome, but the human-harm consequence is severe when it occurs.
rows.sat_rat: Spray-out of residual pressure or fluid on housing opening remains a recurring nuisance where housings lack integral vents, so protection is inconsistent. Filter-housing venting and draining is quick when a bleed valve is present, but slow and messy when it isn't. Correctly relieving a housing before cracking the lid requires modest technique that most technicians learn but occasionally misapply under time pressure. Vented housing designs cost little more, yet many installed filter housings still lack proper pressure-relief provisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unexpected energization of electrical circuits while the technician accesses batching-system connections
rows.plain: Prevent electricity from unexpectedly turning on while a technician reaches the machine's mixing-system connections. This helps protect the technician from electric shock or injury.
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected energization of electrical circuits during access is largely a lockout/tagout safety concern outside the batching function, but electrocution risk makes the consequence potentially fatal.
rows.sat_rat: Electrical LOTO is among the most mature and enforced safety practices, so unexpected energization is rare where the procedure is followed. Electrical LOTO is a standard, time-boxed step that rarely causes meaningful schedule disruption. Verifying absence of voltage and applying electrical isolation is codified work that trained/qualified electricians perform routinely. Lockout devices and voltage testers are cheap and universally available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of releasing residual pressure or process liquid when opening a pump, valve, or connection
rows.plain: Prevent trapped pressure or process liquid from suddenly escaping when opening a pump, valve, or connection. This helps protect the worker and prevent spills.
rows.imp_rat: Residual pressure/liquid release when opening a pump or valve is a maintenance-safety concern only loosely tied to producing a batch, but exposure to hazardous fluid or pressure can seriously injure a technician.
rows.sat_rat: Depressurization practice is common but trapped residual pressure and liquid between isolation points still surprises technicians with real regularity, keeping this only acceptably served. Draining, venting, and depressurizing before breaking a connection is a familiar step that adds manageable time. Judging whether a line is truly relieved before loosening a fitting depends on technician attentiveness and knowledge of the system's dead legs and trapped volumes. Vent valves and drain points are low-cost, but retrofitted systems often lack them, forcing improvised bleeding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production interruption caused by the interface update
rows.plain: Keep the sterile production process interruption as short as possible while the device connections are being updated.
rows.imp_rat: Interface-update-caused production interruption in sterile fill-finish stops the job (relevance moderate) and can spoil sterile product (severity moderate-high), but updates are planned/rare events. Capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Interface updates lack online-update support in most deployments, so disruption is routine. Interface updates typically require stopping sterile production, and the interruption length is the core issue. Applying interface updates safely needs engineers who understand version compatibility. Halting sterile fill-finish wastes batches, yet the need is framed around interruption duration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sterile fill-finish disruption caused by linked services ending before software retirement activities are complete
rows.plain: Keep the sterile production interruption short when connected services are stopped before the software has been fully retired.
rows.imp_rat: Linked services ending prematurely during software retirement is a narrow lifecycle-sequencing error peripheral to routine control, yet disruption to sterile fill-finish still carries costly batch consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Service-end dates and retirement milestones are commonly tracked in disconnected systems, so mistimed cutoffs remain a recurring cause of disruption. Linked services (licenses, cloud, vendor support) frequently end on contractual dates that are poorly synchronized with retirement work, causing avoidable delays. Coordinating dependent third-party service timelines requires procurement and vendor-management attention that is often fragmented across teams. Extending or bridging service contracts to align timing adds cost that is not always anticipated.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that interrupted deployment leaves the designated host in a partially installed state
rows.plain: Prevent an interrupted software installation from leaving the target computer or machine only partly updated.
rows.imp_rat: A partially installed host from interrupted deployment (relevance) can leave the control software in a broken state, but installation is a one-time setup event peripheral to ongoing control (severity moderate since it is usually recoverable via reinstall).
rows.sat_rat: Some deployment tools support rollback and idempotent installs, but many industrial packages still leave hosts in inconsistent states after interruption. Recovering from a partial install costs time, but transactional or rollback-capable installers shorten remediation. Cleaning up a half-installed host can require expert intervention when the installer lacks automatic rollback. Partial installs mainly waste labor and occasionally require host reimaging, a limited cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to residual process material while opening or draining tanks, lines, and mixing components
rows.plain: Protect technicians from contact with leftover process material when they open or drain tanks, pipes, and mixing equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to residual material while opening tanks and lines is a maintenance-side concern, peripheral to producing the batch (lower relevance), yet consequential for worker safety when it happens (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: PPE and pre-cleaning reduce exposure substantially, yet residual material in mixing components still surprises technicians when opened. Purging and flushing before opening tanks and lines adds time to the sequence but is expected. Judging when a component is safe to open despite residual film requires experienced technicians who know the process chemistry. PPE and flushing media are ongoing but modest costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of anomaly-critical live signals unavailable during urgent diagnosis
rows.plain: Keep the important live information needed to quickly investigate a problem available during an urgent diagnosis.
rows.imp_rat: Anomaly-critical live signals unavailable during urgent diagnosis hampers the monitoring job when it matters most (relevance moderate-high) and can prolong a hazardous condition (severity moderate-high). Held to the 5.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Critical signals do become unavailable exactly when a device faults, and standard practice only partially guarantees their availability during diagnosis. Live signal retrieval is fast when the path is healthy, but urgent diagnosis stalls when signals drop. Knowing which signals are anomaly-critical and reacquiring them depends on skilled diagnosticians. Ensuring critical signal availability requires redundant instrumentation and communications, raising cost.

rows.stk: Process operators, control engineers, process engineers
rows.source_job: Diagnose control deviations and process upsets
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the initiating condition is obscured by consequential and nuisance alarms during a sterile fill-finish upset
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the original problem is hidden by a flood of follow-on and unhelpful warnings. This helps workers find and fix the first problem faster.
rows.imp_rat: When nuisance and consequential alarms obscure the initiating condition, operators diagnose slower, moderately impeding the control job (relevance); consequences are delayed response rather than immediate catastrophe (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Alarm floods routinely obscure the initiating event, and few sites have mature dynamic suppression, so this is among the worst-served needs. The root-cause alarm arrives on time, but sifting it from the flood costs operators critical minutes. Identifying the initiating condition amid nuisance and consequential alarms requires expert diagnostic judgement under pressure. Alarm rationalization and suppression engineering per ISA- expensive and frequently incomplete.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a field device becoming unresponsive to the DCS after its firmware update
rows.plain: Lower the chance that a piece of equipment stops responding to the central control system after its software is updated. This helps keep the equipment under control and available for use.
rows.imp_rat: A field device unresponsive after firmware update loses a control point, relevant to the interconnection job (relevance); impact is usually localized to affected devices (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Firmware updates on heterogeneous field devices carry a real, recurring risk of leaving a device unresponsive with limited rollback assurance. Firmware updates plus post-update verification consume noticeable time per device across many field instruments. Recovering a bricked or unresponsive field device after a firmware update often requires vendor-specific expertise. Failed updates can strand devices requiring replacement, an unbudgeted material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of disruption to active batch control or monitoring during export
rows.plain: Keep the interruption to active batch control or watching as short as possible while data is copied out. This helps the batch continue with minimal disturbance.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to active batch control during export directly interrupts the CFJ of continuous monitoring/control (relevance moderate-high), and disrupting an active batch can spoil product or halt production (severity moderate-high), but export is an occasional operation. Capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Export processes are reasonably mature but can still contend for resources with live batch control, causing intermittent monitoring interruptions. Data export can be timed for low-activity periods, but large batch exports can still lengthen or overlap active control windows. Exporting from legacy proprietary formats without disturbing live batch control needs specialist tooling knowledge. Export tooling and staging storage add modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sequence stalls caused by devices not reaching required states
rows.plain: Shorten pauses in an automatic sequence when equipment does not reach the required condition. This helps the process keep moving and makes delays easier to resolve.
rows.imp_rat: Sequence stalls from devices not reaching required states delay the controlled process; relevant to keeping sequences moving but consequences are mostly lost time rather than damage.
rows.sat_rat: Sequencers report step timeouts fairly reliably, but the underlying device non-response still causes recurring stalls that automation alone doesn't prevent. Sequence stalls waiting on devices to reach states directly extend cycle time until someone intervenes. Diagnosing why a device isn't confirming its state requires skilled interpretation of device and sequence logic. Stalled sequences leave lines idle and can jeopardize time-sensitive product, adding real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of compounding interruptions caused by parameter excursions
rows.plain: Reduce repeated interruptions caused when operating settings move outside their allowed ranges. This helps keep work on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Interruptions from parameter excursions are an availability/efficiency concern (relevance 5) with moderate batch-continuity impact (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Control is largely reactive, so parameter excursions still interrupt compounding at a frustrating frequency. Excursion-driven interruptions cause real downtime and the status quo does little to preempt them. Preventing excursions before they interrupt requires proactive process control skill often lacking on shift. Each interruption wastes throughput and sometimes material, a recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false alarms that obscure the actual health of the controlled process
rows.plain: Reduce false warnings that make it harder to tell whether the software is working properly. This helps workers notice real problems more quickly.
rows.imp_rat: False alarms obscuring true process health undermine effective monitoring (relevance moderate-high) and can cause operators to miss or ignore real faults (severity moderate), but alarm noise is a degradation rather than outright control failure. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Despite ISA- , chronic nuisance and false alarms remain a widespread unresolved problem that obscures true process health. False alarms waste operator time chasing non-events during operation. Reducing false alarms depends on skilled alarm rationalization engineers who set proper deadbands and setpoints. Alarm rationalization projects are labor-intensive and costly, and many sites underinvest in them.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of communication loss with a controlled device during coordinated execution
rows.plain: Keep communication with each controlled device from being lost for long during coordinated work. This helps the devices carry out their actions together as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Communication loss with a device during coordinated execution directly breaks control (relevance moderate-high) and can leave sequenced operations mid-state (severity moderate-high), but constrained here to occasional coordinated runs. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Redundancy and watchdogs reduce comms-loss duration, yet single-path devices and network faults still cause disruptive dropouts during coordinated sequences. Communication timeouts are detected within configured intervals but reconnection and resynchronization add delay. Handling comms loss gracefully during coordinated execution depends on engineers who design fallback logic. Redundant network paths that shorten comms loss are standard but add infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unintended repeated device actions resulting from duplicate command issuance
rows.plain: Prevent a device from performing an action more than once because the same instruction was sent twice. This helps avoid unintended movements or changes.
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate command issuance causing unintended repeated device actions directly harms the control job (severity can be significant), but as a comparatively contained fault it is capped at 5.5 mean.
rows.sat_rat: Command debouncing and edge-triggered logic are mature and reliably suppress duplicate issuance in nearly all cases. Command debouncing acts within the control cycle, so it introduces no perceptible delay. Preventing duplicate commands is handled by the system, not by operator care. Duplicate device actions can waste material or cause wear, but debounce logic averts most at no added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exposing certificate private keys during renewal
rows.plain: Prevent the secret information used to prove a system’s identity from being exposed while its security certificate is replaced. Keeping it secret helps prevent unauthorized access.
rows.imp_rat: Exposing certificate private keys during renewal is a niche security operation removed from core control functions (low relevance), but key exposure can enable serious unauthorized access and is a severe security breach (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: HSMs and managed key stores where deployed keep exposure rare, though ad-hoc manual key handling still risks leaks. Secure key handling adds minor steps but is not a time concern. Avoiding private-key exposure demands security expertise that many maintenance teams lack. A key compromise is very costly, but the error itself is the security exposure event.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of control or monitoring access being interrupted during the software ownership transition
rows.plain: Prevent people or systems from losing the ability to control or watch the plant while responsibility for the software changes hands. Continuous access helps keep operations safe and stable.
rows.imp_rat: Interrupted control/monitoring access during ownership transition breaks the CFJ temporarily (relevance moderate) and could leave a live process unsupervised (severity moderate-high), but transitions are rare, one-time events. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Handover checklists reduce gaps, yet dropped access during ownership change remains a recurring failure when responsibilities are unclear. Ownership transitions are planned events, so continuity of access can usually be scheduled without time pressure. Managing handover of accounts, licenses and responsibilities across owners requires coordinated administrative and technical skill that is often ad hoc. Transition overlap periods add cost but are usually accepted to protect continuity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that records subject to regulatory retention requirements are purged during decommissioning
rows.plain: Make sure records that must be kept by law are not deleted when the system is taken out of service. This preserves information that may still be required later.
rows.imp_rat: Purging regulatory-retention records during decommissioning is administrative and tangential to real-time control (low relevance), yet the consequence — regulatory non-compliance and irreversible data loss — is severe.
rows.sat_rat: Strong GMP records-retention procedures and legal holds make accidental purging of regulated records uncommon where controls are enforced. Retention holds are applied before purge on a planned basis, so timing rarely forces mistakes. Interpreting which records fall under regulatory retention requires records-management and compliance expertise that pharma organizations generally maintain. Long-term retention storage and legal review add ongoing cost that is a routine compliance expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the control-logic load being rejected because required target configuration is missing or incompatible
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the machine’s control instructions will be rejected because the required settings are missing or do not work together. Complete, compatible settings help the controls load and operate correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Rejection of a control-logic load due to missing/incompatible target config directly blocks configuring the device (moderate-high relevance), and a failed load during commissioning can halt work with moderate-high consequences (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern engineering tools flag many missing or incompatible configurations before download, so rejections are common enough to notice but often preventable. Pre-load validation of target configuration adds a checking step but is faster than diagnosing a rejected load later. Interpreting compatibility and configuration prerequisites needs platform-specific knowledge that experienced engineers usually hold. Failed loads waste engineering time but rarely material, keeping cost impact moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that control authority is transferred to an unauthorized operator or workstation
rows.plain: Make sure only an approved person or computer can take control of the equipment. This prevents unauthorized changes or actions.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring control authority to an unauthorized operator or workstation is relevant to safe operator-terminal handling (relevance) and can cause unsafe commands (severity), but it is an edge-case access event, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access control and station-scoped authority reliably block unauthorized transfer where security is properly configured, though shared logins still create gaps. Login and authority transfer complete quickly with no operational lag. Role-based access shifts control from operator vigilance to configured policy, lowering the skill burden. Access-control and role management are standard DCS security features at no incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required circuit volume occupied by trapped air after priming
rows.plain: Make sure there is as little trapped air as possible in the fluid lines after they are filled. This helps the lines work as intended and prevents air from disrupting the process.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped air as a percentage of circuit volume after priming is a measurable defect affecting fill accuracy, moderately relevant, but typically corrected during setup so consequences are limited.
rows.sat_rat: Full-fill is generally achieved but hidden air pockets in valves and dead-legs remain a persistent low-level reliability concern. Achieving full liquid-fill circuit volume requires deliberate priming and purge time. Recognizing and clearing residual air volume relies on operator experience with the specific circuit. Air-elimination hardware adds minor cost relative to batch value at risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between detection of a communication fault or invalid device state and execution of the defined response
rows.plain: Act as quickly as possible after a communication problem or an invalid device condition is found. A fast response helps keep the plant in a safe state.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between fault detection and defined response (relevance) affects how quickly the system reacts to invalid states, with moderate severity since slow response can extend an unsafe condition, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deterministic PLC/DCS scan behavior makes the detection-to-response chain highly consistent once configured, though edge-case invalid states can be missed. Modern control platforms ship with configurable watchdog and fault-response timers that fire within defined cycle times, so response latency is largely a solved parameter during preparation. Setting fault-detection-to-response timing correctly still requires an experienced controls engineer who understands scan cycles and safe-state definitions. The timer and diagnostic capabilities are built into standard runtime, adding little incremental cost to configure during preparation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a single network failure disables both communication paths
rows.plain: Make sure one network problem cannot cut off both ways of communicating. This keeps messages and data moving if one connection fails.
rows.imp_rat: A single network failure disabling both communication paths (relevance) defeats redundancy, with moderate-to-high severity because total comm loss halts monitoring and control, but constrained by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant-media practices are mature, but single points of failure hidden in shared infrastructure remain a common recurring gap. Provisioning physically separate network paths adds meaningful setup time but is a well-understood preparation task. Designing true path independence demands scarce network-architecture expertise to avoid hidden shared switches, cabling or power that defeat redundancy. Fully independent dual paths double hardware and cabling spend, which is a real budget pressure that tempts shortcuts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of dashboard equipment states that differ from the actual controlled equipment states
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment conditions shown on the dashboard match what the equipment is actually doing. This helps operators make decisions using accurate information.
rows.imp_rat: Dashboard states diverging from actual equipment states strikes at the monitoring half of the CFJ (relevance high), and misleading states can prompt bad operator decisions (moderate severity), but the ceiling caps this at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time data acquisition keeps most dashboard states aligned with the field, yet comms dropouts and update lags still produce occasional divergence. State polling and event-driven updates refresh the dashboard quickly, so stale states are usually short-lived. Trusting the dashboard requires little skill when it is accurate, but spotting a divergence demands operator vigilance. Acting on a state that differs from reality can cause costly mis-operations, though the display itself is standard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lag between device conditions and displayed process-variable readings
rows.plain: Keep the displayed readings of process conditions up to date with what is actually happening. This helps people make decisions using current information.
rows.imp_rat: Lag between device conditions and displayed readings affects the monitoring role of the software (moderate relevance), and stale readings can lead operators to wrong decisions with moderate-high consequences (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Display lag is usually small and consistent, but network congestion or heavy loading can introduce noticeable delays intermittently. Scan cycles and refresh rates keep display lag behind device conditions to a fraction of a second in normal operation. Minimizing lag is an automated function requiring little operator skill, though tuning scan rates needs engineering. Faster polling and higher-bandwidth networks reduce lag but add infrastructure cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process-variable readings attributed to the wrong device or process point
rows.plain: Make sure each reading about the process is linked to the correct device or measurement location. This helps people make decisions using the right information.
rows.imp_rat: Misattributing process-variable readings to the wrong device undermines the monitoring core of the CFJ (relevance moderate-high), and acting on wrong-point data can cause bad control decisions (severity moderate), but capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured I/O addressing and channel verification during validation make misattribution uncommon, though wiring or config drift after changes still slips through. Device-to-tag binding is configured once at commissioning, adding no runtime delay for operators reading values. Verifying that a reading maps to the right physical device requires engineering knowledge of the I/O map that few operators hold. Misattributed readings can trigger costly wrong interventions, but standard I/O mapping keeps this rare enough to bound cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cleaning or sanitizing fluid escapes from the batching system during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure cleaning or sanitizing liquid does not leak out of the mixing equipment during cleaning. This protects people, equipment, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning or sanitizing fluid escaping during the cycle is a containment issue peripheral to producing the batch itself; consequences involve operator chemical exposure and environmental release, which are serious but occasional.
rows.sat_rat: Gasketed hygienic connections and leak monitoring contain fluid well in most cycles, with occasional seal failures the residual issue. Leak checks and pressure holds add modest time but are integrated into cycle startup. Detecting and sealing leak paths before a cycle is routine mechanical work within most technicians' abilities. Lost cleaning fluid and cleanup from escapes cost money but incidents are intermittent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of residual air trapped in the ingredient feed lines after priming
rows.plain: Remove as much trapped air as possible from the ingredient tubes before use. This helps ensure the right amount of ingredient moves through the tubes.
rows.imp_rat: Residual air trapped after priming affects dosing accuracy and line integrity, moderately relevant to a clean batch start, but its consequences are usually recoverable through re-priming rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: High-point vents and self-priming pumps clear most trapped air, but pockets in complex piping still recur without careful line design. Venting and deaeration during priming adds time to the start-up sequence. Proper venting depends on operators knowing high-point locations and priming sequence. Vent valves and air-eliminators are low-cost standard fittings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of disrupting communication between controllers, terminals, and actuators during network-fault diagnosis
rows.plain: Prevent communication between control computers, operator screens, and moving devices from being disrupted while a network problem is investigated. This helps avoid unintended plant behavior during the investigation.
rows.imp_rat: Disrupting controller-terminal-actuator communication during fault diagnosis touches the network-interconnection core of the DCS (relevance), but occurs in a bounded diagnostic window with moderate consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Redundant network topologies help, but active diagnosis on live proprietary networks still risks disrupting communication, and confident non-disruptive methods are limited. Network fault diagnosis on a live system is often slow because technicians must probe cautiously to avoid dropping segments. Diagnosing proprietary DCS network faults without disrupting traffic demands specialized network and vendor knowledge that is scarce. Diagnostic tools and vendor support contracts add cost but are commonly retained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transient abnormal behavior disappears before its source is identified
rows.plain: Make sure unusual equipment behavior is recorded and investigated before it disappears. This helps workers find and fix the cause of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Transient abnormal behavior disappearing before diagnosis is a diagnostic-quality issue somewhat tangential to core control (moderate relevance), with moderate consequences of unresolved recurring faults (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Typical historians and event logs miss short-lived transients, so their source is routinely lost this is among the worst-served needs. Transient abnormal behavior disappears within seconds, leaving no time to capture it before it is gone. Identifying a source from vanished transient behavior demands expert inference from sparse evidence. High-speed data capture and forensic replay tooling to freeze transients is expensive and rarely fully deployed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an intermittent device fault is no longer observable when the technician investigates it
rows.plain: A temporary equipment problem should remain detectable long enough for the technician to find it. This makes it less likely that the problem disappears before it can be understood and fixed.
rows.imp_rat: Losing observability of an intermittent fault before investigation impedes monitoring (moderate relevance), and unresolved recurring faults can cause moderate ongoing disruption (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard logging often lacks the resolution to preserve fleeting faults, so intermittent problems frequently become unobservable a chronic, poorly served pain. Intermittent faults often clear before the technician arrives, so investigation time is wasted on vanished conditions. Catching intermittent faults demands highly skilled technicians and often live observation that is hard to schedule. Continuous high-resolution capture to preserve intermittent evidence requires costly historian and diagnostic infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-critical alarms assigned an inappropriate priority, suppression status, or setpoint during rationalization
rows.plain: As few important safety warnings as possible should be given the wrong urgency, hidden when they should be shown, or set to go off at the wrong level. Correct warning settings help people respond properly to danger.
rows.imp_rat: Safety-critical alarms given wrong priority, suppression, or setpoint during rationalization degrades operator warning (relevance) with potentially serious but usually procedurally reviewed consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: ISA- -style methodologies and tools help, but inappropriate priority or suppression assignments to safety-critical alarms still slip through human review. Alarm rationalization is a lengthy manual review of thousands of alarms that consumes substantial engineering time. Assigning correct priority, suppression, and setpoint requires deep process and safety judgement from scarce alarm-management specialists. Rationalization projects and specialized software are costly but periodic.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connected control devices or system interfaces that fail to resume communication after repair
rows.plain: Ensure that connected control equipment and system connections start communicating again after software is repaired. This helps the system work normally after the repair.
rows.imp_rat: Devices failing to resume communication after repair blocks control restoration (relevance moderate) with moderate downtime consequence; held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-reconnect and handshake mechanisms exist but heterogeneous devices and interfaces still fail to resume communication with real frequency. Re-establishing failed communications after repair extends the maintenance window when it happens. Diagnosing why a device or interface won't reconnect demands specialist network and protocol knowledge. The failure consumes technician time and reruns rather than large material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of software or device anomalies not appearing in the observed indicators
rows.plain: Make sure problems in the software or equipment show up in the signs people use to check system health. Hidden problems could otherwise go unnoticed.
rows.imp_rat: Anomalies not surfacing in observed indicators defeats the monitoring purpose (relevance moderate), and missed anomalies can lead to undetected failures (severity moderate), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm and diagnostic coverage is configured for known failure modes, so novel or unmonitored anomalies routinely escape observation. Anomalies absent from indicators are found late, often only after downstream impact appears. Detecting anomalies that never surface in alarms demands deep diagnostic skill and mistrust of the displayed picture. Undetected anomalies in sterile fill can escalate to batch rejection or recalls at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of continued processing after the recipe endpoint is reached
rows.plain: Stop the process soon after the planned finish point is reached. This avoids unnecessary extra processing that could waste time or affect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Continued processing after endpoint is a waste/over-processing concern (relevance 5) that can degrade product but is usually bounded (severity 5).
rows.sat_rat: Automated endpoint detection curbs overprocessing well, but manual endpoints still cause continued processing. Endpoint-reached signals let processing stop promptly when automated. Deciding to stop still relies on operator interpretation where endpoints are manual. Overprocessing wastes time and can degrade product, partly controlled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required production records corrupted or made unavailable during patch application
rows.plain: Protect required production records while installing software updates. The records must remain available and usable afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted production records during patching threatens GMP compliance (severity elevated) but is a patch-time data-integrity concern peripheral to the live control job (relevance modest); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Mature backup-before-patch and transactional record practices catch most cases, but corruption during patch application still occurs occasionally. Protecting records before patching adds preparation time but is not primarily about speed. Ensuring records stay intact through a patch requires knowledgeable operators who follow validated backup procedures. Lost production records can trigger costly reconstruction or regulatory penalties, though cost is secondary to the integrity failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch, audit, configuration, or validation records required after retirement become unavailable or corrupted
rows.plain: Keep important records available and undamaged after the control software is retired. These records may be needed later to review past work or prove that requirements were met.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailability of batch/audit/validation records after retirement barely touches ongoing control (low relevance), but corrupted or missing validation records can trigger serious compliance and traceability failures (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Records are generally captured, but format obsolescence and incomplete migration mean corruption or unreadability of retained records still surfaces periodically. Archiving and migration take planned effort but do not typically constrain the retirement timeline. Preserving batch, audit, configuration and validation records in usable form from proprietary systems demands strong migration and format-conversion skills. Long-term readable archiving, including format migration, carries significant ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of revoking shared access rights still required by active control functions in the sterile fill-finish environment
rows.plain: Remove shared access only when it is no longer needed by active functions that operate the clean production area. This helps prevent important operations from being interrupted.
rows.imp_rat: Revoking access rights still needed by active control functions could interrupt live sterile operations (severity elevated), but as an access-management edge case it is peripheral (relevance modest) and capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Shared credentials and poorly documented service accounts mean revocation regularly risks breaking a still-active control function despite review efforts. Access-rights review can be done ahead of retirement, so timing is not the primary constraint. Untangling shared or service accounts to know which rights active control functions still need requires identity and OT security expertise that is frequently thin. Detailed entitlement review is labor-intensive but not capital-heavy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of retired industrial control software remaining executable on in-scope control assets
rows.plain: Make sure retired control software can no longer run on the equipment it once operated. This helps prevent old software from affecting current operations.
rows.imp_rat: Retired software remaining executable is a peripheral lifecycle-hygiene concern for the CFJ of actively controlling devices (moderate relevance), but if stale logic runs on live assets the consequences can be significant safety and integrity issues (higher severity).
rows.sat_rat: Without comprehensive asset inventories, retired software copies persist on overlooked or offline control assets, so residual executables remain a common finding. Disabling or uninstalling executables is quick once the decommissioning step is reached. Ensuring nothing remains runnable across all in-scope control assets requires thorough asset knowledge that is often incomplete for legacy OT fleets. Verification sweeps add labor cost but little material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a replacement module returns to service with an incorrect control configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement unit comes back into use with the correct control settings. Wrong settings could make it operate incorrectly or unsafely.
rows.imp_rat: A replacement module returning with incorrect configuration can misdrive the process, moderately central to reliable control (relevance) with real but detectable consequences on a single module (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Version-controlled configuration backups and automated downloads make correct restoration common, though stale or mismatched backups still occasionally send a module back with the wrong config. Automated configuration download from managed backups makes module replacement reasonably quick. Correct configuration restoration relies on disciplined version control that technicians can follow, reducing skill dependence when procedures are mature. Configuration management systems are largely included in the DCS toolset, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a DCS communication path remaining active after isolation is declared complete
rows.plain: Make sure the communication connection to the control system is truly turned off after isolation is said to be complete.
rows.imp_rat: A communication path staying active after isolation is declared complete is a specific isolation/maintenance failure (relevance) that can energize equipment unexpectedly and injure personnel (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Redundant links, backup buses, and undocumented proprietary connections make a residually-active path a realistic and recurring failure despite isolation checklists. Verifying every communication path is truly inactive after isolation adds tracing work that can extend the isolation phase. Proprietary DCS network topologies mean confirming full isolation requires engineers who deeply understand the specific system's redundant and hidden links. Path verification mainly costs labor time rather than materials or licenses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous material leaking during segregation, staging, or removal from the sterile fill-finish facility
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous material from leaking while it is separated, set aside, or removed from the facility where sterile products are made. This protects workers, the facility, and the surroundings.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous-material leaks during segregation or removal are peripheral to the DCS's core control-networking job (relevance) but carry serious safety and environmental consequences when they occur (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established hazmat and GMP handling procedures reduce leak likelihood, but the sterile-facility constraints and material variety leave recurring residual risk. Careful segregation, staging, and removal of hazardous material from a sterile fill-finish facility is slow because of containment and clearance procedures. Handling hazardous material in a regulated sterile environment demands specialized EHS and cleanroom-trained personnel whose judgement drives whether leaks are avoided. Compliant containment, disposal packaging, and facility downtime make this a materially costly activity.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous residue being released during recovery, packaging, or transfer
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous leftover material from escaping while it is collected, packed, or moved. Safe handling keeps people and the surrounding area protected.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous residue release during recovery, packaging, or transfer sits at the material-handling edge of the scope (low relevance) but is severe when exposure or environmental release occurs (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature hazardous-waste packaging practice makes releases during recovery uncommon, though spills during transfer still occur. Recovery and packaging under controlled transfer adds handling time that is planned for. Proper containerization and transfer of hazardous residues follows waste-handling training that is widely available. Certified containers, labels, and disposal fees are a real recurring cost of hazardous recovery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in signal readings at a fixed reference condition
rows.plain: Make sure the signal gives nearly the same reading each time when the conditions have not changed.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in signal readings at a fixed reference condition affects measurement trust central to monitoring (moderate relevance), and drift can degrade control decisions with moderate consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard calibration reduces signal variation adequately, but drift, noise, and sensor aging still leave recurring variation that is never fully eliminated. Calibration and reference-condition checks during preparation take measurable time but follow routine procedures. Instrument calibration to control signal variation requires trained technicians, a skill that is available but not trivial. Calibration equipment and periodic checks carry recurring cost, though it is a budgeted routine.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that status indicators contradict actual control states during diagnosis
rows.plain: Make sure the lights or messages showing the equipment’s status match what it is actually doing. This helps the technician find and fix problems correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Status indicators contradicting actual control states during diagnosis misleads troubleshooting (relevance moderate) and can prolong faults (severity moderate); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Data validation and consistent I/O mapping keep most indicators aligned, but sensor faults and stale values still occasionally make indicators contradict reality. Contradictions surface at diagnosis time and slow it only through the reconciliation they force. Deciding whether the indicator or the actual state is correct demands experienced diagnostic judgement. Chasing a false indicator wastes technician effort and can prolong downtime, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unavailable health and status indications during sterile fill-finish production
rows.plain: Keep the system’s health and status information available during the filling and finishing of sterile products, so workers can see problems without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of health/status indications during sterile fill-finish blinds operators (relevance moderate to the monitoring job), and in a regulated production run the consequences of unnoticed faults are notable (severity moderate), held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Redundancy and heartbeat monitoring are standard and effective, yet network faults and scan overloads still produce recurring gaps in status availability. Redundant controllers and watchdogs restore health indications quickly, but failover gaps can still leave brief blind windows. Diagnosing why status went unavailable often demands specialist troubleshooting of network and controller health. Loss of health visibility during aseptic production can force conservative holds that waste product and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between fault onset and its visibility to the operator
rows.plain: Show the operator as soon as a problem starts, so it can be handled before it causes more trouble.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between fault onset and operator visibility slows response (relevance moderate) with moderate escalation risk (severity moderate); ranked below batch 1 so capped.
rows.sat_rat: Faults without configured alarm points or those masked in alarm floods can remain invisible far longer than intended, a persistent gap. Event-driven alarming surfaces most faults to the operator within the scan-and-annunciate cycle of a second or two. Fault visibility is largely automated, but recognizing subtle or unalarmed conditions still depends on operator vigilance. Alarm annunciation is a core included function, so surfacing faults quickly carries little marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required control functions unavailable under the renewed license
rows.plain: Make sure all necessary control features still work after the software permission is renewed. Production may be affected if needed features are unavailable.
rows.imp_rat: Required control functions unavailable after license renewal directly removes control capability (relevance moderate), and losing functions during operation can halt production (severity moderate); scored under the 5.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: License renewals generally preserve entitled functions, so missing control functions under renewal are fairly rare. Missing functions under a renewed license cause outages until corrected, but time is not primary. Verifying feature coverage against the renewed license requires familiarity with license tiers. Unavailable functions can halt value-producing work, but the error is the functional gap itself.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of liquid flow interruption caused by an obstructed or incorrectly aligned required circuit during priming
rows.plain: Restore liquid flow quickly when a required path is blocked or set up incorrectly during startup, so the system can begin working without a long delay.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid flow interruption from a misaligned circuit during priming affects throughput and readiness (moderate relevance) but consequences are mostly delay-related and recoverable (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Valve position feedback and flow confirmation catch many misalignments, though intermittent obstructions still cause stoppages. Obstructed or misaligned circuits during priming directly stall flow and delay batch start until cleared. Diagnosing an alignment or blockage issue requires operator troubleshooting familiarity with the circuit. Downtime during priming interruptions carries opportunity cost but little material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous residues during system dismantling
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous leftover material while taking the system apart. This helps prevent harmful contact during shutdown and removal.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during dismantling is a decommissioning safety issue only marginally tied to batch production (low relevance) but severe for the exposed worker (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established PPE and dismantling protocols keep exposure incidents relatively uncommon when followed. Donning appropriate PPE and staging decontamination during dismantling adds routine time. Safe dismantling around hazardous residues leans on trained crews who select correct PPE and sequence and recognize residue risks. Consumable PPE and disposal of contaminated items are recurring but manageable costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous process residues during component extraction
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous leftover material while removing parts that may be reused. This helps prevent harmful contact during the work.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous residues during component extraction is a maintenance safety concern peripheral to batching output (low relevance) yet severe for personnel harm (notable severity).
rows.sat_rat: Protective practice covers most extraction exposure, though hidden residue pockets in pumps and valves still cause occasional contact. Careful component extraction with containment adds time but is a bounded step. Extracting fouled components without spreading residue depends on skilled, attentive technicians handling awkward parts. Containment and PPE consumables represent modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of control-device connectivity loss during transfer from the primary path to the fallback path
rows.plain: Keep the loss of connection to the control device as short as possible when switching from the main connection to the backup connection.
rows.imp_rat: Connectivity loss duration during primary-to-fallback transfer (relevance) creates a brief control gap, with moderate severity depending on transfer time, generally short and recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Where PRP/HSR is deployed the switchover is essentially seamless, making this one of the better-served needs, though ring-recovery topologies can still drop packets. Media-redundancy protocols (PRP, HSR, ring topologies) achieve bumpless or sub-cycle failover that is standard in modern industrial networks. Correctly configuring failover timing parameters requires networking specialists familiar with the specific redundancy protocol. Redundancy-capable switches and NICs carry a premium but are commodity items in this sector.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of coordinated control units left in modes inconsistent with the intended operating state after a mode change
rows.plain: After changing how the system operates, leave as few connected control units as possible in the wrong setting, so all parts work in the intended way.
rows.imp_rat: Coordinated units left in inconsistent modes after a change undermines multi-device control coherence (relevance moderate) with moderate process risk (severity moderate); capped.
rows.sat_rat: Cross-unit mode consistency is a recurring gap because handshaking between controllers is imperfect and units are routinely left in mismatched states after transitions. Coordinated mode changes across control units execute quickly when sequenced, but reconciling stragglers after a change can add manual delay. Ensuring every coordinated unit lands in the intended mode still leans heavily on experienced operators who know the plant's mode interdependencies. Mode-consistency logic is part of the DCS but custom coordination often requires engineering effort, adding project cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the mode shown to the technician differs from the active mode of the controlled unit
rows.plain: Make sure the operating mode shown to the technician is the same mode the equipment is actually using. This prevents the equipment from being controlled based on misleading information.
rows.imp_rat: A shown mode differing from the controlled unit's active mode misleads the operator (relevance moderate) and can cause wrong actions (severity moderate); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback-driven mode display keeps shown and actual modes aligned in most systems, with occasional divergence during transitions or comms loss. Mode indications refresh with normal polling, so any mismatch is usually brief. The technician trusts the displayed mode without special skill, but detecting a mismatch requires vigilance. Acting on a wrong displayed mode can cause mis-operation with real cost, though display sync itself is free.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical equipment states missing from the dashboard
rows.plain: Show all important equipment conditions on the main display, so workers are not missing information they need to operate the system safely.
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical equipment states from the dashboard undermines monitoring completeness (relevance moderate) and can hide dangerous conditions (severity moderate), yet it ranks below batch 1 so stays capped.
rows.sat_rat: Dashboard design conventions and P&ID-driven configuration capture most critical states, but engineering omissions still leave some off the display. A missing state doesn't delay anything directly until the operator needs it and finds it absent. Compensating for a missing critical state forces the operator to infer it from other cues, demanding extra knowledge. An unmonitored critical state can allow an undetected fault to escalate into costly damage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of critical process variables lacking live readings during operation
rows.plain: Keep live readings available for all important conditions that affect the process. This helps the operator notice problems while the process is running.
rows.imp_rat: Critical process variables lacking live readings degrades real-time monitoring (relevance moderate) and can allow undetected excursions (severity moderate); held at the ceiling per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard instrumentation and trending cover most critical variables live, with residual gaps from sensor faults or unconfigured tags. Live variable feeds update continuously, so operators rarely wait for a reading when one is configured. Operating without a live reading forces skilled estimation, but properly configured systems seldom require that. Blind operation on a missing variable risks off-spec product, though instrumentation is already in place.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of lost visibility into process variables during operation
rows.plain: Keep periods when workers cannot see important process readings as short as possible during operation.
rows.imp_rat: Lost visibility into process variables during operation impairs monitoring (relevance moderate) and can delay response (severity moderate); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Visibility loss still occurs during server failovers, network saturation, or historian gaps, leaving recurring blind windows despite redundancy. Live data refresh in modern HMIs is sub-second under normal load, so process-variable visibility is typically continuous. Maintaining visibility requires little operator skill since polling and display are automated, though diagnosing outages needs expertise. Redundant servers and networks that sustain visibility are standard but add hardware and licensing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of irrelevant alarms and status changes obscuring evidence of the anomaly
rows.plain: Show fewer unnecessary warnings and changes so the real problem is easier to see. This helps the person investigating understand what is actually going wrong.
rows.imp_rat: Irrelevant alarms obscuring anomaly evidence slow diagnosis (relevance moderate) and can delay response to a genuine problem (severity moderate). A diagnostic-clarity concern rather than a core control failure; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm floods routinely bury the pertinent evidence, and standard prioritization only partly mitigates this recurring failure. Sorting relevant alarms from irrelevant status changes costs precious diagnostic time during an anomaly. Filtering out noise to find the real signal relies heavily on experienced operators recognizing which alarms matter. Advanced alarm shelving and suppression features exist but require costly configuration and maintenance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that certificates used by the industrial control software expire before renewal is completed
rows.plain: Make it less likely that the software's security credentials will expire before new ones are ready. Keeping them current helps the control software continue working securely.
rows.imp_rat: Expired certificates before renewal can block secure communication and interrupt control (relevance moderate), with consequences of downtime rather than direct process ruin (severity moderate). Administrative in nature; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Manual expiry tracking without robust automated alerting means certificates still expire before renewal more often than acceptable. Timely renewal depends on advance scheduling that current practice often compresses to the last minute. Tracking expiry across many certificates requires vigilant administrators, a burden easily dropped. An expired certificate can halt production at high cost, though the error is the lapse rather than spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of imported control parameters or tag relationships that differ from approved legacy values
rows.plain: Reduce differences between the imported control settings and connections and the approved older settings. This helps ensure the system is set up as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Imported parameters differing from approved legacy values is a migration-integrity issue, moderately relevant to correct control behavior (relevance) with consequences that can degrade control if undetected (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Migrations rely largely on manual comparison with few automated checks, so divergent parameters and tag relationships are a chronic, poorly-caught problem. Comparing imported parameters against approved legacy values is a painstaking reconciliation that lengthens migration work. Judging whether a differing value is an error or an intended update needs deep legacy-system knowledge that is often lost. Manual parameter verification is labor-intensive and errors force costly re-validation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of analog channels left with scaling inconsistent with approved engineering ranges
rows.imp_rat: Analog channels scaled inconsistently with engineering ranges affects reading accuracy, moderately relevant to correct control (relevance); typically caught during calibration with modest consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Loop checks and range documentation catch most scaling errors, but inconsistent analog scaling still recurs across large channel counts. Checking analog scaling against engineering ranges for every channel adds meaningful loop-check time. Verifying scaling is routine engineering work requiring attention rather than scarce skill. Scaling errors are cheap to fix if found early but drive rework if missed until commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of specified control functions that fail their factory acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Control functions failing factory acceptance criteria is a pre-deployment quality gate (relevance); failures are caught before service so operational consequences are limited (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structured FAT protocols are standard practice and catch most functional defects, though incomplete test coverage lets some through. Factory acceptance testing is a scheduled milestone, but failures trigger rework loops that can delay release. Writing and executing meaningful FAT cases requires experienced test engineers, and coverage quality varies with their skill. FAT is a budgeted event, and catching failures in the factory is far cheaper than in the field.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dashboard indicators mapped to the wrong process signals
rows.imp_rat: Dashboard indicators mapped to wrong process signals (relevance) mislead operators monitoring, and severity is moderate since bad readings can prompt wrong decisions, but this is a display-layer issue not the core control loop.
rows.sat_rat: Visual verification and test-point checks catch many mapping errors, though subtle wrong-signal assignments still slip through. Checking dashboard indicators against their intended signals is a straightforward but time-consuming review pass. Verifying indicator-to-signal mapping needs process understanding but is within reach of typical configurers. Mis-mapped indicators mainly cost review labor and rare operational confusion, a limited expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of hazard-bearing areas of the batching system that remain uninspected during the safety review
rows.imp_rat: Uninspected hazard-bearing areas during the safety review is an administrative assurance gap peripheral to producing the batch; when a missed hazard later manifests the consequences can be serious for operator safety, but the review error itself is indirect.
rows.sat_rat: Concealed or inaccessible areas routinely go uninspected, leaving coverage gaps in the safety review. Fully covering every hazard-bearing area in a safety review lengthens the review substantially. Ensuring no hazard zone goes uninspected demands thorough, experienced reviewers who know where hazards hide. The review is labor cost with little material spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of control and monitoring unavailability caused by installation repair
rows.imp_rat: Control/monitoring unavailability during installation repair is a maintenance-window downtime issue (relevance modest) with moderate consequence; it stays below the batch-1 items.
rows.sat_rat: Repairs sometimes reintroduce faults, adding uncertainty to how quickly service resumes. Installation repair typically forces control and monitoring offline for extended periods because redundant fallback rarely covers repair scenarios fully. Fast repair depends on scarce experts who understand the specific system, which lengthens downtime when they are unavailable. Downtime carries real production cost, but the core error is the duration itself rather than resource waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required recovery data is incomplete or unreadable during a scheduled test
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or unreadable recovery data during a scheduled test is a preparedness gap somewhat removed from live control (moderate relevance), with moderate consequences confined to test/recovery scenarios (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled DR tests catch some gaps, yet incomplete or unreadable data still appears because coverage is inconsistent. Incomplete recovery data during a scheduled test extends the test but time is secondary. Judging whether recovery data is complete requires knowledgeable validation staff. Failed tests consume labor and reschedule effort more than material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between recovered control data and the validated pre-test state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between recovered and validated pre-test state affect restore fidelity (moderate relevance), and mismatched state after recovery can cause moderate operational errors (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Comparison tooling is limited, so discrepancies between recovered and validated states persist and are hard to detect fully. Reconciling discrepancies against the validated pre-test state lengthens test closure. Identifying subtle discrepancies requires experts who know the validated baseline in detail. Discrepancies drive rework rather than direct material spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disrupted control or monitoring during configuration collection
rows.imp_rat: Disrupted control or monitoring during configuration collection touches the active-control job (relevance moderate), but the disruption is transient and tied to a maintenance activity (severity low-moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Established online-read practices make disrupted-control episodes uncommon, but legacy device quirks occasionally trigger unexpected stalls. Modern controllers and SCADA systems support online/hot configuration reads so control rarely stops for collection, though older PLCs still force brief upload pauses. Vendor tooling automates configuration pulls, but knowing which reads are safe online still leans on experienced maintenance engineers. Configuration collection uses existing engineering workstations and licenses, adding negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of active control or monitoring interruption caused by backup activity
rows.imp_rat: Control/monitoring interruption caused by backup activity affects the live job (relevance moderate); consequences are contained to backup windows and usually recoverable (severity low-moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Live-system backups are mature and usually complete without disturbing control, though occasional resource contention still causes momentary interruption. Redundant servers and background/agent-based backups let most systems back up while running, keeping active interruption short for typical configurations. Configuring non-disruptive backup windows requires competent IT/OT staff who understand the control system's load, a routinely available skill. Backup infrastructure and storage are standard budgeted items, so avoiding interruption carries little marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of control-system nodes left at inconsistent patch levels after patch application
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent patch levels across nodes (relevance) can cause behavioral mismatches, with moderate severity from potential instability, but this is a maintenance-hygiene issue secondary to the core control job.
rows.sat_rat: Patch orchestration tools help, but partial rollouts on heterogeneous control nodes still leave inconsistent levels. Chasing inconsistent patch levels across nodes adds troubleshooting time later. Keeping all nodes at a consistent level requires disciplined change management expertise. Inconsistency drives rework rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to return the industrial control software to its validated operating state after a failed patch application
rows.imp_rat: Time to return to validated state after a failed patch is a recovery-duration concern (relevance modest) with moderate compliance/downtime impact; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Rollback snapshots usually work, yet incomplete or untested rollback paths introduce uncertainty into recovery. Rollback to a validated state after a failed patch can take hours because restore-and-revalidate procedures are slow. Executing a clean rollback under pressure requires experienced engineers familiar with the validated baseline. Extended downtime is costly but the primary loss experienced is elapsed recovery time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a deprecated-module decommissioning change invalidates the validated state of the industrial control software
rows.imp_rat: A decommissioning change invalidating the validated state (relevance) matters in regulated environments, with moderate severity around requalification effort, but it is a change-management edge case not central to routine control.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change control and impact assessment usually protect validated state, but incomplete impact analysis occasionally lets a change break validation unnoticed. Revalidation after a module decommissioning change extends the timeline but is scheduled within change control. Assessing whether a change invalidates the validated state requires senior validation judgement that is scarce and heavily relied upon. Requalification and documentation are costly, which sometimes discourages thorough revalidation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of archived process and event records being deleted before the required retention period ends
rows.imp_rat: Premature deletion of archived process/event records is peripheral to the software's live control job (low relevance), but losing retention-required records carries meaningful compliance and audit consequences (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Archive retention policies reliably prevent early deletion in managed systems, though records outside the formal archive can still be lost. Retention-clock tracking is automated in many archive systems, so premature deletion timing is largely controlled. Configuring retention rules correctly for varied record types needs archival expertise that is usually available in regulated firms. Archive retention incurs steady storage cost accepted as compliance overhead.

rows.stk: Control system engineers; IT/OT integration teams; decommissioning project teams
rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of disruption to connected systems still supporting sterile fill-finish activities
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to connected systems supporting sterile fill-finish affects surrounding operations (relevance modest) with moderate consequence, ranking below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Integration inventories are often incomplete, so a forgotten connected dependency periodically causes disruption to supporting systems. Interfaces to connected systems can be quiesced on a planned schedule, but staged sequencing across many integrations often stretches the timeline. Understanding the full web of downstream connected systems requires integration expertise that is rarely held in one place. Maintaining interfaces during phased retirement adds cost, especially when legacy connectors need bespoke handling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting the wrong controlled device or process area because hierarchy identifiers are difficult to distinguish
rows.imp_rat: Hard-to-distinguish hierarchy identifiers (relevance) can lead to selecting the wrong device or area, with moderate severity since misselection risks wrong actions, but usability ambiguity is mitigated by operator confirmation.
rows.sat_rat: Naming standards reduce confusion but cryptic or similar tag identifiers still cause wrong-selection errors, so this is only moderately served. Ambiguous identifiers cost time when operators pause to confirm they selected the right device or area. Distinguishing similar hierarchy identifiers relies on operator familiarity and careful attention rather than software safeguards. A rigorous naming convention is inexpensive in principle but requires disciplined engineering governance to enforce.

rows.source_job: Configure data logging and historian settings
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical process and batch tags omitted from logging
rows.imp_rat: Critical process/batch tags omitted from logging harms traceability and compliance records (relevance moderate) and can invalidate batch release (severity moderate), yet does not stop active control. Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Logging configuration is largely manual with weak completeness checks, so critical tags are omitted often enough to make this a recurring gap. Enumerating and verifying every critical tag for logging is a tedious manual checklist step that lengthens preparation. Knowing which process and batch tags are critical to log requires domain judgement that not all configurers possess, so omissions recur. Missed tags are cheap to add during preparation but very expensive if discovered only after a batch runs without them.

rows.source_job: Configure data logging and historian settings
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical process excursions obscured by the configured logging resolution
rows.imp_rat: Logging resolution obscuring critical excursions (relevance) affects diagnostics and forensics rather than real-time control, giving moderate severity since lost records hamper investigation but do not directly cause misoperation.
rows.sat_rat: Default logging resolutions frequently obscure short excursions and this is often discovered only after data is lost, marking a real gap. Choosing and validating logging resolution to capture excursions is an analysis step that takes deliberate effort. Setting resolution that will not obscure critical excursions requires understanding of process dynamics, a specialized judgement. Higher resolution increases storage cost, creating pressure that leads to under-sampling and missed excursions.

rows.source_job: Configure data logging and historian settings
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of timestamp misalignment between logged process data and batch events
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp misalignment between logged data and batch events degrades traceability (relevance moderate) and can compromise batch record integrity and investigations (severity moderate), but does not stop control itself. Capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Mature time-synchronization practice covers most cases, though residual drift and multi-system clock mismatches still occasionally misalign logs and batch events. Configuring synchronized time sources during preparation is a known setup task that adds modest time but is well understood. Standard NTP/PTP and historian timestamping practices are established, so only moderate expertise is needed to align clocks correctly. Time-sync infrastructure is inexpensive and widely available, imposing little cost burden.

rows.source_job: Configure data logging and historian settings
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of historical process data being deleted before the required retention period ends
rows.imp_rat: Premature deletion of historical process data is tangential to the software's control function (low relevance), but violating retention periods carries meaningful compliance consequences (moderate-high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established retention-lock and archiving practices reliably prevent premature deletion in most deployments, with rare failures from misconfiguration. Configuring retention and deletion locks is a one-time setup step that adds little to preparation time. Retention-policy configuration is a standard historian/DB feature requiring only moderate administrative skill. Retention storage is inexpensive and policy enforcement adds negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of dashboard unavailability during sterile fill-finish operation
rows.imp_rat: Dashboard unavailability during sterile fill-finish removes operator visibility (relevance moderate) and can delay reaction to process drift (severity moderate), but underlying control may continue. Capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated GMP environments enforce redundancy so dashboard outages are uncommon, though client, network, or server faults can still briefly interrupt visibility. Dashboard failover in redundant systems is typically fast, minimizing downtime during operation. Sustaining dashboard availability is automated, requiring little operator skill beyond noticing an outage. High-availability visualization servers for sterile operations are costly but standard in validated pharma environments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of relevant critical control points omitted from the operator's filtered view
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical control points from the filtered operator view (relevance) reduces visibility, with moderate severity because a hidden point could delay response, but it is a presentation-configuration concern rather than control execution.
rows.sat_rat: Omitting a relevant critical point from a filtered view is a real risk from misconfiguration, only partially caught by review, so served with recurring gaps. Once configured, filtered views present relevant control points immediately with no time penalty. Ensuring no critical point is omitted requires careful engineering review, a skill-dependent configuration task. Verifying filter completeness adds engineering and validation cost, especially in regulated environments.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of acknowledging an unintended active alarm when similar alarms are displayed
rows.imp_rat: Acknowledging an unintended active alarm among similar alarms is an interface-ambiguity error affecting alarm handling (relevance modest) with moderate safety consequence, capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm grouping and detail views help, but visually similar alarms in a crowded list still make wrong acknowledgment a recurring risk. Acknowledging is instant, so the error is misdirection, not delay. Distinguishing among similar-looking alarms to acknowledge the right one relies on careful operator attention that fails under alarm floods. Acknowledging the wrong alarm can mask a real condition and lead to costly consequences, though no direct spend is involved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of raw ingredient lost through leaks while charging the feed lines and supply tanks
