rows.imp_rat: Raw ingredient lost through leaks while charging feed lines is mainly an efficiency and yield concern rather than a job-blocking one; consequences are cost and minor formulation drift, usually absorbed without endangering the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized sanitary connections make charging leaks infrequent, with occasional gasket failures the residual issue. Leak-driven cleanups and reconnections add incidental time during charging. Proper sanitary connection technique limits leaks and is a well-established operator competency. Sanitary gaskets and clamped fittings largely contain ingredient loss, keeping leak-related material waste low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an actual departure is evaluated as compliant against an incorrect prescribed condition
rows.plain: Make sure a real departure from the required condition is not judged acceptable because it was compared with the wrong requirement. This helps ensure problems are recognized and addressed.
rows.imp_rat: Judging an actual departure as compliant against a wrong prescribed condition defeats enforcement (relevance) and risks releasing out-of-spec product (severity), held at the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-bound limit checks are usually correct, but misconfigured conditions can still cause false-compliant evaluations. Automated compliance evaluation is immediate against loaded conditions. Correct prescribed-condition selection is system-managed, easing operator judgement. Evaluating against a wrong condition can pass a bad batch, a costly but contained risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which reported condition values differ from actual batch conditions during compounding
rows.plain: Make sure the reported conditions match the real conditions of the batch while ingredients are being mixed. This helps workers notice problems and make sound decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Reported values diverging from actual batch conditions during compounding undermines accurate recording/enforcement (relevance) and can mask harmful conditions (severity); at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated logging keeps reported close to actual, bounded by sensor accuracy and calibration. Automated data capture reports conditions in real time. Direct instrument capture reduces operator transcription judgement. Reported-vs-actual divergence can misjudge batches, a partly contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of condition values evaluated against the wrong recipe step or batch
rows.plain: Have fewer readings compared with the wrong product instructions or production batch. This helps identify actual problems instead of creating false ones or missing real ones.
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating condition values against the wrong recipe step/batch directly corrupts enforcement of the recipe (relevance), and misjudged compliance could pass a bad batch (severity); both capped under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES step-context evaluation is reliable, though loosely integrated SCADA points can still be mis-scoped. Correct step/batch context is applied automatically with no delay. System context binding reduces operator burden of matching values to steps. Misevaluation against the wrong step wastes effort and batches, mostly avoided.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which reported values differ from actual batch conditions during endpoint determination
rows.plain: Make sure the reported readings match the real batch conditions when deciding that a recipe step is finished. Wrong readings could lead to ending the step too early or too late.
rows.imp_rat: Reported vs actual divergence at endpoint determination affects whether the recipe is correctly enforced (relevance) and could misdeclare endpoints (severity); capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Instrumented endpoint capture is accurate within sensor limits, but manual endpoint entries still diverge. Endpoint values are captured live without reporting delay. Automated capture at endpoint reduces manual reading errors. Divergent endpoint values can cause wrong disposition, mostly guarded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unapproved parameter adjustments made during batch execution
rows.plain: Make fewer unapproved changes to process settings while producing a batch. This keeps the work consistent with the approved instructions.
rows.imp_rat: Unapproved parameter adjustments during execution directly defeat enforcement of the recipe (relevance) and can produce non-conforming product (severity); capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based approval gating reliably restricts unapproved adjustments where configured, with residual override gaps. Approval workflows for adjustments add authorization time but are enforced inline. Permission gating reduces reliance on operators knowing what is allowed. Unapproved adjustments risk batch quality, mostly prevented by access control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized critical parameter limit changes during recipe execution
rows.plain: Make fewer unauthorized changes to the allowed limits for important process settings. Keeping these limits controlled helps prevent the process from moving into an unsafe or unacceptable range.
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized critical-parameter limit changes during execution subvert enforcement (relevance) and risk unsafe product (severity); held at the ceiling despite high stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration access controls and audit trails reliably restrict unauthorized limit changes. Limit-change controls require authorization with minimal added delay. Access control removes operator discretion over changing critical limits. Unauthorized limit changes can pass bad product, well guarded by permissions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which recorded parameter values differ from observed values during execution
rows.plain: Make sure the values written down during the work match what was actually happening. Accurate records are needed to prove the work was done correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Recorded parameter values differing from observed values during execution corrupts the record accuracy central to the job (relevance) and misleads review (severity), at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic data capture reliably matches recorded to observed values for instrumented parameters. Automated recording captures observed values instantly with no transcription lag. Direct data capture removes operator transcription skill from the loop. Recording errors trigger investigations, largely avoided by automated capture.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that execution evidence is lost or altered after capture
rows.plain: Keep proof of what was done from being lost or changed after it is recorded. This makes the work history trustworthy and supports later review.
rows.imp_rat: Loss or alteration of execution evidence after capture destroys the record integrity the job promises (relevance) and undermines compliance defensibility (severity); ceiling-limited.
rows.sat_rat: Part trails and access controls reliably prevent post-capture loss or alteration. Audit-trailed capture is immediate and secure with no added time. Automated audit trails remove any operator role in preserving evidence integrity. Lost or altered evidence causes major compliance cost, strongly prevented.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a recipe step being blocked by an unavailable required ingredient
rows.plain: Make sure every ingredient needed for the next recipe step is available when needed. Missing an ingredient could stop or slow production.
rows.imp_rat: A step blocked by an unavailable ingredient is mainly a throughput/delay issue (relevance 4) with limited quality consequence if handled (severity 4).
rows.sat_rat: Inventory/MES material-staging checks reduce surprise shortages, though upstream availability gaps still block steps. Ingredient-availability blocks cause real waiting time only partly reduced by inventory visibility. Anticipating shortages requires planning judgement rather than being system-guaranteed. Blocked steps idle labor and equipment, a cost inventory systems partly mitigate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material uses begun before conformance verification is complete
rows.plain: Reduce the number of times materials are used before checks confirm that they meet the required standards. This helps prevent unsuitable materials from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Beginning material use before conformance verification completes undermines the enforce-before-execute logic (relevance) and can incorporate unverified material (severity); ceiling-limited.
rows.sat_rat: Release-status gating reliably prevents use before verification where inventory is integrated. Conformance gating can delay material use until verification completes. Release gating removes the operator's need to judge conformance status. Using unverified material risks batch loss, well prevented by release locks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing a material or lot not specified by the released recipe into the compounding batch
rows.plain: Prevent unapproved materials or product groups from being added to the mixture. This helps ensure the finished product contains exactly what the approved instructions require.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing a non-specified material/lot into the batch is a contamination and formulation failure (relevance) with serious sterile-product harm (severity); capped at 4.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced material verification makes introducing unspecified materials uncommon where scanning is required. Scan verification blocks unspecified materials instantly at addition. Barcode identity checks remove reliance on operator recognition of materials. Scanning infrastructure is low cost and widespread.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with an incorrect identity is cleared for addition to the sterile compounding batch
rows.plain: Confirm that the material is the correct one before allowing it to be added to the sterile batch. This helps prevent the wrong material from entering the batch.
rows.imp_rat: Clearing incorrect-identity material for a sterile batch is a critical contamination/formulation failure (relevance) with severe patient-safety stakes (severity); capped at 4.0 by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Identity verification is strong for scanned items, but sterile-specific identity risks persist where manual handling occurs. Identity scan checks clear materials quickly before addition. Automated identity verification reduces reliance on operator judgement. Uses existing scanning and material master data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch record data is lost or corrupted during capture, transfer, or retention
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that production records are lost or changed while they are being recorded, moved, or stored. Reliable records show what happened during production.
rows.imp_rat: Loss or corruption of batch record data during capture/transfer/retention strikes the record core of the job (relevance) and destroys compliance evidence (severity); at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated MES with backups and audit-controlled retention makes record loss or corruption rare, so this is well served. Automated backups and redundant storage retain data without manual delay. Validated systems handle data integrity so operators need little special skill to preserve records. Data loss is expensive but robust IT infrastructure makes it uncommon.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that unauthorized overrides bypass compliant process boundaries
rows.plain: Unapproved changes should not be used to skip required process rules or limits. This helps keep the work within approved safety requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Overrides bypassing compliant process boundaries directly nullify enforcement (relevance) and can cause harmful excursions (severity); ceiling-limited by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Modern systems enforce role-based override authorization reliably, though misconfiguration can still leave bypass paths. Role-based override controls operate in real time without adding evaluation delay. Access-control and override authorization are standard configurable features needing modest expertise. Preventing unauthorized overrides avoids costly compliance breaches and rejected batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of continued compounding after a process parameter crosses a compliant boundary
rows.plain: Shorten the time mixing continues after a process reading moves outside the allowed range. This helps limit the amount of work done under conditions that may not meet requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Continued compounding after crossing a compliant boundary is a live enforcement failure (relevance) with contamination/quality harm (severity); capped at 4.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Standard control systems stop or hold out-of-bound execution promptly where interlocks are configured, though gaps allow some continuation. Automated alarming and interlocks halt or flag out-of-bound compounding quickly, minimizing continued execution time. Fast automated response reduces reliance on operator vigilance to stop the process. Shorter out-of-bound duration limits scrap and reduces cost of noncompliant product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the temporary output override remains active after the exceptional operating condition ends
rows.imp_rat: A temporary output override lingering after the exceptional condition ends is a specific override-management lapse (relevance) with moderate, generally recoverable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Forced/override lists exist but automatic expiry is uncommon, so stale overrides linger and occasionally cause surprises. Overrides often persist longer than needed because their removal depends on someone remembering to clear them. Tracking and clearing active overrides relies on operator discipline and shift memory rather than automation. Override-tracking displays exist in the base system, so cost is not the barrier.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a mismatch between DCS I/O characteristics and field-device signal or electrical requirements is overlooked before purchase
rows.imp_rat: An overlooked I/O-to-field-device mismatch before purchase is a design/procurement error (relevance) usually surfaced during commissioning rather than causing operational harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mature I/O-list and instrument-index practices catch most mismatches, yet electrical or signal-type subtleties still occasionally get overlooked before purchase. I/O-to-field-device signal checks add engineering review time but are a familiar pre-purchase task. Instrumentation engineers routinely perform I/O matching, so the required skill, while specialized, is broadly available. Overlooked mismatches drive expensive signal-conditioning retrofits, but standard I/O lists keep most costs contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of target-device parameters with conflicting approved requirements
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting approved parameter requirements for target devices create configuration ambiguity (relevance moderate) that can cause misconfiguration, though usually caught in review before harm (severity moderate). Below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo tools rarely cross-check parameters against all approved requirement sources, so conflicting values routinely slip through until commissioning exposes them. Reconciling conflicting parameter requirements against approved specs is a slow manual review cycle that stalls preparation until sign-offs converge. Detecting semantic conflicts among approved requirements demands scarce process and controls engineers who understand both the spec intent and device limits. Conflicts caught late in preparation force rework of already-approved documents, but the direct material cost is modest compared to the engineering time burned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a backup that does not match the required production state
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a backup that doesn't match the required production state is a recovery-time decision error (relevance modest) that could restore the wrong process state (severity moderate). Contingency scope; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Backup labeling and metadata are often poor, so choosing a backup that does not match the required state remains a real risk. Picking the wrong backup and discovering it late wastes recovery time, but the error is mismatch. Selecting the correct backup for a specific production state demands experienced judgement about versions and states. Restoring a mismatched state causes rework and scrap rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the saved backup being unreadable when restoration begins
rows.imp_rat: An unreadable backup at restoration time only matters during recovery (relevance modest to routine CFJ) but when it occurs, recovery fails and data loss can be severe (severity moderate). Rare-event scope; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Backup verification exists but many sites never test restores, so unreadable backups still surface at the worst moment. Discovering an unreadable backup only at restore time massively extends recovery, though the error is integrity. Confirming backup readability before it is needed requires disciplined administrators who run test restores. An unreadable backup can force costly rebuilds, but the loss is chiefly reliability of recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for diagnostic access to the affected control environment during active sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for diagnostic access during active production delays troubleshooting (relevance moderate-low); it slows resolution but does not itself break control (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Access procedures are established but inconsistent under production pressure, so diagnostic access timing remains unpredictable. Getting diagnostic access during active sterile production waits on access approvals, escorts and clean-window scheduling, causing real delay. Remote diagnostic and secure-access tooling exists, but coordinating GMP-compliant access still relies on experienced coordinators. Waiting ties up maintenance staff and can extend downstream schedule cost, though direct spend is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to restore the industrial control software to its validated operating state during a scheduled test
rows.imp_rat: Time to restore validated state during a scheduled test is a recovery-duration delay in a planned window (relevance moderate-low), with bounded consequences since it is scheduled (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Image-based restores are repeatable, yet mismatches between the stored image and current environment sometimes force iterative rework. Validated golden images and automated restore scripts can rebuild the software state fairly quickly, but full revalidation checks still stretch the window. Restoring to a validated state reliably needs engineers who know the validation baseline and dependency chain, a moderately scarce competency. Restore uses existing backup media and workstations, so the primary cost is staff time rather than new spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stored backup data are corrupted or unreadable during recovery
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted/unreadable backup data during recovery is a recovery-only concern (relevance modest) but produces significant loss when it happens (severity moderate). Overlaps heavily with other backup items; held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checksums and redundant copies are increasingly standard, mitigating but not eliminating corruption during recovery. Corruption discovered during recovery stalls the whole restoration, but time is not the primary loss. Detecting and handling corrupted backup data needs skilled staff who can validate checksums and integrity. Corruption forces expensive reconstruction, yet the error itself is data integrity.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the stored backup is inaccessible when recovery is required
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible backup when recovery is needed is peripheral to day-to-day control (relevance modest) but causes recovery failure if it occurs (severity moderate). Rare, contingency-scoped; capped at 5.5.
rows.sat_rat: Offsite and cloud replication are mature enough that inaccessibility at recovery time is fairly uncommon where practiced. An inaccessible backup delays recovery indefinitely until an alternative is found. Ensuring accessibility across storage locations relies on administrators managing offsite and online copies. Redundant accessible storage costs money but prevents far larger recovery losses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of operator acknowledgments that are not recorded against the corresponding active alarm
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded operator acknowledgments is largely an audit-trail integrity error (relevance low for the live control job) with moderate compliance consequence; below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: ISA- -aligned alarm managers reliably record acknowledgments against the correct active alarm, though flood conditions and shared-console ambiguity still occasionally misattribute an ack. Modern SCADA/DCS alarm systems timestamp and bind acknowledgments to the active alarm instantly, so there is no meaningful time cost when the standard mechanism works. Operators simply click the acknowledge control on the alarm banner, requiring little skill for the binding itself which the system handles automatically. The acknowledgment-to-alarm binding is a built-in feature of licensed alarm management packages, so it imposes no incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between recorded timestamps and actual process changes or event occurrences
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp mismatch versus actual process changes is an audit/traceability accuracy error (relevance moderate-low); consequences fall on records integrity rather than immediate control (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: PTP and time servers improve accuracy, but buffering, scan latency and unsynchronized edge devices still introduce recurring mismatches between recorded and actual times. Timestamping happens automatically at capture, adding no operator time. Judging whether recorded times match actual process changes requires validation expertise rarely applied continuously. Timestamp mismatch can undermine GMP records and trigger costly data-integrity remediation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which misaligned timestamps obscure the sequence of control events preceding the anomaly
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned timestamps obscuring event sequence hurts post-anomaly diagnosis rather than real-time control (relevance moderate-low), with moderate consequences for root-cause and compliance analysis (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Time synchronization (NTP/PTP) is common but not universal across all devices, so clock skew still obscures true event ordering during anomalies. Reconstructing event sequences during incident analysis is slowed when timestamps from different subsystems disagree. Correctly reordering misaligned events for root-cause analysis takes considerable analyst expertise. Misordered event sequences can prolong investigations and misdirect corrective spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a required device or process area is absent from its expected hierarchy path
rows.imp_rat: A device or area absent from its expected hierarchy path is a navigation/configuration inconvenience (moderate relevance) with generally recoverable, moderate consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structured tag hierarchies are standard and usually complete, but configuration drift and plant modifications occasionally leave items missing from expected paths. Well-built hierarchy navigation lets operators reach an area quickly when the path is correctly configured. Building a complete and correct hierarchy requires skilled engineering during configuration, not operator skill at runtime. Maintaining an accurate hierarchy across plant changes requires ongoing engineering effort and cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of reported values or states that differ from captured operational records
rows.imp_rat: Reported values differing from captured operational records is a data-accuracy/reconciliation defect (relevance moderate-low), affecting trust and records rather than live control (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Historians and live displays usually agree, but buffering, deadband compression and update timing produce recurring small divergences. Comparing reported values against captured records is a manual reconciliation that takes time when discrepancies arise. Spotting divergence between live display and historian requires knowledgeable cross-checking not all staff perform. Discrepancies erode trust in data and can drive costly re-verification of batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of user accounts assigned command permissions beyond authorized responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Users with command permissions beyond their role (relevance) creates security exposure, and severity is moderate since excess authority could enable harmful commands, but it is an access-governance matter peripheral to controlling devices.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based permission models constrain over-provisioning reasonably well, though excess command rights still leak through in practice. Trimming command permissions to authorized responsibilities is a defined but tedious per-account configuration task. Deciding which command permissions are appropriate requires role knowledge that administrators usually have. Over-permissioning costs little to configure but can be costly if it enables an incident.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of account-role assignments that violate segregation-of-duties requirements
rows.imp_rat: Role assignments violating segregation-of-duties (relevance) is a compliance/security concern rather than control functionality, with moderate severity tied to audit and insider-risk exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access templates help, but SoD conflicts still commonly appear because tools rarely enforce these constraints automatically. Reviewing role assignments for segregation-of-duties conflicts adds a governance step to preparation. Recognizing SoD violations requires compliance and access-control expertise that is not universally present. Manual role review is labor-intensive, and violations found in audit are expensive to remediate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protocol or port settings that conflict with control-device and infrastructure requirements
rows.imp_rat: Protocol/port settings conflicting with device requirements is a configuration correctness issue (relevance moderate-low); it can block connectivity but is typically caught and corrected during setup (severity moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts are common because settings are validated late and by hand, so mismatches surface only during commissioning. Verifying protocol and port settings against requirements is a manual checklist activity that consumes preparation time. Reconciling protocol/port requirements across vendors demands detailed knowledge of each device's communication profile. No significant material cost, but rework from conflicts consumes labor budget.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of imported historical process records missing required traceability metadata
rows.imp_rat: Missing traceability metadata on imported historical records is a data-quality concern peripheral to live control (low relevance), with moderate compliance/traceability consequences (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Historical records frequently arrive missing required metadata and import tools seldom enforce completeness, so gaps persist into the migrated dataset. Cleaning and enriching historical records with traceability metadata before import is a slow, largely manual data-prep effort. Mapping legacy fields to required traceability metadata needs data-domain expertise that is often unavailable for old datasets. Metadata remediation consumes labor, and non-compliant imports risk expensive regulatory findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contradictory fault and device-state indications the technician must reconcile during a diagnosis
rows.imp_rat: Contradictory fault/device-state indications add reconciliation burden during diagnosis (relevance modest) with mostly recoverable consequences (severity modest), placing it below the batch-1 tier.
rows.sat_rat: Fault-correlation features exist but are inconsistently deployed, so technicians frequently still face indications that don't agree. Reconciling contradictory fault and device-state indications adds investigative time to every affected diagnosis. Resolving which of several conflicting indications is true is a skill-intensive task with little automated support today. Time spent reconciling contradictions extends downtime and consumes technician hours.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting the wrong sterile fill-finish area, batch, or unit because its identifier is ambiguous
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous identifiers for a sterile fill-finish area/batch/unit (relevance) is a narrow domain-specific selection risk with moderate severity in that context, but peripheral to the general control software job.
rows.sat_rat: Mature ISA-style hierarchical tagging and confirmation prompts substantially reduce wrong-unit selection, though ambiguous legacy tags and copied identifiers still cause occasional errors. Unique tag naming and asset registries let operators resolve an area or unit identifier in seconds once conventions are set. Correct identifier interpretation still leans on operator familiarity with plant-specific naming schemes that vary between sites. Ambiguity-driven mis-selection in sterile fill-finish risks batch loss worth far more than the modest cost of tagging standards.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from initiating the handoff until the recipient obtains control authority
rows.imp_rat: Handoff latency to transfer control authority is a workflow timing error, moderately relevant to continuous control, with modest consequences since control is not lost, only delayed (severity low-moderate).
rows.sat_rat: Explicit control-arbitration schemes make transfers deterministic, though contention or interlock states occasionally stall the handoff. Digital handoff and control-authority transfer in modern HMIs completes in seconds once initiated. Executing a clean handoff requires operators to understand authority-lock semantics that are not always intuitive. Slow handoffs rarely waste material but can idle equipment and staff briefly during shift transitions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intermittent communication faults remain undetected during periodic verification
rows.imp_rat: Undetected intermittent communication faults during periodic verification can allow latent problems to persist (slightly higher severity), but detecting them during periodic checks is a supporting rather than central function of the CFJ (low-moderate relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Standard periodic verification is inherently blind to faults that self-clear between samples, so many intermittent communication problems remain undetected until they cause a visible process disturbance. Periodic verification runs on scheduled scans that only sample link status at intervals, so intermittent dropouts between checks pass silently and consume no visible time until they escalate. Catching transient faults demands experienced technicians who know to inspect error counters and diagnostic logs rather than trust a momentary green status. The tooling to log communication statistics already ships with most control platforms, so the marginal cost is low even if underused.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prior-batch material remaining in the compounding area at readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch material remaining at readiness confirmation is a cross-contamination risk (relevance) that could compromise a sterile batch (severity), held at ceiling by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Line-clearance procedures help but remain manual and inspection-dependent, leaving residual mix-up risk. Line-clearance checks take real time before run start. Detecting prior-batch material relies on operator visual inspection skill. Residual prior material causes mix-ups and loss, partly guarded by clearance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required equipment items with unverified cleaning or sterilization status before run start
rows.imp_rat: Unverified cleaning/sterilization status before run start is a contamination precondition (relevance) that can compromise the batch (severity), held at ceiling by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment status tracking exists but confirmation is often manual, leaving unverified items possible at start. Cleaning-status checks at readiness add setup time. Verifying cleaning/sterilization status relies on operator record review. Unverified equipment risks contamination loss, partly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies between recorded and actual sterile status during recipe execution
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies between recorded and actual sterile status misstate a safety-critical condition (relevance) and could release compromised product (severity), but ranked under batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile status is largely tracked by manual records, so discrepancies with actual status persist. Reconciling recorded sterile status with actual status is a manual, lagging activity. Sterile-status recording depends on operator entry rather than automated sensing. Status discrepancies risk releasing nonsterile product, weakly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material lots evaluated using evidence from a different lot or container
rows.imp_rat: Using evidence from a different lot when evaluating material touches conformance verification (relevance) and could admit wrong material into a compound (severity), but sits below batch-1 items.
rows.sat_rat: Lot-tracking systems reliably tie evidence to lots when scanned, but manual paper records still allow mismatches. Lot-to-evidence linkage via scanning is fast at receipt and use. Barcode lot tracking reduces reliance on operator matching evidence to lots. Using wrong-lot evidence can pass nonconforming material, mostly guarded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required material lots that fail identity, release, or expiry preconditions before compounding
rows.imp_rat: Material lots failing identity/release/expiry preconditions before compounding gates wrong-material use (relevance) and could compromise the batch (severity), ranked under batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated release checks reliably block unreleased lots where ERP linkage is in place, though gaps exist for edge cases. System status checks confirm lot release quickly at dispensing. Automated release verification reduces reliance on operator lookups. Material status checks use existing ERP/MES integration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recipe departures omitted from the compounding batch record during execution
rows.imp_rat: Recipe departures omitted from the batch record during execution hide non-compliance (relevance) and could conceal quality risk (severity), ranked under batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe departures during execution are frequently omitted from the record because they depend on the operator noticing and logging them. Recipe departures are typically documented after the fact, delaying capture. Recognizing and recording a departure during execution demands operator judgement. Capturing departures relies mostly on manual deviation processes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of executing recipe steps from a recipe revision other than the one released for the batch
rows.imp_rat: Executing from an unreleased recipe revision defeats correct recipe execution (relevance) and can yield wrong product (severity), ranked below batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Effective revision control makes execution from a wrong revision rare where the MES governs recipe release. System-controlled recipe version loading is instantaneous at batch start. Automated version control removes reliance on operators to select the right revision. Recipe version management is built into MES platforms.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of executing recipe steps in an order different from the released recipe sequence
rows.imp_rat: Executing steps out of released sequence violates recipe execution (relevance) and can compromise product (severity), but ranked under batch 1, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced released-sequence execution makes out-of-order runs uncommon in configured systems. Sequence enforcement prevents order errors without slowing execution. Interlocked sequencing removes reliance on operators to follow order from memory. Sequence gating is standard recipe configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which actual parameters deviate from the specified limits while executing a recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Actual parameters deviating from specified limits while executing a step is a core enforcement failure (relevance) that can produce non-conforming product (severity), ranked below batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation magnitude is well contained under closed-loop control but larger for manually controlled steps. Control response minimizes the duration parameters spend outside limits. Automated regulation reduces the operator finesse needed to hold setpoints. Leverages existing control instrumentation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material additions associated with the wrong material identity
rows.imp_rat: Additions associated with the wrong material identity corrupt the formulation (relevance) and can harm product safety (severity), ranked below batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Wrong-identity additions are uncommon where scanning enforcement is in place. Barcode matching flags wrong identity at the point of addition. Scan checks remove reliance on operator material recognition. Low incremental cost on existing scanning setup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material additions associated with the wrong material lot
rows.imp_rat: Additions associated with the wrong lot break traceability and correct formulation (relevance) and can compromise the batch (severity), held at ceiling by ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Wrong-lot additions are largely prevented by scan verification, with residual risk on manual substitutions. Lot scanning verifies the correct lot immediately at dispense. Automated lot checks reduce reliance on operator lot tracking. Uses existing scanning and lot master data.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a batch record change lacks traceable authorship, rationale, or chronology
rows.imp_rat: Batch record changes lacking traceable authorship/rationale/chronology violate data-integrity expectations (relevance) and undermine defensibility (severity), ranked below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature electronic audit trails reliably capture authorship, rationale, and chronology for changes, making this among the best-served needs. Audit trails record authorship and time automatically with no added effort. System-enforced audit trails remove any skill burden for capturing rationale and chronology. Traceability gaps are costly in audits, but Part prevent most.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch documentation is associated with the wrong batch or lot
rows.imp_rat: Associating batch documentation with the wrong batch/lot corrupts traceability (relevance) and can misdirect recalls or release (severity), but ranked lower here.
rows.sat_rat: Scanned lot identification in MES makes wrong-batch documentation infrequent where the system is used, so this is well served. Barcode and system-driven lot association is instantaneous, adding no time. Automated batch-ID binding removes reliance on the operator to match records manually. Misassociated documentation is very costly, but automation keeps it rare.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that relevant compounding evidence is lost or altered before investigation review
rows.imp_rat: Loss or alteration of evidence before investigation review harms data integrity (relevance) and can obscure root cause (severity), held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated systems with locked records and backups strongly protect evidence integrity, though peripheral data outside the system remains vulnerable. Data is persisted immediately and continuously, minimizing windows for loss before review. Immutable storage removes reliance on staff to preserve evidence manually. Preserved evidence avoids costly reconstruction and disputed investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of monitored quality indicators evaluated against outdated or incorrect acceptance limits
rows.imp_rat: Evaluating indicators against outdated/incorrect limits erodes enforcement accuracy (relevance) and could pass violations (severity), but ranked below batch 1, at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Limit governance is manual and error-prone, so indicators evaluated against stale limits remain a recurring problem. Limit updates propagate quickly once entered in the system. Maintaining current acceptance limits requires specialist upkeep and change control. Outdated limits cause false accept/reject decisions with associated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable compliance boundaries omitted from evaluation criteria
rows.imp_rat: Omitting applicable compliance boundaries from evaluation criteria weakens enforcement (relevance) and could let violations pass unchecked (severity), but is bounded below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance boundaries are frequently omitted from evaluation criteria because coverage depends on individual regulatory knowledge rather than a standard framework. Enumerating all applicable compliance boundaries is slow regulatory work often abbreviated under time pressure. Identifying every relevant compliance boundary requires specialized regulatory expertise not always available to the influencer. A missed compliance boundary risks costly regulatory findings far exceeding review effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compounding executions with incomplete or alterable records
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or alterable execution records undermine the record-integrity core (relevance) and jeopardize compliance defensibility (severity), but ranked below batch 1.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporary systems produce complete, non-alterable execution records fairly reliably, though incomplete configurations still leave some gaps. Electronic records are captured automatically at execution, so no added time is incurred. Tamper-evident record capture is a mature standard capability requiring configuration rather than rare skill. Complete immutable records reduce costly investigations and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding events lacking traceability across recipe approvals, parameter changes, overrides, and execution records
rows.imp_rat: Missing traceability across approvals, changes, overrides, and execution undermines the record half of the job (relevance) and impairs accountability (severity), held under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established MES/SCADA audit trails link approvals, changes, overrides, and execution fairly reliably, though completeness depends on configuration coverage. Audit-trail capture is automatic in mature systems, adding no time burden once running. Traceability across approvals and changes is a standard feature needing configuration rather than deep skill. Automated traceability reduces the cost of reconstructing batch histories.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that high-risk recipe execution and enforcement functions are omitted from validation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting high-risk execution/enforcement functions from validation scope indirectly affects assurance (relevance) and could hide failures (severity), but is a validation-process issue held low.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based validation methodology is well established, but scope omissions of high-risk functions still recur when risk assessments are shallow. Scoping validation to include all high-risk execution functions takes considerable analysis time that projects often shortcut. Identifying high-risk enforcement functions demands specialized process-risk expertise that is in short supply. Under-scoping saves money short-term while creating large latent compliance liabilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recorded and enforced compounding parameters are incorrectly treated as equivalent
rows.imp_rat: Treating recorded and enforced parameters as equivalent when they differ conflates record and control (relevance) and can mask enforcement gaps (severity), ranked below batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recorded-vs-enforced reconciliation is only partially automated, so equivalence errors still occur. Verifying that recorded and enforced parameters truly match takes deliberate comparison time. Recognizing subtle non-equivalence requires knowledgeable review of both record streams. Wrongly assuming equivalence can pass defective batches at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch records missing critical parameter values or excursions
rows.imp_rat: Batch records missing critical parameter values or excursions gut the record core (relevance) and hide quality risk (severity), ranked below batch 1 at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: EBR mandatory-field and excursion capture make missing critical parameters comparatively rare. Electronic batch records surface missing values quickly through mandatory-field checks. Automated completeness checks reduce reliance on reviewer vigilance to catch gaps. Preventing missing critical values avoids costly incomplete-record rejections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of displayed trend values that misrepresent actual process conditions during the inspection interval
rows.imp_rat: Trend values that misrepresent actual process conditions can mislead operators (moderate severity) and undermine monitoring accuracy (moderate relevance), but during a defined inspection interval the impact is bounded and ranked below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Trend rendering is generally faithful, but deadband, sampling and interpolation settings routinely smooth or distort actual process behavior. Misrepresented trend values are noticed only after inspection, not prevented in the moment. Interpreting whether a trend faithfully represents the process requires understanding of compression and interpolation settings. Trend misrepresentation can mask problems and lead to costly wrong conclusions during review.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that brief process excursions are obscured when viewing the selected interval
rows.imp_rat: Brief process excursions obscured in the selected interval can hide transient events (moderate severity for diagnostics) but is a viewing-resolution issue peripheral to real-time control (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Time-based aggregation and deadband compression routinely hide sub-interval spikes, and standard trend views rarely preserve min/max within the displayed window. Brief excursions are missed at view time and often require zooming or raw-data pulls to recover, costing analysis time. Recognizing that a display interval hides short spikes requires expert awareness of aggregation behavior. Obscured excursions can hide quality-critical events, risking undetected deviations and expensive investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of out-of-scope control points remaining in the operator's filtered view
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-scope control points in the operator's filtered view is a usability/clutter issue tangential to control accuracy (low relevance), causing minor confusion with limited consequences (low-moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Role and area filters generally exclude out-of-scope points well, though stale filter definitions occasionally leave irrelevant control points visible. Filtered and role-based views are quick to apply once configured, saving navigation time. Defining scope-correct filters requires engineering skill, but operators use them with little effort. View filtering is a standard included capability, so it adds little marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of active alarms acknowledged before the operator identifies the fault source and severity
rows.imp_rat: Acknowledging alarms before identifying source/severity is a procedural-quality error (relevance low) with modest consequence, sitting near the lower end of this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard systems allow one-click bulk acknowledgment with no gate requiring source/severity assessment, so this behavioral error remains common and poorly mitigated. Premature acknowledgment is itself fast, but it costs time later when the unassessed fault escalates. Avoiding acknowledging before understanding the fault depends almost entirely on operator discipline and judgement, which the software barely enforces. No extra tooling cost is involved, but the downstream consequences of blind acks can be expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process values or operational events not recorded during use
rows.imp_rat: Failing to record required process values/events degrades the monitor aspect of the job (moderate relevance) and can hurt traceability (severity), but does not stop active control; held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configured logging captures the defined set well, but scan misses, buffer overflows and unconfigured points still leave gaps in required data. Discovering that required values went unrecorded typically happens after the fact during review, delaying remediation. Ensuring complete data capture requires engineering to correctly configure every required tag and event. Missing required records can force batch investigations or rejection at significant cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of logged records containing incorrect process values or event details
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect logged process values undermine monitoring accuracy and post-incident analysis (severity), but logging is peripheral to the real-time control/configure job (moderate relevance); held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated data-logging pipelines are mature, but sensor faults, scaling errors and mislabeled events still seed incorrect records. Correcting logged records with wrong values is a slow, controlled process under change management. Recognizing that a logged value or event detail is wrong requires process knowledge beyond routine operation. Incorrect GMP records can invalidate batch release, imposing large rework or disposal costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of logged data being lost before the required retention period ends
rows.imp_rat: Losing logged data before retention ends carries compliance severity, but data retention is a supporting recordkeeping function rather than the core control/monitor job; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant historians, backups and validated retention policies make premature data loss uncommon, though misconfiguration and storage faults remain residual risks. Automated archiving and backups run in the background without consuming operator time. Retention is largely policy-driven and automated, needing little day-to-day operator skill. Redundant storage and archival for long retention periods add ongoing storage and validation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the renewal entitlement cannot be applied to the deployed control software
rows.imp_rat: A renewal entitlement that cannot be applied (relevance) is a licensing-administration nuisance with low-to-moderate severity, largely disconnected from the control software's core operational job.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor license managers are mature and usually apply cleanly, so entitlement mismatches are relatively uncommon. A failed entitlement application blocks operation until resolved, but the error is applicability. Applying license entitlements correctly needs staff familiar with the vendor's licensing model. A blocked entitlement can idle production, yet cost is secondary to whether it applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controlled devices with identifiers that conflict with the software configuration
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting device identifiers can cause commands to route to the wrong device, which touches the software's configure/control job, but such conflicts are usually caught at commissioning; capped below the more critical batch above.
rows.sat_rat: Naming/addressing conventions and CM tools catch many conflicts, but drift between as-built devices and software configuration still produces recurring mismatches. Configuration management tools can scan for identifier conflicts fairly quickly once a baseline exists. Detecting and reconciling device-identifier conflicts still depends on engineers who understand the address/tag scheme, an error-prone manual task. Conflict checks use existing engineering tools, adding little cost beyond labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active users holding control permissions beyond their approved responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Excess control permissions create a misuse/security severity risk, but access governance is an administrative overlay to the core control function; moderate relevance keeps it at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic access recertification is standard but often stale between cycles, so excess-privilege users persist more often than desired. Role-based access reviews can be run reasonably fast where an IAM system aggregates permissions. Judging whether a user's control permissions exceed approved responsibilities requires role knowledge and consistent review discipline, which lapse in practice. Access reviews rely on existing IAM tooling and periodic audit labor, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that archived operational records fail integrity verification when retrieved
rows.imp_rat: Archived operational records failing integrity verification is a compliance/audit concern (moderate severity) but distant from live control/monitoring (low relevance); ranked in the lower tier.
rows.sat_rat: Hash-verified, validated archival is mature so retrieval integrity failures are infrequent, though legacy or unmanaged archives still occasionally fail. Integrity checks on retrieved records execute automatically and fast when a records system enforces hashing. Verification is largely automated, requiring little specialist skill once the archive is properly set up. Integrity metadata and validated archive systems add modest storage and licensing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of controllers, servers, and network nodes omitted from synchronization verification
rows.imp_rat: Nodes omitted from synchronization verification could allow timing drift, moderately relevant to a networked control system, but consequences are usually detectable and recoverable.
rows.sat_rat: Time-sync verification is a standard checklist item, but incomplete asset registers mean some nodes are still occasionally omitted. Synchronization verification across many nodes is time-consuming when done thoroughly. Knowing which nodes require sync verification relies on complete asset knowledge that experienced staff hold. Verification tooling is mostly built into the DCS, keeping cost modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of retired data-bearing control components leaving custody with recoverable plant or batch information
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable plant/batch data leaving custody on retired components is a real confidentiality/compliance severity concern but peripheral to the DCS's central control function.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete asset inventories and overlooked embedded storage frequently let components leave custody with recoverable information, so this remains a common gap. Tracking and securing every data-bearing component through custody transfer adds inventory and chain-of-custody steps that lengthen disposal. Knowing which DCS components actually store recoverable plant or batch data requires uncommon familiarity with the system's hardware internals. Certified destruction or secure custody handling carries per-asset cost that is often underbudgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of obsolete user accounts, service accounts, credentials, and remote connections omitted from revocation
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete accounts and remote connections left un-revoked poses a security severity risk, but account hygiene is peripheral to the core distributed-control job.
rows.sat_rat: Undocumented service accounts, vendor backdoors, and legacy remote connections are routinely missed, making full revocation an unreliable outcome. Revoking accounts and connections is quick once identified but the discovery sweep across DCS and integrated systems takes time. Enumerating hidden service accounts and remote links in a proprietary DCS requires system administrators with specific knowledge of the installation. Revocation itself is low cost, involving administrative effort rather than spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configuration data, credentials, process records, or proprietary information remain recoverable after sanitization
rows.imp_rat: Residual recoverable credentials/process records after sanitization carries meaningful data-security severity, yet it is an end-of-life administrative concern with low relevance to running the process.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary formats, embedded flash, and undocumented storage locations mean recoverable data after sanitization is a persistent, poorly-verified risk. Thorough sanitization and verification of every storage medium is time-consuming, especially across many distributed nodes. Effective sanitization of proprietary controllers and embedded media demands specialized data-security skills that most decommissioning crews lack. Certified wiping tools, destruction services, and verification add cost that competes with disposal budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of integration failures between upgraded components and retained control-system assets
rows.imp_rat: Integration failures between upgraded and retained control assets can disrupt operation, but this is a moderate-relevance transitional error with recoverable consequences relative to the core control job.
rows.sat_rat: Mixing upgraded and retained proprietary assets frequently surfaces integration failures that testing does not fully anticipate. Integration testing of upgraded components against legacy assets extends project timelines considerably. Bridging new and retained proprietary assets demands rare engineers familiar with both generations of the system. Compatibility layers, gateways, and extended testing add significant integration cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of retaining a candidate without evidence that it satisfies sterile fill-finish control and pharmaceutical compliance requirements
rows.imp_rat: Retaining a candidate without compliance evidence is a procurement/qualification administrative concern, only loosely tied to running control (relevance) with mostly recoverable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendor claims are often accepted with thin evidence and no structured compliance-mapping practice, so unqualified candidates slip through at an uncomfortable rate. Verifying that a candidate DCS has evidence of meeting sterile and pharma compliance takes considerable review time during evaluation. Judging whether vendor evidence truly demonstrates compliance requires experienced GMP and control-system auditors who are in short supply. Retaining a non-compliant candidate imposes large downstream remediation costs, though the vetting activity itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterile fill-finish compliance obligations omitted from the finalized DCS contract
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sterile fill-finish compliance obligations from the contract has regulatory severity, but as a procurement-document error it is moderately relevant to actual control operation.
rows.sat_rat: Contracts frequently omit specific sterile-compliance clauses because there is no standardized obligation register, leaving recurring gaps. Transcribing every compliance obligation into contract language is time-consuming legal-technical work. It takes rare coordination between procurement, quality, and legal to ensure obligations are captured, and that alignment is often weak. A missed contractual obligation can mean the buyer absorbs compliance work later at significant unbudgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of specified control, interlock, alarm, I/O, and performance requirements omitted from factory acceptance testing
rows.imp_rat: Requirements omitted from factory acceptance testing could let defects reach the plant, giving moderate severity and relevance, but the ranking caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability-matrix-driven FAT is a widely deployed practice that catches most requirement coverage gaps, though some interlock or performance items still slip. Preparing FAT protocols that cover all requirements is a well-established but lengthy step before shipment. FAT test authoring is a mature discipline with experienced test engineers generally available. Requirements missed at FAT cost far more to fix in the field, but standard FAT practice curbs most of that exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that software deployment places the DCS outside its approved validated state
rows.imp_rat: Deploying software that leaves the DCS outside its validated state is relevant to a controlled process-control job and severe for compliance, but the ceiling caps it; keeping average at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Mature GMP change-control and version-management practices keep most deployments within validated state, with occasional lapses. Change-control and validation-state verification add procedural steps that slow software deployment. Managing validated-state deployment requires quality and validation specialists familiar with regulated change control. Formal change control and revalidation consume documented effort but are standard budgeted overhead in GMP environments.

rows.source_job: Connect power supply to system nodes
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of system nodes receiving voltage outside their specified limits during energization
rows.imp_rat: Energizing nodes with out-of-spec voltage risks hardware damage (severity), and relates to reliable startup, but is a bounded commissioning event capped by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-energization checks and power-quality verification are mature practice, so out-of-limit voltage at nodes is uncommon and typically caught. Voltage verification at energization is a fast, standard commissioning step that rarely delays work. Power checks follow established electrical procedures within ordinary technician competence. Standard test equipment makes voltage verification inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of documented hardware, network, I/O, logic, and alarm details that differ from the installed DCS configuration
rows.imp_rat: Documentation diverging from installed DCS configuration can mislead maintenance and validation, giving moderate severity and relevance, but held within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: As-built documentation routinely diverges from the installed DCS, making this one of the most persistently under-served needs in this stage. Reconciling as-built documentation against installed configuration is a tedious effort that consistently trails actual work. Accurate documentation reconciliation is diligence-heavy but chronically deprioritized against limited engineering attention. Documentation drift is cheap to ignore short-term but expensive when it causes downstream errors and audit findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of intended control devices unreachable from the computer running the software
rows.imp_rat: Intended control devices unreachable from the computer directly blocks the software's ability to control and monitor them (moderate relevance) with real operational loss (moderate severity), but ranking caps it under 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Mature discovery and diagnostic tools catch most unreachable devices before go-live, though intermittent or misconfigured nodes still slip through. Network scans and ping sweeps quickly reveal unreachable devices during preparation, so discovery is fast. Diagnosing why a device is unreachable (VLANs, addressing, firewall) still leans on experienced network/controls engineers. Reachability tooling is largely built into standard industrial networking stacks with little added spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connection failure caused by incompatible communication interfaces
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible communication interfaces can prevent connection to control devices, touching the core job, but such incompatibilities surface at integration time and are worked around; capped by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Documented compatibility matrices help, but interface mismatches still cause connection failures found only at integration. Checking interface compatibility during preparation is a bounded, front-loaded task. Identifying incompatible physical/protocol interfaces requires engineers who know each device's supported media and profiles. Gateways or converters add cost when incompatibilities are found, but these are catalog items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of device access authorization being rejected during connection setup
rows.imp_rat: Rejected device access authorization during connection setup [delay] touches the core connect-and-control task, but it is a setup-time nuisance that is retried and resolved, capping importance below the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard credential and certificate provisioning works consistently once configured, with occasional rejections from mismatched keys or expired certs. Authorization rejections during connection setup surface quickly and are usually resolved on the spot. Configuring device credentials and certificates requires moderate controls-security know-how. Fixing device authorization is labor-only and low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of configured parameter values that revert after configuration
rows.imp_rat: Parameters reverting after configuration touches the configure function of the CFJ (moderate relevance), but the fault is usually detectable and correctable before operational harm, keeping severity contained per the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern tools with download-verify and checksum comparison catch most reversions, though volatile or uncommitted settings still occasionally revert. Detecting reverted parameters usually requires re-checking after a reload, adding rework time. Understanding which parameters persist versus need explicit commit demands knowledge of the specific software's save model. Reconfiguring reverted values consumes labor but little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of calibration points evaluated against invalid reference values
rows.imp_rat: Calibration points evaluated against invalid reference values degrade measurement accuracy (moderate severity) but this is a configuration/validation task rather than core control operation (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Calibration evaluated against invalid references still happens when reference data is stale or mis-entered, a moderate recurring gap. Loading correct reference values for calibration points is a bounded preparation task. Ensuring reference validity requires metrology knowledge and traceable standards handling. Reference standards cost money but are already part of calibration budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled sterile fill-finish activity disrupted by the control-logic load
rows.imp_rat: Disruption of scheduled sterile fill-finish by control-logic load affects production continuity (moderate relevance) and can cost batch time (moderate severity), yet it is ranked lower and constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Online-edit capabilities help some platforms, but many loads still force downtime affecting scheduled activity. Control-logic loads that disrupt scheduled sterile activity are hard to shorten because loads often require controller stops. Minimizing disruptive load windows needs expert planning of online-edit versus download strategies. Disrupted scheduled sterile activity destroys product and time, a high-cost failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish process states omitted from the dashboards
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical sterile fill-finish states from dashboards affects monitoring visibility (moderate relevance) with potential process-quality consequences (moderate severity), yet remains a dashboard-configuration issue held under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Design reviews against P&IDs and functional specs catch many omissions, yet coverage completeness still depends heavily on reviewer diligence. Auditing dashboards for missing process states is a manageable review task in preparation. Knowing which sterile fill-finish states are critical to display requires deep process and GMP expertise. Omissions caught late can force costly requalification but the check itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of renewed certificates containing identities or permitted uses that do not match their industrial control endpoints
rows.imp_rat: Renewed certificates with mismatched identities/uses can break trusted endpoint communication (severity), but certificate accuracy is a security-support task not central to the control CFJ; held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: OT certificate management is often manual and immature, so identity/usage mismatches on renewed certificates occur too frequently. Automated PKI can issue renewals quickly, but verifying each certificate's identity and permitted uses against endpoints adds manual delay. Matching subject names, SANs and key usages to specific control endpoints demands PKI-literate staff uncommon in plant maintenance teams. Mismatched certificates cause outages and reissuance cycles that cost far more than the certificates themselves.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dependent control nodes and trusted integrations omitted from certificate replacement
rows.imp_rat: Omitting dependent nodes from certificate replacement can silently break trusted integrations (severity), but certificate lifecycle is a supporting security task rather than the core control job (lower relevance); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate inventories are commonly incomplete in OT, so omitted nodes and integrations are a routine cause of post-rotation failures. Enumerating all dependent nodes and trusted integrations for certificate replacement is slow because dependency maps are often incomplete. Complete coverage requires deep architectural knowledge of every trust relationship, expertise that is scarce and frequently undocumented. Missing a node forces emergency rework and unplanned outages, driving costs well above the planned rotation effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of required configuration, batch-history, or audit-trail data lost during deprecated-module decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Loss of configuration/batch-history/audit data during deprecated-module decommissioning [defect] risks compliance and recovery gaps, but it is a retirement-time event apart from the core control job, capping importance at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Data tied to a single retiring module is a common loss point because it lacks a dedicated capture step. Pulling config, batch-history, and audit data out of a specific deprecated module adds unplanned time to decommissioning. Module-specific export knowledge is scarce once the module is deprecated. Targeted extraction effort is small but seldom scheduled.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of archived process and event records being corrupted during transfer or long-term storage
rows.imp_rat: Corruption of archived process/event records during transfer or storage [defect] matters for compliance and traceability but not for live control; the loss is serious yet outside the core operating job, held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checksums and RAID/redundant archives catch most corruption, though long-term bit-rot on neglected media still bites occasionally. Transferring archives to durable media is reasonably quick with modern backup tooling. Standard checksum and verified-copy practices reduce dependence on specialists for transfer integrity. Storage and verified-transfer tools are inexpensive and commonly available.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required configurations, control logic, and historical records omitted from the retirement export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required configurations, control logic, and historical records from the retirement export is a lifecycle-end concern peripheral to the live control-configure-monitor job (relevance low); severity is moderate since lost artifacts can hamper future audits or migrations, but the ranking caps this below the more central live-control needs.
rows.sat_rat: Omissions are routine because there is rarely an authoritative checklist of everything that must be exported. A complete retirement export of configs, control logic, and history is a long, careful operation. Ensuring nothing is omitted requires engineers who know the full application scope. Export tooling exists, so incremental cost is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of required batch, audit, or configuration records lost during removal
rows.imp_rat: Loss of required batch, audit, or configuration records during removal [defect] threatens traceability and compliance but occurs at end-of-life outside the active control job; serious yet peripheral, held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Required records are frequently partially lost because there is no verified completeness check against a retention manifest. Extracting all batch, audit, and configuration records before removal is time-intensive and often rushed. Knowing where every required record lives across the system needs experienced operators. Extraction labor is the main cost and is commonly under-resourced at teardown.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of access credentials remaining valid after disposal
rows.imp_rat: Credentials remaining valid after disposal is a security decommissioning gap only tangential to live device control (low-moderate relevance), though residual access carries some breach risk (moderate severity), bounded by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: IAM offboarding processes catch most user accounts, but hardcoded, shared and service credentials frequently remain valid after disposal. Credential revocation can be executed quickly once accounts are identified. Enumerating every credential, including embedded and service accounts, requires identity-management expertise that is often incomplete for OT systems. Credential cleanup is low material cost, mostly administrative labor.

rows.stk: Control system engineers; IT/OT integration teams; decommissioning project teams
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that connections recorded as disconnected continue exchanging commands or data
rows.imp_rat: Connections recorded as disconnected still exchanging commands is a retirement-state inconsistency slightly closer to control integrity (low-moderate relevance) and could cause unintended commands (moderate severity), but stays under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Records marked disconnected frequently keep exchanging data through forgotten redundant paths, a chronic and dangerous surprise in OT teardown. Confirming that a nominally disconnected link is truly dead requires slow manual traffic checks that are often skipped under schedule pressure. Detecting ghost command paths needs deep protocol and network expertise rarely present on a disposal team. Verification tooling exists but is seldom funded for a system already written off.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sterile product is contaminated during the operational handover
rows.imp_rat: Contamination during operational handover threatens sterile product (severity), and handover is relevant, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping importance.
rows.sat_rat: Mature aseptic transfer SOPs handle most handovers, though residual contamination risk during transition remains a real recurring gap. Handover procedures add coordination time but are scheduled around production. Aseptic handover of sterile product requires trained operators following validated SOPs. Any contamination event during handover risks losing an entire sterile batch, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing an incorrect filtration element in the batching system
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incorrect filtration element can compromise sterile assurance (severity), and filtration is relevant to preparation integrity, but this item sits below the higher batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Part-number control, labeling, and kitting make incorrect filter installation relatively uncommon in disciplined operations. Selecting the correct filter is a quick step when part numbers are documented. Matching filter micron rating and type to the recipe requires modest training and reference documentation. Wrong filters waste product batches, but correct elements themselves are low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that validated recipes, calibration parameters, or audit records are altered during the update
rows.imp_rat: Alteration of validated recipes, calibration, or audit records during an update (item 8) has moderate severity for data integrity, but it is an update-maintenance event peripheral to routine batching. Bounded by the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails, backups, and validated data-integrity controls make unintended alteration of recipes or calibration data rare in mature systems. Backup and verification of records before updates adds modest overhead within the maintenance window. Following data-integrity procedures needs trained personnel but is largely procedural rather than deeply expert. Version control and audit-trail systems are already in place, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the measurement device remains in service beyond its approved calibration interval
rows.imp_rat: Running a measurement device past its calibration interval (delay error) is moderately relevant to batching accuracy, but the ceiling caps it; severity is real since out-of-cal data can compromise batch quality yet is often caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS/calibration-management due-date tracking keeps most devices within interval, though occasional overdue instruments slip through. Automated scheduling minimizes delay in pulling overdue devices from service. Following recall schedules is procedural and requires little judgement. The recall process itself is inexpensive relative to the risk of out-of-cal operation.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibration records assigned to the wrong measurement device or reference standard
rows.imp_rat: Calibration records assigned to the wrong device (ambiguity) is a records-integrity error with limited centrality to physical batching, though misattribution could allow a bad instrument to run and affect a batch.
rows.sat_rat: Asset tagging and barcode-linked records make wrong-device record assignment uncommon in electronic systems, though manual entry still errs. Record entry and linking is quick within digital calibration systems. Assigning records correctly is a low-skill data-entry task aided by scanned IDs.
