Misassignment cost is mostly rework and audit findings rather than material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect wear, leakage, or lubricant degradation during mechanical inspection
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect wear, leakage, or lubricant degradation during inspection can lead to failures, but as a preventive-maintenance detection gap it is indirect to the batching job and held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual/tactile inspection is inconsistent and misses early-stage degradation, making undetected wear a common outcome. Thorough inspection takes time and is often compressed within tight maintenance windows. Detecting subtle wear, incipient leakage, or lubricant degradation depends heavily on inspector experience and judgement. Missed early wear leads to unplanned failures that are far costlier than the inspection itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of existing leaks, corrosion, deterioration, and component wear not detected during the inspection
rows.imp_rat: Missing existing leaks, corrosion, or wear during inspection risks later breakdowns; severity is moderate but relevance to the core batching act is secondary and bounded by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of existing defects routinely goes undetected because manual inspection coverage and consistency are inherently limited. Comprehensive inspection of all surfaces is time-consuming and often abbreviated. Reliably spotting all corrosion, deterioration, and wear across a complex system demands high vigilance and expertise that varies by inspector. Undetected deterioration escalates into larger failures, wasting far more than the inspection cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a sealing component incompatible with the process fluid, operating conditions, or hygienic requirements
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incompatible sealing component can cause failure or contamination, which is fairly severe, but as an installation/materials decision it is upstream of and less central than core batching execution.
rows.sat_rat: BOM/spare-part specifications and compatibility charts prevent most mismatches, yet substitution errors still occur under time pressure. Verifying seal material compatibility adds lookup time but is a discrete pre-task step. Correct seal selection requires materials/process knowledge, though reference charts reduce the expertise burden. Wrong seals cause failures and contamination, but correct parts are inexpensive to specify upfront.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of environmental contaminants entering sterile fluid paths during extended storage
rows.imp_rat: Environmental contaminants entering sterile paths during extended storage has real severity for sterility, but this storage-phase error is less central to the active batching job and is capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard sealing practice reduces ingress but extended storage of decommissioned systems still sees contaminant entry when seals degrade unmonitored. Capping and sealing fluid paths for storage is quick with standard closures. Proper sealing and desiccant use is routine technician work needing modest skill. Protective closures and controlled storage add modest cost that is usually accepted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of designated liquid released from circuit connections during priming
rows.imp_rat: Designated liquid released from circuit connections during priming is a minor waste and housekeeping issue largely peripheral to completing the batch; consequences are small volume loss and cleanup, easily absorbed.
rows.sat_rat: Clamped and welded sanitary joints reliably contain priming release, with rare seal failures remaining. Minor releases at connections during priming cause small cleanup delays. Making tight aseptic connections is routine trained work that limits release. Leak-tight sanitary connectors contain most designated-liquid release, limiting product waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a contaminated utility supply entering the batching system after hook-up
rows.imp_rat: A contaminated utility supply entering after hook-up carries real sterility severity and is relevant to system integrity, but the ranking places this item below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Utility qualification and point-of-use filters help, but post-hookup contamination still occurs and is detected inconsistently, leaving this only partially served. Confirming utility supply integrity after hook-up requires sampling or checks that take time. Judging utility cleanliness needs trained utilities/quality personnel. A contaminated utility entering the system can spoil batches and force cleaning, a substantial cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination entering the product-contact path during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Contamination entering the product-contact path during validation is serious for product safety, yet as a validation-batch-specific event the ranking holds its combined importance at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic design, cleaning validation and environmental monitoring provide solid safeguards, but contamination risk during validation is never fully eliminated, giving acceptable coverage. Confirming contamination control in the product-contact path during validation requires sampling that takes time. Assessing product-contact contamination needs microbiological and aseptic-process expertise. Contamination in the validation batch scraps the batch and delays qualification at high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical batch parameters outside approved limits during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Critical batch parameters outside approved limits during a validation batch harm compliance, but this is a validation-phase concern rather than routine production, limiting relevance to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Validation protocols with automated data capture and alarms make parameter monitoring fairly robust, so this is well served. Monitoring and confirming batch parameters during the validation batch takes dedicated time. Interpreting whether parameters stay within approved limits requires validation and process expertise. Out-of-limit parameters fail the validation batch, wasting materials and repeat runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning and reset exercises completed with contamination hazards
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning and reset completed with contamination hazards has meaningful severity for downstream batches and is relevant to preparation readiness, yet it is capped below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: CIP procedures and cleaning validation are established, but residual contamination hazards during resets still recur, giving acceptable but imperfect coverage. Completing and verifying cleaning/reset exercises without hazards is time-intensive. Proper cleaning execution and hazard recognition depend on trained operators following validated procedures. Cleaning failures waste chemicals, water, and force re-cleans and lost batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process piping runs connected to incorrect system or plant ports
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect piping runs to wrong plant ports risk misrouting, but this is largely a one-time installation/setup concern rather than a recurring batch-execution failure, keeping relevance modest.
rows.sat_rat: Tagging, line-check procedures and mechanical keying give solid but not foolproof protection against wrong-port connections. Confirming piping runs match the correct system/plant ports adds verification time. Correct routing relies on personnel interpreting drawings and tags accurately. Wrong port connections can cause cross-contamination and costly batch loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of completed piping connections containing unintended crevices or stagnant pockets
rows.imp_rat: Crevices or stagnant pockets in piping connections foster contamination (severity) and are relevant to sterile integrity, but the ranking caps this item below the higher-priority batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary fittings and design standards reduce crevices, but manual assembly still introduces them, leaving recurring gaps. Inspecting each connection for crevices or stagnant pockets slows commissioning. Identifying crevices and stagnant pockets needs hygienic-design expertise during assembly. Crevices harbor contamination that leads to failed batches and costly remediation.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps omitted or placed out of order in the configured sequence
rows.imp_rat: Recipe steps omitted or out of order in the configured sequence can produce an incorrect batch, but as a pre-execution configuration error it ranks under the prior batch and is capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence entry is typically manual with limited automated completeness checks, so omissions and ordering errors recur, leaving the need only partially served. Sequencing steps and re-verifying order by hand is a slow, iterative task. Getting the sequence right and complete leans on a process specialist who knows the intended procedure. Out-of-order or missing steps can ruin batches and consume rework resources.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of configured temperature, mixing, or ingredient-delivery setpoints outside validated operating limits
rows.imp_rat: Setpoints outside validated limits directly affect batch quality (severity) and are relevant to correct preparation, but this configuration error is capped below the higher-priority batch.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe systems increasingly support range checks against configured limits, so many out-of-range setpoints are caught, giving acceptable-to-good coverage. Checking each setpoint against validated limits adds time but is a defined task. Knowing the validated operating limits requires process engineering knowledge held by specialists. Out-of-limit setpoints can waste product and energy or damage equipment.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of configuring an obsolete recipe revision for production use
rows.imp_rat: Configuring an obsolete recipe revision is moderately relevant to producing a correct batch, and severity is meaningful since wrong formulation wastes product, but ranking caps this below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Version control exists in most electronic systems but manual selection still lets obsolete revisions slip in, so the need is only acceptably served. Verifying a revision is current takes a lookup against the recipe register, adding modest time. Distinguishing an obsolete revision requires disciplined change-control awareness by the configurer. Producing with an obsolete recipe can scrap an entire batch, a significant avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of users blocked from required monitoring, editing, or command tasks by insufficient permissions
rows.imp_rat: Users blocked by insufficient permissions from monitoring/editing/command tasks directly impedes performing the CFJ (moderate relevance) and can stall needed operator actions (moderate severity), but the ranking places it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Role templates and RBAC frameworks handle most cases reliably, but edge-case tasks still surface unanticipated permission gaps. Permission problems are often found only when a user is blocked, creating stop-and-fix delays. Configuring role-based access correctly requires administrator familiarity with the software's security model. Fixing permissions is labor-only and generally cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of user accounts associated with the wrong individuals
rows.imp_rat: Accounts associated with the wrong individuals (relevance) is primarily an identity/audit accuracy issue with modest severity, largely peripheral to executing control functions.
rows.sat_rat: Central identity/directory integration makes wrong-individual account associations uncommon where such systems are in place. Associating accounts with the correct individuals is quick when tied to directory services during preparation. Account provisioning is routine IT work needing only standard administrative skill. Directory integration makes correct identity assignment low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between simulated process and device behavior and the target sterile fill-finish environment
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between simulated and real device behavior affects validation fidelity (relevance modest, it's pre-production) and can let latent errors reach the live sterile environment (severity moderate). Peripheral to live control; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Simulations routinely diverge from real sterile fill-finish behavior because edge conditions and equipment quirks are hard to capture, leaving this need poorly served. Building and tuning a simulation to mirror a sterile fill-finish line is a lengthy iterative effort with no shortcut in typical practice. Faithful process/device modeling of a GMP sterile environment requires rare combined expertise in the physical process, controls, and simulation tooling. High-fidelity simulation environments and validation effort are expensive, and inaccurate models cause costly downstream commissioning surprises.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of control or monitoring unavailability caused by the installation
rows.imp_rat: Control/monitoring unavailability caused by installation is central to the CFJ when it happens (moderate relevance) with meaningful downtime consequences (moderate severity), but it is transient and installation-bound, so kept under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Redundant and hot-cutover practices exist but unavailability during installation is still a routine occurrence. Installation-caused control/monitoring unavailability is minimized via phased cutover but rarely eliminated during preparation. Sequencing installs to preserve control coverage takes experienced commissioning planning. Unavailability windows carry production-loss cost that motivates redundant staging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of communication interruptions to active sterile fill-finish controls during network configuration
rows.imp_rat: Communication interruptions to active sterile fill-finish controls during network configuration touch the core monitoring/control job and can spoil sterile batches (higher severity), but the ranking places it below batch 2, so both proxies are held down to respect the 3.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Live-system network changes remain risky and interruptions to active fill-finish controls are inadequately prevented today. Any communication interruption to active sterile fill-finish controls during network config is critical and current staging practices only partly bound it. Executing network changes without disrupting live aseptic controls requires rare, highly trained engineers. A disrupted sterile batch is extremely expensive, raising the stakes of any interruption.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of control-device or infrastructure endpoints assigned conflicting IP addresses
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting IP addresses break endpoint connectivity affecting control/monitor functions, yet these are typically detected quickly during network setup with limited lasting consequence; capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Duplicate-IP conflicts still occur regularly when address plans are maintained manually across teams and vendor-preset devices. IP planning spreadsheets and DHCP reservations make address assignment reasonably quick when disciplined. Basic IP management is a routine networking skill, though large multi-vendor plants still need careful coordination. Address-management tooling is inexpensive or free, so cost is a minor factor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required communication paths between the industrial control software and connected endpoints that lack end-to-end connectivity after configuration
rows.imp_rat: Required communication paths lacking end-to-end connectivity after configuration directly impede the software's ability to reach endpoints (moderate relevance) with real control-loss consequences (moderate severity), but held to the ceiling by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Point-to-point testing is standard but full coverage of every required path is often incomplete, leaving some paths unverified after configuration. End-to-end connectivity checks after configuration are increasingly automated by network scan tools, saving time. Interpreting connectivity-test results across all required paths still needs networking and protocol skill. Test tooling is inexpensive relative to the cost of missed connectivity found at commissioning.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm thresholds
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of technician delay caused by missing approved safety requirements
rows.imp_rat: Technician delay from missing approved safety requirements is peripheral to the software's runtime control job (moderate relevance), though a safety-requirement gap can have real consequences (moderate severity). Capped per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or unapproved safety requirements at the point of work is a common, poorly-mitigated stall. Technicians frequently idle waiting for approved safety requirements because sign-off pipelines are slow and serial. The delay stems from approval bottlenecks and expert availability rather than technician skill. Idle skilled-labor time during waits is a direct and visible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of switching between normal and fallback behavior during intermittent communication
rows.imp_rat: Frequent switching between normal and fallback behavior during intermittent communication does affect control stability, but this instability is an edge condition rather than the central job (moderate-low relevance); severity is meaningful because oscillation can disturb the process, yet the ranking places it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Redundancy and hold-timer features exist, but intermittent comms still cause flapping between normal and fallback modes that is hard to fully eliminate. Fallback switching itself is fast, but tuning to reduce chatter takes iterative testing time. Setting debounce and failover thresholds well requires experienced network and controls tuning judgement. Excess switching wastes little money but instability risks costly process disruption.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical alarms, interlocks, or batch-status information are obscured by the customized layout
rows.imp_rat: Customized layouts obscuring critical alarms/interlocks/batch-status touches the monitoring role of the CFJ (moderate relevance) and could delay operator response (moderate severity), but the ranking caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-management standards and templates help, but customization freedom means critical information can still be obscured without disciplined review. Layout review to confirm alarms are visible is a quick visual check during preparation. Designing layouts that keep critical alarms and interlocks prominent requires HMI design expertise and standards knowledge. Poor layouts risk expensive incidents but the design effort itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required checks blocked by unavailable process conditions or connected control devices
rows.imp_rat: Required checks blocked by unavailable process conditions or devices delays verification workflows (moderate severity) but is a procedural dependency rather than core control (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Simulation and staged commissioning cover some cases, but many checks genuinely cannot proceed without live process states, leaving real gaps. Waiting for the right process conditions or connected devices to run checks introduces unavoidable delays during preparation. Devising valid workarounds or simulation stand-ins for absent conditions requires seasoned commissioning judgement. Blocked checks often force costly return visits or idle staff time waiting for plant conditions.

rows.source_job: Configure data logging and historian settings
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of process-data gaps caused by historian connection interruptions
rows.imp_rat: Process-data gaps from historian connection interruptions affect historical record completeness rather than live control (lower relevance) with modest severity since real-time control continues; held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Modern historians buffer during outages and backfill data, so process-data gaps are well mitigated though not fully eliminated. Historian reconnect and store-and-forward mechanisms recover gaps quickly once configured. Configuring buffering and reconnection needs moderate historian-specific knowledge. Store-and-forward buffering is a standard licensed feature with modest cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unplanned control-device disconnection while redundancy settings are configured
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned control-device disconnection while configuring redundancy is a transient setup-phase risk (low relevance to steady-state control); severity is notable since a disconnect can briefly interrupt supervision, but it remains capped below 3.5 per the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Hot-standby and bumpless-transfer features shorten outages where supported, but many setups still require brief disconnects. Configuring redundancy often forces device disconnection windows that directly extend downtime. Minimizing the disconnect window requires expert sequencing of redundant path cutover. Unplanned disconnection during redundancy setup can idle equipment at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of fallback communication paths with connection parameters rejected by their assigned control devices
rows.imp_rat: Fallback communication paths rejected by control devices weaken backup connectivity (moderate severity) but fallback is a secondary layer relative to the primary control job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Fallback paths are commonly under-tested in preparation, so parameter rejections appear only when failover is exercised. Verifying fallback path connection parameters is a straightforward but often deferred check. Getting fallback parameters accepted by each device needs device-specific communication expertise. Little direct cost beyond the labor to test and correct parameters.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of control devices whose redundancy settings conflict with the control software configuration
rows.imp_rat: Redundancy settings that conflict with the control software configuration can undermine failover (moderate severity) and touch reliable device control (moderate relevance), but ranked below batch 2 and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Redundancy-setting conflicts surface fairly often because device and software configs are prepared by different teams or tools. Reconciling device redundancy settings with software config is a checklist step done during preparation. Matching redundancy parameters across device and software layers requires specialized platform expertise. No material cost, but conflicts drive rework labor.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of required sequence execution events missing from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Missing sequence execution events from batch records affects regulatory batch traceability (severity) but is a logging/reporting concern peripheral to real-time control; capped.
rows.sat_rat: Batch execution engines (S88/ISA- log phase transitions reliably, but transient steps and exception handling can still omit required events. Gaps in sequence-execution logging surface during batch record review rather than in real time. Confirming that all sequence steps were logged requires familiarity with the batch phase model. Missing sequence events can render a batch record incomplete and unreleasable, driving costly rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the technician waits for a mode change because required process conditions or control authority are unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Technician waiting for a mode change due to unresolved conditions is largely a productivity/delay nuisance (relevance moderate-low), with limited direct harm to the control job (severity low).
rows.sat_rat: Interlock and permissive logic reliably prevent unsafe mode changes, but visibility into what is blocking the change is often poor, extending the wait. Waiting for permissives and process conditions to clear before a mode change is an inherent, often multi-minute delay. Operators must interpret which unresolved condition blocks the mode change, requiring good process understanding. Idle waiting for mode changes ties up equipment and personnel without direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a controlled equipment state change and its appearance on the dashboard
rows.imp_rat: Dashboard lag showing an equipment state change is a monitoring-freshness delay (relevance moderate-low); short lags rarely cause serious harm (severity low), well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deterministic polling and event-driven updates make near-real-time dashboard reflection the norm, with lag mainly under abnormal network or CPU load. Modern SCADA scan and update rates push equipment state changes to the dashboard within a second or less in normal load. No special skill is needed to see a state update once the system is configured for the polling rate. Update infrastructure is already in place, so faster dashboard reflection adds little marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the operator misinterpreting equipment conditions from graphical cues
rows.imp_rat: Operator misinterpreting equipment conditions from graphical cues [ambiguity] can affect decisions central to monitoring, but its consequences are usually caught and the ceiling caps it; both proxies kept modest.
rows.sat_rat: High-performance HMI standards (ISA- improve consistency where adopted, but many installed systems retain ambiguous graphics that produce recurring misreads. Misreading a graphical cue does not primarily cost time; the harm is the wrong interpretation itself. Correctly interpreting color, shape and animation cues still leans heavily on operator training and tacit familiarity, and inconsistent legacy graphics keep this dependence high. Misinterpretation can lead to costly wrong actions, but the cost is downstream rather than intrinsic.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of altering a critical batch parameter while testing the suspected fault
rows.imp_rat: Altering a critical batch parameter while testing a fault (instability) could spoil the batch, giving meaningful severity, but it is a contained troubleshooting-context risk rather than central to normal batching.
rows.sat_rat: Access controls, permissives and parameter locks reduce accidental changes, but troubleshooting often requires overrides that reopen the risk. Careful testing that avoids disturbing critical parameters slows troubleshooting. Avoiding accidental changes to a critical parameter while probing a fault demands disciplined, knowledgeable technicians. An inadvertent parameter change can ruin the batch in progress, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches confirmed at endpoint while defined criteria remain unmet
rows.imp_rat: Confirming batches at endpoint while criteria remain unmet (item 2) directly touches the release decision, which is fairly central, but the severity here is moderated since such false confirmations are usually caught downstream. Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-enforced endpoint criteria reduce premature confirmation, yet partially manual endpoint sign-offs still let some batches through with unmet criteria. Automated endpoint checks confirm criteria without added review time. Configured endpoint logic reduces operator judgement about whether all criteria are met. Prematurely confirmed batches that later fail are expensive, but interlocked endpoints keep this infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing the prepared batch to the wrong intermediate vessel
rows.imp_rat: Routing a prepared batch to the wrong intermediate vessel (item 11) can cause mix-ups, giving moderate severity, but it is a handling/routing step of moderate centrality. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked transfer paths, tag-based valve verification and recipe-driven routing make correct-vessel delivery a well-established, mostly dependable practice with occasional gaps on manual overrides. Automated routing via configured recipes and interlocks executes vessel selection quickly once set up, adding little delay. Modern batch control systems with S88-style phase logic and valve interlocks reduce reliance on operator judgement for correct routing. A misrouted batch can scrap costly product, but interlocks and confirmation prompts make such events relatively uncommon in automated plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which critical process conditions deviate from approved limits during manual control
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of critical conditions during manual control (item 17) risks off-spec product, but manual-mode operation is a fallback scenario of moderate relevance. Bounded by the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated PID control in the loop, deviations under manual control are frequent and inconsistent, leaving this need poorly served. Manual control keeps the batch running but operator reaction time to correct drifting conditions is slow relative to automated loops. Manual control of critical process conditions leans entirely on scarce, attentive, well-trained operators, making it highly skill-dependent and error-prone. Manual excursions beyond approved limits routinely cause off-spec material and rework, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required pump, valve, or agitator actions omitted or performed out of sequence during the batch step
rows.imp_rat: Omitting or misordering pump, valve, or agitator actions during a batch step directly affects batch correctness, but the ranking caps it below the prior batch's items.
rows.sat_rat: S88 phase logic and equipment interlocks make correctly sequenced actuation a mature, widely deployed practice, with residual risk mostly in manual or degraded modes. Automated phase sequencing executes pump, valve and agitator actions in the right order without slowing the batch. Recipe-driven sequence control removes the need for operators to remember or manually order each actuation, sharply lowering skill dependence. Omitted or out-of-order actions can ruin a batch, but sequence interlocks make such errors uncommon in automated systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of programmed ingredient-addition steps skipped during a batch run
rows.imp_rat: Skipping programmed ingredient-addition steps degrades batch quality with moderate severity, yet the combined ordering places it beneath higher-priority errors, holding it under 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-managed batch execution with step confirmation and electronic records makes skipped ingredient additions an uncommon, well-controlled failure in automated plants. Programmed addition steps execute automatically in sequence, so no time is lost to operators tracking each step. Automated recipe execution enforces every ingredient-addition step, greatly reducing reliance on operator vigilance to avoid skips. A skipped addition can scrap a batch, but recipe enforcement and step confirmation make skipped steps rare, limiting cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a verification sample changes composition through contamination before analysis
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of a verification sample before analysis (item 1) undermines batch confirmation, but sampling is a peripheral verification step relative to the core batching job; consequences are moderate since a bad sample typically triggers a re-test rather than a released defective batch. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sample containers, chain-of-custody and prompt analysis keep pre-analysis composition change fairly uncommon, though handling and hold-time lapses still occur. Proper sample containers and prompt transfer to the lab add little time. Correct aseptic sample handling requires basic trained technique rather than scarce expertise. Contaminated verification samples trigger costly retesting but such events are individually infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incoming ingredient temperature variation causes degradation before batching is complete
rows.imp_rat: Incoming ingredient temperature variation causing degradation before batching completes affects product quality moderately, but as an inbound-material condition it is somewhat tangential and ranking-capped.
rows.sat_rat: Jacketed temperature control and ingredient pre-conditioning are standard, but variation in incoming feed temperature still causes occasional degradation, leaving recurring gaps. Temperature-driven degradation is a race against batching duration, and status-quo control keeps most batches within acceptable time windows. Managing incoming ingredient temperature variation needs some operator attention to jacketed control and staging, a moderate skill load. Temperature-induced degradation spoils material, but pre-conditioning and jacketed vessels prevent most losses, keeping cost impact moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between the sample composition and the liquid at the designated sampling point
rows.imp_rat: A sample not matching the liquid at the sampling point (defect) affects quality-measurement fidelity and is moderately relevant, with severity bounded since it distorts test data rather than the batch itself.
rows.sat_rat: Well-placed sample points and good mixing yield representative samples in most cases, but stratification and dead legs still bias some draws. Drawing a representative sample and confirming it takes modest time. Proper sampling technique to get a representative draw requires trained personnel. Non-representative samples cause rework and material loss, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the active batch cycle continues after the operator issues the pause command
rows.imp_rat: A batch cycle continuing after a pause command is a control-responsiveness fault with safety implications, but relative to core batching it ranks below the prior batch and stays under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch control systems reliably honor pause/hold commands as a standard feature, so the cycle continuing past a pause is an uncommon failure. Pause commands act on the control system essentially immediately, so lag between command and cessation is negligible. The pause function is built into the control interface and requires no special skill to invoke correctly. A cycle continuing after a pause command could cause overfill or spoilage, but such command-execution failures are rare with tested control logic.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pausing the batch cycle leaves the system outside safe pressure or temperature limits
rows.imp_rat: Pausing the batch leaving the system outside safe pressure or temperature limits carries genuine safety severity, but as a pause-state edge case it is bounded by the batch-2 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems have safe-hold logic, but not all pause points leave pressure and temperature within safe limits, so gaps remain for non-ideal pause moments. Reaching a safe held state after pause depends on how fast pressures and temperatures settle, which control logic manages reasonably quickly. Safe-state pausing is mostly designed into hold logic, but complex batches may require operator judgement to confirm safe conditions. An unsafe pause state risks equipment or batch damage, a real but infrequent cost given typical safe-hold design.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of resuming the interrupted batch from an incorrectly identified system state
rows.imp_rat: Resuming from an incorrectly identified system state (ambiguity) could produce an off-spec restart, giving moderate severity, but it applies only to interruption recovery and is bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch state tracking and hold/restart logic help, but incomplete state capture after an interruption still leads to resuming from a misidentified condition. Confirming the correct state before resuming adds restart delay. Judging the true system state before resuming an interrupted batch is a high-stakes skill-dependent decision. Resuming from a wrong state can spoil the batch, a large cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from approved batch-condition limits during the interruption
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from batch-condition limits during an interruption (item 16) can degrade quality, giving moderate severity, but interruption handling is an edge condition rather than the core batching flow. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Few systems have robust automated hold-state control for unplanned interruptions, so deviation from batch-condition limits during an interruption is a poorly served, recurring pain. During an interruption, keeping conditions within limits depends on how fast operators or systems react, and delays in response commonly let parameters drift. Holding a batch within approved limits mid-interruption demands experienced operators making judgement calls, so skill dependence is high. Deviations during interruptions frequently force reprocessing or scrapping, creating recurring material and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the approved interruption hold limit is exceeded before batch resumption
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the approved interruption hold limit before batch resumption can compromise product integrity, but as a delay/hold-timing control it is peripheral to the core batching job and capped by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Hold timers and resumption procedures exist but are inconsistently enforced, so overruns before resumption still occur regularly. The error is interruption duration exceeding the approved hold limit before resumption, and restart delays are common and hard to bound. Timely, correct resumption needs experienced operators to safely restart from a held state, adding skill dependence. Interruptions that overrun the hold window force disposition of the batch, a recurring material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that measurement faults are mistaken for actual batch conditions
rows.imp_rat: Mistaking measurement faults for actual batch conditions (item 4) can trigger wrong actions, giving it moderate severity, but it is a measurement-interpretation issue peripheral to the core batching job. Kept at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Sensor diagnostics and redundancy help flag faults, but single-sensor readings are still routinely mistaken for actual batch conditions. Diagnosing whether an alarm is a sensor fault or a real condition can consume investigation time. Distinguishing instrument faults from true batch deviations relies on experienced troubleshooting. Reacting to false faults or ignoring real ones drives unnecessary rework or spoilage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between onset of a critical batch anomaly and operator recognition
rows.imp_rat: Delay in operator recognition of a critical anomaly can let problems escalate, giving some severity, but the ranking caps the combined importance at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems and trend displays help, but nuisance alarms and screen clutter mean critical events are still sometimes noticed slowly. Time-to-recognition of a critical anomaly depends heavily on alarm configuration and operator attention, and is often longer than it should be. Distinguishing a genuine critical anomaly from noise demands experienced operators, especially in busy periods. Late recognition can escalate to a scrapped batch, a substantial cost when it occurs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical alarm is obscured by simultaneous noncritical alarms
rows.imp_rat: A critical alarm obscured by noncritical alarms can delay urgent response, adding severity, but alarm management is a supporting concern kept below 3.5 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization and prioritization per ISA- and help, but many plants still run poorly rationalized systems where critical alarms get buried. Alarm flooding delays the moment an operator locates the one critical alarm among many. It takes trained judgement to triage a cascade of alarms and pick out the critical one under pressure. A missed critical alarm can cost an entire batch, but rationalization projects are cheap to specify.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the finished batch is transferred to the wrong downstream destination
rows.imp_rat: Transferring the finished batch to the wrong downstream destination (item 12) risks cross-contamination and moderate consequences, yet remains a downstream-routing concern relative to the core batching job. Held to the batch limit.
rows.sat_rat: Path verification, destination interlocks and electronic batch records make correct downstream transfer a mature practice, with residual risk mainly during manual reroutes or shared header lines. Automated transfer sequencing routes finished batches to the correct destination without meaningful added delay once paths are validated. Destination logic in the batch engine offloads the decision from the operator, lowering skill dependence for correct dispatch. Sending finished product to the wrong tank risks cross-contamination and loss of a full high-value batch, though such failures are infrequent under interlocked control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scaled batch quantity falls outside the system's validated operating range
rows.imp_rat: A scaled batch falling outside the validated operating range (instability) is fairly relevant to reliable batching and could cause off-spec product, but the ceiling caps it and validation limits are usually checked before scale-up.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management with configured min/max charge limits catches most out-of-range scaling, but manual scale-ups and undocumented ranges still allow violations. Validated-range checks are near-instant when embedded in recipe management. Recognizing that a scaled quantity exceeds validated limits requires process and validation knowledge, especially outside automated recipe systems. A batch scaled outside the validated range risks scrap and rework, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scaled batch exceeds validated sterile hold limits before filling
rows.imp_rat: Scaled batch exceeding validated sterile hold limits before filling risks contamination, which carries serious consequences, but relative to the batching CFJ it is a downstream hold-time concern ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile hold-time excursions before filling remain a well-known, poorly buffered failure mode in aseptic operations despite validated limits, so the need is only partly served. The sterile hold clock is tight and any filling-line delay eats into it, making elapsed time a constant pressure. Managing scaled batches within validated sterile hold limits requires trained aseptic-process staff coordinating tightly, a high skill demand. Exceeding sterile hold limits forces rejection of an entire scaled, high-value sterile batch, an expensive and recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile conditions while troubleshooting the batching fault
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile conditions while troubleshooting (contamination-risk) carries high severity because contamination can ruin a batch, but the ceiling caps it and this occurs only during fault handling, limiting relevance to the routine job.
rows.sat_rat: Closed systems, SIP and aseptic SOPs protect sterility, but any physical troubleshooting intervention still carries real contamination risk. Maintaining sterility during troubleshooting adds procedural steps and time. Preserving asepsis while intervening requires trained aseptic-technique discipline. A compromised sterile batch is a total loss plus potential CIP/SIP recovery cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician misidentifies the underlying cause of the batching fault
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the root cause of a batching fault (defect) has some relevance to restoring correct operation, with severity moderate since a wrong diagnosis prolongs downtime but is usually revisited.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics, historians and alarm logs help, but root-cause identification remains inconsistent and expertise-bound. Fault diagnosis can drag on when the cause is not obvious, extending downtime. Correctly identifying the underlying cause of a batching fault depends heavily on scarce, experienced troubleshooters. Misdiagnosis leads to wasted parts, repeat batches and prolonged downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of connecting an inlet, outlet, or recirculation line to the incorrect batching-system port
rows.imp_rat: Misconnecting an inlet, outlet, or recirculation line to the wrong port can cross-contaminate or misroute product, moderately central to batching integrity, but the ranking caps it below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling, keyed/unique fittings and line lists reduce misconnection meaningfully, so this is acceptably-to-well served though human error persists. Verifying each line goes to the correct port adds check time during hook-up. Correct connection depends on operators reading P&IDs and labels correctly. Misconnected lines can cross-contaminate or misroute product, wasting batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of imported formulation quantities altered by incorrect unit conversion or decimal precision handling
rows.imp_rat: Unit-conversion or decimal errors in imported formulation quantities (item 6) can distort a batch, giving moderate severity, but this is an import/data-entry edge case rather than a core batching function. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Automated recipe imports with unit enforcement help, but inconsistent formats and manual overrides leave conversion errors a recurring risk. Conversion or precision errors surface as delays only when reconciliation catches them. Correct unit conversion and decimal handling during import still relies on knowledgeable configuration and checking. Import validation is cheap but a mishandled conversion can ruin an expensive batch.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pressure, temperature, or flow limits exceed the equipment's configured safe envelope
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding pressure, temperature, or flow safe envelopes can damage equipment or endanger operators, giving meaningful severity, but ranking forces the mean to stay at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Configured interlocks, relief devices and alarm limits are mature safeguards, so exceeding the safe envelope is comparatively well prevented. Verifying limits stay within the safe envelope is handled by defined checks that add modest time. Setting safe-envelope limits needs engineering knowledge, but interlocks reduce reliance on vigilance. Exceeding the safe envelope risks equipment damage and lost batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that intermittent fault behavior cannot be reproduced under the reported process conditions
rows.imp_rat: Inability to reproduce intermittent faults [unreliability] hampers diagnostics but is a troubleshooting-support concern rather than a direct control failure; consequences are frustration and slower fixes, keeping both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: Status-quo logging rarely captures the full contextual state at the moment of an intermittent fault, so the condition frequently cannot be reproduced on demand and the diagnosis stays inconclusive. Reproducing an intermittent fault often requires waiting for the same rare process conditions to recur, stretching diagnosis over days or weeks. It takes seasoned engineers to recreate the exact load, timing and environmental state under which a transient fault appears, and that judgement is scarce. Idling or specially loading a production line to reproduce a fault carries real opportunity and energy costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical instruments with expired or unverified calibration status before run start
rows.imp_rat: Instruments with expired/unverified calibration before run start affect measurement trust (relevance) and could yield wrong readings (severity), but is a readiness check, moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems reliably flag expired instruments where integrated, with gaps for un-tracked devices. Calibration-status checks are fast when tracked in a calibration system. Calibration status flags remove operator judgement about instrument validity. Expired-calibration data can invalidate a batch, mostly prevented by tracking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of assigned operators with unverified qualification or authorization before run start
rows.imp_rat: Unverified operator qualification before run start is a readiness gate for correct execution (relevance) with potential error consequences (severity), scored moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Training/qualification-linked access control reliably blocks unauthorized operators where integrated. Operator qualification checks at login are near-instant. System authorization gating removes reliance on supervisors verifying credentials. Unqualified operators risk errors and rework, well guarded by access control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of open batch-impacting deviations lacking approved disposition at readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Open batch-impacting deviations without approved disposition at readiness is a gating concern (relevance) that could let a batch proceed with unresolved risk (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems are often separate from MES, so open-deviation blocks are inconsistently enforced at run start. Checking open deviations at readiness requires manual quality-system review that adds time. Judging deviation disposition impact depends on quality expertise. Starting with unresolved deviations risks batch rejection, weakly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed compounding steps lacking execution evidence
rows.imp_rat: Completed steps lacking execution evidence leave record gaps (relevance) and weaken proof of correct execution (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: EBR-enforced evidence capture reliably ensures completed steps carry execution evidence. Automated evidence capture at step completion adds no time. System capture removes operator burden of documenting each step. Missing evidence forces rework or rejection, mostly avoided by auto-capture.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compounding step is recorded as complete before execution finishes
rows.imp_rat: Recording a step complete before execution finishes falsifies the record (relevance) and can advance the batch prematurely (severity), but is a bounded sequencing defect.
rows.sat_rat: EBR completion gating reliably prevents recording a step complete before execution finishes. Completion signing occurs at the actual step end with system timestamps. System-enforced completion sign-off reduces reliance on operator sequencing discipline. Premature completion records cause deviations, mostly prevented by gating.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of execution records attributed to the wrong batch, step, or operator
rows.imp_rat: Execution records attributed to the wrong batch/step/operator corrupt traceability (relevance) and mislead review (severity), kept moderate-low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES reliably attributes records to the correct batch, step, and logged-in operator. Automatic attribution tagging is instantaneous. System context binding removes operator responsibility for correct attribution. Misattributed records cause investigations, mostly prevented by auto-tagging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding deviations and interventions left unrecorded
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded deviations and interventions leave gaps in the batch record (relevance) and can hide quality-relevant events (severity), but sit below top batch items.
rows.sat_rat: System prompts capture flagged deviations, but operator-observed interventions still go unrecorded. Recording deviations in real time takes operator effort that can lag the event. Recognizing what counts as a recordable deviation depends on operator judgement. Unrecorded deviations cause compliance and quality gaps, partly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required instruments with expired, missing, or unacceptable calibration or qualification status
rows.imp_rat: Instruments with bad calibration/qualification status affect measurement trust (relevance) and could yield wrong enforcement (severity), but is a readiness check, moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration interlocks catch many expired instruments, but not all devices are linked to execution gating. Calibration status lookups are quick where instruments are tracked in a system. Automated calibration flags reduce reliance on operators knowing instrument status. Calibration management systems add cost but are common in regulated sites.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required precondition values missing or outside recipe-defined ranges before execution
rows.imp_rat: Missing/out-of-range precondition values before execution is a readiness gate (relevance) that could start a bad batch (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Configured precondition gates are reliable, but not every precondition is systematized, leaving gaps. Precondition checks at step start avoid downstream delays from bad starting values. Automated precondition gating reduces reliance on operator verification. Precondition rules are configured in the recipe once.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of step confirmations attributed to the wrong batch, vessel, or recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Step confirmations attributed to the wrong batch/vessel/step corrupt the record (relevance) and mislead traceability (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Context binding makes misattributed confirmations rare where scanning and login are enforced. Session-bound context assigns confirmations instantly to the right target. Automated attribution relieves operators of manual tagging. Part of the standard MES session model.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps confirmed outside the required execution sequence
rows.imp_rat: Steps confirmed outside the required sequence violate execution order (relevance) and can affect product quality (severity), but ranked below batch 1, moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced execution order makes out-of-sequence confirmations uncommon in configured EBRs. Sequence enforcement prevents out-of-order confirmation without slowing the flow. Interlocked sequencing removes the need for operators to police step order. Sequence logic is standard recipe configuration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from a parameter leaving expected conditions until the executor recognizes the deviation
rows.imp_rat: Delay until the executor recognizes a parameter leaving expected conditions prolongs uncontrolled state (relevance) and can worsen the batch (severity), moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-driven recognition is consistent for instrumented parameters but blind to unsensed conditions. SCADA alarming shortens the interval from deviation to operator awareness. Automated detection lessens dependence on operator attention. Uses installed control and alarm infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation entries linked to the wrong compounding batch or recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Deviation entries linked to the wrong batch/step mislead investigations (relevance) and can misdirect corrective action (severity), but remain recoverable, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations are frequently linked to the wrong batch or step because the association is entered manually. Deviation linking is often a manual post-hoc step, adding delay. Correctly associating a deviation to batch and step relies on operator understanding. Deviation management modules add licensing cost not all sites deploy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of parameter excursions beyond defined acceptable ranges during the compounding run
rows.imp_rat: Frequent parameter excursions beyond acceptable ranges signal enforcement instability (relevance) and can degrade quality (severity), held moderate-low under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory control holds parameters in range well, though disturbances and manual steps still cause excursions. Automated control loops correct excursions quickly to keep parameters in range. Closed-loop control reduces reliance on manual operator adjustment. Control loops are part of the existing automation investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a parameter leaving its acceptable range and the executor recognizing the excursion
rows.imp_rat: Delay in the executor recognizing an out-of-range excursion prolongs uncontrolled conditions (relevance) and can worsen impact (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time alarms make excursion recognition timely for monitored parameters, though unmonitored ones lag. Continuous monitoring alerts the operator quickly when a parameter leaves range. Automated excursion alarms reduce reliance on operator vigilance. Alarming rides on existing control system capability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of captured entries attributed to the wrong batch, material, or compounding unit
rows.imp_rat: Misattributed captured entries harm the record-keeping half of the job (relevance) and mislead traceability (severity), but are recoverable administrative errors, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode-bound context makes wrong-batch attribution uncommon where scanning is enforced. Barcode-driven context assignment is instantaneous at the point of action. Scanning batch and material IDs removes reliance on operators to remember attribution. Scanning hardware is inexpensive and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed material additions omitted from the compounding batch record
rows.imp_rat: Completed material additions omitted from the batch record undercut recording accuracy (relevance) and traceability (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow-enforced additions are seldom omitted, though manual additions outside the flow can be missed. Scan-to-add workflows record additions in real time, leaving few unlogged. Enforced dispensing steps reduce reliance on operators to remember to log each addition. Dispensing integration uses existing weigh and scan equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material additions linked to the wrong recipe requirement or step
rows.imp_rat: Additions linked to the wrong recipe requirement/step misrecord execution (relevance) and can misstate compliance (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement linkage is reliable within the workflow but weaker for out-of-flow additions. Workflow linkage ties additions to the right requirement in real time. Guided dispensing reduces reliance on operators mapping material to step. Part of standard recipe and dispensing configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter records attributed to the wrong batch, material, vessel, or recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Parameter records tied to the wrong batch/vessel/step weaken traceability (relevance) and can mislead investigations (severity), yet remain below top-tier items.
rows.sat_rat: Automated context binding makes misattribution rare, though manual entries outside the workflow can still be misfiled. Context is bound automatically when the operator logs into a batch and vessel. Session-bound attribution reduces the operator's memory load for tagging records. Attribution logic is part of the standard MES configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding events with conflicting parameter records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting parameter records create ambiguity in the recorded process (relevance) and can confuse review, but rarely cause direct product harm (severity), placing it low.
rows.sat_rat: Single-source capture reduces conflicts, but parallel paper or auxiliary logs still produce contradictions. A single system-of-record reduces the reconciliation delay from conflicting sources. Centralized records lower the effort of resolving which value is correct. Consolidating records into one MES avoids the cost of maintaining parallel logs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch record entries lacking attributable performer or approver identity
rows.imp_rat: Entries lacking attributable performer/approver identity weaken accountability (relevance) and impair audit (severity), kept moderate-low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic signature requirements reliably attach performer and approver identity, making missing attribution rare. Login-based e-signatures capture identity instantly at each entry. Enforced authentication removes any operator skill needed to attribute entries. Unattributed entries fail audits, but e-signature enforcement prevents most.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compounding parameter readings unavailable during recipe execution
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable parameter readings during execution impair enforcement visibility (relevance) and can leave excursions undetected (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated instrumentation supplies most required readings, but sensor faults and manual measurements still leave occasional gaps. Missing readings during execution force pauses to retrieve or re-measure, adding delay. Filling gaps requires the operator to know which readings are needed and how to obtain them. Unavailable readings can stall a batch, a throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter readings attributed to the wrong batch, vessel, or recipe step during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Parameter readings misattributed to the wrong batch/vessel/step during the shift weaken the record (relevance) and mislead review (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Where equipment is integrated, readings map reliably to batch and step, though manual entries and shared vessels still cause occasional misattribution. System-tagged readings are attributed automatically with no added time. Automatic tagging by vessel and step reduces the operator's burden to attribute readings correctly. Misattributed readings can cause investigations, but automation limits frequency.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required compounding steps and parameters omitted from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required steps/parameters from the batch record leaves documentation incomplete (relevance) and weakens reconstruction (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: MES that gates progression on completed entries makes omitted required steps fairly uncommon, so this is reasonably served. Enforced electronic sequencing prompts each step in turn, adding little time. Step-by-step EBR guidance reduces reliance on the operator remembering every required entry. Missing steps cause deviations, but enforced workflows keep these relatively rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviations, alarms, and manual interventions omitted from batch documentation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting deviations, alarms, and interventions from batch documentation weakens the record (relevance) and impairs later review (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual interventions and alarms are frequently under-documented despite EBR prompts, leaving recurring gaps. Reconstructing omitted alarms and interventions after the fact is time-consuming during record review. Complete documentation depends on the operator's diligence to log every manual intervention amid the work. Omissions trigger deviation investigations and rework, a documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between detection of a recipe or parameter deviation and required escalation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between deviation detection and escalation slows response (relevance) and can let excursions persist (severity), but detection itself already occurred, moderating both.
rows.sat_rat: Alert-to-escalation handoffs are inconsistently automated, so escalation timeliness varies widely by shift and site. Automated alerting can trigger immediately, but escalation still often depends on manual routing and human acknowledgement. Judging severity and the correct escalation path relies on experienced staff. Delayed escalation extends exposure and increases containment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required record attributes and traceability links the recommended approach cannot preserve
rows.imp_rat: Inability to preserve record attributes/traceability links weakens documentation completeness (relevance) and hampers reconstruction (severity), but is a capability gap, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporary systems preserve many record attributes and links, yet completeness against specific traceability requirements is inconsistently verified. Checking that every record attribute and traceability link is preserved is detailed work often abbreviated. Knowing which record attributes and links are required needs data-integrity expertise not uniformly held. Unpreserved attributes create costly remediation and re-documentation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that conflicting compounding records obscure the authoritative recipe value
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting records obscuring the authoritative recipe value risks executing the wrong value (relevance) with quality consequences (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple record sources still obscure the authoritative recipe value more often than acceptable. Untangling conflicting records to find the authoritative value adds investigation time. Identifying the true authoritative value requires skilled record analysis. Acting on a wrong value from obscured records carries batch-loss cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches with conflicting evidence about the recipe and parameter requirements in effect
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting evidence about the in-effect recipe/parameter requirements creates enforcement ambiguity (relevance) that could cause wrong execution (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting evidence about requirements in effect is common and not systematically resolved. Resolving conflicting evidence about requirements in effect is a slow investigative task. Determining the authoritative requirement demands experienced interpretation. Conflicting evidence prolongs disposition and risks costly wrong decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to display hierarchy contents during time-critical navigation
rows.imp_rat: Slow hierarchy display during time-critical navigation [delay] mildly impedes the monitoring aspect of the job, but rarely causes lasting harm; the error is an inconvenience, so relevance and severity remain low.
rows.sat_rat: Occasional lag on large hierarchies or slow networks still surprises operators, keeping this short of fully solved. Contemporary HMI rendering and caching display hierarchy contents within a second or two in most deployments, which usually suffices even under time pressure. No special skill is needed to trigger the display; it is an automatic system response. Faster rendering may require better client hardware but imposes no ongoing resource waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required process data, alarms, or events omitted from the report
rows.imp_rat: Omitted process data/alarms from reports weakens reporting completeness but has limited operational impact and is a downstream output function; both proxies moderate, under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Automated reporting from historians is standard, but template scope errors and data-source gaps still cause required items to be omitted. Report generation is largely automated, so omissions are found only when the report is scrutinized later. Defining a complete report template correctly requires reporting configuration expertise. Incomplete reports trigger re-generation and review cycles that add compliance labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the stored baseline backup is corrupted after archival
rows.imp_rat: A corrupted baseline backup severely hampers recovery when needed (severity), but backup integrity is a contingency concern rarely exercised and peripheral to routine control; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Checksums, redundancy and periodic restore tests make silent corruption of a baseline backup relatively rare where standard practice is followed. Integrity verification of archived backups runs automatically and quickly with checksums during and after archival. Modern backup systems handle corruption detection with little operator expertise required beyond configuring verification. Redundant copies and verification add storage cost but are standard and inexpensive at typical scales.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the stored baseline is altered without authorization
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized baseline alteration is a tampering severity risk, yet baseline protection is a security-support function outside the active control/configure job; low relevance holds it under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Signing and immutable archives make unauthorized baseline alteration detectable and uncommon, though not every site enforces them consistently. Tamper detection via hashing and immutable storage flags unauthorized changes quickly on retrieval. Configuring WORM/immutable storage and signing needs security-aware admins, a moderately available skill. Immutable or air-gapped storage adds some cost but is a standard, budgetable control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controlled devices unavailable for end-to-end communication checks
rows.imp_rat: Devices unavailable for end-to-end communication checks is a commissioning/verification coverage gap (low-moderate relevance); missing a check delays confidence but does not itself break control (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Communication test coverage is well practiced, yet devices offline for production or maintenance regularly leave gaps in end-to-end verification. Running end-to-end communication checks is reasonably fast once devices are online, but unavailable devices force reschedules. Coordinating full-loop comms checks needs network and control expertise that is generally available in maintenance teams. Devices left unavailable waste scheduled test windows and staff time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized alteration of the documented control software maintenance history
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized alteration of maintenance history is a records-security/compliance matter (moderate severity) with little bearing on the runtime control job (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Electronic audit trails and access controls make undetected alteration of maintenance history rare where compliant systems are deployed. Audit-trail systems log maintenance history automatically, so tamper evidence is available without delay. Enforcing Part -style audit trails needs validated systems and administrators, an expertise generally present in GMP settings. Validated audit-trail software is a standard compliance investment already carried in regulated plants.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of damaged or missing installation components left unrestored after repair
rows.imp_rat: Unrestored damaged installation components after repair affects install/repair reliability rather than the core control loop (low-moderate relevance); consequences are recoverable via reinstallation (moderate-low severity), so it stays under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Install verification tools and manifests catch most gaps, but incomplete restoration still slips through when repairs are ad hoc. Identifying damaged or missing installation components after repair is fairly quick with installation manifests and validation checks. Confirming full component restoration needs technicians familiar with the software's install footprint, a moderately common skill. Unrestored components cause later failures and callback repairs, raising overall maintenance cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of reported faults assigned an incorrect root cause during troubleshooting
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect root-cause assignment during troubleshooting [defect] slows and misdirects repairs but is a diagnostic-support activity, not the primary control job; consequences are wasted effort, keeping both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: Without structured causal evidence, technicians commonly fix a symptom rather than the cause, so the same fault recurs. Chasing a wrong root cause wastes troubleshooting hours on components that were never at fault. Correct root-cause attribution leans heavily on the diagnostic intuition of experienced control engineers, and that expertise is unevenly distributed and often absent on the maintenance shift. Misdiagnosis leads to unnecessary part swaps and repeated site visits that consume budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of fault reports lacking the operating-state and event evidence needed to isolate the root cause
rows.imp_rat: Fault reports lacking operating-state/event evidence [ambiguity] impede diagnosis but are a support-data completeness issue rather than a control failure; the delay in isolating causes is the main cost, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Fault reports today routinely arrive without the timestamped operating-state and event context needed to isolate the cause, forcing guesswork and leaving many reports unresolvable. Missing operating-state evidence forces technicians to re-run the fault to gather data, adding delay to every diagnosis. Deciding what evidence is even needed presumes an expert's mental model of the fault, which junior staff lack. Event and state logging is largely built into modern controllers, so capturing evidence is cheap when enabled.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configuration data collected for the audit are incomplete or internally inconsistent
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete or inconsistent configuration data collected for an audit undermines audit accuracy but is a periodic administrative task, not the live control job (low relevance); severity is moderate as it can misinform compliance but does not stop operations.
rows.sat_rat: Automated configuration-collection tools exist and are moderately mature, but partial coverage across mixed device fleets still leaves gaps and inconsistencies in the audit dataset. Assembling configuration data for an audit is slow when it must be gathered manually from scattered devices. Judging whether a configuration dataset is internally consistent requires domain knowledge of how parameters interrelate. Configuration-management add-ons exist but licensing and setup cost keeps many sites on manual collection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the executed batch sequence and the approved sequence during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between executed and approved sequence during validation affects qualification, but it is a bounded validation-batch event with limited recurring consequence to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Automated batch execution engines enforce approved sequences closely, so deviations during the validation batch are comparatively well controlled. Comparing executed to approved sequence during validation is a defined but time-consuming review. Detecting sequence deviations relies on validation staff familiar with the approved procedure. Sequence deviations invalidate the batch and force costly re-execution.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between detecting a consistency deviation and applying the corresponding parameter adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Delay between detecting a consistency deviation and applying adjustment slightly worsens product quality, but short lags are typically absorbed without lasting damage.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop control responds consistently where deployed, but reliance on lab or grab-sample results introduces variable delays and misses. Inline sensors and automated control shrink detect-to-adjust latency to seconds when instrumented, though manual sampling loops still add minutes. Automated correction reduces reliance on an operator to interpret and act, but manual regimes still demand attentive judgement. Faster correction limits off-spec volume, saving material without major added expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of consistency oscillations caused by successive parameter adjustments during the batch
rows.imp_rat: Consistency oscillations from successive adjustments reduce product uniformity, but such oscillations are usually damped and recoverable, so relevance and severity remain low-moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Deadband, rate limits and inline viscosity/consistency sensing curb chasing, yet lagging feedback still lets successive adjustments overshoot fairly often. Consistency responses take real settling time and successive adjustments before the loop stabilizes, but the core issue is variability not speed. Knowing to wait for a process to settle before the next adjustment is an experience-dependent skill that inexperienced operators frequently lack. Repeated adjustments consume added additive and mixing energy, a moderate recurring waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of lag between a process change and its appearance in the trend view
rows.imp_rat: Lag between a process change and its trend-view appearance is a display latency concern (low relevance to core control) with limited consequence (low severity), clearly a lower-tier item.
rows.sat_rat: Historian collection intervals introduce a small, consistent lag that is predictable but not eliminable in standard practice. Trend views update within seconds of a process change under normal historian polling rates. Reading a near-live trend requires no special skill once display settings are correct. Trend update infrastructure already exists, so reducing lag costs little more.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of timestamp misalignment across selected operating variables
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp misalignment across operating variables [instability] mildly complicates correlation and analysis but does not break control; its impact is on data interpretation, keeping both proxies modest under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Time synchronization (NTP/PTP) is common but not universally tight, so cross-variable timestamp skew remains a real and recurring data-integrity gap. Timestamp misalignment forces analysts to spend time reconciling trends before trusting them. Detecting and correcting for skewed timestamps requires an experienced engineer who understands the acquisition chain. The misalignment wastes little material but can invalidate analyses that cost rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of filters from a previous batch or production phase remaining active after the operating context changes
rows.imp_rat: Stale filters remaining active after context change is a display/data-view housekeeping error only loosely tied to controlling devices (low-moderate relevance), and it mainly causes operator confusion rather than control failure (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Most HMIs do not reliably scope filters to the operating context, so filters persisting across batch or phase changes is a recurring silent failure that standard practice only partially mitigates with manual resets. Stale filters cost operators seconds to notice and clear, but the delay is minor compared to the confusion they cause. Recognizing that a lingering filter is hiding relevant data demands experienced operators who know the system's context-switching quirks.
