The residual cost is mostly the occasional missed event rather than direct material or money loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration between a filter scope change and the displayed view reflecting current control-point states
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a filter scope change and the view reflecting current control-point states is a UI refresh concern (low relevance) with minor operational consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: View filtering reliably reflects current states on refresh, though large tag sets or heavy loads occasionally lag the update briefly. Applying a filter scope change refreshes the displayed control-point states within about a second in modern HMIs. Filtering views is a routine operator action requiring minimal skill. Filter refresh is a lightweight software operation with negligible marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of I/O channels remaining outside accuracy tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: I/O channels out of accuracy tolerance affect signal fidelity into the controllers (some relevance) but this is a calibration-maintenance task and errors are usually flagged; severity is moderate. Kept at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized I/O calibration keeps most channels within accuracy tolerance, with only occasional out-of-tolerance results from equipment or documentation lapses. I/O channel calibration is systematic and reasonably quick with modern tools. Channel calibration follows clear procedures well within routine technician capability. The task uses existing calibration equipment with low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reported control faults lacking complete diagnostic, event-log, or network records
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete diagnostic/event-log records on reported faults hampers troubleshooting, a supporting concern with moderate severity but limited centrality to core control.
rows.sat_rat: Event logs and historians capture most data, but faults still get reported with incomplete records where logging coverage or retention gaps exist. Assembling complete diagnostic, event-log, and network records for a fault can take time when logs are dispersed. Correlating records across subsystems requires analysts who know where each data source lives. Historian and logging storage cost is generally already provisioned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical DCS spare is degraded or damaged when retrieved for an unplanned failure
rows.imp_rat: A degraded spare retrieved during an unplanned failure delays restoration of control (relevance real but a spares-logistics matter, not the core control loop); consequences can extend downtime but redundancy usually cushions the impact. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Spares are commonly stored but rarely periodically re-verified, so degraded or damaged critical spares are discovered only at the moment of need. Retrieving and re-verifying a spare during an unplanned failure adds delay at the worst moment. Judging whether a stored spare is still serviceable takes experienced staff. Proper climate-controlled spare storage and periodic testing carry ongoing cost often under-funded.

rows.stk: Maintenance technicians, instrumentation and controls engineers
rows.source_job: Inspect control system hardware and wiring
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of physical deterioration remaining undetected because control components, terminations, or cable runs are obscured
rows.imp_rat: Undetected physical deterioration of obscured components is a maintenance-inspection concern with gradual severity and modest relevance to the immediate control job.
rows.sat_rat: Deterioration behind obscured components and cable runs frequently goes undetected until failure because routine inspection cannot easily reach it. Inspecting obscured cable runs and terminations requires slow physical access and disassembly. Detecting hidden deterioration depends on experienced technicians knowing where to look behind panels and trays. Thorough inspection needs labor and sometimes intrusive access, but tooling cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of live process streams remaining dependent on the retiring DCS before removal
rows.imp_rat: Live streams still dependent on a retiring DCS could cause loss of control during removal (some relevance and real severity), but this is a transition-planning error confined to decommissioning. Held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Live streams frequently remain unexpectedly tied to the retiring DCS at removal because hidden dependencies are hard to fully enumerate. Migrating live process streams off a retiring DCS is a protracted, phased effort. Identifying and safely transferring every live dependency demands deep system and process knowledge that is scarce. Parallel running and migration engineering are costly during decommissioning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required controller configurations, tuning parameters, and historical process records omitted from the pre-removal export
rows.imp_rat: Omitting controller configs and historical records from a pre-removal export is a decommissioning-phase concern, peripheral to the DCS's live control job (relevance modest); lost configuration data can hamper future rebuilds but is recoverable through other archives, so severity is bounded. Capped at 3.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard backup and export procedures capture most configuration and history, though obsolete media formats and scattered records leave occasional omissions. Exporting controller configurations, tuning, and history is a defined pre-removal task that fits within planned decommissioning time. Vendor tools help, but knowing every parameter and record that must be captured relies on experienced control engineers. Export uses existing engineering tools with negligible incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that transferred DCS hardware retains confidential process or configuration data
rows.imp_rat: Transferred hardware retaining confidential data is a moderate-severity confidentiality error but only tangentially relevant to the DCS's active control mission.
rows.sat_rat: Hardware is regularly transferred with residual configuration or process data because verification is inconsistent and hard to confirm. Confirming transferred hardware is clean before hand-off adds a verification step that is often rushed under transfer timelines. Judging whether resold or redeployed DCS hardware retains confidential data requires expertise in the device's internal memory, which is scarce. Sanitization before transfer is cheap relative to hardware value but is frequently skipped to save effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical alarms, deviations, equipment states, or aseptic interventions omitted from the shift handover
rows.imp_rat: Alarms or interventions omitted from shift handover is a procedural communication error with moderate severity and low centrality to the DCS's automated control function.
rows.sat_rat: Handover is still largely manual and narrative, so critical alarms, deviations or interventions are frequently omitted, making this poorly served. Assembling a handover takes time at shift end, but timing is not the core failure. Completeness of handover depends heavily on the outgoing operator's diligence and memory, a fragile human process. Electronic shift-log tools exist but structured handover modules are often not purchased or used.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical operating events omitted from the operator log during sterile fill-finish production
rows.imp_rat: Operating events omitted from the operator log is a record-keeping error—moderate compliance severity but low centrality to the control system's active function.
rows.sat_rat: System-driven event and audit logs capture most operating events reliably, but manually-entered contextual events are still sometimes missed. Automatic event capture logs occur in real time without operator effort. Automatic logging removes reliance on operators to remember to record most system events. Sequence-of-events and audit-trail logging is standard, though extended retention adds storage cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which remotely viewed process data lag actual sterile fill-finish conditions
rows.imp_rat: Remote data lag versus actual sterile conditions affects monitoring accuracy modestly; low-to-moderate relevance since local control still functions and severity is limited.
rows.sat_rat: Web/thin-client historian views give reasonably timely remote data, though buffering and polling can leave remote views modestly behind live conditions. Remote data lag depends on historian and network refresh rates, which introduce seconds-scale delay for offsite viewers. Interpreting whether remote data is current requires some awareness of update timing, a modest burden. Remote access infrastructure and secure gateways carry real IT cost but are widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of field instruments remaining outside their specified tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Field instruments out of tolerance after calibration feed the control loops, so there is genuine relevance, but the calibration task is a maintenance activity and drift is usually caught; severity is contained. Held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration procedures and traceable standards keep most instruments in tolerance, though drift and technique errors leave some outside spec. Field instrument calibration is a well-scheduled but labor-intensive routine that takes measurable time per loop. Calibration is a mature, procedure-driven task technicians perform reliably with standard reference equipment. Calibrators and reference standards are established recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterile fill-finish compliance deliverables omitted from the DCS purchase order
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance deliverables in the purchase order is a paperwork-stage error—modest severity and low centrality to executing distributed control.
rows.sat_rat: POs are typically commercial documents that under-specify compliance deliverables, so omissions are common and repeatable. Building a purchase order that itemizes all compliance deliverables adds procurement processing time. Ensuring PO line items reflect quality deliverables needs quality-aware procurement staff that many organizations do not have. Deliverables missed from the PO are often supplied only at extra charge or not at all, raising total cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of submitted evidence that does not cover the proposed DCS configuration and sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Submitted evidence not covering the proposed configuration is a qualification-documentation gap with moderate severity and limited relevance to running the process.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors often submit boilerplate evidence not tied to the offered configuration, and status-quo review rarely enforces configuration-specific traceability. Cross-checking that submitted evidence covers the exact proposed configuration is a slow, document-heavy review. Determining evidence relevance to the specific sterile use case needs experienced reviewers who understand both the configuration and the application. Accepting generic evidence risks paying later for configuration-specific validation, a notable cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting an unstable DCS communication link as verified
rows.imp_rat: Accepting an unstable communication link as verified touches the network backbone of the DCS (some relevance) and could cause intermittent control issues (moderate severity), but the ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor diagnostics, redundancy testing and continuity checks are mature and catch hard failures well, yet intermittent or marginal links can still be signed off as verified, leaving a real recurring gap. Verifying link stability requires soak periods and repeated loopback tests that consume real bench and commissioning time before a link can be signed off. Judging whether an intermittently working proprietary link is truly stable takes network specialists who can interpret error counters and diagnostics, a scarce skill during preparation. Accepting an unstable link is costly later, but the verification tooling itself is comparatively inexpensive relative to the overall DCS.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing DCS hardware at a location that conflicts with approved installation documentation
rows.imp_rat: Installing hardware at a location conflicting with approved docs is a commissioning-phase error with limited consequences once corrected and modest relevance to the control job.
rows.sat_rat: Installation drawings and inspection make location conflicts relatively uncommon and readily caught during walkdown. Verifying hardware placement against installation drawings is a quick physical check within normal timelines. Confirming location against drawings is basic technician work requiring little specialized skill. Correct placement uses standard installation drawings at negligible incremental cost.

rows.source_job: Configure security controls and access protections
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of DCS identities assigned permissions beyond approved sterile fill-finish role requirements
rows.imp_rat: Excess permissions beyond role requirements is an access-governance error with moderate security severity but only peripheral relevance to executing distributed control.
rows.sat_rat: Access-review practice is reasonably mature, but excess permissions still slip through where role definitions are loosely maintained. Configuring and reviewing role permissions against approved matrices adds modest setup and audit time. Standard role-based access frameworks reduce reliance on specialists, though correct mapping to sterile roles needs care. Permission configuration uses built-in features at low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of dashboard lag following actual process-state changes
rows.imp_rat: Dashboard lag following process-state changes degrades the monitoring facet of the CFJ (moderate-low relevance); severity is limited because operators still eventually see accurate state, so delayed refresh is inconvenient rather than catastrophic.
rows.sat_rat: Event-driven data architectures deliver consistently prompt refreshes, with lag only under heavy load or poor networks. Modern SCADA polling and event-driven updates keep dashboard lag behind process changes low. Achieving low latency needs some tuning of scan rates and network settings by a competent engineer. Adequate update performance comes with standard hardware at little extra cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in dashboard labels and status indicators presented to operators
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in dashboard labels and status indicators can cause operator misreads (some relevance to monitoring), but clear alternate context usually mitigates it; severity is moderate-low and the item is ranked beneath the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Naming conventions and standards reduce ambiguity, but subjective wording choices still leave indicators open to misreading. Reviewing labels for ambiguity is a quick pass during dashboard preparation. Writing unambiguous labels and status indicators requires human-factors and process knowledge that is inconsistently applied. Improving labels is essentially free editing effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operators being denied access to configured dashboards at handover
rows.imp_rat: Operators denied configured dashboards at handover [unreliability] delays visibility but does not stop the control function itself; the error is recoverable and largely peripheral to the CFJ, so both proxies stay low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-handover access verification catches most issues, but permission-to-dashboard mapping errors still occasionally block operators. Access denials at handover cause immediate but usually short interruptions. Verifying dashboard access requires only routine admin confirmation. Resolving denied access is inexpensive administrative effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of internal zones left untreated because they cannot be accessed during decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Untreated internal zones during decontamination can seed contamination, but this is a cleaning/access issue tangential to the batching CFJ and constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard CIP/SIP routines are designed for production, not disposal, so internal zones routinely go untreated with no reliable verification at end of life. Decontaminating dead legs and inaccessible internal zones adds significant time as crews improvise access or extend flush cycles. Identifying and reaching untreated internal zones on aging, undocumented systems demands scarce process and mechanical expertise. Disassembly, extra cleaning agents, and rework to reach hidden zones raise disposal cost materially.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by system removal and handover
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption from system removal and handover (delay) is a transitional event not part of routine batching; its severity is bounded because it is a planned, scheduled downtime rather than an unexpected failure.
rows.sat_rat: Coordinated removal plans usually keep disruption bounded, though unforeseen tie-ins extend interruptions. System removal and handover directly consumes production time that planned-shutdown scheduling minimizes but cannot eliminate. Rigging and safe removal of large batching skids needs specialized crews. Downtime and specialist removal labor represent significant but planned costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sterile fill-finish batching interruption during the transition
rows.imp_rat: Batching interruption during transition (delay) is a one-time changeover concern peripheral to the ongoing CFJ, with severity limited to planned lost-production time.
rows.sat_rat: Transition planning generally keeps interruptions predictable, with occasional overruns. Batching interruption during transition is scheduled into changeover windows to limit duration. Sequencing the transition to protect ongoing batches needs experienced production planners. Interrupted batching means idle lines, a real but anticipated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of loose, degraded, or corroded batching-system connections remaining undetected after inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loose or corroded connections after inspection can cause leaks, a moderately severe outcome, but this inspection-quality error is peripheral to the batching CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Without instrumented connection monitoring, degraded or loose connections are commonly missed during walkdown inspections. Checking every connection is tedious and frequently rushed during turnarounds. Identifying loose or corroded connections relies on systematic technician attention that is easy to lapse. Undetected bad connections cause later leaks and failures, generating repeat callouts and downtime.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified tolerance after calibration affects measurement accuracy, but it is a peripheral maintenance-quality issue with limited direct stakes for a single batch.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable reference standards and documented calibration procedures reliably bring devices well within tolerance in mature programs. Calibration procedures are well-defined and executed within scheduled service time. Standardized calibration methods and instruments reduce reliance on individual expertise. Calibration consumables and instrument time are modest and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that accessing tanks, piping, pumps, valves, mixers, sensors, or fittings for inspection causes damage or misalignment
rows.imp_rat: Damage or misalignment from accessing components for inspection (rework) is a maintenance-handling error peripheral to producing a batch, with consequences usually limited to repair rework.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and access aids reduce incidents, but accidental damage or disturbed alignment during access still occurs with some regularity. Careful access to avoid damage slows the inspection but is factored into the plan. Accessing sensitive components without causing damage or misalignment relies on experienced, careful handling. Access-induced damage creates avoidable repair costs and additional downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required recovered functions and data left unchecked
rows.imp_rat: Leaving recovered functions and data unchecked bears on recovery completeness, only indirectly related to the CFJ's monitoring/control (low-moderate relevance), and gaps are typically caught in later verification (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recovery checklists exist but are often incomplete, so some required functions and data routinely go unverified. Verifying every required recovered function and data set against a checklist takes substantial time in a recovery test. Complete verification depends on someone knowing the full functional scope, so coverage varies with tester experience. Unchecked functions surface as failures later, converting cheap verification into expensive incidents.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of residual software components or access paths that remain active after formal retirement
rows.imp_rat: Residual software components/access paths after formal retirement is a decommissioning cleanup issue peripheral to controlling devices (low relevance), with security implications (moderate severity) kept under 3.5 mean by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete asset and dependency inventories mean residual components and access paths commonly remain active after formal retirement. Removing residual components is quick per item but the full sweep is repeatedly deferred. Confirming that no residual software components or access paths remain requires comprehensive system knowledge that is hard to assemble for aging installations. Thorough post-retirement verification adds labor cost that is often trimmed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of discovering undocumented dependencies after decommissioning of the industrial control software begins
rows.imp_rat: Discovering undocumented dependencies after decommissioning begins is a one-time end-of-life risk peripheral to routine control (low relevance); severity can be higher because surprise dependencies may disrupt other systems, but the ranking keeps the mean at or below 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Undocumented dependencies surfacing late is one of the most chronic, unmitigated failures in OT disposal. Discovering hidden dependencies mid-decommissioning forces disruptive rework and delay. Anticipating undocumented dependencies relies almost entirely on deep tribal knowledge that is scarce. Unplanned dependency surprises drive expensive emergency remediation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of exported artifacts corrupted or unreadable after extraction
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted or unreadable exported artifacts touch only the retirement/export workflow rather than active device control (low relevance); consequences are recoverable in most cases since exports can often be regenerated, keeping severity modest under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Checksum-on-extract catches most corruption, though inconsistent adoption leaves some unreadable exports undetected. Validating exported artifacts adds a quick verification pass with existing tools. Standard hash-verification requires little special skill. Verification is essentially free with existing utilities.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that proprietary process data, credentials, or sensitive information remains in overlooked storage locations after the purge
rows.imp_rat: Residual credentials/proprietary data after a purge carry real disclosure severity, yet purge completeness is a peripheral data-hygiene task versus the active control job; low relevance under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary data and credentials routinely persist in overlooked storage because complete data mapping of legacy control environments is rarely achieved. Searching all possible storage locations is time-consuming and often truncated under schedule pressure. Finding sensitive data hidden in backups, caches, shares and forgotten hosts requires investigative data-discovery expertise that is frequently lacking in OT contexts. Comprehensive data-discovery tooling and effort are costly and often under-resourced for control systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recoverable sensitive data remaining on retired industrial control software media
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable data on retired media poses a data-leak severity risk, but media decommissioning is an end-of-life administrative activity essentially unrelated to running control software; low relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Established sanitization standards work well, but overlooked or spare media and incomplete asset collection still leave recoverable data more often than intended. Media sanitization is a quick, well-defined step once assets are collected. Standard wiping and degaussing tools require only modest operator skill following documented procedures. Certified sanitization or media destruction adds a modest, well-understood cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of user, service, and system entitlements that remain active after retirement of the industrial control software
rows.imp_rat: Lingering user/service/system entitlements after retirement is an access-governance loose end unrelated to the active control job (low relevance), carrying moderate unauthorized-access risk (moderate severity).
rows.sat_rat: Without a consolidated entitlement inventory, active credentials routinely survive decommissioning and become an audited gap, so outcomes are inconsistent. Manually walking every user, service, and system account across HMIs, SCADA servers, and controllers is slow, so entitlements often linger long after retirement. Identifying every orphaned service and machine account demands scarce experts who understand both the OT stack and its identity plumbing. The labor to hunt down and revoke scattered entitlements is modest but rarely budgeted for a system being sunset.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of in-scope regulated production data remaining after required removal
rows.imp_rat: Regulated production data remaining after required removal is a retention-compliance issue (moderate severity) with essentially no impact on the core control function (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Without authoritative data inventories, residual regulated production data commonly remains and its true percentage is hard to confirm. Verifying near-complete removal of regulated data takes substantial time that is often compressed. Quantifying residual regulated data across dispersed stores demands data-governance skill that is unevenly available. Achieving and confirming near-zero residual data requires expensive exhaustive scanning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active access paths to the retired industrial control software remaining after required revocation
rows.imp_rat: Active access paths to retired software after revocation is a retirement-hygiene concern outside the operational CFJ (low relevance); it poses a security exposure (moderate severity) but does not affect ongoing control.
rows.sat_rat: Firewall and access reviews close most paths, yet forgotten remote or backdoor access routes to legacy control software regularly persist. Closing known access paths is fast once mapped, but discovery adds lead time. Mapping all network and remote access paths to a retired system needs OT security expertise that is scarce. Access-path auditing is labor-intensive but not material-cost-heavy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the designated host does not meet the software prerequisites
rows.imp_rat: Host failing to meet software prerequisites [unreliability] is a one-time deployment check that is peripheral to ongoing control; it is caught early and remediated, keeping both proxies low.
rows.sat_rat: Automated prerequisite checks and documented system requirements make host mismatches rare and detected early. Prerequisite checkers and installers verify host requirements in minutes before install. Reading a compatibility matrix and running a prerequisite check needs little specialized skill. Prerequisite validation is essentially free with vendor tooling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of source configuration or process-data files being rejected as incompatible
rows.imp_rat: Rejected source config/process-data files as incompatible can block setup workflows, but this is an import/format friction issue that is usually resolvable; moderate on both proxies, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Version-mismatch rejections remain common when files move between tool revisions or vendors during preparation. Checking file/format compatibility before import is quick once tools flag versions. Understanding version and format compatibility across engineering-tool releases needs vendor-specific expertise. No material cost, but rejected files cause labor to reformat or re-export.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controls for different fill-finish units or batch phases that are visually confusable in the customized workspace
rows.imp_rat: Visually confusable controls for different units/phases is a UI ambiguity risk secondary to core control (low-moderate relevance); it could cause an operator misclick but is mitigable by layout discipline (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Style guides and templates reduce confusability, though free customization still permits ambiguous groupings to survive. Spotting visually confusable controls is a fast side-effect of layout walkthroughs. Avoiding confusable controls across units and phases demands disciplined HMI ergonomic design skill that is unevenly applied. Design conventions cost little but confusion-driven errors can be expensive downstream.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of deployed configuration changes while validation checks are being executed
rows.imp_rat: Deploying config changes during validation runs can invalidate checks, a process-timing concern that touches configuration integrity but is usually procedurally controlled; moderate on both, at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Concurrent changes during validation are a recurring problem because version and change control in OT preparation is often weak. Freezing configuration during validation windows is a scheduling discipline that costs coordination time. Enforcing change freeze relies on process discipline more than technical skill, but requires attentive change management. Little direct cost, though uncoordinated changes cause costly invalidated validation runs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batching tasks performed out of the required sequence during multi-operator production
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence batching tasks in multi-operator production can cause errors, but they are usually recoverable procedural slips with moderate relevance to the core batching job.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic work instructions and sequence enforcement help, but human handoffs in multi-operator production still produce out-of-sequence execution with notable frequency. Out-of-sequence tasks across operators cause rework and waiting, adding avoidable time in multi-operator runs. Keeping the required task order across multiple operators depends heavily on procedural discipline and coordination skill. Sequence errors can spoil batches or require reprocessing, a recurring cost in busy multi-operator production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among process readings used to verify the batch endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among readings used to verify endpoint complicates confirmation, but it is a bounded verification-step issue with limited consequence to the overall job.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-parameter endpoint checks and redundant sensors reduce disagreement, but calibration drift and single-point measurements still leave conflicting readings unresolved. Reconciling disagreeing readings at endpoint takes extra verification time. Judging which reading to trust when instruments disagree is a distinctly skill-dependent call. Redundant instrumentation to reduce disagreement adds capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that endpoint criteria cannot be matched unambiguously to the available process readings
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous mapping of endpoint criteria to process readings (item 3) can delay confirmation, but this is largely a definitional/configuration concern with moderate relevance and modest, recoverable consequences. Within the 3.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint criteria often reference qualities not directly measured by installed sensors, leaving recurring interpretive ambiguity. Mapping endpoint criteria to available readings is quick when instrumentation is well-defined. Interpreting whether existing process readings unambiguously satisfy endpoint criteria demands engineering judgement. Ambiguity causes disputed dispositions and delays rather than large direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the allowable batch hold limit while completing the step manually
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the allowable hold limit while completing a step manually (item 18) can compromise a batch, but it is a manual-fallback timing concern of moderate relevance and modest severity. Within cap.
rows.sat_rat: Timers and alarms exist to flag approaching hold limits, but manual completion means breaches still happen with real frequency. The core error is elapsed time exceeding a hold limit while a step is done manually, and manual execution is inherently slow and variable. Beating the hold clock manually requires practiced operators who work efficiently under pressure, so skill matters. Exceeding hold limits forces disposition or scrap of the batch, a recurring cost when steps run long.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between recognizing a process deviation and completing the required setpoint correction
rows.imp_rat: Time between recognizing a process deviation and completing setpoint correction affects responsiveness, but modest delays rarely cause irreversible harm to the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard alarm-to-action workflows are well established, though inconsistent operator response and procedure gaps leave residual variability. Recognizing a deviation and executing the setpoint correction is fast on modern HMIs, though root-cause confirmation before acting adds delay. The correction magnitude depends on operator judgement, so response time varies with expertise. Prompt correction bounds off-spec production, a meaningful but not dominant saving.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of process-parameter oscillation caused by competing setpoint changes during an active batch
rows.imp_rat: Parameter oscillation from competing setpoint changes degrades batch stability, but the effect is typically transient and correctable, keeping both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: PID tuning, cascade control and interlock logic in standard DCS/PLC systems dampen most oscillation, though poorly tuned loops and manual overrides still cause recurring hunting. Modern batch controllers apply setpoint changes within seconds, so oscillation is not primarily a delay problem. Avoiding competing setpoint changes still leans on experienced operators who understand loop interactions and hold discipline during a batch. Oscillation wastes reagents and energy through overcorrection, but the recurring cost is modest relative to a lost batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unintended residual fluid trapped at piping connections after transfer or cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residual fluid trapped at piping connections is relevant to cleaning and cross-contamination with moderate severity, but this hygienic-design detail ranks below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic connection design mitigates trapping, but field connections still trap fluid inconsistently, so the need is only partially served. Draining and verifying no trapped fluid at connections adds handling time. Recognizing and clearing dead-leg trapping requires hygienic-design and operator knowledge. Trapped residual fluid causes contamination and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that obsolete or unapproved recipe versions are designated as current after import
rows.imp_rat: Designating obsolete recipe versions as current after import is relevant to formulation correctness with moderate severity, but as an import-administration error it ranks below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Import routines often don't enforce approval status automatically, so unapproved versions can be flagged current, leaving a recurring gap. Confirming the approval status of an imported version after import adds a verification step. It takes quality/change-control judgement to distinguish approved from unapproved versions during import. Designating an unapproved version as current risks scrapped batches and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that authorized users cannot perform assigned control-system tasks because required permissions are missing
rows.imp_rat: Missing permissions blocking authorized users (defect) is largely an access-administration issue peripheral to the batching CFJ, with modest consequences as it usually causes short workflow delays rather than batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access provisioning is standard practice yet role definitions are frequently incomplete at preparation time, so authorized users still routinely hit missing-permission walls. Missing permissions surface as work stoppages that must be escalated and remediated, adding unpredictable delay during commissioning. Correctly mapping assigned control-system tasks to the exact permission set requires administrators who understand both the DCS role model and the operational duties, a combination that is often thin. Blocked authorized users idle expensive commissioning resources, but the incremental money lost per incident is usually modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of operating limits outside validated ranges for the handled liquid and sterile preparation process
rows.imp_rat: Operating limits outside validated ranges is relevant to process compliance with moderate severity, but overlaps other items and ranks below the prior batch, so it is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment of configured limits to validated ranges relies on manual cross-referencing to validation documents, so mismatches persist and the need is poorly-to-acceptably served. Verifying that limits match validated ranges for a specific sterile process is a detailed, time-consuming review. This requires specialized sterile-process and validation expertise that is scarce. Limits outside validated ranges risk failed sterile batches with high scrap and reprocessing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the combined capacity, flow, and timing limits make the required batch cycle infeasible
rows.imp_rat: Infeasible batch cycles from combined capacity/flow/timing limits are a design-phase constraint, largely resolved before production, keeping relevance to routine execution low.
rows.sat_rat: Established sizing calculations and simulation tools make most cycle-time bottlenecks visible before startup, though hidden interaction effects still occasionally render a required cycle infeasible. Capacity and flow feasibility checks during preparation are typically done up front through process calculations, so they rarely add delay once the design basis is set. Verifying that combined capacity, flow, and timing limits support the batch cycle demands experienced process engineers whose judgement is scarce and not fully proceduralized. A missed feasibility limit surfaces late and forces costly rework or equipment changes, but this is caught often enough that catastrophic overspend is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator pauses the wrong batch cycle during an urgent intervention
rows.imp_rat: Pausing the wrong batch during an urgent intervention has moderate severity and some relevance to safe operation, but as an operator-action error it is capped below the higher batch.
rows.sat_rat: Unit-specific HMI displays and clear batch tagging reduce mis-selection, but crowded multi-unit screens still allow wrong-batch pauses during urgent intervention. Selecting the right cycle to pause under urgency can cost seconds of navigation, a minor delay in a crisis. Correctly identifying the target batch among several depends on operator familiarity with the interface and unit naming, a real skill demand under pressure. Pausing the wrong batch can disrupt or spoil an unrelated batch, an infrequent but costly mistake.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether the active batch cycle has fully paused
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether the batch cycle has fully paused is relevant to safe intervention and carries moderate severity, but it is a peripheral confirmation error ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Modern HMIs give explicit hold/paused state indication, making pause confirmation generally clear, though ambiguous or lagging indicators still occur in older or partial systems. State feedback appears on the HMI quickly after a pause, so operators are not left waiting long to know the status. Reading pause/hold status from the HMI requires minimal skill given standard status indicators. Uncertainty about pause state could prompt costly wrong actions, but clear status displays keep such errors limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning the wrong initiating event when responses cascade through the batching system
rows.imp_rat: Assigning the wrong initiating event in a cascade (ambiguity) is a diagnostic-analysis error peripheral to the batching CFJ itself, with consequences mostly extended troubleshooting effort.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence-of-events recording and time-stamped logs help where present, but many systems lack the resolution to reliably isolate the first cause. Untangling a cascade to find the initiating event is time-consuming during a trip. Assigning the true first-cause among cascading responses is a demanding diagnostic skill few operators possess. Chasing the wrong initiating event wastes labor and can trigger unnecessary corrective spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between indicated batch status and the actual process state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between indicated and actual batch status can mislead operators, but such gaps are often reconciled through checks, giving moderate-low relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: State machines and batch execution software (e.g. S88) track status well, yet sensor faults and unmodeled states still create discrepancies between indication and reality. Status updates on the HMI are near real-time, so lag is minor. Interpreting whether indicated status truly matches the physical state still requires process understanding. Acting on a false status can waste a batch, but the monitoring itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required ingredients are unavailable in sufficient quantities for the enlarged batch
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient unavailability for an enlarged batch (delay) relates to scale-up logistics rather than core batching function, and the consequence is typically a schedule slip rather than a ruined product.
rows.sat_rat: ERP/MRP and inventory systems reliably flag shortages before batching, though manual dispensing errors and unlogged stock still occasionally cause surprises. Checking ingredient availability before scaling is a quick step in most planning workflows. Inventory checks are routine and require little specialized skill. Holding sufficient inventory for enlarged batches ties up working capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the batch exceeding allowable hold limits before the batching fault is resolved
rows.imp_rat: The batch exceeding hold limits before a fault is resolved (item 20) can waste material, but it is a fault-recovery timing concern of moderate relevance and modest severity. Held to the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fault diagnosis and repair times vary wildly with no standard fast-recovery practice, so hold-limit breaches during faults are a frequent, poorly served pain. The clock keeps running while a batching fault is diagnosed and fixed, and fault resolution time is unpredictable and often long. Fast fault resolution requires scarce troubleshooting expertise, so the outcome hinges heavily on who is available. Unresolved faults that push a batch past hold limits routinely destroy batch value, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required materials that are unreleased, expired, quarantined, or unavailable before run start
rows.imp_rat: Unreleased/expired/quarantined materials before run start is a readiness gating concern (relevance); if bypassed it risks bad material (severity), but the check is preventive, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Inventory release-status gating reliably blocks unreleased or expired materials before run start. Material-readiness checks are quick where inventory is integrated. System status flags reduce operator responsibility for material readiness. Unavailable or quarantined materials cause delays and loss, mostly guarded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which room or utility conditions deviate from approved limits at readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Room/utility conditions deviating at readiness confirmation is an environmental precondition (relevance) that could affect quality (severity), but is a preventive check, low.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring reliably reports room conditions, though gating on them at run start is uneven. Room/utility condition checks at readiness are near-real-time via SCADA. Automated environmental monitoring reduces operator judgement on readiness conditions. Out-of-limit conditions at start risk the batch, partly guarded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of active-run stoppages caused by unavailable recipe execution
rows.imp_rat: Active-run stoppages from unavailable recipe execution disrupt runs (relevance) and could jeopardize in-progress batches (severity), but is largely availability-driven, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: System redundancy reduces execution outages, but SCADA/MES downtime still halts runs occasionally. Recipe-execution unavailability stops active runs, with recovery time varying. Restoring execution may require IT/engineering support beyond the operator. Active-run stoppages waste in-process material, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which environmental conditions or supporting utilities deviate from recipe-defined limits before execution
rows.imp_rat: Environmental/utility deviations before execution is a precondition concern (relevance) that could affect quality (severity), but is preventive, scored low.
rows.sat_rat: EMS integration flags many out-of-limit conditions, but not all utilities are interlocked to execution start. Environmental monitoring provides near-real-time status before execution. Automated utility and environmental checks reduce operator judgement. Environmental monitoring infrastructure is a significant but common installed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a recipe departure and its documentation in the compounding batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay documenting a recipe departure risks contemporaneous accuracy (relevance) and could obscure the deviation (severity), but the departure is still captured, low.
rows.sat_rat: Delayed departure documentation increases the chance detail is lost or inaccurate. The gap between a departure and its documentation is often long because logging is manual and deferred. Prompt documentation depends on operator diligence amid active processing. No dedicated real-time departure capture exists in most systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records missing required event, condition, action, or disposition details
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records missing event/action/disposition detail reduce record completeness (relevance) and hamper follow-up (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Required-field templates help, but deviation records still frequently miss condition or disposition detail. Templated deviation forms prompt fields but still require after-the-fact completion. Deciding what event, action and disposition details matter depends on operator experience. Form templates are a modest configuration cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records assigned an incorrect occurrence timestamp
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect occurrence timestamps on deviation records weaken chronology (relevance) and can mislead investigations (severity), but are a bounded record defect.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation occurrence times are commonly inaccurate because they are reconstructed rather than captured automatically. Occurrence timestamps are often entered from memory well after the event. Judging the true occurrence time depends on operator recall. Timestamp capture for deviations lacks dedicated tooling in many systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps unintentionally executed more than once during a compounding batch
rows.imp_rat: Steps unintentionally executed twice cause rework (relevance) and could over-process or double-dose (severity), but is a contained execution error, low.
rows.sat_rat: Completed-step locking makes unintended re-execution uncommon where interlocks exist. State tracking prevents accidental re-execution without added delay. System state flags reduce reliance on operators to track completed steps. Step-state logic is included in recipe configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of alarm responses recorded with missing or incorrect action, sequence, or disposition details
rows.imp_rat: Alarm responses with missing/incorrect detail leave documentation gaps (relevance) that can obscure response adequacy (severity), but are peripheral, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm response records commonly miss action, sequence, or disposition detail because capture is manual and post-event. Alarm responses are often logged after handling, delaying accurate detail capture. Recording correct action and sequence depends on operator recall under pressure. Alarm-response documentation tooling is limited in typical setups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between executing a compounding action and documenting it in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay between executing an action and documenting it risks record accuracy/contemporaneity (relevance) and can lose detail (severity), but is a timing gap, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: EBR encourages at-the-moment recording, though manual steps and busy periods still stretch the gap sometimes. Contemporaneous electronic entry keeps the gap between action and documentation short. Guided real-time prompts reduce reliance on operators remembering to record later. Delayed documentation causes reconstruction cost, which EBR contemporaneity reduces.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch events documented with an incorrect timestamp or sequence
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect timestamps/sequence on batch events distort chronology (relevance) and can mislead reconstruction (severity), but are a bounded record defect.
rows.sat_rat: Synchronized system time in EBR makes incorrect timestamps or sequence uncommon, so this is well served. System clocks stamp events instantly, eliminating timing delay. Automatic timestamping removes reliance on operators recording sequence manually. Timestamp errors trigger investigations, but automation keeps them rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement about recipe state, executed parameters, and batch impact during a disruption
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement about recipe state during a disruption can slow correct response (relevance) and confuse impact assessment (severity), but is a transient coordination matter.
rows.sat_rat: With data split across MES, paper, and memory, disagreement about executed state during disruptions is common and poorly resolved. Reconciling differing accounts of recipe state during a disruption consumes time in meetings and record hunts. Establishing a shared truth requires interpreting fragmented data across roles, a skill-intensive reconciliation. Disagreement delays decisions and risks wrong disposition, driving cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved excursions omitted from shift handoff information
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved excursions missing from shift handoff is a communication gap (relevance) that could delay resolution (severity), but is peripheral to core execution.
rows.sat_rat: Informal or minimally structured handoffs routinely lose unresolved items, making this poorly served. Handoff briefings are rushed at shift change, leaving little time to convey every unresolved excursion. Complete handoff depends on the outgoing overseer's recall and thoroughness, a fragile human factor. A dropped excursion can escalate on the next shift, causing costly downstream failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of excursions not linked to the applicable recipe step and affected product units
rows.imp_rat: Excursions not linked to recipe step/affected units weaken impact tracing (relevance) and could understate affected product (severity), kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Step-level and unit-level traceability of excursions is inconsistent, leaving many excursions poorly linked. Linking excursions to specific steps and affected units requires time-consuming manual correlation. Mapping an excursion to the applicable recipe step and impacted units needs process expertise. Poor linkage over- or under-scopes impacted product, driving unnecessary cost or risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among event timestamps, batch identifiers, material lots, recipe revisions, and parameter records
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among timestamps/IDs/lots/revisions weakens record coherence (relevance) and can mislead reconstruction (severity), but is a bounded data-quality issue.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple unsynchronized systems produce timestamp and identifier mismatches that persist as a common data-integrity issue. Reconciling timestamps and identifiers across systems is a slow manual chore. Detecting and resolving inconsistencies requires analysts familiar with each system's quirks. Inconsistent data prolongs investigations and inflates labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that related batches, material lots, recipe revisions, equipment, or processing stages are omitted from investigation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting related batches/lots/revisions from investigation scope is an investigation-thoroughness concern (relevance) that could miss impact (severity), but peripheral to execution.
rows.sat_rat: Genealogy and cross-linking are often fragmented, so relevant related batches, lots and equipment are frequently missed from scope. Assembling all related entities into scope is a slow, largely manual effort. Determining complete investigation scope depends heavily on investigator expertise and system knowledge. Omitted scope causes repeat investigations and expanded impact cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among recipe controls derived from the same applicable requirement
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among recipe controls from the same requirement introduces enforcement ambiguity (relevance) that could cause misexecution (severity), kept low.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely automated linkage from a requirement to all its derived controls, so inconsistencies commonly go undetected. Checking consistency across derived controls is a manual, periodic effort. Ensuring controls from the same requirement agree needs deep requirements knowledge. Inconsistent controls cause conflicting decisions and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between occurrence of a compounding deviation and its visibility in monitored indicators
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a deviation and its visibility in indicators slows detection (relevance) and can prolong an excursion (severity), but is a monitoring latency, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time monitoring helps for parameterized signals but many deviations only surface in periodic indicators, so visibility lag remains. Latency between a deviation occurring and its appearance in indicators can be significant due to batch-cycle reporting. Interpreting when an indicator reflects a specific deviation requires analytical skill. Detection lag extends exposure and increases the cost of any resulting containment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatibilities with compounding equipment and control interfaces remaining unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved equipment/control-interface incompatibilities are an integration reliability concern (relevance) that can disrupt runs (severity), but are peripheral to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Interface incompatibilities are partially surfaced through vendor compatibility lists but many remain unresolved until integration is attempted. Resolving equipment and interface incompatibilities requires integration testing that timelines rarely fully allow. Diagnosing control-interface incompatibilities demands specialized integration engineering skill. Unresolved incompatibilities drive expensive custom middleware and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding exceptions with unverified safe recovery and record continuity
rows.imp_rat: Unverified safe recovery/record continuity for exceptions is an edge-case reliability concern (relevance) with potential but limited consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recovery and record continuity for exceptions is inconsistently verified, so many exception cases remain unproven at recommendation time. Verifying safe recovery and record continuity for exceptions is slow and usually cut short. Confirming safe recovery paths requires operational and control expertise that is scarce. Unverified recovery leads to costly batch loss and record gaps in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required validation evidence that the evaluated approach cannot generate
rows.imp_rat: Validation-evidence generation capability is peripheral to executing recipes (relevance) and its lapse is mainly a documentation gap (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Modern systems generate substantial validation evidence, but gaps in what any given approach can produce are only partially uncovered during assessment. Determining what validation evidence a candidate can generate takes probing that the timeline squeezes. Assessing validation-evidence capability requires CSV expertise that the influencer often must source externally. Missing evidence generation forces expensive manual documentation later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process parameters with inconsistent units, ranges, or precision between recipes and execution controls
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent units/ranges/precision between recipes and controls introduce ambiguity (relevance) that could cause misexecution (severity), but is a bounded configuration issue.
rows.sat_rat: Unit, range, and precision inconsistencies are common and only partially caught by manual review, so mismatches frequently persist. Reconciling units, ranges, and precision across recipes and controls is tedious and often deferred. Detecting unit and precision mismatches requires meticulous domain and engineering attention that is easy to miss. Undetected mismatches cause costly dosing errors and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical recipe behaviors lacking documented test evidence
rows.imp_rat: Critical recipe behaviors lacking test evidence is a validation-completeness concern (relevance) that could leave failure modes unverified (severity), but peripheral to executing runs.
rows.sat_rat: Test protocols and traceability matrices help ensure coverage, but critical behaviors lacking documented evidence still slip through. Documenting test evidence for every critical behavior is laborious and frequently trails execution. Knowing which behaviors are critical and what constitutes adequate evidence requires trained validation judgement. Gaps in evidence are cheap to overlook but costly to remediate during audit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations with undetermined impact on system acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations with undetermined acceptance impact is an evaluation-completeness concern (relevance) with contained consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation management systems track items but impact determinations frequently remain open or ambiguous at the decision point. Determining deviation impact on acceptance is slow because it waits on investigations and cross-functional input. Assessing undetermined impact requires experienced judgement blending technical and quality perspectives. Unresolved deviations carry rework and delay costs that are hard to bound.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compliance evidence unavailable or incomplete when acceptance conditions are formulated
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete compliance evidence when acceptance conditions are formulated is an evidence-gathering concern peripheral to execution (relevance) with contained consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evidence is frequently incomplete when needed because it is scattered and not assembled proactively. Gathering complete compliance evidence at recommendation time is often a slow scramble across systems. Knowing what evidence is required demands regulatory experience that not all reviewers have. Incomplete evidence forces expensive re-reviews and delays batch disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the criteria for determining whether each acceptance condition is satisfied
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance-condition satisfaction criteria is an evaluation-clarity concern (relevance) that could cause misjudgment (severity), but is peripheral to execution.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria are often qualitatively worded, so satisfaction determinations vary by interpreter. Ambiguous acceptance criteria prolong deliberation as reviewers debate interpretation. Resolving ambiguity leans on senior judgement because criteria are rarely fully operationalized. Ambiguity drives rework and inconsistent decisions that carry downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recommended acceptance conditions lacking traceability to a compliance expectation or identified risk
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance conditions lacking traceability to a compliance expectation is an evidence-linkage gap (relevance) with contained consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability is inconsistently documented, so many conditions lack a clear link to an expectation or risk. Building traceability from each condition back to a requirement is a manual, time-consuming documentation step. Establishing sound traceability requires reviewers who understand both the risk and the regulatory basis. Untraceable conditions provoke audit findings and costly justification exercises.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations or exceptions omitted from the acceptance recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations omitted from the acceptance recommendation is an evaluation-completeness gap (relevance) that could pass unaddressed risk (severity), but peripheral, low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-management systems help but linkage into the final recommendation is imperfect, so omissions occur. Tracking every open deviation into the recommendation is a slow manual reconciliation. Ensuring no exception is missed relies on diligent reviewers rather than automated closure gates. Omitted deviations surface later as costly findings and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of claimed DCS cybersecurity capabilities lacking sufficient verification evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unverified cybersecurity capability claims are a procurement-evidence ambiguity (low direct relevance to control execution) with potentially serious downstream security exposure, but the batch ranking caps it at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cybersecurity capability claims are commonly accepted without independent verification evidence, making this one of the least reliably served needs today. Verifying cybersecurity claims demands lengthy specialized review that acquisition schedules seldom accommodate. Assessing DCS/OT cybersecurity evidence requires very scarce ICS-security specialists rarely present on acquisition teams. Unverified security gaps can force expensive post-purchase hardening or breaches, a serious cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered controllers, terminals, and modules that differ from the purchased configuration
rows.imp_rat: Delivered components differing from the purchased configuration is a receiving/procurement mismatch (low relevance to running control) that could delay a project (moderate severity) but is detected before energizing. Capped at 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list and configuration-verification practices at receipt are mature and reliably catch most delivered-versus-ordered discrepancies. Verifying delivered items against the purchased configuration is a quick receiving-inspection task. Checking serial numbers and part lists against the order requires only standard receiving-inspection skill. Configuration mismatches caught at receiving avoid costly field surprises, keeping cost impact low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controllers, I/O modules, and terminals secured in the wrong enclosure or mounting location
rows.imp_rat: Components mounted in the wrong enclosure is an installation defect (low relevance to ongoing control logic) that could create wiring or environmental problems (moderate severity) but is typically caught at commissioning. Bounded to 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Clear labeling and installation drawings make wrong-enclosure placement relatively rare and easily caught during inspection. Verifying enclosure and mounting placement is a fast physical inspection within normal installation timelines. Matching modules to their enclosures is basic labeled work needing little specialized skill. Correct mounting uses standard drawings and labels at negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered components with concealed shipping damage accepted as undamaged
rows.imp_rat: Concealed shipping damage accepted as good is a receiving-inspection defect (low relevance to control operation) that can cause later failures (moderate severity) but is a one-time incoming-goods risk. Bounded to 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Typical practice relies on visual checks, shock/tilt indicators and packing-list counts that reliably catch external damage but routinely let internal or latent damage through, so the outcome remains inconsistent. Standard receiving inspection adds bounded time at the dock, but concealed damage by definition surfaces later, forcing time-consuming reopening of already-accepted crates. Detecting concealed shipping damage on cabinets, cards and terminations demands experienced inspectors who know DCS failure modes, and such judgement is unevenly available across receiving crews. Missed concealed damage is expensive because a defective component that passes inspection can trigger rework, warranty disputes and freight-claim losses once the packaging window has closed.

rows.source_job: Define and configure historian data collection
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical process variables omitted from the historian configuration
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical variables from the historian configuration affects analysis and traceability rather than active control execution (relevance limited); the process still runs, so severity is low. Kept under 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Historian setup is routine, but omissions of critical variables recur where the requirement list is incomplete. Building and checking historian configuration against the critical-variable list is a moderate, schedulable task. Deciding which process variables are critical for historization requires process knowledge that varies across teams. Historian configuration uses standard tools with low incremental cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe fields left blank before recipe release
rows.imp_rat: Required recipe fields left blank before release is a configuration-completeness gap with modest severity and limited centrality to the actual batching operation.
rows.sat_rat: Most modern recipe systems enforce mandatory-field checks at release, so this need is acceptably served though configuration gaps remain. Checking for blank required fields adds a review pass before release. Knowing which fields are truly required needs recipe-management familiarity but is fairly routine. Blank fields caught at release cost little; caught in production they cost batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of logged records altered without a traceable history
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable log alterations weaken audit integrity, a compliance concern, but this administrative traceability error is largely peripheral to the control/configure/monitor CFJ; low relevance keeps it well under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Part -compliant audit trails are standard in pharma control software and make untraceable alteration of records genuinely rare. Audit-trail systems capture changes automatically without adding operator time. Little skill is needed to benefit from tamper-evident logging once the compliant system is deployed. Compliant audit-trail software carries licensing cost but averts far larger data-integrity penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unexpected resets of the current hierarchy position while moving between plant, area, unit, and device levels
rows.imp_rat: Unexpected resets of hierarchy navigation position are a UI navigation annoyance peripheral to the control CFJ (low relevance); the consequence is lost place while browsing, easily re-navigated (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Modern navigation frameworks preserve context reasonably well, though intermittent resets during level transitions still occur under load or on refresh. When the hierarchy position resets, operators lose a few seconds re-navigating, which matters during fast-moving situations. Re-orienting after an unexpected reset takes familiarity with the plant tree but not deep expertise. There is little direct resource cost beyond wasted operator attention.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of report entries lacking clear batch, equipment, phase, or event attribution
rows.imp_rat: Report entries lacking batch, equipment, phase, or event attribution is a reporting-quality issue distant from real-time control (low relevance); severity is limited to traceability confusion, keeping it low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-aware reporting systems (e.g., S88-structured records) attach context automatically in mature deployments, but entries lacking clear attribution still appear where integration is incomplete. Poorly attributed report entries force reviewers to spend time hunting for context. Reconstructing missing batch or phase attribution demands someone who knows the process history. Ambiguous attribution can trigger costly investigations or compliance findings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operator annotations attached to the wrong process event or alarm occurrence
rows.imp_rat: Annotations attached to the wrong process event or alarm degrade traceability (low relevance to core control); severity is limited to misattributed records, easily corrected, so it stays low under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Manual selection of the target event in busy alarm lists frequently produces mis-linking, and few systems enforce or verify correct association. A misattached annotation costs time only when someone later untangles the mismatch. Attaching a note to the correct event under alarm floods demands attentive, experienced operators. Misattached annotations rarely cost resources directly but can mislead costly reviews.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of operator annotations containing inaccurate process context
rows.imp_rat: Operator annotations with inaccurate process context is a record-quality issue not central to controlling devices (low relevance); severity is modest as it misleads later review rather than affecting live operation.
rows.sat_rat: Annotation accuracy is almost entirely dependent on fallible manual entry with little automated validation, so inaccurate context is a chronic, poorly served problem. Inaccurate annotation context does not cost time up front; the harm surfaces later when someone relies on it. Capturing accurate process context depends heavily on the operator's judgement and understanding at the moment of writing. Wrong context can drive costly wrong conclusions in investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process events or alarm occurrences left without a required operator annotation
rows.imp_rat: Process events or alarms left without a required operator annotation is a documentation-completeness gap peripheral to active control (low relevance); severity is moderate for later audit/analysis but does not impair the live monitoring function.
rows.sat_rat: Some systems flag mandatory-comment alarms, but coverage is patchy and events routinely pass without the required annotation. Missing required annotations create delay only when reviewers must chase operators for the explanation later. Knowing which events require a note relies on operator training and procedural discipline. Un-annotated events can cause costly compliance gaps during audits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time renewal authorization remains pending as license expiration approaches
rows.imp_rat: Renewal authorization pending as expiration approaches is an administrative licensing-timing concern remote from control/monitor duties (very low relevance); severity is moderate since a lapse could disable software, but it is procedurally avoidable and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Approval outcomes are consistent once submitted; the problem is timing, not whether renewal ultimately happens. Renewal authorization typically stalls in procurement and approval queues, and this waiting time is exactly the error being targeted. Pushing an authorization through requires little technical skill, mostly administrative persistence. Expedited or lapsed-license handling can incur penalty fees or emergency purchasing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deployed control-software instances omitted from the renewal
rows.imp_rat: Deployed instances omitted from the renewal is a licensing-administration error (very low relevance to control/configure/monitor); severity is moderate since an unrenewed instance could lapse, but this is easily remediated and capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Asset inventories in industrial estates are frequently incomplete, so shadow or forgotten control-software instances are routinely missed at renewal. Reconciling deployed instances against a renewal list is a periodic manual task that is not itself time-critical. Tracking every deployed instance requires disciplined inventory practice more than deep expertise. An omitted instance means unlicensed or unsupported software running, which carries compliance and downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of broken references encountered during historical batch review after the purge
rows.imp_rat: Broken references in historical batch review after a purge is a data-housekeeping annoyance (low severity) far removed from controlling devices (low relevance).
rows.sat_rat: Purge routines rarely fully account for referential integrity, so broken links during historical review remain a recurring problem. Detecting broken references after a purge is slow because it usually surfaces only during later batch review. Predicting and preserving cross-record dependencies before purging demands data-model expertise that is often lacking. Broken references trigger costly investigations and reconstruction during regulated batch review.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of records with conflicting purge eligibility under applicable retention rules
rows.imp_rat: Records with conflicting purge eligibility under retention rules is a data-governance ambiguity (low relevance to control) with limited direct consequence (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Retention logic is frequently ambiguous and inconsistently encoded, so records with conflicting eligibility are common. Resolving conflicting purge eligibility is slow because retention rules must be reconciled record by record. Interpreting overlapping retention rules requires records-management and regulatory judgement that is scarce on plant teams. Wrongful purge or over-retention creates compliance exposure and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of selected-variable samples missing from the inspection interval
rows.imp_rat: Selected-variable samples missing from an inspection interval affects a data-sampling sub-task, not the primary control loop (low relevance); severity is modest since gaps typically degrade analysis rather than cause process harm.
rows.sat_rat: Historians buffer and backfill reasonably well, yet dropped samples from network or store-and-forward failures still puncture inspection intervals often enough to matter. Missing samples send analysts back to re-query or wait for the historian, adding delay. Judging whether gaps are significant requires process knowledge but not rare expertise. Gaps waste prior collection effort and can force costly re-runs of an inspection.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of divergence between model predictions and actual sterile fill-finish process behavior
rows.imp_rat: Model-prediction divergence from actual fill-finish behavior is an advanced-control/modeling concern peripheral to basic DCS control (low relevance) with moderate product-quality stakes. Bounded to 3.0.
rows.sat_rat: Models drift from real fill-finish behavior as conditions change and are rarely re-tuned promptly, leaving prediction divergence a chronic and poorly-served problem. Models compute predictions quickly, so latency is not the limiting issue. Building and maintaining accurate process models demands scarce advanced-control and data-science expertise. APC and modeling packages are costly to license and even costlier to keep re-validated over time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shared production and quality values that differ from their source values
rows.imp_rat: Shared production/quality values diverging from source is a data-integration integrity issue (limited relevance to core control) with moderate compliance/decision impact. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interface engineering keeps most shared values aligned, but mapping errors, latency and manual re-entry still produce recurring discrepancies between systems. Interface transfers between DCS, MES and quality systems run frequently but not always in real time. Reconciling divergent shared values often falls to specialists chasing mapping and timestamp mismatches. Robust integration middleware and validation carry significant cost and are often minimally implemented.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch execution events omitted from the DCS record
