Leaks found during testing or startup add repair and retest time, but detection is usually prompt. Making sound leak-free connections is a well-practiced fitter skill supported by torque specs and gaskets. Fluid leaks waste product and require cleanup, though testing catches most before production quantities are involved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of mechanical stress imposed on system connection points by misaligned piping
rows.imp_rat: Mechanical stress from misaligned piping on connection points risks leaks over time (relevance) and potential failures (severity), but this sits below the prior batch at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Good fabrication practice and strain gauges reduce imposed stress, but hidden pipe-strain defects still slip through routinely. Correcting misaligned piping to relieve connection stress adds fit-up and rework time during assembly. Proper alignment and strain-free fit-up depend on skilled fitters and their judgement about acceptable tolerances. Misalignment stress can damage flanges, seals, and nozzles, driving replacement and repair costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that final commissioning changes are omitted from the baseline documentation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting final commissioning changes from baseline docs is a records-completeness error peripheral to the preparation job (low relevance), correctable retroactively (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late commissioning changes are the ones most likely to escape documentation, so omissions from the baseline remain a common problem. Omitted final commissioning changes cost time later when the baseline no longer matches reality. Capturing last-minute field changes into the baseline depends on disciplined change control that hinges on individual conscientiousness. An out-of-date baseline drives future errors whose remediation cost outweighs the modest effort to document changes.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of control parameter and recipe values recorded incorrectly in the baseline documentation
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect control parameter/recipe values in baseline documentation is a documentation-accuracy defect adjacent to the CFJ (low relevance) and correctable before operation (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual documentation of parameters remains error-prone with limited cross-checking, so incorrectly recorded baseline values are a common and consequential failure. Incorrect baseline parameter values surface later as troubleshooting time when the system behaves unexpectedly. Accurate transcription of recipe and control values into baseline docs depends on careful expert review that is easy to shortcut under schedule pressure. Wrong baseline values propagate into off-spec batches whose material and rework cost can be substantial.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required instrument calibration certificates missing from the closeout documentation
rows.imp_rat: Missing calibration certificates in closeout documentation is an administrative documentation gap only tangentially tied to the batching job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration documentation is a well-known deliverable yet certificates are routinely incomplete at closeout, making this a persistent gap. Chasing missing calibration certificates at closeout adds late scrambling that delays turnover. Knowing which certificates are required and matching them to instruments takes documentation discipline more than deep skill. Missing certificates can force re-calibration or re-inspection, incurring real avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of documented pipe routes or instrument connections that differ from the physical installation
rows.imp_rat: Documented pipe routes differing from physical installation is an as-built documentation discrepancy tangential to batching (low relevance) and fixable via redlines (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: As-built reconciliation is notoriously incomplete in practice, so documented routes frequently diverge from the physical plant. As-built discrepancies are usually discovered during troubleshooting, adding significant investigation time downstream. Detecting that documented routing differs from physical installation requires diligent walkdowns by experienced personnel who may be scarce. Undocumented deviations cause misdirected work and potential safety incidents that are expensive to correct.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested unsafe conditions that fail to inhibit startup or continued operation
rows.imp_rat: Tested unsafe conditions failing to inhibit startup is safety-significant, yet the batch ranking caps importance; held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Permissive and inhibit logic is routinely tested, yet untested condition combinations occasionally slip through, leaving a real residual gap. Startup-inhibit testing is part of scheduled commissioning and adds bounded time. Designing tests that exercise every unsafe condition demands strong safety-engineering judgement that is not universally available. Failure to inhibit startup can cause damaging events, but preventive testing is standard budgeted work.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the time from emergency-stop actuation until liquid transfer and hazardous motion cease
rows.imp_rat: Though emergency-stop response time is inherently safety-critical, the batch ceiling forces both proxies low; scored at the 2.0 cap despite genuinely higher real-world stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Hardwired safety-rated e-stop circuits are a mature, code-driven practice that reliably halts transfer and motion quickly, so this need is well served. Emergency-stop response time is validated during commissioning and is inherently fast by design. E-stop verification follows well-defined procedures requiring modest specialized skill. Standard safety-rated hardware delivers rapid shutdown without significant added cost.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of representative loss-of-containment conditions not detected by containment sensors
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loss-of-containment conditions carry real hazard weight, but per the batch ceiling importance cannot exceed 2.0; held at cap.
rows.sat_rat: Leak and containment detection is often partial and difficult to test across all failure modes, so meaningful detection gaps persist. Containment-sensor coverage testing adds moderate time to the validation program. Determining representative loss-of-containment scenarios and confirming detection needs specialized process-safety expertise that is scarce. Undetected containment loss risks costly spills and environmental exposure, justifying but not always driving thorough testing.

rows.stk: Safety engineers, commissioning engineers, system operators.
rows.source_job: Verify safety interlocks and shutdown functions
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested hazardous conditions producing missing or incorrect alarm indications
rows.imp_rat: Missing or incorrect alarm indications for hazardous conditions matters for safety, but the batch ceiling constrains both proxies; capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm testing is standard but alarm-to-condition mapping errors are common enough that incorrect indications still appear during qualification. Alarm verification is included in commissioning testing without major schedule impact. Confirming correct alarm indication for each hazardous condition requires methodical test planning that leans on experienced engineers. Missing or wrong alarms rarely cause immediate cost but raise operational risk cheaply mitigated by testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of vendor proposals missing required technical, commercial, or regulatory evidence
rows.imp_rat: Vendor proposals missing evidence is a procurement/administrative error far from the running control job (low relevance); it slows selection but is caught before deployment, so severity is minor. Well within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: RFP compliance matrices are a common practice that reliably flags missing technical and commercial evidence, with regulatory gaps being the weaker area. Screening proposals for completeness is routine and reasonably quick with a checklist. Basic completeness screening needs modest procurement skill, though judging regulatory sufficiency requires more expertise. Incomplete proposals cause rework cycles but rarely large direct costs at this stage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the stated support horizon and the expected DCS service life
rows.imp_rat: A mismatch between stated support horizon and service life is a lifecycle-planning/procurement error far from real-time control (low relevance) with slow-onset, manageable consequences. Kept well under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Support-lifecycle mismatches are frequently underestimated because vendor roadmaps are vague and buyers rarely formally reconcile them against a multi-decade DCS service life. Comparing vendor support horizons against expected service life is a fairly fast lookup during evaluation. Interpreting lifecycle and obsolescence roadmaps needs some asset-management judgement that is only partly available. A support-horizon shortfall can force premature costly migration, a significant lifecycle cost risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of timing misalignment among control devices during sterile fill-finish execution
rows.imp_rat: Timing misalignment among control devices during sterile fill-finish touches the monitoring/control role, but as a downstream execution concern its relevance to the software's core job is modest and consequences here are bounded within the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Deterministic fieldbuses and time-synced controllers coordinate devices well in validated lines, yet residual timing drift remains a monitored risk. Timing misalignment among fill-finish devices is not primarily a delay; it is a coordination defect. Tuning multi-device synchronization for sterile fill demands specialized controls engineering. Misalignment risks spoiled sterile product, a high-value loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the report applies incorrect boundaries to the defined shift or production run
rows.imp_rat: A report applying incorrect shift/production-run boundaries affects reporting accuracy rather than control operation (low relevance) with correctable reporting-error consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reporting tools with defined shift calendars handle standard boundaries reliably, though edge cases like shift changes, DST, or non-standard runs still slip through. Wrong shift boundaries force a re-run of the report, costing turnaround time. Configuring correct run boundaries takes a knowledgeable engineer but is a one-time setup. Boundary errors mostly waste rework effort rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from the end of the shift or production run until the report is available for review
rows.imp_rat: Delay from shift end until a report is available affects reporting timeliness, not control (low relevance), with modest review-scheduling consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reports occasionally stall on data-source delays or job failures, but the standard scheduled-generation practice is mature and dependable. Automated end-of-shift report generation typically makes reports available within minutes of run close, largely answering this need. Scheduled reporting requires little operator skill once configured. Automation removes the labor cost of manual report compilation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process events or alarm occurrences with conflicting operator annotations
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting operator annotations on events/alarms affect record clarity rather than the core control function (low relevance), and the consequence is documentation ambiguity that can be reconciled (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Across shifts and multiple operators there is seldom a mechanism to detect or reconcile conflicting annotations, so contradictions persist unresolved. Conflicting annotations cost time to reconcile during review rather than at capture. Resolving contradictory notes requires senior judgement about which account is correct. Conflicts can prolong costly investigations but consume little material.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between the process event or alarm occurrence and its operator annotation
rows.imp_rat: Latency between an event/alarm and its operator annotation affects record timeliness rather than control execution (low relevance) with modest documentation consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Some HMIs prompt immediate comments on key alarms, but workload commonly pushes annotation well after the event. The lag between an event and its annotation is the very error here, and operators often defer notes until a quieter moment. Prompt annotation depends on disciplined operators remembering context accurately, which erodes as the delay grows. Delayed notes carry little direct cost unless the delay degrades their accuracy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from issuing a command until execution feedback is available to the operator
rows.imp_rat: Command-to-feedback delay touches the monitoring aspect of the CFJ, but the ranking caps it low; when it occurs the operator experiences lag rather than failure, so severity stays modest.
rows.sat_rat: Feedback delivery is highly consistent under normal conditions, with only occasional lag during network congestion or heavy alarm load. Modern control systems deliver command-to-feedback confirmation in near real time over the HMI, so perceived latency is typically sub-second for normal operations. No special operator skill is needed to receive execution feedback since it is presented automatically on the interface. The responsive feedback loop is a standard designed-in feature carrying no incremental operating cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of verified initial configuration elements omitted from the archived baseline
rows.imp_rat: Omitting verified initial configuration elements from the archived baseline is a records-completeness concern peripheral to the CFJ of actively controlling and monitoring devices (low relevance), and consequences are recoverable documentation gaps (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: When baselining is automated by the configuration tool, verified elements are captured completely, but manual archiving still drops elements occasionally. Confirming that every verified element made it into the archive is a checklist task added at baseline time. Ensuring completeness is procedural and needs little specialized skill. Archiving configuration elements is cheap storage-wise.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unverified configuration changes included in the baseline
rows.imp_rat: Unverified configuration changes included in the baseline can corrupt the reference used for control comparison; modestly relevant but its consequences are held within the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Baseline updates often fold in changes without an explicit verification gate, so unverified changes slip into the baseline. Screening out unverified changes before baselining adds a gating step to the update process. Judging whether a change was properly verified before promotion requires reviewer discipline and process knowledge. The gating is a procedural cost rather than a material one.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required traceability identifiers missing from the archived baseline
rows.imp_rat: Missing traceability identifiers in the archived baseline is a documentation-metadata gap far from the core control job (low relevance), causing audit inconvenience rather than operational failure (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability identifiers are frequently entered manually and are inconsistently applied, so required identifiers are sometimes missing from the archive. Backfilling missing traceability identifiers later is slow and error-prone. Applying correct identifiers requires knowing the traceability schema, a modest procedural skill. Identifier tagging is inexpensive when built into the archiving workflow.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of quality-critical events missing from the diagnostic and event logs for the reviewed production period
rows.imp_rat: Missing quality-critical events in diagnostic logs weakens monitoring completeness, a supporting facet of the CFJ (low relevance); it can obscure quality issues but the ranking caps its importance low (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated event logging captures most critical events reliably, though buffer overruns, filtering, or unconfigured tags still cause some to be missing. Diagnostic and event logs are captured automatically in real time, so quality-critical events are recorded without added delay when logging is enabled. Ensuring the right events are configured to log requires up-front engineering setup, and gaps come from missed configuration rather than ongoing effort. Comprehensive logging consumes storage and configuration effort but is generally affordable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of timestamp misalignment across control-component log entries for the same incident
rows.imp_rat: Timestamp misalignment across log entries for one incident degrades forensic correlation (moderate severity), but as a logging concern it is capped at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Where time synchronization is deployed alignment is decent, but many control components still run on unsynchronized or drifting clocks, leaving recurring misalignment for the same incident. Aligning misaligned timestamps during incident analysis adds correlation time. Correcting for clock skew across components requires expertise to know which sources drift and by how much. Time-sync infrastructure like NTP has modest cost and is often already present.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of log entries that cannot be associated with the affected batch, control device, or operator action
rows.imp_rat: Log entries not tied to a batch, device, or operator action hamper traceability and post-incident analysis (moderate severity), but this is a supporting audit concern that must remain within the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many logs lack consistent contextual tagging, so a persistent share of entries cannot be tied to their affected batch, device, or action. Manually correlating orphan log entries to a batch, device, or operator consumes significant investigation time. Associating ambiguous entries demands deep system knowledge and context that only experienced staff possess. Unassociable entries have little direct cost but waste analyst effort during investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recalibration changes lacking required approval or audit records
rows.imp_rat: Recalibration changes lacking approval/audit records is a documentation-compliance error; relevance to the core control job is peripheral and severity, though regulatory, is held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recalibration approval and audit trails are only partly automated in many plants, so some calibration changes still lack the required records. Verifying that every recalibration carried the required approval adds review time when records are scattered. Following the calibration approval workflow correctly is procedural rather than expertise-intensive. Recording approvals is inexpensive where a calibration management system is already in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of command-and-response exchanges lacking complete verification records
rows.imp_rat: Command-and-response exchanges lacking complete verification records weaken auditability of control actions (moderate severity), but as a records concern it stays within the 2.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Some protocols log confirmations, but complete end-to-end verification records for every exchange are inconsistently captured. Verifying command-and-response completeness after the fact takes time, but the missing verification records are the primary issue. Interpreting whether a command-response exchange was fully verified requires protocol and control expertise. Incomplete verification records seldom cost money directly until they undermine an incident investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that accounts remain active after their holders no longer require access
rows.imp_rat: Accounts lingering active after holders no longer need access is an access-hygiene issue only tangential to running the control software (low relevance); the security exposure is real but latent and rarely realized (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Deprovisioning frequently falls through the cracks when HR and control-system access processes are not linked, so stale accounts persist. The lag between a holder losing their need for access and the account actually being disabled is often long because deprovisioning relies on manual notification. Disabling an account is a routine administrative action needing minimal expertise. Lingering active accounts are a security liability more than a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that authorized production personnel are unable to access required control functions after an access change
rows.imp_rat: Delay in production personnel regaining control-function access after an access change touches operations but the batch ceiling caps it; relevance and severity are held low as ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning eventually completes reliably; the recurring issue is the interim delay rather than failure to grant. The delay before authorized personnel can actually use required control functions after an access change is the core error, and manual provisioning makes this lag common. Granting the correct access requires knowing the role-to-permission mapping, a modest but real administrative skill. Blocked personnel can idle production while waiting for access, incurring downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of account and permission changes lacking a complete, attributable audit record
rows.imp_rat: Permission changes lacking an attributable audit record is a governance/compliance gap unrelated to controlling devices (low relevance), with severity limited to audit and accountability exposure (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Where identity-integrated control systems are deployed, permission changes are automatically and attributably logged, so complete audit records are the norm on modern installations. Reconstructing missing change history after the fact is slow and sometimes impossible. Producing an attributable record requires disciplined process adherence more than scarce expertise. Audit-logging capability is standard in modern access-control modules, so the capability itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required operational records omitted from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required operational records from the archive is an archival-completeness issue distinct from active device control (low relevance), with recoverable record-keeping consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated data historians reliably archive most operational records, so omissions are relatively uncommon where a historian is deployed. Reconstructing an omitted operational record after archiving may be impossible, not just slow. Knowing which records are required for the archive is procedural and codified in standards. Storage for operational records is cheap, so omission is rarely a cost decision.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of unauthorized or unintended configuration changes omitted from the audit
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized changes omitted from the audit is an audit-completeness concern rather than core control; relevance is peripheral and consequences, while compliance-related, are constrained by the batch ranking to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without continuous change capture, some unauthorized or out-of-band modifications escape the audit entirely, undermining its completeness. Detecting unauthorized changes after the fact requires slow forensic comparison rather than real-time catch. Recognizing an unintended change against expected state requires configuration expertise. Change-detection tooling exists but comprehensive coverage across a plant is costly to deploy.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of comparing the current configuration against an outdated or unapproved baseline
rows.imp_rat: Comparing current configuration against an outdated baseline weakens drift detection reliability; somewhat relevant to monitoring but the consequence is misleading comparisons, held to the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Baseline version control is inconsistently practiced, so comparisons against stale or unapproved baselines happen with some regularity. Confirming that the comparison baseline is current adds a verification step before every drift check. Knowing which baseline is the approved one requires configuration-management discipline more than deep skill. Maintaining baselines is a low-cost activity within existing tools.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of approved configuration changes incorrectly classified as drift
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying approved changes as drift generates false alarms in configuration monitoring, mildly relevant to the CFJ; consequences are limited to wasted review effort, so severity is low. Capped by batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration-monitoring tools often lack a clean link to the change-approval record, so approved changes are commonly misflagged as drift. Investigating false drift alerts consumes technician time chasing changes that were legitimately approved. Distinguishing approved change from true drift requires knowing the approval history, which demands cross-referencing skill. False positives waste labor but carry little direct material cost.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of retained chemical residue streams assigned an incorrect regulatory classification
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying retained chemical residue streams is a regulatory/administrative error with only modest severity and almost no relevance to executing the liquid batching job itself.
rows.sat_rat: Classification relies on manual judgement against complex regulations, so incorrect assignments recur despite established waste-broker practice. Classifying retained residue streams involves slow manual review of SDS and regulatory tables that delays shipment. Correct regulatory classification requires scarce hazardous-waste specialists interpreting shifting rules. Misclassification triggers fines, rejected loads, and rehandling costs that hit the disposal budget hard.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the specified batching system configuration falls short of the required batch-volume range
rows.imp_rat: A configuration falling short of the required batch-volume range limits capability, but it is a specification-fit defect peripheral to executing the CFJ (relevance low) and its consequences are capped by the batch-3 ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Batch-volume range requirements are usually explicit and reliably checked, with only occasional shortfalls. Checking specified configuration against the batch-volume range is a quick verification against the URS. Confirming batch-volume coverage is a fairly routine engineering check requiring modest specialized skill. Configuration shortfalls mean costly upgrades, but the verification cost is minimal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rental candidates missing qualification, cleaning, or material-compatibility records required for sterile fill-finish use
rows.imp_rat: Missing qualification or material-compatibility records on rental candidates is a documentation gap peripheral to executing a batch; relevance is low and severity contained since it concerns procurement records rather than direct batch preparation.
rows.sat_rat: Rental fleets routinely lack the full sterile-use documentation, making this one of the least reliably served needs in acquisition. Assembling qualification, cleaning and compatibility records for rental candidates is slow and often stalls on missing paperwork. Judging whether a rental unit's records suffice for sterile fill-finish use demands scarce quality expertise applied to unfamiliar assets. Missing rental records force costly requalification or disqualification, and chasing them consumes staff effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required sterile fill-finish capabilities omitted from the quoted scope remain undetected
rows.imp_rat: Undetected omission of sterile fill-finish scope from a quote is a quoting/scoping error somewhat tangential to physically batching liquid (low relevance); consequences are contractual (moderate severity). Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Omissions of unstated capabilities are notoriously hard to catch and standard practice frequently misses them until commissioning. Detecting capabilities silently omitted from quoted scope requires slow, line-by-line scope reconciliation. Recognizing that a required capability is simply absent from a quote demands deep familiarity with what a complete sterile system must include. Undetected scope gaps become expensive post-award additions, while the checking effort itself is modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned simulation interruptions during a practice session
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned simulation interruptions during a practice session affect training rather than live control of devices (low relevance), and the consequence is a disrupted practice run without production impact (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature simulation platforms run practice sessions fairly stably, but crashes, resource contention, or license hiccups still interrupt sessions occasionally. An unplanned interruption stops the exercise and forces a restart, costing session time. Recovering from a simulation crash may need instructor or IT intervention. Interruptions waste allocated training hours and attention.

rows.source_job: Notify affected stakeholders of system retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of affected operations, safety, and maintenance stakeholders omitted from DCS retirement notifications
rows.imp_rat: Missing stakeholders from retirement notifications is a communication/administrative gap almost unrelated to the control job (very low relevance); surprise for maintenance or safety staff has moderate consequences. Held below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Change-management processes usually reach the main stakeholders, but peripheral or downstream parties are still sometimes omitted. Issuing retirement notifications is fast once the stakeholder list is assembled. Identifying all affected operations, safety, and maintenance stakeholders needs organizational knowledge but not deep technical skill. Notification is essentially a communication task with negligible material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of approving industrial control software that does not support an in-use firmware or protocol variant
rows.imp_rat: Approving software that lacks support for an in-use firmware or protocol variant is a selection error affecting compatibility; relevant but pre-deployment, and its consequences are bounded within the ceiling for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility lists rarely enumerate every firmware/protocol variant, so approvals still occasionally proceed on unsupported combinations. Verifying firmware/protocol variant support requires cross-referencing that lengthens the evaluation phase. Confirming support for a specific in-use firmware or protocol variant demands deep controls-engineering knowledge of the installed base. Discovering unsupported variants after purchase forces expensive remediation or replacement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict or obsolescence among available compatibility claims for the devices in use
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting or obsolete compatibility claims is a procurement/reference-data nuisance peripheral to executing control (low relevance) with consequences limited to selection confusion (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility claims are frequently outdated or contradictory between OEM and integrator sources, and there is no authoritative reconciled feed, so surprises persist. Chasing current compatibility matrices across vendor sites and datasheets adds substantial lookup time before a decision. Interpreting whether a claim is stale or conflicting requires seasoned engineers familiar with the specific device fleet. The effort is mostly labor rather than direct spend, so cost impact is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a package from an unauthorized source is accepted as authentic
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a package from an unauthorized source is a supply-chain authenticity check at install time, tangential to ongoing control (low relevance); scored modestly per the batch ranking. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Code signing and official distribution reduce unauthorized-source risk substantially, though social-engineering and unofficial resellers still occasionally slip through. Verifying source authenticity via signatures or trusted portals is quick within established procurement channels. Downloading only from official vendor portals is a simple, teachable practice. Authenticated distribution channels add little to no direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a corrupted package is accepted as intact
rows.imp_rat: Accepting a corrupted package as intact is an install-time integrity check tangential to ongoing device control (low relevance); while consequences could be notable, the ranking places it here so it is scored modestly. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cryptographic hashes and signed packages are mature, widely deployed practice that catches corruption reliably when applied. Checksum and hash verification is near-instant and adds negligible time. Running a hash comparison requires minimal skill with widely available tooling. Verification tools are free or bundled, imposing essentially no cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a verified package is altered before installation handoff
rows.imp_rat: A verified package being altered before installation handoff is an install-chain integrity concern tangential to ongoing control (low relevance); scored modestly per the batch ceiling. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Signed packages and handoff verification are effective, but gaps between verification and install are not always closed, leaving occasional exposure. Re-verifying integrity at installation handoff is a fast repeat of hashing. Maintaining chain-of-custody through handoff needs disciplined process but modest skill. Controls are inexpensive, relying on existing IT hygiene.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that registration data from the sterile fill-finish control environment cannot reach the vendor
rows.imp_rat: Registration data from the sterile fill-finish environment failing to reach the vendor affects support connectivity (moderate severity) but is peripheral to the control job and capped at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Network isolation in sterile environments frequently blocks registration data paths, and workarounds are ad hoc rather than a reliable standard. Air-gapped sterile fill-finish environments require manual data-transfer workarounds that slow registration data reaching the vendor. Moving registration data out of a segregated GxP network without violating controls demands specialized IT/OT and compliance expertise. Manual transfer and compliant intermediaries add labor cost but little material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an unsupported combination of operating system, virtualization platform, and database is overlooked during compatibility confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking an unsupported OS/virtualization/database combination affects deployment compatibility, tangential to the runtime CFJ (low relevance); it can cause install failures but is caught before production (bounded severity), and ranking caps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor compatibility matrices rarely cover every OS/hypervisor/database permutation, so unsupported combinations are still overlooked. Confirming full OS/virtualization/database stack support requires cross-checking multiple matrices, adding time. Assessing an unsupported stack combination demands IT-infrastructure expertise alongside controls knowledge. An overlooked unsupported combination forces costly platform changes post-purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compatibility is confirmed when available processing, memory, storage, or network capacity is insufficient
rows.imp_rat: Falsely confirming compatibility when processing, memory, storage, or network capacity is insufficient can cause control failures (moderate severity), but the ranking places it below the prior batch, so both proxies stay at or below 2.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility is frequently confirmed on nominal specs while real peak capacity is insufficient, and standard datasheet checks miss these mismatches. Capacity fit-checks against processing, memory, storage and network are often deferred to deployment, adding rework time when confirmation proves wrong. Accurately mapping software resource demands to available infrastructure needs specialized sizing expertise that acquisition staff seldom have. Over- or under-provisioning driven by false compatibility confirmations wastes hardware and integration spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the purchased license entitlements and the required features, capacity, deployment instances, or subscription period
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between purchased license entitlements and required features/capacity/period is a procurement-fit issue peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded cost/compliance impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendor feature matrices and eval licenses reduce mismatch, yet capacity and subscription-period misfits still recur at acquisition. Feature/capacity mapping is done during evaluation but back-and-forth to confirm subscription terms extends the cycle. Matching required features and deployment instances to license editions demands product-specific expertise that not every buyer has. Buying the wrong edition or period wastes money and triggers rework purchases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between licensed entitlements and the intended tag count, seats, and deployment scope
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between licensed entitlements and intended tag/seat/deployment scope is a licensing-accuracy matter peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded compliance/cost fallout (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendor sizing worksheets help, but scope mismatches still slip through because deployment plans evolve after the license is purchased. Mapping intended tag counts and seats to entitlements is a defined step but re-scoping late causes delay. It takes an engineer who understands both the plant's tag architecture and the vendor's licensing metrics to avoid mismatch. Getting it wrong forces costly true-ups or unused seats, and the estimation tools are only partial.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of licensing expense exceeding the approved lifecycle budget
rows.imp_rat: Licensing expense exceeding the approved lifecycle budget is a purely administrative/financial concern with essentially no bearing on the software's ability to control, configure, and monitor devices (low relevance); overspend is a bounded, recoverable budget consequence (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Overruns recur predictably because true multi-year licensing cost is hard to pin down at approval time despite standard budgeting practice. Budget reconciliation happens during quote review but iterative negotiation cycles stretch the timeline before a final licensing figure is locked. Procurement and controls engineers together can estimate licensing needs, though translating tag/seat requirements into vendor SKUs still demands experienced judgement. Quotes, budget approval templates and lifecycle cost worksheets exist, but opaque vendor pricing tiers and mid-cycle add-ons routinely push spend past the approved envelope.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that claimed cybersecurity capabilities differ from observable behavior in the target industrial environment
rows.imp_rat: Claimed cybersecurity capabilities differing from observed behavior is a security-assurance gap tangential to core control functionality (low relevance); it poses risk but is capped low by ranking (bounded severity).
rows.sat_rat: Vendor cybersecurity claims routinely diverge from observable behavior in real plant conditions, and no standard acceptance test reliably catches this gap during acquisition. Verifying that claimed cybersecurity capabilities match real behavior requires slow, iterative testing in the target environment that current acquisition timelines rarely accommodate. Interpreting vendor security claims against observed device behavior demands scarce OT-security expertise that most acquiring teams lack. Independent validation of security claims is expensive, so buyers usually rely on vendor attestations rather than pay for full verification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that cited cybersecurity certifications do not apply to the proposed software version or configuration
rows.imp_rat: Cybersecurity certifications not applying to the proposed version is a procurement/assurance mismatch peripheral to control operation (low relevance); it can create compliance gaps but is caught during evaluation (bounded severity), capped low by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Certifications are often cited generically without version/configuration scoping, and buyers rarely verify applicability, leaving this poorly served. Confirming that certifications apply to the exact version/configuration requires slow cross-referencing of certification scopes. Interpreting whether a cybersecurity certification covers the proposed version and configuration demands specialized security-compliance expertise. Relying on inapplicable certifications can force costly re-assessment or rejection later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required historical and live process values missing from the selected analysis window
rows.imp_rat: Missing values from a selected analysis window impairs offline study, not the DCS's active control mission (low relevance); the gap is an inconvenience easily re-queried, so low severity. Within ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Store-and-forward buffering preserves most data through outages, but compression, deadbands and collector gaps still leave some values missing from analysis windows. Historian retrieval over a chosen window is fast for typical query ranges. Recognizing and dealing with gaps in retrieved data requires analyst awareness of collection settings. Historian capacity and high-resolution collection cost money, and undersized systems drop or decimate data.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production unavailability beyond the maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged sterile fill-finish unavailability beyond the maintenance window is downstream of batching and only moderately central to the batching CFJ (relevance low); consequences can be real but the ranking places this below batch 2, capping severity.
rows.sat_rat: Return-to-service timing is unpredictable because unexpected findings during maintenance frequently extend the outage. Overruns of production unavailability are common because re-qualification and cleaning after maintenance take longer than planned. Restoring sterile production quickly hinges on skilled technicians and QA staff whose availability is limited. Each extra hour of sterile fill-finish downtime carries very high batch-value cost that current practice poorly contains.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required batching-system maintenance tasks omitted from the maintenance schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required maintenance tasks from the schedule is an administrative planning defect with limited centrality to the immediate batching job; relevance and severity both stay low as consequences are deferred and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS-driven PM schedules capture most routine tasks, though gaps for less common components still recur. Omitted tasks eventually surface as unplanned work that consumes time later. Building a complete task list depends on planner knowledge of every component's requirements. Missed tasks lead to premature failures and rework costs that a complete schedule would avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance due dates are calculated from incomplete or inaccurate operating and condition data
rows.imp_rat: Calculating maintenance due dates from incomplete condition data is an administrative reliability error, tangential to the core batching job; relevance is low and severity mild since it affects scheduling accuracy rather than immediate batch quality.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems still rely on calendar intervals with incomplete runtime/condition inputs, so due-date accuracy is frequently compromised. Due dates computed from stale data can trigger premature or late work, disrupting timing. Judging whether underlying condition data is complete requires expertise that few maintenance planners apply consistently. Bad due-date math causes either over-maintenance or failure-driven repairs, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching-system unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Batching-system downtime beyond the scheduled window affects availability but is an uptime concern rather than the accuracy of preparation itself (relevance low); severity constrained by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard work packages and spares kitting help, but overrun frequency remains a persistent, only partly mitigated issue. Maintenance overruns beyond the scheduled window happen regularly when parts or unexpected faults appear mid-job. Bringing the batching system back on time depends on experienced maintainers who can diagnose and correct quickly. Extended outages consume overtime labor and lost production capacity that current practice only partly limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical diagnostic alerts go unnoticed during production
rows.imp_rat: Unnoticed diagnostic alerts touch on reliability, but as an alert-monitoring concern it is somewhat removed from the core preparation job (relevance low) and severity is bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems exist and are configured, but alarm flooding and inconsistent acknowledgment mean critical alerts still slip through. Alerts surface in near real time on SCADA, but operator response can lag during busy production. Noticing and correctly triaging critical alerts depends heavily on attentive, trained operators amid many competing signals. Missed critical alerts can cause expensive damage, though the alerting infrastructure itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false diagnostic alerts that obscure actionable batching system faults
rows.imp_rat: False diagnostic alerts obscuring real faults is a reliability/noise defect somewhat removed from the preparation job (relevance low); consequences bounded under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization is known practice but poorly implemented at many sites, so false alerts commonly obscure real faults. False alerts waste responder time chasing non-issues during otherwise productive periods. Distinguishing genuine faults from nuisance alarms requires experienced judgement that varies by operator. Nuisance alarms drive unnecessary inspection labor and desensitize staff at ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process trends being misinterpreted as normal equipment behavior
rows.imp_rat: Misinterpreting process trends as normal is a diagnostic-ambiguity error at the margin of the core job (low relevance), and mistakes are usually caught downstream (low severity). Capped per batch rule.
rows.sat_rat: Historian trending exists but interpretation is manual and inconsistent, so normal-looking drift is routinely missed. Trend drift is slow and easily unnoticed until a failure forces attention, wasting lead time. Correctly reading process trends as early degradation demands scarce analytical expertise rarely present at the operator level. Misread trends lead to late interventions and higher repair cost, but trend tools themselves are cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of decommissioned system materials assigned incorrect disposal classifications
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect disposal classification of decommissioned materials is an end-of-life compliance task; it barely touches the active batching CFJ and its consequences are limited to regulatory paperwork.
rows.sat_rat: Material classification is guided by established waste-management practice but still error-prone for mixed-material pharma equipment. Sorting decommissioned materials into disposal classes manually slows the whole teardown. Distinguishing contaminated from clean, recyclable from hazardous material requires trained judgement that not every crew has. Wrong classification means paying hazardous rates for benign material or costly recalls of misrouted waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection stoppage caused by hazardous stored energy in retired system components
rows.imp_rat: Inspection stoppage from stored energy in retired components (item 10) concerns decommissioned equipment, so relevance to active batching is minimal though the safety consequence is non-trivial. Well below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge procedures usually restore inspection quickly, but unexpected stored energy still causes intermittent unplanned stoppages. Stored energy in retired components forces inspection stoppages while it is bled down or made safe, directly costing schedule time. Rapidly and safely discharging stored energy to resume inspection requires trained technicians. The main loss is idle inspection time rather than materials.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of interface configuration changes with incomplete records
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete records of interface configuration changes reduce change traceability (moderate severity), but this documentation concern must remain at or below 2 per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Interface configuration changes are frequently logged partially or informally, so a notable percentage of records remain incomplete. Reconstructing incomplete interface configuration records later is time-consuming, but the recording gap is the core failure. Complete configuration documentation depends on the engineer's diligence and understanding of the interface, and is often shortchanged. Incomplete records cost little immediately but complicate future changes and audits.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of active control, monitoring, or reporting dependencies discovered only after decommissioning begins
rows.imp_rat: Discovering active dependencies only after decommissioning begins is peripheral to the ongoing CFJ of controlling devices (low relevance) and occurs at end-of-life where impact is contained to the retirement effort (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Active dependencies are routinely found only after work starts because dependency mapping is rarely complete. Late-discovered active dependencies halt teardown and stretch the timeline. Only long-tenured engineers can predict which live control or reporting links still exist. Each late discovery triggers costly re-planning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required process and event records omitted from the archive
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required process and event records from the archive is an archival-completeness defect peripheral to real-time device control (low relevance), with recoverable compliance/audit consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Historian and event-log exports exist but coverage gaps are common, so a portion of required records is routinely omitted from the archive. Archiving records takes time but that is not where the failure is felt. Deciding which process and event records must be archived requires records-management judgement that is unevenly applied. Omitted records may trigger regulatory penalties or reconstruction costs later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of archived process and event records lacking metadata needed to establish origin and audit traceability
rows.imp_rat: Archived records lacking origin/audit metadata is a traceability-metadata shortfall peripheral to live control (low relevance), producing audit gaps rather than functional loss (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Exports from control software historians often lack the provenance metadata needed for audit traceability, so archived records are frequently unverifiable. Adding metadata is not chiefly a time constraint at disposal. Capturing origin and audit-traceability metadata correctly requires data-governance expertise rarely present at decommissioning. Metadata-poor archives raise the cost of any future audit or investigation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of historical records exported without timestamps, source identifiers, or audit metadata
rows.imp_rat: Exporting historical records without timestamps or audit metadata is an export-completeness defect peripheral to real-time control (low relevance), causing audit traceability gaps (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual or bulk exports commonly strip or omit timestamps and source identifiers, undermining record integrity. Attaching timestamps and identifiers is quick when tooling supports it, so time is minor. Ensuring every export carries source IDs and audit metadata demands disciplined export practices that are inconsistently followed. Records missing identifiers lose evidentiary value, raising downstream compliance cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that temporary files or purge records expose sensitive data during decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Temporary files or purge records exposing sensitive data during decommissioning is an end-of-life data-handling risk peripheral to the active control job (low relevance); a leak would matter but is confined and capped by the batch ceiling (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Temporary artifacts and purge records routinely persist and leak sensitive process or credential data because no standard end-to-end sanitization practice is applied to industrial control software at disposal. Secure erasure and purge-record scrubbing add little to the timeline when done, but the step is often skipped entirely so time is not the felt pain. Reliably finding and sanitizing every temp file, cache, and purge log requires forensic-level expertise that decommissioning crews for control software rarely possess. Dedicated data-sanitization tooling and validation are cheap relative to the breach cost, yet are seldom budgeted for a retiring system.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of remaining host applications impaired by removal of shared dependencies
rows.imp_rat: Host applications impaired by shared-dependency removal is a lifecycle/retirement side-effect, tangential to the active control job; severity is contained since it affects retiring systems. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Removal of shared components frequently breaks other running applications because dependency inventories for legacy control software are incomplete or nonexistent. Discovering shared-dependency impacts before removal is slow, but the harm is felt as breakage rather than delay. Mapping which other host applications rely on shared DLLs, drivers, or runtimes demands deep system knowledge that is usually gone by the time the software retires. Unexpected impairment of neighboring applications drives emergency remediation costs that dwarf any orderly dependency-check effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of access being revoked before shutdown, validation, and record-retention activities for the industrial control software are complete
rows.imp_rat: Access revoked before shutdown/validation/retention completes is an end-of-life sequencing risk distant from active control (low relevance); consequences are contained to the decommissioning workflow (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Access is sometimes cut too early, blocking required close-out work, because sequencing is loosely governed. Premature revocation can stall shutdown and validation tasks, adding delay. Sequencing revocation against retention activities requires coordinated planning expertise. Getting the ordering wrong causes rework cost but no large material spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required installers, patches, or configuration artifacts missing from the archived baseline
rows.imp_rat: Missing installers or configuration artifacts in the archived baseline affects future restore capability, tangential to live control (low relevance); it can slow recovery but is bounded and low-ranked (modest severity).
rows.sat_rat: Installers, patches, and configs are often incompletely archived because they were scattered across media and not fully inventoried. Collecting installers and patches takes modest time when they are on hand. Knowing the complete set of required installers and configuration artifacts requires system-specific knowledge that may have departed. Missing baseline artifacts make future reinstatement or forensic work far more expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of third-party access privileges remaining active after linked service agreements end
rows.imp_rat: Third-party privileges remaining active after agreements end is an access-hygiene lapse tangential to control operation (low relevance), carrying latent but real security exposure (moderate-low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Third-party privileges commonly outlive their service agreements because contract-to-access linkage is rarely tracked. Reconciling third-party access against ended agreements is a slow manual audit. Tracking vendor entitlements tied to contracts needs both procurement and OT identity knowledge. The reconciliation labor is modest but frequently deprioritized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required service records or retained data becoming inaccessible when linked services end
rows.imp_rat: Service records becoming inaccessible when linked services end is a data-retention lifecycle concern peripheral to live control (low relevance), with recoverable access consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: When dependent services or platforms are shut down, retained records tied to them routinely become inaccessible because migration is not planned. The loss surfaces later as inaccessibility, not as a timing delay during disposal. Foreseeing which linked-service dependencies will orphan retained data requires architecture knowledge that is usually lost. Recovering or reconstructing data stranded by ended services is expensive when even possible.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required user accounts unavailable at operational handover
rows.imp_rat: Missing user accounts at handover [delay] is a peripheral administrative onboarding issue with little bearing on the software's core control/monitor job; consequences are minor and quickly remedied by provisioning accounts.
rows.sat_rat: Account checklists and directory integration make most accounts ready, though last-minute handover gaps still recur without tight coordination. Provisioning missing accounts at handover causes short but disruptive delays. Standard account setup needs modest admin skill following a checklist. Account provisioning is low-cost administrative work.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of intended operator stations where customized workspace elements overlap, clip, or become unreadable
rows.imp_rat: Overlapping or unreadable workspace elements across stations [defect] is a cosmetic HMI layout defect largely peripheral to the control job; readability is a minor annoyance, so both proxies are the lowest tier.
rows.sat_rat: Testing on target station resolutions catches most rendering problems, though station-specific display variations still cause overlaps. Overlap and clipping issues are quickly seen when opening each station's display. Building resolution-robust layouts requires HMI design discipline that varies by engineer. Fixing overlapping elements is low-cost editing work.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of trained operators who execute sterile fill-finish control workflows incorrectly
rows.imp_rat: Trained operators executing fill-finish workflows incorrectly reflects human-execution error rather than the software's control capability (low relevance); the ranking positions it here despite potential process impact. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic batch records and interlocks catch some deviations, but reliance on operator correctness within permissive control screens leaves a meaningful incorrect-execution rate. Sterile fill-finish workflows are long and sequential, and there is little in-line pacing help, so trainees rush or lose place under time pressure. Correct execution depends on deep procedural memory and vigilance across many aseptic steps, and today's control software offers limited step-guidance or interlocking to reduce that expertise burden. Incorrect execution wastes high-value sterile product and media, making errors expensive though the tooling to prevent them is only partially deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of trainees unable to perform hands-on control tasks because required permissions are missing
rows.imp_rat: Trainees blocked by missing permissions is a training-access provisioning issue distinct from controlling production devices (low relevance), delaying practice rather than harming operations (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access exists in most control platforms, yet mismatches between assigned roles and the specific hands-on tasks a trainee must perform recur frequently, so trainees still get locked out mid-exercise. Provisioning delays are common because permission requests route through IT or QA approval queues before a trainee can touch the HMI, often costing hours to days. Configuring role-based access control in modern industrial control software demands administrator expertise that trainers rarely have on hand, so gaps surface only at the workstation. The direct cost is modest but wasted trainer and trainee time during blocked sessions adds up across cohorts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that competency gaps in alarm response and batch exception handling remain undetected after training
rows.imp_rat: Undetected competency gaps after training is a training-assessment concern separate from the software's control function (low relevance), and gaps can be caught later through supervision (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice tests happy-path operation far more than rare alarm and exception scenarios, so competency gaps in exactly those edge cases routinely slip through undetected. Competency assessment during training is largely a manual, post-hoc review that lags real performance and rarely catches gaps in the moment. Detecting whether an operator truly handles alarm floods or batch exceptions relies heavily on an experienced assessor's judgement, a scarce resource that limits coverage. Undetected gaps are cheap to overlook during training but expensive when they cause deviations in production, and structured simulation testing is under-invested.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of divergence between simulated control behavior and the current production configuration
rows.imp_rat: Divergence between simulated and production configuration undermines simulation confidence but not live control; relevance is modest and severity contained since it affects models, not the plant.
rows.sat_rat: Simulation models drift from the live configuration unless deliberately synchronized, and automatic config-to-sim propagation is rare, so divergence recurs. Aligning the simulator to current config costs engineering time whenever production is modified. Maintaining a faithful simulation model demands scarce simulation and controls expertise. Divergent simulations can waste effort and mislead validation, incurring rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of shift handoffs that omit current batch status or pending actions
rows.imp_rat: Shift handoffs omitting batch status is a communication lapse peripheral to the physical preparation task (low relevance) with typically recoverable consequences (low severity). Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Shift handoff still relies largely on verbal exchange and paper logs, so omission of current batch status or pending actions is common and poorly systematized. Handoffs that omit pending actions cost the incoming shift time to rediscover the state. Effective handoff depends heavily on outgoing-operator diligence and communication skill rather than any system. Missed handoff information can cause batch errors and rework, an occasional but significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percent of prior-batch residue remaining on wetted surfaces after rinsing
rows.imp_rat: Prior-batch residue on wetted surfaces after rinsing is a cross-contamination concern, but the ranking constrains both relevance and severity to 2 as this item sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP with conductivity/turbidity endpoint checks reliably removes prior-batch residue in most cases, though shadowed geometries and sticky products still leave occasional carryover. CIP cycles take fixed validated time and rarely need extension for cleanliness. Automated CIP removes most operator judgement from cleaning effectiveness. CIP consumes water, chemicals and energy, a known recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of rinse fluid remaining in the batching system when the next batch begins
rows.imp_rat: Residual rinse fluid when the next batch begins can dilute or alter concentration, but per the ceiling relevance and severity are held to 2 given its lower ranking position.
rows.sat_rat: Air blows and gravity drains handle most residual rinse, but pockets and dead legs leave variable holdup that can contaminate the next batch. Draining rinse fluid before the next batch adds a short verification step. Confirming the system is empty is routine, aided by drain valves and low-point sensors. Residual rinse fluid dilutes the next batch or wastes water and effluent capacity, a modest ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch lost through leakage or overflow during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Batch lost through leakage/overflow during transfer causes material waste (moderate severity) and touches the batching job (some relevance), but it sits below batch 2 in ranking. Capped.
rows.sat_rat: High-level switches and flow totalizers reliably prevent gross overflows, yet minor leakage and seal wear remain recurring sources of lost batch. Leak or overflow events during transfer trigger cleanup and re-batching that cost meaningful time. Avoiding overflow relies partly on correct level-sensor setup and operator vigilance where automation is incomplete. Level/flow instrumentation and high-level trips catch most overflow risk, but slow leaks and transfer losses still erode yield and generate waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the prepared batch remaining in the source vessel after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Prepared batch remaining in the source vessel after transfer is a yield defect ranked below the prior batch; relevance and severity both stay at 2 as it concerns recoverable material rather than the core job's success.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer completeness is reasonably repeatable but clingy or high-viscosity products leave inconsistent amounts behind. Chasing the last of a transfer can extend pump-out time modestly. Achieving full transfer depends mostly on vessel and pump design, not operator finesse. Residual product left in the source vessel is a direct recurring yield loss, partly mitigated by conical bottoms and scraped surfaces.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of liquid containment while independently actuating system functions
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment while independently actuating functions is a commissioning/test-time event, peripheral to core batching (low relevance) with limited severity in that context. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature interlock logic, double-block-and-bleed valving and pressure/level permissives make containment loss during independent actuation an uncommon event in well-maintained systems. Interlock-mediated actuation adds minor sequencing delays but does not materially slow the operator's independent function control. Modern PLC-driven valve sequencing and permissive logic remove much of the manual judgement once needed to actuate functions without spilling. Containment failures during actuation are costly in lost product and cleanup, but engineered valve interlocks keep such events rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch material discarded after an incorrect recipe change
rows.imp_rat: Batch material discarded after an incorrect recipe change is a waste consequence with some batching relevance and moderate severity, but placed in the lower tier. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems with version control reduce errors, but confirmation gaps and manual overrides still let incorrect recipes reach production. Discovering an incorrect recipe change mid-batch forces a stop that consumes time before material can be salvaged or dumped. Preventing bad recipe changes depends on operator diligence and change-control discipline that varies widely by site. A wrong recipe change typically means the whole batch is scrapped, a large and direct material loss that current safeguards only partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe ingredient pathways not exercised during accuracy verification
rows.imp_rat: Untested ingredient pathways during accuracy verification is a verification-coverage defect; it affects assurance rather than the batching job directly (relevance low) and severity is bounded by the ranking below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Accuracy verification commonly samples representative paths rather than all of them, leaving untested pathways as a latent, poorly addressed reliability gap. Verifying every ingredient pathway extends validation testing time considerably. Designing coverage that exercises all pathways demands validation expertise that many operating teams lack. Unexercised pathways rarely cost money directly until an unverified route fails in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish area during functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during functional testing is a test-phase risk peripheral to routine batching (low relevance); severity constrained here despite contamination stakes. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic protocols and environmental monitoring control most risk, but any intrusion into the fill-finish zone carries real, hard-to-eliminate contamination risk. Aseptic gowning, cleaning, and re-sanitization around testing add substantial time. Maintaining sterility during functional testing demands rigorous aseptic technique and constant vigilance from trained staff. A contamination breach can scrap batches and force costly line re-sterilization.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish activity caused by functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish from functional testing is a scheduling disruption peripheral to core batching (low relevance) with recoverable severity. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled maintenance windows and staged testing reduce disruption, but unplanned test-driven interruptions to sterile activity still recur. Functional testing that touches sterile fill-finish forces line stoppages that are hard to fully overlap with production. Coordinating testing to minimize interruption requires planning expertise but not rare technical skill. Lost sterile production time during interruptions is expensive per hour.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a required batch-size or operating-range mismatch during evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a batch-size or operating-range mismatch during evaluation is an assessment-defect step upstream of batching (relevance low); consequences bounded by the batch-3 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Operating-range mismatches are sometimes overlooked in evaluation because they hide in detailed specifications. Catching batch-size or operating-range mismatches during evaluation requires deliberate cross-checking that adds review time. Recognizing subtle operating-range mismatches depends on an experienced evaluator's attention to detail. Overlooked mismatches lead to expensive rework, while the evaluation check is low-cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of wetted components whose material identity is unverified
rows.imp_rat: Unverified wetted-component material identity risks compatibility and contamination issues (relevance) with quality consequences (severity), but the ceiling keeps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material certificates cover many components but positive verification of every wetted part is inconsistently done, leaving unverified items. Verifying material identity via mill certs and testing adds documentation and lab time. Confirming actual wetted-component material identity needs metallurgical or PMI expertise beyond routine review. Material testing and traceability add cost, and unverified materials risk expensive failures.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intended fluid chemistry and process requirements missing from the compatibility evaluation basis
rows.imp_rat: Missing fluid chemistry and process requirements from the compatibility basis can lead to material failures (relevance) and contamination (severity), but this batch is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility evaluation bases are frequently incomplete because some process conditions get overlooked, a recurring reliability gap. Compiling all fluid chemistry and process requirements into the compatibility basis is an upfront effort that adds time. Knowing which chemistry and process conditions must be captured requires experienced process and materials specialists. Missing requirements in the basis cause costly compatibility failures, though compilation cost is modest.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that submitted certifications are expired, invalid, or outside the proposed batching system's scope
rows.imp_rat: Expired or out-of-scope certifications are a procurement/compliance-vetting error only loosely connected to running a batch (low relevance) and correctable before use (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate expiry and scope checks are well-defined and reliably performed, though scope-mismatch subtleties occasionally slip by. Verifying certification validity and scope is a fast lookup against issuing bodies. Checking certificate currency and scope requires only modest training once the requirements are known. Certification verification is cheap and prevents costly acceptance of invalid credentials.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between compliance evidence and the proposed batching system configuration
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between compliance evidence and system configuration is a documentation-alignment issue rather than a batching-function failure (low relevance), reconcilable without harm (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence often pertains to similar-but-not-identical configurations, and this mismatch is only partially caught in practice. Matching compliance evidence to the exact proposed configuration adds a reconciliation step to review. Detecting that evidence covers a different configuration than proposed takes an attentive, knowledgeable reviewer. Configuration-evidence mismatches cause costly requalification, but checking is inexpensive.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish requirements omitted from the requirements baseline
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile requirements in the baseline is a documentation error with downstream but indirect impact on the batching CFJ (low relevance) and manageable consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: URS templates and regulatory checklists exist, but incomplete baselines remain a common root cause of downstream problems. Building a complete requirements baseline is an upfront effort that adds time before RFQ release. Assembling a complete critical-requirement set relies heavily on experienced engineers and quality staff to recall all applicable sterile requirements. Baseline development is mostly staff time, but omitted requirements drive costly late redesign.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between specified batching capacity and projected production demand
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between batching capacity and projected demand is a planning-level defect that affects throughput but not the core act of preparing a batch correctly (relevance low); severity is bounded and ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Demand-to-capacity sizing is well-established practice, though uncertain demand forecasts still leave residual mismatch risk. Capacity sizing against demand projections is a routine calculation that adds little time. Matching batching capacity to demand uses standard process-engineering sizing methods that most engineers handle. Mismatched capacity wastes capital or forces re-procurement, but the analysis itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fluid leaking from inlet, outlet, or recirculation piping joints during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Fluid leaking from piping joints during transfer touches batch delivery, yet the ranking places it below the prior batch so relevance and severity are both held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Proven jointing methods and post-assembly testing make transfer-path leakage uncommon in mature installations. Joint leaks during transfer cause interruptions, but well-made connections rarely require in-service repair. Assembling leak-tight inlet, outlet, and recirculation joints is standard competent-fitter work with defined torque and gasket practice. In-service joint leaks waste process fluid and cleanup effort, though these are infrequent after testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of import delay caused by source records that cannot be interpreted
rows.imp_rat: Import delay from uninterpretable source records is peripheral to physically batching and preparing liquids (low relevance), and its consequences are a recoverable data-ingest slowdown (low severity). Capped at 2.0 per batch ceiling.
