rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applicable recycling channels refuse separated system materials
rows.plain: Make sure the separated materials from the system are accepted by the appropriate recycling services. This prevents recyclable materials from being refused or sent to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Recycling channels refusing separated materials is an end-of-life disposal issue (relevance 1) far from active batching, with low operational stakes (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycling channels for common metals and plastics accept most materials, though contamination or specialty alloys occasionally trigger refusal. Confirming recycler acceptance can require lead time to arrange, occasionally delaying material routing. Waste brokers and recyclers guide acceptance, reducing the skill burden on the facility. Rejected materials incur return, re-handling, or landfill fees, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration separated recyclable materials remain onsite awaiting transfer
rows.plain: Move separated recyclable materials offsite sooner. This keeps storage areas from becoming crowded while the materials wait for pickup.
rows.imp_rat: Recyclable materials awaiting transfer onsite is a waste-handling delay error remote from liquid batching; consequences are storage congestion. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycling and scrap-hauler arrangements make transfer fairly consistent with only minor delays. Recyclable materials sometimes linger onsite awaiting haulers but pickup is generally arranged within normal cycles. Managing recyclable transfer is routine waste-handling work requiring little expertise. Onsite storage of recyclables carries low cost and materials retain scrap value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of legal ownership of the batching system remaining disputed after transfer
rows.plain: Make clear who legally owns the mixing system after it is handed over. This prevents later arguments about ownership and responsibility.
rows.imp_rat: Disputed legal ownership after transfer is a contractual/title error peripheral to the batching system's function; consequences are legal exposure but not operational failure. Capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Bills of sale, asset transfer agreements, and title documentation make clean ownership transfer the norm, with disputes rare. Legal ownership transfer paperwork can lag but is typically completed around the transfer event. Clearing title requires legal and asset-management input that is generally available for major disposals. Disputed ownership can expose the firm to liability or lost salvage value, though this is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required validation, maintenance, and safety records that are missing or inconsistent at handover
rows.plain: Make sure the new owner receives complete and consistent records about testing, upkeep, and safety. This matters so they can operate and care for the system safely after the handover.
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation/maintenance/safety records at handover is administrative documentation, peripheral to producing batches; severity kept at 1 per the mandatory batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Handover record sets are commonly incomplete or inconsistent because record-keeping over a system's life is fragmented across systems and people, with only partial checklists to catch gaps. Chasing down scattered validation, maintenance, and safety records at handover consistently delays closeout because they were never consolidated in advance. Knowing which records are required and where they live relies on institutional memory that is often gone by decommissioning time. Reconstructing missing records or re-doing lost documentation adds avoidable labor and rework cost at handover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batching system components damaged during removal and relocation
rows.plain: Avoid damaging parts of the material-mixing system when it is taken apart and moved. This helps keep the equipment usable and reduces repair or replacement work.
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during removal and relocation is a handling error tangential to the batching CFJ itself, with recoverable impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Rigging plans and lifting procedures reduce damage, but shared-line congestion and rushed schedules still cause frequent incidents. Careful disassembly and rigging for relocation extends teardown timelines. Safe removal and handling of batching components relies on experienced riggers and millwrights. Damaged components during relocation directly erode salvage or reuse value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to adjacent active equipment caused by disconnecting a shared interface
rows.plain: Disconnect a shared connection without interrupting nearby equipment that is still operating. This lets the work continue while other equipment remains in use.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to adjacent equipment from disconnecting a shared interface is a decommissioning-phase error peripheral to the batching CFJ; consequences are localized downtime. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation planning and blinding procedures exist but shared-interface surprises still cause unplanned adjacent-equipment downtime. Disconnecting shared interfaces often forces extended shutdowns of adjacent equipment that are hard to minimize. Isolating shared utilities without disrupting live equipment requires skilled process and safety engineers. Disruption to active adjacent equipment can halt production, imposing significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging facility-side piping or fittings while separating connections
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the site's pipes or connecting parts while taking the connections apart. This helps keep the site's piping usable and leak-free.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging facility piping during disconnection is a defect risk, but the mandatory ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard lockout, isolation, and mechanical disconnection practices make facility-side damage uncommon when procedures are followed. Careful disconnection to protect facility piping adds modest time but is a routine planned activity. Skilled millwrights and pipefitters generally know how to isolate and separate connections without damaging facility-side fittings. Occasional damage to facility piping incurs repair cost but is usually contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of process parameters transferred to the replacement system that differ from approved settings
rows.plain: Make sure the operating settings moved to the replacement system are the same as the approved settings, so the process works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Transferred parameters differing from approved settings could affect quality, but the batch-4 ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and validation practice during system replacement reliably reconciles most parameters, though manual re-entry still introduces occasional deviations. Transferring approved parameters to a replacement system is a defined data-migration task with predictable duration. Verifying transferred settings against approved values requires trained validation engineers. Parameter deviations can spoil batches on the new system, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production batches without a clear system assignment during the transition
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should be left without a clear connection to the system that made them during the changeover. This makes each batch easier to identify and track.
rows.imp_rat: Batches without clear system assignment during transition touches production but as a transitional admin gap; the ranking places it below higher batches, so relevance and severity stay at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Transition planning tools exist but batch-to-system mapping gaps still occur during cutover, leaving some batches unassigned. Reassigning production batches during transition can stall if system ownership is unclear. Correct batch reassignment requires production planners who understand both old and new system capabilities. Misassigned batches risk scrapped or delayed product, a direct production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operational responsibilities without clear ownership after handover
rows.plain: Make sure every ongoing work duty has a clearly named owner after the handover. This prevents important duties from being left undone.
rows.imp_rat: Operational responsibilities lacking ownership after handover is a governance/handover error with only indirect bearing on batching function; consequences are coordination confusion. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handover often relies on informal RACI or meeting notes, leaving responsibilities ambiguous and frequently orphaned. Clarifying ownership of residual responsibilities during handover often drags on past the transition. Defining who owns each remaining operational duty needs managerial judgement across functions. Ownership gaps create low direct cost but risk costly lapses in safety or compliance duties.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of major components or subsystems omitted from the final condition record
rows.plain: Record the condition of every major part of the system at the end, so problems are not overlooked and others know what they are receiving.
rows.imp_rat: Components omitted from the final condition record is an end-of-life documentation gap, low relevance to producing batches; forced to 1.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Final condition records are typically checklist-driven yet still omit minor or hidden subsystems because coverage is not systematically enforced. Walking down every component for the final condition record takes significant time that decommissioning schedules often compress. Knowing which subsystems and components must be captured requires competent asset knowledge, not just a walkthrough. Thorough condition recording costs inspection labor but avoids downstream disputes over omitted assets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operational condition cannot be verified after supporting utilities are disconnected
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the machine’s working condition cannot be checked after its supporting equipment and connections are turned off or removed. This helps create a reliable record of its final condition.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to verify operational condition after utilities are disconnected relates to decommissioning (relevance 1) and its consequences are contained (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice does not capture verified operational state before de-energizing, so condition frequently cannot be confirmed afterward. Once utilities are disconnected there is a narrow window and verifying operational condition afterward is often impractical, causing delays if attempted. Confirming operational condition post-disconnection needs test-rig setups and technicians familiar with the system. Re-energizing or rigging temporary utilities to verify condition is costly and seldom justified at disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition entries associated with the wrong component or subsystem identifier
rows.plain: Make sure each condition note is linked to the correct part or section of the system. This prevents people from making decisions based on information about the wrong item.
rows.imp_rat: Condition entries tied to the wrong identifier is a data-mapping error with low centrality to preparing liquids and minor consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, spreadsheet-based condition logging with degraded or removed tags makes wrong-component entries a common, poorly caught error. Correctly matching condition entries to component IDs slows the assessment step when tags are missing. Accurate association demands personnel who know the system's tagging and P&ID conventions. Misassigned condition data causes downstream valuation errors and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-critical interlocks, alarms, or sequence steps that behave incorrectly after transfer
rows.plain: Avoid having important safety connections, warnings, or operating steps work incorrectly after the control settings are moved to another system. This helps the equipment keep operating safely.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-critical interlocks behaving incorrectly after transfer is a contamination risk, yet the batch ceiling requires both proxies remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Transferred sterility interlocks are frequently mis-mapped and current requalification testing catches only a fraction, leaving this poorly served. Verifying interlock and alarm behavior after transfer adds substantial testing time that is often compressed under restart pressure. Validating sterility-critical sequence logic requires specialized validation and controls expertise that few staff possess. An interlock behaving incorrectly can scrap sterile batches, making the cost of failure very high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required control logic, parameters, and automation settings absent from the replacement configuration at cutover
rows.plain: Put all needed operating instructions, settings, and automatic controls into the replacement system before it is put into use, so it behaves as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Missing control logic and automation settings at cutover is relevant to correct batching operation, but the batch ceiling caps this item; severity of an incomplete replacement configuration is constrained here.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual configuration inventories and spreadsheets that routinely omit undocumented tweaks, so undetected gaps at cutover remain common. Configuration transfer at cutover is a slow, manual reconciliation that stretches decommissioning timelines when logic is discovered missing late. Correctly capturing every control parameter and automation setting demands scarce automation engineers who understand both the retiring and replacement systems. Missed logic surfaces as rework and post-cutover firefighting, driving up commissioning cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a superseded or unapproved retiring-system configuration as the migration source
rows.plain: Avoid choosing an outdated or unapproved setup from the system being replaced as the starting point for the move. Using the wrong setup could cause errors in the new system.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a superseded configuration as migration source is a data-migration risk at retirement, peripheral to active batching; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Weak or ambiguous version control on retiring systems makes selecting a superseded configuration a real and recurring risk with few safeguards. Verifying which configuration is the approved current one adds time when revision histories are unclear. Distinguishing an approved baseline from a superseded one demands configuration-management expertise that is often thin at disposal. Migrating from the wrong source configuration causes costly rework or invalid replacement builds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of transferred configuration elements lacking traceability to an approved source record required for release
rows.plain: Make sure every moved setting can be traced back to an approved record before the system is put into use. This shows where each setting came from and supports safe approval.
rows.imp_rat: Configuration elements lacking traceability to approved source records affect release integrity, but ranking places it below prior items; capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy systems often lack complete approved source records, so a meaningful share of configuration elements cannot be traced for release. Tracing each transferred configuration element to an approved source record is a slow, document-intensive effort. Establishing traceability for release requires quality and validation specialists who understand data integrity requirements. Gaps in traceability trigger costly investigations and delay release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production restart delay caused by configuration migration at cutover
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to restart germ-free medicine production while the old equipment settings are moved to the replacement. This helps the replacement start with the right settings sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Restart delay from configuration migration affects throughput, but this item is capped below the prior batch, keeping both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Restart timing is unpredictable because configuration issues surface only during requalification, so schedules routinely slip. Restart delay from configuration migration is the core error and current practice offers little to shorten the migration-driven downtime. Speeding restart depends on experienced migration teams working in parallel, a scarce capability. Idle sterile fill-finish lines are enormously expensive per day, so delays translate directly into lost production value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required disposal approvals or notifications omitted from the submission plan
rows.plain: Include every required approval and notice for disposing of the equipment in the submission plan. This helps avoid delays or violations during disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal approvals omitted from the submission plan is a compliance-paperwork gap peripheral to batching with contained consequences; ceiling of 1.0 enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Established EHS checklists cover common approvals but omissions still occur for unusual materials or jurisdictions. Building the full list of required approvals and notifications takes time researching applicable jurisdictions. Identifying every required approval demands regulatory affairs expertise that varies by site. Omitted notifications risk penalties and rework costing time and money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approval applications are rejected for incomplete or inaccurate system-disposal information
rows.plain: Make sure disposal applications contain complete and accurate information about the system being discarded. This helps prevent the applications from being rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal-approval rejections for incomplete information are administrative end-of-life paperwork, peripheral to the CFJ of batching liquid product (low relevance); consequences are delay rather than harm and this batch is capped at 1.0 (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard application templates reduce errors but incomplete or inaccurate system information still triggers periodic rejections. Correcting rejected applications and resubmitting lengthens the disposal timeline. Accurate system-disposal applications require experienced regulatory preparers. Rejections cause delay-driven storage and re-filing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of regulatory review beyond the planned decommissioning start date
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for government approval to dispose of equipment after the planned shutdown date. This helps the shutdown stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory-review delay past decommissioning start is a schedule/compliance issue at end-of-life, low relevance to core batching; ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Review durations are highly variable and unpredictable, making schedule slippage the norm rather than the exception. Regulatory review of decommissioning routinely overruns planned start dates because agency timelines are outside the operator's control. Skilled regulatory-affairs staff can shorten review, but such expertise is scarce and unevenly available. Extended review keeps assets and staff in limbo, accruing standby and holding costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between environmental and safety authorities over applicable disposal requirements
rows.plain: Resolve differences between environmental and safety officials about how the waste must be disposed of. This prevents delays and helps ensure the disposal is accepted by all authorities.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between authorities over disposal requirements is a regulatory-coordination concern at end-of-life, peripheral to batching; held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Jurisdictional overlap between environmental and safety authorities produces genuine conflicting requirements with no reliable arbitration mechanism, a chronic pain. Resolving conflicts between environmental and safety authorities drags out approvals unpredictably. Reconciling competing regulatory regimes requires specialized legal and compliance judgement that few disposal teams possess. Prolonged inter-agency conflict inflates consulting and delay costs with no clear owner.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an approval or permit expires before disposal activities begin
rows.plain: Make sure the required approval or permit is still valid when disposal begins. This prevents disposal work from starting without permission.
rows.imp_rat: An expiring disposal permit is a decommissioning scheduling issue distant from the batching CFJ (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Calendar and compliance-tracking tools reliably flag expirations for most sites, though coordination lapses occasionally let permits lapse. Permit expiry before disposal begins is fundamentally a timing/scheduling failure between approval and execution. Tracking permit validity windows needs organized regulatory administration. An expired permit forces costly re-application and idle-asset storage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controllers, PLCs, and field devices with missing or inaccurate model, firmware, or protocol details
rows.plain: Avoid having incorrect or missing information about the control devices, their software versions, or how they communicate. Accurate details help confirm that the equipment will work together.
rows.imp_rat: Missing model/firmware/protocol details on devices touches configuration accuracy but is capped at 1 for relevance and severity in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Device inventories are notoriously incomplete and stale, so missing or wrong details are a chronic source of acquisition error. Compiling accurate model, firmware and protocol details for every controller and field device is a lengthy inventory effort. Accurate device profiling requires experienced controls engineers who know the installed base. Inaccurate device data drives mismatched purchases and costly rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required control, configuration, and monitoring functions unsupported despite nominal device compatibility
rows.plain: Make sure devices that are supposed to work with the system can actually be controlled, set up, and watched by it. This prevents problems caused by devices that technically connect but do not work fully.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported control/config/monitor functions despite nominal compatibility touches the CFJ but is scored low by the mandatory batch-5 ranking, so both relevance and severity are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor compatibility claims frequently overstate real function support, so this failure is common and hard to detect before buying. Confirming that required control, configuration and monitoring functions actually work despite nominal compatibility often demands slow pre-purchase testing or trials. Distinguishing nominal compatibility from true functional support requires deep controls and integration expertise. Discovering unsupported functions after purchase forces expensive workarounds or replacement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for access to representative controller, PLC, and field-device configurations
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to review representative settings for the control system and connected devices. This helps confirm sooner whether the new equipment can work with the existing controls.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for representative controller/PLC/field-device configurations is a test-setup delay tangential to executing control (low relevance), and severity is capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Representative configurations are frequently unavailable or only partially accurate, so buyers routinely proceed without confident evidence of compatibility. Getting access to representative controller, PLC and field-device configurations often means waiting on lab availability or plant scheduling, causing long delays. Assembling a representative test rig demands scarce controls-engineering skill and hardware familiarity. Physical controller and device hardware is expensive to procure or replicate for evaluation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between entitlement approval and receipt of the activation credentials
rows.plain: There should be less time between approval to use a product and receiving the information needed to activate it. This lets people start using the approved product sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between entitlement approval and credential receipt is a procurement latency issue barely touching the control CFJ (low relevance), and severity is held to 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Automated entitlement delivery is fairly consistent but occasionally stalls behind manual vendor approval or account provisioning errors. License portals and automated entitlement systems now issue activation credentials within minutes to a day for most mainstream vendors, though enterprise deals still see delays. Retrieving credentials is a low-skill portal task once the process is known. The credential-delivery step carries essentially no incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of received activation credentials rejected during verification
rows.plain: Have fewer software activation codes fail when they are checked. This helps ensure the purchased software can be used without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected activation credentials is an onboarding hiccup only tangential to the core control job (low relevance), and the ceiling forces severity to 1 even though it delays deployment.
rows.sat_rat: Modern licensing servers make credential acceptance largely dependable, with only occasional rejections. Activation credential verification is fast, usually completed at install time. Entering and validating activation credentials is a low-skill routine step. Rejected credentials are typically reissued at no cost by the vendor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of license entitlements that do not match the required software configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the software permissions match the software setup that is actually needed. A mismatch could prevent the software from working as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Entitlements not matching the required configuration can impede deployment, yet the batch ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Complex module/option licensing makes configuration mismatches a common and hard-to-detect failure. Verifying that entitlements match the required software configuration adds review time before and after purchase. Mapping license SKUs to the actual needed feature/module configuration demands product-specific expertise. Mismatched entitlements mean paying for the wrong capability and buying again.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required licensed capacity or functionality absent from the received entitlements
rows.plain: Make sure the received license keys provide all the features and usage capacity that were paid for. This prevents the software from being less useful than expected.
rows.imp_rat: Missing licensed capacity in received entitlements could constrain deployment, but the ranking caps this batch, so relevance and severity are both held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Entitlement mismatches happen but vendor license portals and order confirmations catch most shortfalls, giving acceptable coverage. Verifying received entitlements against ordered capacity is a moderately quick reconciliation at delivery. It takes license-management familiarity to match entitlement tokens to purchased functionality. Missing entitlements mean paying again or delaying use, a real but usually recoverable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in mapping received credentials to their intended control-system instances
rows.plain: Make it clear which received access codes belong to which control-system machines or installations. This prevents the wrong code from being used on the wrong system.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous mapping of credentials to control-system instances touches configuration integrity, but the batch ceiling caps it; relevance and severity both held at 1 per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Naming and asset-management conventions help, but ambiguity in credential-to-instance mapping still occurs on larger deployments. Mapping received credentials to their target control-system instances is a moderate manual matching task. It takes system-administration familiarity to associate license keys with the correct instances. Misassigned credentials cause rework but rarely large financial loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent resolving conflicts between package identifiers and approved release records
rows.plain: Spend less time fixing disagreements between the labels on packages and the approved records showing they can be released. This helps prevent the wrong package from being accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Time resolving package-identifier versus release-record conflicts is a version-control admin task marginal to the CFJ (low relevance), with severity held to 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Identifier schemes rarely align cleanly with approval records, so conflicts recur and resolving them lacks any authoritative single source, leaving frequent uncertainty. Reconciling package identifiers against approved release records is a manual, time-consuming lookup across disconnected systems. It requires configuration-management expertise to interpret version strings, build numbers and approved-release nomenclature correctly. The labor to resolve mismatches is a recurring soft cost but rarely a large hard expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of license or entitlement identifiers rejected during registration of the industrial control software
rows.plain: Make sure all software permission codes and identifying details are accepted when the industrial control software is registered. Rejected details could prevent the software from being properly recorded or used.
rows.imp_rat: Rejected license identifiers during registration block activation but rank below the prior batch; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Key transcription errors and mismatched identifiers still cause rejections often enough to be a recurring nuisance. A rejected identifier during registration triggers a support-ticket loop that can stall activation for hours to days. Resolving rejected keys usually just needs following vendor support steps, requiring modest skill. Registration rejections mostly cost troubleshooting time rather than the license value itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the administrator lacks the authorization required to register the software license with the vendor
rows.plain: The administrator should have permission to register the software with the company that supplied it. This helps ensure the software can be properly activated and supported.
rows.imp_rat: Administrator lacking authorization to register the license is a provisioning permission gap; the ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Administrator authorization for license registration is often assumed rather than verified, so mismatches recur during onboarding. Finding the correctly authorized administrator to register the license can stall activation while roles are clarified. Knowing which admin holds vendor-registration authority requires specific license-governance knowledge that is unevenly held. Authorization gaps mainly cost administrative time, not resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the industrial control software license is already assigned to another customer account
rows.plain: Make sure the software’s permission to be used is not already linked to another customer. This allows it to be registered to the correct account.
rows.imp_rat: A license already assigned to another customer account is a provisioning conflict; ranked below prior batch, held at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Modern license-management platforms enforce uniqueness reliably, so double-assignment is now uncommon. A license already assigned elsewhere surfaces at activation, forcing a support cycle that delays deployment. Detecting duplicate license assignment relies on vendor systems rather than buyer skill. Duplicate-assignment disputes cost support time but rarely material resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the registered license is associated with the wrong facility or control system
rows.plain: The software registration should point to the correct site and control system. This ensures the right location and equipment receive the software’s support and permissions.
rows.imp_rat: A license registered to the wrong facility is a licensing-record error with limited operational impact on device control; relevance and severity are capped at 1 as this batch ranks below the prior one.
rows.sat_rat: Facility/system binding depends on manual entry during registration and lacks strong validation, so wrong-facility errors recur in multi-site buyers. Correcting a license bound to the wrong facility later requires reactivation cycles that delay use. Mapping licenses to the correct facility or control system needs asset-management knowledge that is unevenly applied. Misregistration causes administrative rework rather than material waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required control-device interfaces and network paths omitted from the compatibility assessment
rows.plain: Check every required connection between control devices and every communication route, so it is clear whether the new system can work with the existing setup.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting device interfaces and network paths from a compatibility assessment is an evaluation-scoping error, marginal to running control operations (low relevance), with severity capped at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Interface and network-path coverage is often incomplete, so omissions are found during integration. Compatibility assessment of interfaces and network paths requires iterative discovery that extends the timeline. Mapping control-device interfaces and network paths needs specialized network and controls expertise. Omitted paths cause rework and integration cost later rather than immediate spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict among documented software requirements, infrastructure configuration records, and stakeholder accounts
rows.plain: Make sure the software needs, equipment setup records, and stakeholders’ descriptions of the system all agree. This helps confirm that the existing infrastructure can support the software.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict among requirements, config records, and stakeholder accounts affects documentation consistency, not core device control; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement, configuration, and stakeholder sources frequently disagree with no standard reconciliation practice, so conflicts persist. Reconciling software requirements, infrastructure records, and stakeholder accounts is a slow cross-referencing effort. Resolving conflicts requires an analyst who can arbitrate between technical documents and stakeholder claims. Undetected conflicts drive costly mismatched purchases and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for authoritative infrastructure configuration details needed for compatibility confirmation
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for reliable details about how the facility’s technology is set up. This helps confirm sooner whether the new equipment will work with it.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for authoritative infrastructure details for compatibility is an assessment-prerequisite delay peripheral to the core job (low relevance), with severity forced to 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Infrastructure documentation is often outdated, incomplete or contradictory, so the details obtained are frequently not authoritative and compatibility confirmation stays uncertain. Chasing authoritative infrastructure configuration details drags on because ownership is spread across IT, OT and facilities teams. Interpreting network, OS and infrastructure specs for compatibility confirmation needs cross-domain IT/OT expertise. The effort is mostly labor cost with little direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable safety, security, and quality requirements lacking supporting evidence
rows.plain: Make sure there is proof that all relevant safety, security, and quality requirements have been met. This makes it possible to show others that the requirements were followed.
rows.imp_rat: Missing evidence for safety/security/quality requirements matters for assurance but is capped at 1 for both proxies by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence completeness is highly variable because vendors present certifications inconsistently and buyers lack a standard verification checklist. Assembling supporting evidence for safety, security and quality requirements is a slow document-chasing exercise across vendors. Judging which safety/security/quality certifications satisfy a requirement needs specialist compliance and OT-security knowledge. Gaps discovered late force expensive re-procurement or remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplied compliance evidence that does not cover the software version, configuration, and intended use proposed for sterile fill-finish
rows.plain: Make sure the compliance evidence applies to the exact software version, setup, and intended use proposed for sterile production and packaging. Evidence for a different version or use may not prove that this system is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Compliance evidence not matching the proposed version/config is a coverage gap in due diligence, not core to the control job (low relevance), with severity capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors typically supply version-generic or reference-configuration evidence that rarely maps to the buyer's exact deployment and intended sterile use, so coverage gaps are the norm rather than the exception. Matching evidence to the exact proposed version and configuration for sterile fill-finish adds slow back-and-forth with the vendor. Judging whether generic evidence actually covers the specific version, configuration and intended use requires deep validation and GxP expertise. Gaps discovered late force expensive revalidation, though the assessment activity itself costs little.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplied compliance evidence based on expired assessments or superseded regulatory and industry requirements
rows.plain: Use compliance evidence based on current checks and current rules, not expired reviews or outdated requirements. This helps show that the work meets the rules that apply today.
rows.imp_rat: Compliance evidence based on expired assessments is a documentation currency problem tangential to controlling devices (low relevance), and the ceiling limits severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: The status quo routinely delivers compliance packets containing expired certificates or references to superseded standards, and there is no standard automated freshness check, so this outcome fails often. Buyers spend significant time cross-checking dates and revision histories because vendors seldom flag when an assessment has lapsed. Detecting expired or superseded requirements demands scarce regulatory-affairs expertise that most acquisition teams lack in-house. Relying on stale evidence risks costly audit findings and rework, but the direct spend on checking is modest.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the origin or validity of supplied certificates and assessment reports cannot be authenticated
rows.plain: Make sure the certificates and assessment reports are genuine and can be traced back to their source. This helps others trust that the compliance evidence is valid.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to authenticate origin of certificates and assessment reports is a trust-verification concern, but the ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is no universal standard to authenticate supplied certificates and assessment reports, so their origin and validity often cannot be confirmed at acquisition. Authenticating certificate and report origins can require back-and-forth with issuing bodies, adding time. Validating certificate provenance and assessment-report authenticity needs specialized audit and PKI knowledge. Verification is mostly labor with little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved contradictions among declarations, certificates, assessment reports, and technical documentation
rows.plain: Resolve contradictions among statements of compliance, certificates, assessment reports, and technical documents. This helps show clearly whether the equipment meets the required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Contradictions among certificates and documentation is a paperwork-reconciliation ambiguity peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with reviewable consequences (low severity). Capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Contradictions across document sets frequently persist because reviews are shallow and siloed. Cross-checking declarations, certificates, and reports for contradictions is a slow manual document review. Spotting subtle contradictions across technical and compliance documents demands expert domain literacy. The review consumes skilled reviewer time but little material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the account is associated with the wrong legal entity or purchasing organization
rows.plain: Make sure the purchasing account is linked to the correct company or buying group.
rows.imp_rat: Associating the account with the wrong legal entity is a procurement-record error; it ranks low so relevance and severity are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor account setup usually captures the correct legal entity, though multi-subsidiary buyers still see occasional misassignment. Correcting a wrong-legal-entity association later requires slow account re-provisioning and contract re-linking. Entity mapping is administrative and needs limited specialized skill. Misassociation can create billing and licensing rework costs but modest direct waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the account is created without the required purchasing and license-management permissions
rows.plain: Make sure the account includes all the permissions needed to make purchases and manage user licenses.
rows.imp_rat: An account created without required purchasing/license permissions delays setup but is peripheral to control; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Default account provisioning often omits required purchasing or license-management permissions, and this recurs without a standard checklist. Discovering missing purchasing/license permissions mid-process forces role reconfiguration that delays procurement. Correctly scoping account roles and permissions demands understanding of vendor license models that few acquirers possess. Permission gaps cause rework labor rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from application submission until the administrator receives usable account credentials
rows.plain: Give the new account holder usable login details as soon as the application is submitted. This lets them begin using the account without unnecessary waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Time to receive usable account credentials is an onboarding delay, marginal to controlling industrial devices; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning succeeds most of the time but manual approval steps and license-server dependencies introduce recurring delays. Time from application to usable credentials varies from same-day self-service portals to multi-day manual provisioning for industrial license servers. Administrators generally know the process, though vendor-specific activation quirks demand some familiarity. Waiting on credentials idles commissioning resources, incurring modest holding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of mandatory organization fields with conflicting or unclear requirements
rows.plain: Make sure all required organization information has clear, consistent instructions. This prevents people from entering conflicting or uncertain details when creating an account for a purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting organization fields are a form-entry ambiguity far from the CFJ of controlling devices (low relevance), and the consequence is easily corrected data entry (low severity). Capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Form-field ambiguity causes recurring but low-severity entry errors that are usually caught and corrected. Clarifying conflicting mandatory organization fields adds only short back-and-forth to the order process. Interpreting ambiguous required fields on vendor/registration forms leans on someone who knows the organization's registration conventions. The impact is minor administrative rework rather than material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ordered industrial control software entitlements missing from the delivery
rows.plain: Make sure all ordered licenses for the equipment-control software are included. Without them, the required software may not be legally or fully usable.
rows.imp_rat: Missing ordered entitlements from delivery is a procurement fulfillment gap peripheral to the software's control function (low relevance) and resolvable through redelivery (low severity). Held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Order-to-delivery reconciliation is a mature practice, so shortfalls are usually caught, though split or partial deliveries still cause misses. Detecting missing entitlements from a delivery requires a reconciliation pass that adds modest time. Comparing delivered items against the order needs basic diligence rather than deep expertise. Missing entitlements delay deployment and may require expediting, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of delivered entitlements with product, version, edition, or site terms that differ from the order
rows.plain: Make sure the delivered software access matches the order, including the product, release, package, and location covered.
rows.imp_rat: Delivered entitlements differing from the order in version/edition/site terms is a fulfillment discrepancy (low relevance) correctable by reconciliation (low severity). Capped per batch-5 rule.
rows.sat_rat: Standard delivery-verification steps catch most term mismatches, though edition or site discrepancies still occur. Checking delivered entitlements against order terms is quick once credentials arrive. Matching product, version, edition, and site terms is a straightforward verification task. Mismatched entitlements can require re-purchase but are usually correctable at modest cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered entitlements that expire before the planned installation
rows.plain: Make sure the delivered licenses or access rights remain valid until the planned installation. Otherwise, they may expire before they can be used.
rows.imp_rat: Entitlements expiring before installation cause rework but are a timing/procurement issue; relevance and severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Because project schedules slip and expiry tracking is manual, entitlements lapsing pre-install still happens with some regularity. Checking entitlement validity windows against the install schedule is a quick lookup once records are in hand. Reading expiry dates needs little specialized skill. Entitlements expiring before install waste the entire purchase, a direct and avoidable loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that delivered entitlement records remain inaccessible to the Software Asset Manager
rows.plain: Give the person responsible for managing software licenses access to the records showing what software rights were delivered. This helps them confirm what was purchased and received.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible entitlement records delay the asset manager but are administrative to the CFJ; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Portal-based delivery is fairly consistent, but fragmented delivery channels mean records sometimes remain lost or unlinked. Entitlement records are increasingly delivered through vendor license portals accessible quickly, though email/PDF delivery still leaves gaps until the SAM tracks them down. Locating and ingesting entitlement records into asset systems takes moderate SAM tooling familiarity. Inaccessible entitlements risk under- or over-buying licenses, a real but deferred cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered entitlements that cannot be matched unambiguously to order lines
rows.plain: Make sure each delivered user right or license can be clearly matched to a specific order item. This helps confirm that everything ordered was delivered correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Entitlements not matchable to order lines is a reconciliation error, administrative to the CFJ; both proxies held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Inconsistent entitlement naming versus PO line items makes unambiguous matching frequently fail. Matching delivered entitlements to order lines is a manual reconciliation that consumes SAM time after each delivery. It takes an attentive asset manager familiar with the vendor's SKU and order conventions to reconcile ambiguous entries. Unmatched entitlements risk paying for licenses that can't be tracked or claimed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of licenses assigned to the wrong control-system deployment
rows.plain: Make sure each software permission is assigned to the correct control-system installation. Assigning one to the wrong installation could prevent the intended system from working.
rows.imp_rat: Assigning licenses to the wrong deployment is a peripheral administrative allocation error relative to actually controlling and monitoring devices (low relevance), and the consequence is a reassignable licensing mismatch (low severity). Capped per batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: With manual assignment tracking across multiple deployments, misassignment remains a fairly frequent error. Correcting a license assigned to the wrong deployment requires re-hosting or reissue cycles that take time. Correct assignment relies on someone tracking which entitlement belongs to which control system, an attention-heavy task. Wrong assignments can strand paid licenses on the wrong system, wasting spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required external authorization unavailability during license activation
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for outside approval needed to turn on paid software. This helps people start using the software sooner.
rows.imp_rat: External authorization unavailability during activation can block start-up, but the ceiling limits both relevance and severity to 1 in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Activation servers and licensing infrastructure are generally dependable, yet occasional server outages or token lapses still block activation unpredictably. Most vendors now offer online activation portals that authorize within minutes, but offline or air-gapped OT environments still hit multi-day manual authorization waits. Standard activation is largely self-service, though offline license file exchanges require someone who understands the vendor's process. Idle commissioning crews waiting on authorization represent a real but usually bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of commissioning delay caused by incomplete license activation
rows.plain: Avoid delaying the machine’s start-up because the software access has not been fully turned on. This lets the machine be tested and put into use sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Commissioning delay from incomplete license activation slows deployment but is a timing issue; the batch ranking holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Activation processes work but frequently stall at the last moment, making delay a recurring rather than rare outcome. Commissioning delays from incomplete license activation recur when activation depends on manual vendor steps or offline license servers. Activating licenses correctly needs vendor-specific know-how, and errors compound the delay. Each day of commissioning delay idles crews and equipment at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required industrial control device types with unverified package support
rows.plain: Confirm that each type of equipment the software must control is supported by the software being considered. This avoids choosing software that cannot work with some of the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Unverified package support for device types is a pre-purchase due-diligence ambiguity (low relevance) with a checkable, low-consequence outcome (low severity). Held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: With reliance on unverified vendor matrices, unsupported device types are a chronic and consequential surprise. Verifying package support for each required device type before purchase needs slow compatibility research or trials. Judging genuine device support across many industrial device types demands broad, scarce controls expertise. Unverified support leads to buying software that can't drive the devices, wasting the purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of collected package capability information that is inaccurate or outdated
rows.plain: Reduce the share of collected information about what the software package can do that is wrong or no longer current. This helps people compare available software accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccurate collected capability information is a data-quality issue in the evaluation phase, tangential to executing device control; relevance and severity both pinned at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor-supplied capability information is routinely outdated or optimistic and no standard verification practice reliably catches this, so buyers frequently eat the error. Verifying capability claims against reality takes weeks of back-and-forth with vendors and reference customers, and stale datasheets persist through that window. Distinguishing marketing claims from actual capabilities requires seasoned automation engineers who know each vendor's history, a scarce judgement. Inaccurate capability data drives costly rework and mid-project change orders when advertised features fail to materialize.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in cross-vendor comparisons caused by inconsistent capability terminology
rows.plain: Use the same clear meaning for feature descriptions when comparing software from different suppliers. This prevents one supplier's wording from making its software seem better or worse than it is.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity from inconsistent capability terminology in cross-vendor comparisons is a pre-purchase evaluation friction, peripheral to control; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: No industry-standard capability taxonomy exists, so cross-vendor comparisons remain ambiguous and error-prone. Normalizing inconsistent vendor terminology for comparison consumes significant analysis time. Only experienced engineers can decode differing vendor jargon into equivalent capabilities, a heavy dependence on scarce expertise. Misread comparisons lead to buying the wrong product, an expensive mistake.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for vendor documentation needed to complete package research
rows.plain: Receive the supplier’s documents quickly so the available software choices can be fully compared. This helps the buyer decide which software may be suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for vendor documentation for research is peripheral to the control job; relevance and severity held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation availability and quality vary greatly by vendor, so research completeness is inconsistent. Obtaining full vendor documentation (datasheets, compatibility matrices, licensing terms) often requires opening a sales channel and waiting days for responses. Interpreting incomplete or technical vendor docs to complete package research relies on experienced engineers. The cost is chiefly the research staff's stalled time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile fill-finish control and compliance requirements omitted from the comparison criteria
rows.plain: Include all requirements for making sterile medicines, including how the work must be controlled and checked, when comparing software choices. This helps ensure the selected software fits the work and its oversight needs.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted sterile fill-finish requirements in comparison criteria is a narrow evaluation-criteria defect (low relevance) fixable during selection (low severity). Capped per batch-5 rule.
rows.sat_rat: Comparison criteria frequently omit niche sterile fill-finish requirements, so gaps go unnoticed until validation. Building sterile fill-finish control and compliance criteria into comparison sheets is time-intensive and often rushed. It demands deep GMP sterile-manufacturing plus controls expertise that very few acquisition teams possess. The specialist effort is costly, and omissions cause expensive qualification rework later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required industrial protocols with unconfirmed support across candidate packages
rows.plain: Confirm that each required way for equipment and software to communicate works across the software choices being considered. This makes it easier to compare options without discovering connection problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed protocol support across candidates is an evaluation-stage ambiguity (low relevance) that is verifiable before commitment (low severity). Capped per the batch-5 rule.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor protocol claims are often vague or unverified until integration, so unconfirmed support frequently slips through and surfaces as a late failure. Confirming protocol support requires back-and-forth with vendors and manual cross-checking of datasheets, which stretches acquisition timelines. Interpreting vendor protocol claims versus actual device-level implementations demands scarce automation-engineering expertise that many buyers lack. Chasing protocol confirmation consumes engineering hours but rarely large capital during acquisition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of licensing and lifecycle cost omitted from candidate pricing models
rows.plain: Include the software’s permission fees and all expected costs over its useful life in the price comparison. This gives a more accurate picture of what each software choice will really cost.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle cost from pricing models is a procurement-estimation gap that does not affect device control; both proxies capped at 1 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standard practice ensuring all lifecycle cost elements are captured, so omissions are the norm. Building a full lifecycle cost model takes considerable time collecting maintenance, upgrade, and support pricing from vendors. Estimating total cost of ownership over decades requires financial and technical expertise that is often lacking. Candidate pricing models routinely omit maintenance renewals, support tiers, and upgrade fees, so the true cost is understated and overspend follows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in definitions for advertised control and monitoring features
rows.plain: Make the advertised features for controlling and watching the system clearly defined. This helps people understand what the software really does before choosing it.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous definitions of advertised features is a marketing-clarity issue affecting selection, not runtime control; relevance and severity held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Feature descriptions are inconsistently defined across vendors with no standard glossary, so ambiguity persists. Clarifying what advertised control and monitoring features actually do requires time-consuming vendor demos and Q&A. Interpreting feature definitions accurately depends on domain-expert judgement about real behavior versus label. Ambiguous feature definitions lead to buying functionality that underdelivers, wasting spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of current and projected device, tag, data-point, and site demand omitted from the scalability assessment
rows.plain: Reduce the share of today's and expected future needs—such as connected devices, labels, recorded values, and locations—left out of the growth assessment. This helps show whether the system can handle future demands.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting demand data from a scalability assessment is a planning-input gap that does not stop the software from controlling devices; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Projected demand is frequently guessed or partially captured with no standard completeness check, so gaps in the assessment are common. Assembling current and projected device, tag, and site demand into a scalability picture is a slow manual data-gathering exercise across siloed teams. It takes an experienced architect to forecast tag and data-point growth, and that expertise is thin on most acquisition teams. Omitted demand leads to under-licensing and expensive capacity expansions later, but the immediate loss is the missed requirement itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that scalability evidence fails to represent peak concurrent workloads in sterile fill-finish operations
rows.plain: Make sure testing shows how the system performs when many tasks happen at the same time during the busiest work periods.
rows.imp_rat: Scalability evidence failing to represent peak concurrent workloads in fill-finish is a validation-representativeness concern, but the ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor scalability evidence seldom reflects true peak concurrency in sterile operations, leaving a persistent unverified gap at acquisition. Building scalability evidence that represents peak concurrent sterile fill-finish workloads requires lengthy load testing rarely done pre-purchase. Designing representative peak-load scenarios needs scarce performance-engineering and process expertise. Realistic peak-load evaluation is costly, so buyers accept vendor benchmarks instead.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in licensing units, scaling thresholds, and multi-site usage rights
rows.plain: Clearly state how software use is counted, when extra use changes the price or permission, and whether it can be used at more than one location. This helps people know whether the software will still suit them as their operations grow.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in licensing units and multi-site rights is a contractual-clarity issue that does not impair device control; relevance and severity held at 1 per the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Industrial software licensing terms are complex and often ambiguous, so disputes over scaling and multi-site rights recur. Pinning down licensing units and multi-site rights requires drawn-out contract clarification. Deciphering scaling thresholds and multi-site terms needs licensing specialists who are hard to access. Misunderstood licensing units cause true-up penalties and unexpected fees.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of site-specific availability, segregation, qualification, and data-retention requirements omitted from the scalability assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the growth review includes all location-specific needs for access, separation, testing, and keeping records. This helps show whether the system will work as operations grow.
rows.imp_rat: Missing site-specific requirements from a scalability assessment is an upfront planning omission, peripheral to the runtime control job; relevance and severity held at 1 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: These heterogeneous, poorly documented requirements are routinely omitted with no standard checklist that reliably surfaces them all, making this among the worst-served needs. Chasing down site-specific availability, segregation, qualification, and data-retention rules across regulated sites is a protracted effort. Interpreting qualification and data-retention obligations demands niche regulatory and site knowledge rarely held by the acquisition team. Missing a segregation or retention requirement forces costly retrofits or non-compliance penalties after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that trial actions alter live device states or disrupt sterile fill-finish equipment
rows.plain: Make sure testing the software cannot change the settings of working equipment or interrupt sterile product-filling operations. This helps protect live production from accidental disruption.
rows.imp_rat: Although trial actions disrupting sterile fill-finish equipment could be serious, this batch's ranking forces both proxies to 1; the error is confined to the evaluation stage and capped accordingly.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standardized safe-trial mode for industrial control software, so preventing trial actions from altering live device states relies on fragile manual precautions. Ensuring trial actions cannot touch live equipment requires careful isolation setup that lengthens evaluation prep. Safely isolating trials from live sterile fill-finish equipment demands high-end controls and safety expertise. A disruption to sterile equipment during a trial could cause enormous batch and downtime losses, making this a high-stakes cost risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the trial package cannot run in the approved evaluation environment
rows.plain: Make sure the trial software can run in the approved setup used for testing. This helps prevent wasted time and an incomplete evaluation.
rows.imp_rat: A trial package failing in the evaluation environment only delays pre-purchase assessment, not live control; relevance and severity stay at 1 under the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Trials frequently fail to run in the approved, restricted evaluation environment because vendor trials assume unrestricted setups. Getting a trial package to run in the approved evaluation environment often needs setup and approval work that delays evaluation. Configuring a trial to run within a validated evaluation environment requires specialized OT and compliance expertise. Standing up a compliant evaluation sandbox adds cost that some buyers cannot justify.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that security or validation restrictions block access to representative device configurations
rows.plain: Make sure security and approval rules do not prevent people from trying realistic versions of the device settings.
rows.imp_rat: Security or validation restrictions blocking access to representative device configurations is a workflow friction; ranked below prior batch, capped at 1 on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Security and validation restrictions routinely block access to representative configurations, so evaluations often run on unrepresentative setups. Obtaining representative device configurations for evaluation is delayed by change-control and security approvals. Navigating validation restrictions to expose representative configs safely requires niche compliance and controls expertise. Setting up sanitized representative environments adds cost but avoids larger downstream waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required control, configuration, and monitoring functions excluded from the trial
rows.plain: Test every required way the software controls, sets up, and watches the equipment, so important functions are not overlooked before choosing it.
rows.imp_rat: Control, configuration, and monitoring functions excluded from a trial touches the CFJ but only at the evaluation stage (low relevance here), and the batch ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Trials commonly exclude some required functions due to limited scope or environment, leaving coverage gaps. Setting up a trial covering required functions takes setup time but is generally feasible pre-purchase. Exercising all control, configuration, and monitoring functions in a trial demands engineering skill to design representative tests. Trials are usually free or low-cost, limiting financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from requesting the trial until runnable access is granted
rows.plain: Spend less time between asking to try software and being allowed to run it. This lets the user evaluate the software sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Delay from trial request to runnable access is an evaluation-onboarding wait, not central to control operations (low relevance), and the ceiling forces severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Trial access is reasonably dependable for common products but industrial vendors often route requests through sales gating that introduces variable waits. Many industrial software vendors offer download-and-run trials or demo licenses granted within a day, though gated evaluations still require sales contact. Requesting and launching a trial is generally straightforward and low-skill. Trials are usually free, so cost is a non-issue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in usage rights for production, test, redundant, and disaster-recovery environments
rows.plain: Clearly state where the software may be used, including for production, testing, backup systems, and recovery after a major failure. This helps people choose permission levels that cover their real operations.
rows.imp_rat: Unclear usage rights across environments is a licensing-governance concern, peripheral to controlling devices; both proxies held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-environment usage rights are inconsistently documented, so ambiguity about DR and redundant instances is common. Confirming usage rights across production, test, redundant, and DR environments requires extended contract review. Understanding which environments are covered demands licensing expertise often outside the team. Assuming rights that don't exist for DR or test environments leads to compliance fees.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of licensing-model selection delays caused by conflicting scope and usage requirements
rows.plain: Spend less time choosing a payment and usage plan when the required work and expected use do not agree. This helps the organization select the right plan sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Licensing-model selection delays are a procurement-scoping error, peripheral to running the control software; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is no standardized comparison tooling, so outcomes vary widely with the individual reps and buyers involved. Reconciling conflicting scope and usage requirements to pick a licensing model still involves back-and-forth between procurement, engineering and vendor sales reps that routinely stretches over weeks. Interpreting industrial control license models (per-seat, per-device, floating, runtime) demands scarce specialist judgement that most acquisition teams lack in-house. Delays here mostly burn staff time rather than direct money, though wrong picks force costly renegotiation later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the intended operating period not covered by the vendor's product lifecycle commitment
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier promises support for nearly all of the time the product is expected to be used. This reduces the risk of being left without help while the product is still needed.
rows.imp_rat: Gaps between operating period and vendor lifecycle commitment are a long-term risk-planning concern, not central to immediate device control; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors publish lifecycle policies but rarely commit contractually to a buyer's full operating period, leaving frequent uncovered tail years. Confirming a vendor's lifecycle commitment against a - operating period requires slow negotiation and roadmap review. Judging whether a stated support horizon is credible needs experience with vendor discontinuation patterns. Coverage gaps in the operating period trigger forced migrations whose costs dwarf the original license.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in support response and restoration commitments for production-critical incidents
rows.plain: Clearly state how quickly help must arrive and how quickly service must be restored after a serious production problem. This helps people understand how much downtime they can expect.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in support restoration commitments matters during incidents but is administrative relative to the CFJ; capped at 1 for both proxies by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: SLAs are standard practice and cover response times, yet restoration commitments for critical incidents often remain vaguely worded. Clarifying support response and restoration commitments happens during SLA negotiation, a bounded process. Interpreting SLA language for production-critical incidents needs some contractual and operational judgement. Weak restoration commitments expose the buyer to costly downtime, but SLAs cap much of this.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of security and quality-related update conditions left unspecified in the update policy
rows.plain: Clearly state what security and quality conditions must be met when updates are provided. This prevents important expectations from being left open to interpretation.
rows.imp_rat: Unspecified update conditions in the policy is a governance-documentation defect peripheral to core control (low relevance) with clarifiable consequences (low severity). Capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Update policies are commonly vague on security and quality triggers, leaving critical conditions unspecified at acquisition. Clarifying update conditions requires waiting on vendor policy statements that are often slow to arrive. Assessing whether update terms cover security and quality obligations needs specialized contract and cybersecurity judgement. Unspecified update terms create uncertain future spend but little immediate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts among support tiers, update terms, and end-of-life dates in the vendor's acquisition documents
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier’s documents agree about levels of help, software or product updates, and when support will end. This helps the buyer understand what support will be available and for how long.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts in support tiers and end-of-life dates are contract-documentation errors, peripheral to control; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: With no automated cross-checking, conflicting terms routinely slip through into signed agreements. Spotting conflicts among support tiers, update terms and end-of-life dates requires slow manual reading of scattered acquisition documents. Reconciling contractual terms across documents relies on careful contract-savvy reviewers. Undetected conflicts lead to unexpected support-gap or renewal costs later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for vendor clarification of unresolved support, update, and product lifecycle terms
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for a seller to explain unclear promises about support, updates, and how long a product will be available. This helps the buyer understand what help and future changes to expect.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for vendor clarification of lifecycle/support terms is a contractual-clarity delay far from executing control (low relevance), and the ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Answers arrive inconsistently and are sometimes vague or non-binding, leaving lifecycle terms unresolved at decision time. Vendor clarification of support, update and lifecycle terms typically requires email or sales cycles that stretch over days to weeks. Framing the right contractual and lifecycle questions takes procurement and technical judgement. Delays carry indirect cost but the clarification process itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required target device connections omitted from the add-on purchase scope
rows.plain: Reduce the number of needed connections to devices that are accidentally left out of the software add-on purchase. This helps ensure the added software can connect to everything it needs to.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted target-device connections in add-on scope is a purchase-scoping ambiguity (low relevance) that is amendable (low severity). Held at the batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Connection scope is frequently misjudged during purchase, so omissions surface after the order is placed. Reconciling device connection counts against add-on scope adds review cycles but is not the main delay. Understanding which connections map to which entitlements needs engineering knowledge that buyers often lack. Under-scoping connections leads to costly change orders or extra license purchases later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of procured add-ons incompatible with the core platform or target device firmware
rows.plain: Buy fewer software add-ons that do not work with the main system or the device software. This helps prevent wasted money and delays.
rows.imp_rat: Procured add-ons incompatible with platform/firmware touches functionality but the batch ranking places it lowest, so both relevance and severity are capped at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility matrices exist but are incomplete, so incompatible add-ons still get procured. Confirming add-on compatibility with platform and firmware often needs pre-purchase testing that adds delay. Assessing firmware and platform compatibility requires specialized controls-engineering knowledge. Incompatible add-ons become wasted spend and forced replacements.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required deployment instances not covered by the acquired add-on entitlements
rows.plain: Buy enough extra software features to cover every place where they will be used. This prevents some installations from lacking the required permission or capability.
rows.imp_rat: Deployment instances uncovered by add-on entitlements is a licensing-scope shortfall peripheral to core control (low relevance) and remediable by purchasing coverage (low severity). Capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Instance counting versus complex add-on terms is error-prone, so coverage gaps recur. Verifying add-on entitlement coverage against all required deployment instances adds counting and mapping effort before purchase. Understanding add-on licensing scope per instance requires product-specific licensing knowledge. Under-covered instances mean scrambling to buy more add-ons, often at less favorable terms.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between purchase authorization and availability of required add-ons for integration
rows.plain: There should be less time between approving a purchase and receiving the extra software needed to connect it with other systems. This helps the new software be integrated sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Time to make purchased add-ons available for integration is a supply-timing concern peripheral to the running software's job (low relevance), with severity capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Whether an add-on is promptly available depends on inconsistent vendor and reseller fulfillment, making timing unpredictable. Required integration add-ons often come from third parties or separate procurement cycles, so availability after purchase authorization can lag days to weeks. Identifying and sourcing the right add-ons needs moderate integration knowledge but is manageable for typical engineers. Add-ons are separately priced and licensing delays can idle staff, adding indirect cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of procured add-ons lacking evidence required for qualification in the sterile fill-finish project
